A 30-year-old man with psoriatic arthritis (on adalimumab for 6 years), hereditary spherocytosis, and a history of cutaneous herpes zoster infection presented with two weeks of intermittent fevers and headaches and a 10-lb weight loss over 9 months. He had temporarily discontinued and then restarted adalimumab and, one month after returning from travel to Mexico, developed worsening headache, persistent fever, left facial paresis, and loss of taste.

On examination, visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/50 in the left eye, with a left seventh-nerve palsy. Anterior segment examination showed only exposure keratopathy of the left eye, with no anterior chamber cell or flare. Funduscopic examination revealed dozens of 100–200 µm, round, creamy yellow-white choroidal infiltrates disseminated throughout both fundi, without vitritis, vasculitis, or optic nerve edema. Optical coherence tomography demonstrated hyporeflective choroidal lesions without macular cysts or thickening.

Laboratory studies showed negative serum testing for Lyme disease, syphilis, tuberculosis, and West Nile virus. Complete blood count and chemistry panel were within normal limits. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 8 white blood cells/mm3 (91% lymphocytes), normal glucose and protein, and negative cryptococcal antigen and other organisms. MRI of the brain and orbits demonstrated enhancement of the left facial nerve. Chest CT was performed and revealed mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fungal or mycobacterial meningitis — “Given the patient's history of immunosuppressive therapy and travel to Mexico, he was admitted for urgent evaluation for suspected fungal or mycobacterial meningitis… CSF examination was similarly negative for organisms including cryptococcal antigen…” 2. Other infectious etiologies (Lyme, syphilis, tuberculosis, West Nile virus) — “Serum testing for Lyme, syphilis, tuberculosis (TB), and West Nile virus was negative.” 3. Birdshot chorioretinopathy — “Lymphoma and birdshot chorioretinopathy (BSC) are important masquerades for sarcoid-associated choroiditis; however, workup for lymphoma was negative and retinal imaging and examination did not support a diagnosis of BSC.”

title: Utility of nerve ultrasound in the management of primary neurolymphomatosis: Case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 52-year-old man presented with progressive right upper limb weakness and sensory disturbance. In 2018, he had right facial paralysis that resolved with corticosteroids; two years later, he noted numbness of the left face and neck. In February 2022, he developed persistent pain and numbness in his right hand, and by June 2022 he was unable to raise his right arm. He was initially treated with two courses of intravenous methylprednisolone (1,000 mg daily for three days) and one course of intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg daily), without improvement. The weakness progressed, and his right facial paralysis recurred, prompting referral in July 2022.

On examination, he had right facial paralysis and diffuse right upper limb weakness (Medical Research Council grade 0–1/5) with severe muscle atrophy and areflexia from C5 to T1 distributions. There was severe dysesthesia and hypoesthesia in the right upper limb and mild dysesthesia in the left face and anterior neck. Vitals were normal.

Blood tests showed negative vasculitis-associated immune markers, including antinuclear and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and an elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor level (1,198 U/mL). Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 14 cells/μL and a normal protein level.

Conventional nerve conduction studies demonstrated absent compound muscle and sensory nerve action potentials in the right median and ulnar nerves, with markedly reduced amplitude in the right axillary nerve; studies in the lower limbs were normal. The pattern indicated axonal degeneration involving postganglionic segments.

Nerve ultrasound (18 MHz) of the right median nerve at the forearm showed marked enlargement (cross-sectional area, 54 mm²) with swollen fascicles and perineurium, and a fusiform hypoechoic mass between the nerve and thickened epineurium. The right ulnar and musculocutaneous nerves also showed segmental enlargement with loss of the normal fascicular pattern. Color Doppler ultrasound revealed massive enlargement and increased vascularity of the right brachial plexus and cervical nerve roots. Subsequent MRI confirmed massive swelling of the right median nerve, nerve roots, and brachial plexus. Fluorine-18 fluorodeoxyglucose PET/CT demonstrated abnormal FDG uptake in these same structures, suggesting neurolymphomatosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Non‐systemic vasculitic neuropathy was initially considered due to asymmetric multiple mononeuropathies without systemic findings — “Because of the asymmetric clinical symptoms, suggesting multiple mononeuropathies, and the lack of particular clinical and laboratory findings in other organs, non-systemic vasculitic neuropathy (NSVN) was initially suspected.” 2. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy was considered based on plexus hypertrophy on MRI — “Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) was also suspected based on the hypertrophy of the right brachial plexus observed on MRI, although NCS suggested axonal neuropathy.” 3. NSVN was made less likely by negative vasculitis markers — “Blood tests revealed negative vasculitis-associated immune markers, including antinuclear and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies.” 4. CIDP was unlikely given an axonal pattern on nerve conduction studies — “the absent or diminished CMAP indicated that the main pathophysiology of the patient was axonal degeneration.” 5. Neurolymphomatosis was suspected based on characteristic ultrasound findings matching prior cases — “These findings were compatible with those reported in past NL cases.”

title: Secondary Syphilis with Pulmonary Involvement
case_prompt:
A 39-year-old Japanese man presented with three weeks of left chest pain and a rash on his hands and feet. His history included allergic rhinitis; he smoked 5–10 cigarettes per day and drank socially. Three months earlier, he had sexual intercourse with a commercial sex worker; he had not traveled abroad. 

He denied fever, cough, sputum production, or weight loss. A local plain CT scan performed three weeks earlier showed multiple nodular shadows in the left lower lobe, and metastatic lung cancer was suspected. Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT one week later again showed multiple nodular shadows in the left lower lobe, slight enlargement of left mediastinal and bilateral axillary lymph nodes, and a small left pleural effusion; upper and lower endoscopy were normal. He was referred for further evaluation.

On arrival, temperature was 36.8°C, pulse 85/min, blood pressure 102/60 mmHg, and SpO2 99% on room air. Examination revealed several millimeter oral aphthae, numerous soft, non-tender cervical lymph nodes bilaterally, and multiple pale red, scaly, nodular erythematous patches 3–8 mm in size on the palms and soles. Heart and lung examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 10,600/μL, platelet count of 621,000/μL, γ-glutamyl transpeptidase of 161 U/L, C-reactive protein of 3.13 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 62 mm/h, and soluble interleukin-2 receptor of 1180 U/mL. Tests for HIV antigen/antibody, hepatitis B and C, HTLV-1 antibody, and tumor markers (CEA, SCC, CYFRA, Pro-GRP, NSE) were negative. β-D glucan, rheumatoid factor, MPO-ANCA, and PR3-ANCA were all negative.

Chest radiography showed blunting of the left costophrenic angle without discrete lung field opacities. Doppler ultrasonography of the neck confirmed multiple enlarged lymph nodes with preserved hila. PET-CT demonstrated high uptake in bilateral cervical, subclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, and pelvic lymph nodes but no abnormal uptake elsewhere.

Cervical lymph node biopsy revealed lymphoid follicular hyperplasia, enlarged germinal centers, paracortical dilation, vascular proliferation, and fibrosis. Bacterial and acid-fast bacillus smears and cultures were negative, as was PCR for Mycobacterium DNA. Immunostaining of germinal centers was negative for B-cell lymphoma, and flow cytometry and genetic studies supported reactive lymphadenopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary or metastatic lung cancer — “metastatic lung cancer was suspected at the first visit.” 2. Malignant lymphoma — “immunostaining of the germinal centers was negative for B-cell lymphoma.” 3. Tuberculosis — “tests for Mycobacterium DNA using polymerase chain reaction” were all negative. 4. Mycosis — “β-D glucan was negative.” 5. Rheumatoid nodules — “rheumatoid factor was negative.” 6. Septic embolization — “bacterial culture smear ... were all negative.”

title: Beyond Cat Scratch Disease: A Case Report of Bartonella Infection Mimicking Vasculitic Disorder
case_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman presented with a history since February 2008 of painful left axillary lymphadenopathy accompanied by low-grade fever. Initial excisional biopsy showed changes suggestive but not diagnostic of Castleman’s disease. A CT scan revealed an 8×6 cm colliquative mass in the left axillary and supraclavicular regions, smaller reactive nodes in the right axilla and inguinal areas, and subcentimeter nodules in the lung and liver. Repeat lymph node biopsies through 2010 demonstrated nonspecific necrotizing lymphadenitis; microbiologic and virologic studies, including HHV-6 and HHV-8, were negative. A subsequent CT scan confirmed persistent axillary and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, bilateral inguinal enlargement, and a perianal mass. Review of prior histology noted necrotizing lymphadenitis with epithelioid and giant cells and vasculitis without lymphoproliferation.  

In January 2011, rheumatologic evaluation revealed nasal chondritis with crusted lesions, chronic conjunctivitis, violaceous purpuric areas on the extremities, ulcerative palmar and gluteal lesions, and persistent evening fevers. Examination showed mobile, nontender axillary and inguinal nodes. Nasal mucosal biopsy demonstrated acute and chronic inflammation but no vasculitis or granulomas. Laboratory studies revealed neutrophilic leukocytosis and elevated acute-phase reactants. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were negative, whereas IgG and IgM anti–β2-glycoprotein I and anticardiolipin antibodies were positive. Cytomegalovirus IgM and Epstein–Barr virus IgM were detected, but antigenemia was negative and PCR non-significant. Bartonella henselae serology returned a titer of 1:128.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Castleman’s disease — “the first biopsy with histology suggestive but not diagnostic for Castelman's disease was made.” 2. Wegener’s granulomatosis — “necrotizing lymphadenitis with epithelioid/giant cells and vasculitis in the absence of lymphoproliferation signs was observed. These aspects were considered as not inconsistent with vasculitic disease.” 3. Viral infections (CMV and EBV) — “IgM Cytomegalovirus-antibodies (with negative antigenemia) and IgM Epstein-Barr virus antibodies (with not significant PCR amplification) were found.”

title: Sertoli-Leydig Cell Tumor as a Cause of Elevated 17-OH Progesterone
case_prompt:
A 22-year-old Chinese woman was referred for evaluation of oligomenorrhea. Menarche was at age 12 with previously regular 30-day cycles. Over the past year, she noted spotting two to three times weekly. She denied weight gain, hirsutism, Cushingoid features, or thyroid symptoms. She denied medications or exogenous androgens.

On examination, blood pressure was 128/70 mm Hg, BMI 18.1. She had mild facial acne, deeper voice, and mild clitoral enlargement (10 × 5 mm); there was no hirsutism by Ferriman–Gallwey score. Abdominal, cardiac, respiratory, thyroid, and neurologic examinations were normal.

Initial laboratory evaluation showed:
– Total testosterone 5.35 nmol/L (154.3 ng/dL) [reference 0.48–1.85 nmol/L]  
– 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17OHP) 18.9 nmol/L (623.7 ng/dL) [follicular phase 0.3–3.3; luteal phase 2.9–15.2 nmol/L]

A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound demonstrated a normal uterus and a left ovarian cyst measuring 18.2 × 16 mm, interpreted as a simple physiological cyst; the right ovary was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ovarian hyperthecosis and polycystic ovary syndrome were included within the ovarian differential for hyperandrogenism. “main differentials at this point included … ovarian causes including virilizing ovarian tumors (VOTs), ovarian hyperthecosis, or polycystic ovary syndrome.”

title: Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis syndrome following meningitis and systemic lymphadenopathy with persistent Toxoplasma immunoglobulin M: a case report
case_prompt:
A 34-year-old Japanese man with a history of gastric ulcer treated with esomeprazole presented with 2 days of fever to 40 °C, headache, myalgia, fatigue, and vomiting starting 2 days after a finger laceration during sewage-plumbing work. Influenza A/B tests were negative; he received amoxicillin and ibuprofen without improvement. 

On day 6, he was alert with temperature 38.5 °C, heart rate 88 bpm, blood pressure 133/69 mmHg, neck stiffness, positive Kernig sign at 60°, and tender bilateral cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy. White blood cell count was 8200/mm3 (6.0% eosinophils). Serum creatinine was 0.91 mg/dL; BUN 6.0 mg/dL. ESR was 55 mm/hr; CRP 11.58 mg/dL. Urinalysis showed no protein or cells. Head CT was normal. CSF opening pressure was 235 mmH2O; CSF had 1 cell/µL, glucose 66 mg/dL, protein 31 mg/dL; Gram stain and cultures of CSF and blood were negative. Oral levofloxacin was started.

Despite CRP falling to 0.67 mg/dL by day 17, creatinine rose to 2.74 mg/dL and peripheral eosinophils reached 12.5%. Levofloxacin and esomeprazole were stopped on day 17. He was transferred on day 20 with creatinine 2.09 mg/dL, urinary β2-microglobulin 1589 µg/L, N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase 7.9 IU/L, and FeNa 2.1%. Renal sonography was unremarkable.

Renal biopsy on day 22 showed focal interstitial edema, mononuclear and eosinophilic infiltrates, mild tubulitis, and four noncaseating granulomas; glomeruli and vessels were preserved. Immunofluorescence was negative. Acid-fast and Grocott stains were negative. ACE level was normal. Chest X-ray and CT were normal; gallium scan showed no uptake. Autoantibodies including ANA, ANCA (MPO and PR3), anti-SS-A/SS-B, anti-dsDNA, and anti-GBM were negative; viral serologies (HIV, hepatitis, EBV, CMV, mumps) were not consistent with acute infection.

On day 29, he developed bilateral eye pain, conjunctival hyperemia, photophobia, and blurred vision. Best-corrected visual acuity was 1.2 OD and 1.5 OS; intraocular pressures were normal. Slit-lamp examination revealed bilateral iritis with keratic precipitates and anterior chamber cells (1+ OD, 0.5+ OS). Dilated funduscopic exam was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sarcoidosis was considered but excluded because lymphadenopathy was tender rather than firm and nontender, and there was no hilar lymphadenopathy, proteinuria, or elevated ACE on imaging and labs — “lymph node enlargement in sarcoidosis is typically firm, nontender, and freely movable. As opposed to this clinical phenotype, our case had lymphadenopathy with tenderness and did not have hilar lymphadenopathy, proteinuria, elevated ACE, and other findings such as hepatomegaly, nephrocalcinosis, or nephrolithiasis on CT scan and on Gallium scintigraphy.” 2. Systemic lupus erythematosus was considered but excluded because ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies were negative — “ANA… and anti-dsDNA… were negative.” 3. EBV-associated infectious mononucleosis was considered but excluded because EBV serologies were not compatible with acute infection — “Epstein–Barr (EBV)… serologies were not compatible with acute infection.” 4. Tuberculosis was considered but excluded because acid-fast staining was negative and chest imaging showed no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy — “Acid-fast staining for Mycobacterium was negative… Chest X-ray and computed tomography did not reveal any abnormal masses, or mediastinum or hilar lymphadenopathy in the lung.” 5. Bacterial and fungal infections were considered but excluded because no organisms were seen on Gram stain, cultures were negative, and Grocott staining was negative — “no organisms were observed on Gram stain… No bacterial growth was detected… Grocott staining for detection of fungi was also negative.” 6. TINU syndrome was confirmed because the patient met criteria for biopsy-proven acute interstitial nephritis and bilateral uveitis without evidence of other systemic diseases — “Hence, the diagnosis of acute tubulointerstitial nephritis and bilateral uveitis (TINU) syndrome was made,”

title: Use of multiple anticholinergic medications can predispose patients to severe non-exertional hyperthermia
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old woman was found unresponsive by her husband in their non–air-conditioned home during a heatwave, with outdoor temperatures above 33°C (90°F). Her medical history included bipolar depression, diabetes mellitus, migraine headaches, and a known seizure disorder. Home medications were:
• Amitriptyline 175 mg daily  
• Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg daily  
• Lurasidone 80 mg daily  
• Benztropine 1 mg three times daily  
• Topiramate extended release 100 mg daily  
• Clonazepam 0.5 mg daily  
• Trazodone 100 mg daily  
• Sitagliptin 25 mg daily  
• Erenumab 70 mg monthly

Emergency medical services noted that she was febrile en route. In the emergency department, her blood pressure was 84/42 mmHg, and rectal temperature was 42°C. Her skin was warm and dry, and her mucous membranes were dry. On neurologic examination she was responsive only to painful stimuli, with no focal deficits, hyperreflexia, or rigidity. Initial laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 11 400 cells/mm3 and a normal creatine phosphokinase level. A screening electrocardiogram was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sepsis was the initial consideration given hypotension, high fever, and leukocytosis — “she was initially thought to be severely septic and was immediately started on broad spectrum antibiotics.” 2. Serotonin syndrome was considered due to multiple serotonergic medications but judged unlikely because of absent neuromuscular findings — “Serotonin syndrome was also considered due to the serotonergic actions of trazodone, amitriptyline, lurasidone and cyclobenzaprine. However, there was low suspicion for this as she presented with no neurological deficits other than altered mental status.” 3. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome was a potential mimic but excluded by lack of rigidity or hyperreflexia — “She did not have any focal neurological deficits, hyperreflexia or rigidity.”

title: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to the heart: an unusual cause of ST elevation—a case report
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue, treated 16 months earlier with hemi-glossectomy and chemoradiation, presented with a 1-month history of progressively worsening severe, sharp, constant right shoulder and chest pain, associated with right arm numbness, productive cough, and dyspnoea on exertion. The pain was exacerbated by right arm movement. She denied trauma or travel.

Her medications included pembrolizumab and recent head and neck radiation. She had a 1.5-year smoking history (0.5 pack per day), social alcohol use, and no significant family history of cancer aside from a grandmother with brain cancer.

On examination, she appeared frail with weight loss. Vital signs: heart rate 86 bpm; blood pressure 121/74 mm Hg. Pulmonary auscultation revealed coarse rales and rhonchi in the left lower lung field. Cardiac examination showed a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. She had tender right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and swelling with erythema over the right suprascapular area.

Laboratory studies showed haemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, haematocrit 28.8%, total calcium 10.5 mg/dL, ionized calcium 1.51 mmol/L. Admission ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads and T-wave inversions in the anterolateral leads. Troponin I peaked at 0.29 ng/L (normal <0.05 ng/L). Chest radiograph revealed a left lower lobe infiltrate, progression of a known left upper lobe cavitary lesion, and a new right clavicular lesion.

Differential diagnosis at this point included acute coronary syndrome, myocarditis due to pembrolizumab, and Takotsubo cardiomyopathy in a cancer patient.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Myocarditis due to pembrolizumab — “Differential diagnosis at this point included … myocarditis due to pembrolizumab …” 2. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy — “Differential diagnosis at this point included … Takotsubo cardiomyopathy in a cancer patient.”

title: Complete gallbladder torsion diagnosed with sequential computed tomography scans: a case report
case_prompt:
An 84-year-old woman was admitted with a  one-day history of left lower abdominal pain and nausea without fever. She had a history of hysterectomy, femoral neck fracture repair, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Her body mass index was 18.2 kg/m2; vital signs were within normal limits. On examination, there was tenderness in the left lower abdomen without peritoneal signs and no Murphy sign. Laboratory studies, including inflammatory markers, liver function tests, and biliary enzymes, were all within normal limits. 
A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen at admission showed an enlarged gallbladder with wall thickening but no gallstones, along with prominent small-bowel gas, mild intestinal dilatation, and slight wall thickening. Abdominal ultrasonography confirmed an enlarged, thick-walled gallbladder without stones. She was diagnosed with acute enterocolitis or subileus and managed conservatively with bowel rest and intravenous fluids.
Six hours later, she developed sudden severe right upper quadrant pain and vomiting unresponsive to analgesics or antiemetics. A repeat CT scan demonstrated that the gallbladder had become more distended and thickened, had rotated approximately 180°, and had shifted toward the midline; the cystic duct was now located on the right side of the gallbladder.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Initial consideration of acute enterocolitis or subileus — “we diagnosed her condition as acute enterocolitis or subileus and began a starvation and transfusion cure.” 2. Consideration of bile duct malignancy intraoperatively — “we could not completely exclude the possibility of bile duct malignancy (painless gallbladder swelling and wall thickening in an elderly patient).”

title: Sclerosing Angiomatoid Nodular Transformation of the Spleen: Lessons from a Rare Case and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 41-year-old woman with no prior medical history was referred for evaluation of suspected early breast cancer. She was asymptomatic. On examination, there were no palpable masses in the breasts, axillae, or abdomen. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count and serum tumor markers (CEA 1.8 ng/mL, CA15-3 11.4 U/mL, BCA225 30.7 U/mL, NCC-ST-439 1.3 U/mL, soluble IL-2 receptor 322 U/mL), were all within normal limits.

Mammography revealed a high-density mass with clustered microcalcifications in the inferior lateral right breast. Chest CT confirmed a well-enhancing right breast mass without axillary lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology of the breast lesion yielded class V cells, consistent with malignancy.

As part of staging, abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a heterogeneous hypoechoic mass with internal blood flow signals arising from the splenic periphery. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed a 40-mm well-circumscribed splenic lesion with less enhancement than the surrounding parenchyma in the arterial and portal phases, becoming isodense in the delayed phase. MRI of the abdomen revealed a lesion isointense to the spleen on T1- and T2-weighted images, with slow centripetal enhancement reaching parity with the spleen at 10 minutes post-contrast. PET-CT showed no significant FDG uptake in the splenic lesion.

The preoperative differential diagnoses for the splenic mass included benign vascular neoplasms (hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, inflammatory pseudotumor), angiosarcoma, solitary splenic metastasis of breast cancer, and primary splenic lymphoma. Given the inability to establish a definitive diagnosis noninvasively, the decision was made to perform a partial mastectomy with sentinel node biopsy and a simultaneous laparoscopic splenectomy for therapeutic diagnosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignant lymphoma — considered as another possible malignant splenic lesion — “we were unable to rule out the possibility of solitary splenic metastasis of the breast cancer or another malignant lesion, such as malignant lymphoma.” 2. Hemangioma — considered as the most common benign splenic vascular tumor — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.” 3. Lymphangioma — considered among benign vascular differentials — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.” 4. Littoral cell angioma — included in benign vascular differential — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.” 5. Hamartoma — considered in the benign vascular spectrum — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.” 6. Angiosarcoma — considered as a primary splenic malignancy — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.” 7. Inflammatory pseudotumor — considered due to potential fibrosclerotic change in splenic lesions — “Differential benign diagnoses of the splenic mass included hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, angiosarcoma, and inflammatory pseudotumor.”

title: Hypoglycemia After Ingestion of “Street Valium” Containing Glyburide, Alcohol, and Cocaine
case_prompt:
A 33-year-old woman was found unresponsive and diaphoretic by paramedics. A point-of-care blood glucose measurement was 18 mg/dL. She received 12.5 g of intravenous dextrose, regained consciousness, and her glucose rose to 109 mg/dL. 

On arrival at the hospital, she was alert. Vital signs were blood pressure 117/68 mm Hg, heart rate 73 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination showed mild distress, moist oral mucosa, and a nonfocal neurologic examination with normal pupils.

In the emergency department, her venous blood glucose was 15 mg/dL. She received two additional doses of dextrose 50% in water, improving her glucose to 45 mg/dL, followed by an infusion of dextrose 5% in water, which raised her glucose to 96 mg/dL.

Once stable and alert, she reported that the previous evening at 5 pm she took two light blue pills given by a friend for anxiety, believing they were diazepam. She felt unwell overnight with nausea and diaphoresis, awoke at 4 am with tremulousness, improved after a large lunch at 11 am, then used cocaine and consumed three beers before losing consciousness at dinner around 7 pm.

Her history was notable for substance use disorder and anxiety. She denied personal or family history of diabetes or use of hypoglycemic medications. Current medications included methadone; she had no allergies. Social history included daily tobacco use and occasional beer consumption.

Urine toxicology was positive for benzodiazepines, ethanol, and cocaine. Laboratory studies revealed a blood glucose of 44 mg/dL, insulin level of 47.4 mIU/mL (normal 2.6–24.9), and C-peptide of 5.4 ng/mL (normal 1.1–4.4). Morning cortisol, thyroid-stimulating hormone, free thyroxine, and insulin antibodies were within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consider ecstasy, amphetamine, or cannabis as causes of hypoglycemia — “Illicit drugs that are known to cause hypoglycemia include ecstasy, amphetamine, canabis, and sulfonylureas marketed as benzodiazepines.” 2. Consider alcohol-induced hypoglycemia due to decreased gluconeogenesis — “Alcohol is metabolized by the liver, resulting in decreased gluconeogenesis, which can lead to hypoglycemia.” 3. Consider cocaine intoxication mimicking hypoglycemia — “Cocaine is not known to cause hypoglycemia, but cocaine use and hypoglycemia can have a similar clinical presentation with agitation, tachycardia, increased perspiration, and variable neurologic signs and symptoms.” 4. Distinguish exogenous insulin from endogenous hyperinsulinism — “Laboratory values were consistent with endogenous hyperinsulinism demonstrated by elevated insulin and C-peptide levels and a low-blood glucose level.” 5. Explain sedation as hypoglycemia mimic of benzodiazepines — “Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia leads to clinical sedation, mimicking benzodiazepine effects.”

title: Giant cell myocarditis following COVID‐19 successfully treated by immunosuppressive therapy
case_prompt:
A 46-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of new-onset heart failure. She reported exertional dyspnea (NYHA class II) and fatigue. Her history was notable for lichen planus dermatitis treated with immunosuppression several years earlier and TPO antibody–positive hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. One month earlier, she had mild COVID-19 confirmed by RT-PCR, with no hypoxemia or pulmonary involvement, which resolved with outpatient therapy. Three weeks later, she developed severe chest discomfort and shortness of breath. ECG showed ST-segment elevations in leads V2–V6 and troponin-I was elevated, but coronary angiography revealed normal epicardial arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal LV size, apical hypokinesia, and an EF of 35–40%; RV size and function were normal. Cardiac MRI showed mildly enlarged LV size (LVEF 35%), a thin and aneurysmal apex, and widespread patchy subepicardial to mid-wall fibrosis in inferolateral and septal segments with subendocardial fibrosis at the apex; the report recommended evaluation for recent viral, autoimmune, or sarcoidosis-related myocarditis.

On exam in January, blood pressure was 115/75 mmHg, heart rate 90/min, oxygen saturation 96%, and bi-basilar crackles were present. Labs showed hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, normal renal and liver function, NT-proBNP 885 pg/mL, normal thyroid tests, and elevated anti-TPO. She was started on guideline-directed heart failure therapy. A few days later, she developed worsening dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, abdominal pain, and nausea. On admission, blood pressure was 85/60 mmHg, heart rate 120/min, jugular venous distention, hepatomegaly, mild ascites, and 2+ pitting edema. Creatinine was 1.7 mg/dL, ALT 46 U/L, AST 40 U/L, total bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL, NT-proBNP >18,000 pg/mL, and troponin-I remained elevated. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with low-voltage QRSs, Q waves in leads I, III, aVF, and V1–V6, ST elevations, and T-wave inversions. Echocardiography revealed mild LV enlargement, EF 15–20%, global hypokinesia, apical akinesia, mild RV enlargement with severe dysfunction (TAPSE 10 mm, RV Sm 6 cm/s), moderate to severe mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, a plethoric IVC (23 mm), and TR gradient 25 mmHg. She received IV milrinone and diuretics. Right heart catheterization showed a cardiac index of 1.4 L/min/m2, CVP 16 mmHg, PCWP 17 mmHg, and mixed venous O2 saturation 48%. She was scheduled for emergent endomyocardial biopsy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute myocardial infarction was initially suspected given ST elevations and troponin elevation, but “coronary angiography … revealed normal epicardial coronary arteries.”

title: A Case of Nonislet Cell Tumor Hypoglycemia Due to Metastatic Salivary Myoepithelial Carcinoma
case_prompt: A 63-year-old woman with metastatic myoepithelial carcinoma of the left parotid gland, type 2 diabetes (managed with metformin until recently), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and hypothyroidism presented for routine chemotherapy infusion and was found to have a preinfusion serum glucose level of 30 mg/dL. She reported several episodes over the past week of morning and evening confusion and tremulousness after fasting several hours; finger-stick glucometers confirmed values in the 30s and symptoms resolved with sugar-containing foods, fulfilling Whipple’s triad. Metformin had been discontinued after hypoglycemia began. On admission, renal function was normal. Liver enzymes were stably elevated, with normal bilirubin, INR, and a mildly decreased albumin. During a hypoglycemic episode (glucose 33 mg/dL), laboratory evaluation showed insulin <1 μU/mL (reference 3–25), C-peptide <0.5 ng/mL (reference 1.1–4.3), β-hydroxybutyrate <1.0 mmol/L (reference <3), and random cortisol 23 μg/dL (reference morning 8–25). Thyroid studies showed TSH 8.3 mIU/L (reference 0.3–4.7) and free T4 0.80 ng/dL (reference 0.80–1.70), and her levothyroxine dose was increased. Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) was 15 ng/mL (reference 41–279), and IGF-2 was 147 ng/mL (reference 180–580), yielding an IGF-2:IGF-1 molar ratio of 10.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Medication‐induced hypoglycemia was considered and ruled out — “after medication-induced hypoglycemia and insulinoma have been excluded.” 2. Insulinoma was considered unlikely given suppressed insulin and C-peptide — “the patient’s low insulin, C-peptide, and β-hydroxybutyrate levels during an episode of hypoglycemia indicated a noninsulin mediated process.” 3. Impaired gluconeogenesis from liver metastases was considered but deemed unlikely because synthetic liver function remained intact — “this was felt to be unlikely as the patient’s liver synthetic function was intact.”

title: Romidepsin-induced neutrophilic urticaria
case_prompt: A 72-year-old Caucasian woman with Sézary syndrome, previously treated with interferon alfa-2b and photopheresis, began romidepsin therapy (14 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15) with bexarotene. Five hours after the second infusion, she noted bright, hot, salmon-pink, nonscaly, edematous nummular patches and plaques in body folds, on the face, upper chest, and back. She described her skin as feeling like a heat rash with minimal pruritus. She had no fever, skin pain, lymphadenopathy, mucositis, arthralgia, transaminitis, neutrophilia, or eosinophilia. Topical desonide and triamcinolone were applied. A punch biopsy showed a neutrophilic infiltrate in the papillary and superficial reticular dermis with mild spongiosis and no epidermal necrosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Conventional drug-induced urticaria was considered because drug eruptions often present as urticarial lesions; however, “Histologically, there is dermal edema with perivascular and interstitial infiltrates of lymphocytes, eosinophils, and neutrophils,” which differs from the predominantly neutrophilic infiltrate in this case. 2. Drug-induced Sweet syndrome was considered because of the dense neutrophilic infiltrate; however, “In drug-induced Sweet syndrome, erythematous plaques are typically painful or tender along with a dense neutrophilic infiltrate and fever, which our patient lacked.”

title: Adipocyte-Like Differentiation in a Posttreatment Embryonal Rhabdomyosarcoma
case_prompt:
A 16-year-old boy noted a progressively enlarging lump at his posterior left knee over one year. MRI revealed a 13 cm mass in the left posteroinferior thigh, centered in the semitendinosus muscle and extending to the biceps femoris. Fine-needle aspiration showed a heterogeneous population of ovoid to tapered spindle cells with densely eosinophilic cytoplasm and occasional multinucleated cells; no lipogenic differentiation was seen. Immunohistochemistry was positive for desmin and myogenin and negative for CD68, keratin, S-100, SOX-10, HMB-45, and Melan-A. FISH for a FOXO1 13q14 rearrangement was negative. A diagnosis of embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma was made, and the patient received 10 weeks of vincristine/irinotecan chemotherapy per protocol ARST0531. 

The tumor was then completely excised. Grossly, it was a mixed cystic and solid mass with a thin white capsule. H&E sections revealed diffuse sheets of round cells with eosinophilic rhabdomyoblastic cytoplasm and large nuclei. Throughout the tumor, a subset of cells exhibited a spectrum of cytoplasmic vacuolation, from small vacuoles to multiple vacuoles indenting the nucleus, resembling adipocytes or lipoblasts. These vacuolated cells were positive for adipophilin, Oil Red O, and S-100 protein; the vacuoles were negative with periodic acid–Schiff stain, with or without diastase. Immunostaining for desmin was positive in the vacuolated cells, whereas myogenin was negative in their nuclei. There was cystic change, scattered histiocytes, extracellular hemosiderin, low mitotic activity (1 mf/10 hpf), and no coagulative necrosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Glycogen vs lipid distinction favored lipid — “the histochemical and immunohistochemical features of the posttreatment excision support lipid accumulation rather than glycogen in the vacuolated cells.” 2. Preexisting lipogenic differentiation unsampled at biopsy considered but unlikely — “the biopsy contained no vacuolated cells whereas such cells were present diffusely in every section of the excision specimen.” 3. Postchemotherapy cytodifferentiation into adipocyte-like cells supported by staining pattern and decreased myogenin — “the decrease in density of myogenin positive cells after chemotherapy suggests a different expression pattern in the posttreated tumor compared to the pretreatment biopsy.”

title: Ureteral Stone Mimics Appendicitis: A Point-of-care Ultrasound Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_prompt: A 17-year-old male presented with several hours of right lower quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and subjective fevers. He had a heart rate of 106 beats per minute, blood pressure of 130/90 mm Hg, and was afebrile. He appeared comfortable at rest. On abdominal examination, he had normal bowel sounds and isolated right lower quadrant tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory tests showed a normal white blood cell count, normal C-reactive protein level, and normal creatinine. A point-of-care ultrasound of the right lower quadrant was performed to evaluate for appendicitis and revealed a hyperechoic circular focus with acoustic shadowing within a hypoechoic, non-peristalsing, non-compressible, tubular structure adjacent to the iliac vessels. There was no surrounding mesenteric fat stranding, free fluid, or hyperemia, and the walls of the structure were thinner and lacked the alternating layered appearance typical of intestinal structures.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Appendicitis was the leading concern but was deemed unlikely on ultrasound because “This was not the typical sonographic appearance of an infected appendix as there was no surrounding mesenteric fat stranding, free fluid, or hyperemia.” 2. Intussusception was included in the initial differential for pediatric RLQ pain — “other diagnoses including intussusception, ovarian pathology, and inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohns must be considered.” 3. Ovarian pathology was also part of the broad differential — “other diagnoses including intussusception, ovarian pathology, and inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohns must be considered.” 4. Inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohn’s colitis was considered in right lower quadrant pain — “other diagnoses including intussusception, ovarian pathology, and inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohns must be considered.” 5. A ureteral stone was suspected based on the ultrasound finding of “a hyperechoic circular focus with acoustic shadowing … within a hypoechoic, non-peristalsing, non-compressible, tubular structure adjacent to the iliac vessels.”

title: Intrasellar arachnoid cyst: A case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt: A 49-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of headache. Physical, neurologic, and ophthalmologic examinations, including visual acuity, fundoscopy, and visual fields, were unremarkable. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a sellar cystic lesion measuring 18 × 14 × 14 mm that extended from the suprasellar cistern through the diaphragma sellae to the dorsum sellae and compressed the optic chiasm. The lesion was hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images; in contrast studies, rim-like peripheral enhancement was noted. The normal pituitary gland and stalk enhanced typically and were displaced laterally to the right by the lesion. Computed tomography of the sellar region showed a hypodense cystic lesion with no contrast enhancement. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, routine biochemistry, pituitary hormone levels, and urinalysis, were within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Rathke’s cleft cyst was considered — “In contrast studies rim like enhancement of the periphery of the lesion was noted… However this finding was thought to be stretched, normal pituitary gland in our case.” 2. Craniopharyngioma was considered — “Solid, contrast-enhancing parts within the cyst are typical features of craniopharyngioma.” 3. Nonfunctional pituitary adenoma was considered — “IACs clinically resemble that of a nonfunctional pituitary adenoma.”

title: Immunoglobulin A dominant membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis in an elderly man: A case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 55-year-old man presented with 1 month of edema and 2 weeks of decreased urine output, fever with chills, breathlessness, anorexia, nausea, and recurrent vomiting. He denied hematuria, cough, sore throat, skin rashes, or joint pains.  
On examination, blood pressure was 180/90 mmHg; there was no organomegaly.  
Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL; WBC 9,300/mm3; platelets 120,000/mm3; serum creatinine 7.0 mg/dL; total protein 6.9 g/dL; albumin 3.4 g/dL. Urine output was 1800 mL in 24 hours, with 4+ protein, 15–20 RBCs/hpf, and 12–15 WBCs/hpf. Twenty-four-hour proteinuria was 4.2 g. Complement levels were C3 68.8 mg/dL (normal 60–120) and C4 17.6 mg/dL (normal 15–25). Serologies for ANA, anti-GBM, and ANCA were negative; HIV, HBsAg, HCV, and cryoglobulins were negative.  
A renal biopsy was performed after hemodialysis.  
Light microscopy showed 22 enlarged glomeruli with lobular accentuation, capillary-wall thickening, endocapillary proliferation, segmental neutrophilic exudate, occasional loop necrosis, hyaline thrombi, and no crescents. Silver stain revealed diffuse basement-membrane splitting. Tubules exhibited acute tubular necrosis; interstitium and vessels were unremarkable.  
Immunofluorescence demonstrated 4+ granular capillary-wall and mesangial deposits of IgA and C3, 2+ IgG with a similar pattern, and negative IgM and C1q; kappa and lambda chains were equal.  
Electron microscopy showed extensive subendothelial electron-dense deposits, GBM duplication with mesangial interposition, some capillary-luminal and mesangial deposits, focal foot-process effacement, and no subepithelial deposits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. IgA-dominant MPGN was favored because “Considering the histology, pattern and type of immune deposits and ultrastructural features, a diagnosis of IgA-dominant Ig mediated MPGN was considered.” 2. IgA nephropathy with an MPGN-like pattern was excluded because “diffuse MPGN-like pattern is not described” in IgA nephropathy and “presence of extensive subendothelial deposits without prominent mesangial or paramesangial deposits is not seen in IgA nephropathy.” 3. IgA-dominant post-infectious glomerulonephritis was ruled out because “electron microscopy resembles post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis and typically shows subepithelial ‘hump’-shaped electron-dense deposits,” yet “No subepithelial deposits were found.”

title: A woman with adult‐onset Still's disease and acute intestinal pseudo‐obstruction
case_prompt:
A 47-year-old woman was admitted with a 10-day history of remittent fevers up to 39°C, fatigue, and sore throat. Initial outpatient treatment with azithromycin followed by garenoxacin yielded no improvement; she then developed muscle pain and lower-extremity edema. 

On admission: temperature 39.7°C; heart rate 135 bpm; respiratory rate 26/min; oxygen saturation 91% on 1 L nasal cannula; mild respiratory failure. Abdominal examination revealed distension, tympany on percussion, and near-absent bowel sounds. Lung auscultation detected wheezing. Both proximal interphalangeal and ankle joints were swollen and tender, with lower-extremity edema. No rash was present.

Despite treatment with sulbactam/ampicillin, fevers persisted above 39°C. Abdominal radiography showed marked colonic gas and upward diaphragms. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated mediastinal lymphadenopathy, dilated colon with fluid levels, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and a small pericardial effusion—without mechanical obstruction. Colonoscopy confirmed no obstructing lesion. A diagnosis of acute intestinal pseudo-obstruction was made on hospital Day 5; nasogastric decompression and metoclopramide yielded no improvement. Clostridium difficile tests were negative, and metronidazole produced no response.

On Day 6 her respiratory status worsened (PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio 177), and she was intubated for presumed ARDS. A salmon-pink, edematous erythema appeared on the trunk and extremities. Discontinuation of all potential culprit drugs failed to improve the rash.

Laboratory studies: WBC 18 560/µL (89% neutrophils); AST 36 U/L; ALT 45 U/L; LDH 430 U/L; ferritin 3551.4 ng/mL; CRP 27.6 mg/dL; ESR 134 mm/h; procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL; ANA <1:40; rheumatoid factor 29 IU/mL; C3 and C4 normal; blood cultures and viral serologies (EBV, hepatitis) negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial infection — “ruled out bacterial infections after seeing no response to the administration of antibiotics and based on the results of various culture tests,” 2. Viral infection — “ruled out viral infection from results of serum antibody tests.” 3. Clostridium difficile infection — “GDH antigen and C difficile (CD) toxin tests were negative, and the response to metronidazole was poor.” 4. Drug-related eruption — “We suspected a drug‐related eruption and discontinued the administration of all suspected drugs, but her rash did not improve.”

title: Rare case of testosterone producing serous cystadenoma
case_prompt: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman with a history of total abdominal hysterectomy 20 years earlier for fibroids presented with a 1-year history of clitoral enlargement, hirsutism, deepening of voice, decreased libido, and increasing abdominal girth. Baseline renal and hepatic chemistries, TSH, and lipid panel were unchanged from 1 year prior except for mildly elevated LDL. Transvaginal ultrasound showed an anechoic, nonvascular, cystic left adnexal lesion measuring 10.5 × 5.9 × 6.2 cm. CT of the abdomen and pelvis confirmed the cystic left adnexal mass and demonstrated no adrenal mass or other intra-abdominal abnormalities. Initial hyperandrogenism workup revealed a free testosterone of 4.2 pg/ml (normal) but a total testosterone of 264.3 ng/dl (elevated), with DHEA-S 29.2 μg/dl, CA-125 5 U/ml, and inhibin-A 1 pg/ml all within normal limits. Three months later, the cyst was unchanged but symptoms persisted. On referral to endocrinology, repeat labs showed free testosterone 4.7 pg/ml, total testosterone 276 ng/dl, FSH 41.7 IU/L, LH 26.6 IU/L (postmenopausal range), suppressed 8 am cortisol of 1.0 μg/dl, and normal DHEA, DHEA-S, and estradiol. Repeat ultrasound showed slight increase of the adnexal mass to 11.7 × 5.5 × 8.3 cm. A multidisciplinary team recommended laparoscopic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Evidence for ovarian rather than adrenal source — “High testosterone levels but normal DHEA and DHEAS levels indicate an ovarian origin for the increased androgens.” 2. Adrenal tumor unlikely by imaging — “…no adrenal mass was noted….” 3. Epithelial cystadenoma recognized as a possible androgen source — “Cystadenomas can secrete beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-hCG), which can possibly stimulate androgen secretion resulting in virilization.” 4. Sex cord–stromal tumors included in differential because of usual androgen secretion by these cells — “Most androgen secreting tumors of the ovaries arise from sex cord cells (thecal/granulosa) that surround the oocyte or from stromal cells.”

title: Brucellosis with rare complications and review of diagnostic tests: a case report
case_prompt:
A 17-year-old Asian woman from a rural, animal‐husbandry family presented with a 1‐month history of abdominal pain, nocturnal fevers, and worsening lower back pain. She had no chronic illnesses or relevant family history. Initially, she was diagnosed elsewhere with urinary infection and lumbar discopathy. After persistent symptoms, a standard tube agglutination test (STA) returned positive at 1/320, and she was started on doxycycline and rifampicin. Despite treatment, her back pain intensified, and she was referred to our emergency department.

On admission: temperature 37.8 °C; other vitals stable. Examination revealed diffuse tenderness over the thoracolumbar spine; otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, white blood cell count 10,900/mm3 (53% neutrophils), C‐reactive protein 92.9 mg/L (0–5), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 71 mm/h, and Rose Bengal plate test negative. Chest radiograph was normal. Blood cultures were obtained.

Lumbar MRI was normal. Contrast‐enhanced thoracic MRI demonstrated bone marrow edema in T11 and T12, a hyperintense T2 signal in the corresponding disc space with intense contrast enhancement of these vertebrae, and a 2 mm rim‐enhancing collection consistent with epidural and anterior paravertebral abscess at T11–T12. Ceftriaxone was added; neurosurgery declined surgical intervention given absence of neurologic deficits. A Brucella Coombs test on serum returned positive at >1/1280. Tuberculin skin testing was negative, and blood cultures remained sterile.

On hospital day 4, she developed fever to 39 °C, white blood cell count rose to 21,000/mm3, and CRP to 420 mg/L. Evaluation for a new infectious focus revealed a left pleural fluid collection. Thoracic surgery placed a chest tube, yielding hemopurulent, exudative fluid with numerous leukocytes but no organisms on Gram stain and no growth on culture.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Coagulase-negative staphylococci, E. coli, and Pseudomonas species were considered, especially in patients with injection drug use, but the patient had no such history and cultures were negative — “Coagulase-negative staphylococci, E. coli, and Pseudomonas species may also be responsible, especially in those with a history of injecting drug use.” 2. Mycobacterium tuberculosis was considered given the endemic setting and empyema formation, but PPD was negative, ARB and PCR on pleural fluid were negative, and cultures showed no growth — “purified protein derivative (PPD) test was negative”; “Acid Resistant Bacillus (ARB) was negative, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR was negative. No growth in culture for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.”

title: Development of Compartment Syndrome after Radial Artery Puncture in a Patient with Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure due to COVID-19
case_prompt: A 71-year-old man with coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, gout, and benign prostatic hyperplasia was transferred from a long-term acute care facility with a 2-day history of right wrist and hand pain, tenderness, and swelling. Eighteen days earlier, he was admitted for COVID-19 pneumonia with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring high-flow nasal cannula and bilevel positive-pressure ventilation. During a 16-day ICU stay at the referring hospital, he underwent frequent arterial blood gas sampling from both radial arteries, multiple venous blood draws, and peripheral IV placements but never had an indwelling arterial line. He reported fatigue, shortness of breath, generalized weakness, and easy bruising and bleeding. His medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, carvedilol, lisinopril, sublingual nitroglycerin as needed, and therapeutic enoxaparin 90 mg subcutaneously twice daily. On admission, his temperature was 96.7°F, blood pressure 158/70 mm Hg, heart rate 104 beats/min, respiratory rate 30 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 88% on 10 L nasal cannula. Examination revealed diffuse bilateral inspiratory crackles, anasarca, and mild tenderness and swelling of the right wrist and hand with bruising on the volar wrist. Radial and ulnar pulses were palpable; sensation and motor strength were intact; the right fingers felt cool. Electrolytes and renal function were normal. The admission chest radiograph showed bilateral infiltrates consistent with COVID-19 pneumonia.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Digital small artery ischemia from vasopressor use, septic shock, DIC, or HIT remained a possibility given systemic factors — “Digital small artery ischemia, as can be seen with vasopressor treatment, septic shock, disseminated intravascular coagulation, or heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, remained a possibility.” 2. Venous thrombosis was entertained due to limb swelling and ongoing anticoagulation — “Venous thrombosis remained a concern, and venous Doppler studies were pending.”

title: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease: A case report with bilateral involvement of the temporomandibular joints and concurrence of psoriatic arthritis
case_prompt: A 40‐year‐old woman with asthma, hypertension, and a 20‐year history of psoriatic arthritis involving both temporomandibular joints presented with a 4‐month history of a hard, tender swelling (1.5×2.0 cm) in the left preauricular region. Her prior TMJ interventions included bilateral discectomy and synovectomy 14 years earlier, right‐side arthroplasty with synovectomy 3 years ago, and left‐side arthroplasty with dermis‐fat interpositional graft 1 year ago, during which surgeons noted and removed whitish “chalky” material. A year later, examination showed a well‐defined, tender preauricular nodule with normal overlying skin. An initial MRI demonstrated a well‐defined encapsulated lesion adjacent to the TMJ, and fine‐needle aspiration cytology was nondiagnostic. A dedicated TMJ MRI showed progressive bilateral calcification around both TMJs. Subsequent CT scans revealed extensive calcification with deformation and arthrosis of both TMJs, and a well‐circumscribed calcified nodule caudal to the left TMJ.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Inflammatory pseudotumor was suggested by MRI findings — “MRI examination showed a well‐defined encapsulated lesion related to the TMJ, possibly an inflammatory pseudo‐tumor….” 2. Synovial cyst was raised in the MRI differential — “MRI examination showed … possibly … synovial cyst….” 3. Postoperative changes were included as a possibility given her surgical history — “MRI examination showed … possibly … postoperative changes.” 4. Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition disease was favored after imaging demonstrated bilateral TMJ calcification — “A new MRI dedicated to the TMJ… showed progressive bilateral calcification around the TMJ. A diagnosis of calcium pyrophosphate dehydrate deposition disease was suggested.”

title: Hepatitis-associated Aplastic Anemia with Rapid Progression of Liver Fibrosis Due to Repeated Hepatitis
case_prompt:
A 26-year-old man was admitted for evaluation of recurrent hepatitis and persistent cytopenias. One year earlier, he developed acute hepatitis (AST 1750 U/L, ALT 3110 U/L, T-bil 8.9 mg/dL) one month after influenza vaccination; a liver biopsy at that time showed prominent lymphocytic portal infiltration. Six months later he had a second episode of hepatitis (AST 957 U/L, ALT 1970 U/L) accompanied by leukocytopenia and thrombocytopenia; viral serologies (hepatitis A, B, C, E, cytomegalovirus) and autoantibodies were negative, and liver enzymes normalized without treatment, but cytopenias persisted. Seven months later, he presented with fatigue and again had elevated transaminases (AST 434 U/L, ALT 1017 U/L). 

On admission he was afebrile, normotensive, without jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory findings included: AST 501 U/L, ALT 1165 U/L, ALP 429 U/L, γ-GTP 103 U/L, T-bil 1.0 mg/dL, albumin 4.5 g/dL, PT-INR 0.96. Complete blood count: WBC 2.35×10^3/μL, neutrophils 0.94×10^3/μL, hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, reticulocytes 8.0×10^4/μL, platelets 3.0×10^4/μL. Serologies remained negative for HBsAg, HCV IgG, CMV IgM, EBV IgM, antinuclear and antimitochondrial antibodies; immunoglobulin levels were within normal limits. Abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT showed mild splenomegaly but unremarkable liver morphology. 

A transjugular liver biopsy was performed and compared with the specimen from one year earlier. The earlier specimen showed prominent lymphocytic infiltration in portal areas; the current specimen showed disappearance of lymphocytes, replacement by histiocytes, bridging hepatic necrosis in portal regions, and new centrilobular fibrosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral hepatitis was considered but excluded — “the results of tests for typical viruses, such as hepatitis A, B, C, and E, and cytomegalovirus … were negative.” 2. Vaccine-induced or drug-related hepatitis was suspected after the first episode — “the first episode of acute hepatitis appeared after a flu shot, suggested that an excessive immune response to the vaccination might have caused severe hepatitis.” 3. Hepatitis-associated aplastic anemia was suspected given persistent cytopenias following recurrent hepatitis — “pancytopenia appears two or three months after acute hepatitis attack,” consistent with this patient’s timeline of leukocytopenia and thrombocytopenia after hepatitis.

title: Not all that is miliary is tuberculosis: Metastatic medullary thyroid carcinoma mimicking miliary tuberculosis
case_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of 8 months of progressive shortness of breath, intermittent fever and chills, productive (non–blood-stained) cough, and a 25-kg unintentional weight loss over 6 months. She had no night sweats, dysphagia, or hoarseness. Her medical history was notable only for hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She was a passive smoker, denied tuberculosis exposure, and had no family history of malignancy or pulmonary disease. She had been empirically treated with antituberculosis therapy at another center without improvement.

On examination, she was afebrile (37.8 °C), blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, pulse 84/min, respiratory rate 12/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Lung auscultation was clear, and no thyroid nodules, neck masses, or cervical lymphadenopathy were palpable.

Laboratory studies showed TSH 0.08 mU/L (normal 0.35–4.9), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.76–2.24), and serum calcitonin 128 pg/mL (elevated). Other blood tests were normal.

Chest radiography revealed diffuse bilateral 1–3 mm nodular infiltrates with a miliary pattern. Noncontrast chest CT showed innumerable small nodules with a random distribution and a confluent mass in the right lung. Smears and cultures for acid-fast bacilli were negative, Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR was negative, and blood cultures were sterile.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Miliary tuberculosis was initially suspected but became unlikely when “results were negative for acid‐fast bacilli (AFB) smear and culture, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis was not detected by polymerase chain reaction (PCR)…” 2. Primary lung adenocarcinoma was considered because of the lung mass and miliary nodules, but this diagnosis is rare and was not supported on further work-up: “Rarely, primary lung adenocarcinoma could be the etiology of miliary nodules on chest radiography.” 3. Hematogenous metastases were suspected given the random distribution of micronodules: “Random micronodular patterns are seen in entities such as hematogenous spread of malignancies, in line with the case reported here.” 4. Papillary thyroid carcinoma can produce micronodular pulmonary lesions, but “micronodular densities are more often associated with papillary thyroid carcinoma,” and the markedly elevated calcitonin level pointed toward a C-cell tumor instead.

title: Swallowing difficulties in adolescents: A case report and suggestion of treatment model
case_prompt:
A 16-year-old girl presented with a 4-day history of increasing difficulty swallowing solid foods and a painless globus sensation. She denied ingestion of caustic substances or foreign bodies. An ENT examination was unremarkable; she could swallow saliva and small amounts of water. A barium swallow study the next day was normal.

Two weeks later, her dysphagia persisted. She was limited to soft foods and liquids, with no weight loss or reflux. She described three choking episodes in the month before her first visit and anxiety about solids lodging in her throat. She denied additional trauma. Her history included mild anxiety and depression managed in outpatient psychiatry after self-harm one year earlier; she had no current psychiatric treatment. Repeat ENT examination was again normal; she declined fiberoptic endoscopy but agreed to evaluation by a speech and language pathologist (SLP).

Three weeks later, SLP assessment showed normal oral sensorimotor function, an EAT-10 score of 10/40 (normal <3), and a prolonged swallowing capacity of 150 ml water in 26.9 s (normal <15 s). A video fluoroscopic swallow study (VFSS) performed four weeks later was inconclusive: she could ingest only minute solid or semisolid pieces, and liquids were within normal limits but taken in very small sips.

At a pediatric clinic consultation, neurological, cardiac, ENT, and abdominal examinations were normal. Laboratory studies, including electrolytes, liver enzymes, blood count, thyroid function, vitamins, and celiac serology, were all within reference limits. She was referred for gastro-esophagoscopy with biopsy and psychological evaluation. Endoscopic biopsies of the esophagus were normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Eosinophilic esophagitis — “Esophageal biopsies were normal so eosinophilic esophagitis … was ruled out.” 2. Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) — “ARFID was considered, but was rejected due to criterion D, which stipulates that the disorder should not be explained by a simultaneous medical condition.”

title: Limb girdle muscular dystrophy type 2B masquerading as inflammatory myopathy: case report
case_prompt:
A 10-year-old boy of West African origin, born to non-consanguineous parents, presented with a one-year history of bilateral knee pain radiating into his legs. The pain began in the right knee after a long walk, was of fairly rapid onset, and was associated with swelling; within weeks, the left knee became similarly affected. The pain was localized to the knees and was continuous with intermittent exacerbations, being worse in the mornings and at rest. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs provided only mild relief.

Two months after symptom onset, laboratory studies showed an ESR of 5 mm/hr, CRP of 4 mg/L, and normal ACE level; sickle-cell trait testing was negative. MRI of the right knee demonstrated a small joint effusion without synovial thickening. Synovial fluid aspiration yielded an almost acellular specimen; cultures were negative. A transient ecchymosis-like rash around the knee was attributed to warm compresses.

Over the next nine months, he was admitted several times with severe knee pain but no fevers, systemic rash, or other systemic features. On initial rheumatology evaluation, his vital signs were normal. Examination revealed a small right knee effusion and pain on flexion; the knee posture was held in slight flexion but could be extended to neutral.

One month later, he reported difficulty standing and required assistance with dressing because of lower-limb weakness. Examination revealed bilateral calf and thigh hypertrophy (more pronounced in thighs). Strength was 5/5 in neck, upper limbs, abdominal, and shoulder muscles; assessment of lower limbs was limited by pain, but quadriceps strength appeared reduced. Timed rises from the floor and from lying were within normal limits. He had tight long flexors of the arms, hamstrings, and gastrocnemius. Hyperpigmented patches were noted over the right knee and thigh, thought secondary to compresses.

Subsequently, he experienced worsening nocturnal leg pain. Laboratory studies showed ALT 145 U/L, CK 13 126 U/L, LDH 2245 U/L, with persistently normal ESR and CRP; ANA was negative. Muscle ultrasound of the quadriceps was normal. MRI of the thigh and calf muscles revealed mildly reduced posterior-compartment muscle bulk with asymmetric edema. On fat-suppressed STIR images, there was signal hyperintensity in the right sartorius and medial gastrocnemius extending into adjacent fascia, and similar edema in other selective thigh and calf muscles.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Inflammatory myopathy (dermatomyositis) considered because the MRI showed muscle edema—“These changes are similar to the imaging appearances of inflammatory conditions such as dermatomyositis, but in an atypical distribution.” 2. Thrombophlebitis or focal myositis considered because calf swelling can mimic these conditions—“Calf swelling at onset of dysferlinopathies, however, is rare and may lead to misdiagnosis of thrombophlebitis or focal myositis.”

title: Poncet’s disease: two case reports
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old Asian woman presented with a 3-week history of constant bilateral heel pain and difficulty walking. She also reported migratory pain involving her ankles, knees, and hips of the same duration; the pain worsened with activity and was not associated with morning stiffness. She had no history of sexual activity, no family history of tuberculosis or rheumatologic disease, and had received BCG vaccination at birth. 

On examination, she was afebrile. There was mild swelling of the left ankle without erythema, tenderness, or limitation of movement; all other joints and the remainder of her examination were normal. Initial complete blood count and creatine phosphokinase level were normal; radiographs of the hands and knees showed no erosions. She was treated with NSAIDs and other symptomatic measures, but her pain worsened over the next week, now involving her wrists and small joints of the hands bilaterally. She failed to respond to escalated analgesic therapy, and developed low-grade fevers to 37.8 °C, anorexia, fatigue, a 2-kg weight loss, and a mild nonproductive cough. 

Laboratory testing revealed an ESR of 72 mm/hour and a CRP of 4.9 mg/dL. A tapering course of corticosteroids produced partial relief of pain but persistent daily fevers. Ten days after steroids were discontinued, she returned with worsening polyarthritis, fevers to 37.8 °C, an additional 2-kg weight loss, and cough. Her ESR was 70 mm/hour, CRP 5.3 mg/dL, and ferritin 204 mg/dL; ANA was positive, but ASMA, AMA, anti–dsDNA, and RF were negative. Chest radiography revealed patchy right perihilar and lower-lobe infiltrates without lymphadenopathy or effusion. A PPD skin test showed 22 mm of induration at 72 hours, compared with a negative test 2 years earlier. Attempts to obtain sputum and synovial fluid for AFB were unsuccessful, and the patient declined invasive procedures.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Systemic lupus erythematosus considered — “Rheumatologic investigations revealed positive ANA but ASMA, AMA, anti-dsDNA, and RF were negative.” 2. Septic (bacterial) arthritis considered — “By definition, the arthritis is sterile, and bacterial involvement should first be ruled out.” 3. Osteoarticular tuberculosis considered — “ostearticular TB rarely involves more than one joint… and responds rather slowly to ATT.” 4. Rheumatoid arthritis considered — “TB arthritis can be confused easily with RA due to the same radiologic features including periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions” and “Anti-CCP is no more specific to RA and frequently seen in patients with active TB.”

title: Multiple Unerupted Permanent Teeth Associated with Noonan Syndrome
journal: Annals of Medical and Health Sciences Research
case_prompt:
A 13‐year‐old boy was referred to pediatric dentistry for irregularly placed upper and lower front teeth and unerupted teeth. His medical history was otherwise noncontributory, aside from an epileptic attack in childhood; dental history included extraction of his lower front teeth one year earlier.  
On examination, he had hypertelorism, a flat nasal bridge, saddle nose, mild webbing of the neck, pectus excavatum, arachnodactyly, and clubbing of the nails. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable, and electrocardiography was normal. A hand–wrist radiograph showed a missing carpal bone with no fusion of the remaining carpal bones. Hematology revealed an elevated alkaline phosphatase level (179 U/L).  
Intraoral examination showed multiple submerged and retained deciduous teeth (numbers 53, 54, 55, 63, 64, 65, 73, 74, 75, 84, and 85), a deep bite, a high‐arched palate, gingival overgrowth with supernumerary teeth in the lower anterior region, and grade I mobility of the lower permanent incisors. Numerous permanent teeth (12, 14, 15, 22, 24, 25, 33, 34, 35, 43, 44, 45, and 46) were unerupted; all erupted teeth were caries‐free.  
An orthopantomogram revealed three supernumerary lower anterior teeth, congenitally missing teeth (12, 22, 38, and 48), multiple impacted permanent teeth, and an impacted mandibular right first permanent molar (tooth 46) with divergent roots. Cephalometric analysis demonstrated a skeletal Class I malocclusion with orthognathic maxilla and mandible, a horizontal growth pattern, and retroclined upper and lower incisors.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. William’s syndrome was ruled out — “The absence of cardiovascular abnormalities in our patient has ruled out the possibility of William's syndrome.” 2. Noonan syndrome was favored — “Based on the clinical and radiographic features observed a provisional diagnosis of Noonan's syndrome was established after ruling out other differential diagnosis.”

title: Leptospirosis as an unusual culprit of acute pancreatitis and portal vein thrombosis in a New Yorker
case_prompt:
A 61-year-old homeless African American man with hypertension presented with 1 week of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. He had presented 6 days earlier with similar symptoms but left against medical advice. On arrival, he reported intractable nausea, vomiting, fatigue, headache, photophobia, anuria, and myalgia; he denied cough, fevers, chills, or night sweats. He used cocaine regularly but denied alcohol, tobacco, and intravenous drugs. He had no recent travel.

Vital signs: afebrile; blood pressure 177/65 mm Hg; heart rate 77/min; respiratory rate 16/min; oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed scleral icterus, conjunctival suffusion, jaundice of the soft palate, and a supple neck. Cardiopulmonary exam showed a heaving, laterally displaced point of maximal impulse with regular rate and rhythm. Abdominal exam disclosed epigastric tenderness without guarding or rigidity, normal bowel sounds, and a negative Murphy’s sign.

Laboratory studies showed: 
• Lipase >3× upper limit of normal  
• Mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic liver injury (elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, AST, ALT)  
• Thrombocytopenia  
• Acute renal failure (creatinine 10.4 mg/dL)  
• Elevated creatine kinase  

Urinalysis revealed hematuria, pyuria, leukocyte esterase, and hyaline casts.

Initial differential: hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP).  
• Peripheral-blood smear: thrombocytopenia without schistocytes.  
• ADAMTS13 activity: normal.  
• Stool cultures: negative for Escherichia coli, Shigella, and Salmonella.

Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia was suspected but anti–platelet factor 4 antibodies were negative. Serologic and PCR testing for CMV, EBV, and HIV were negative. Workup for glomerulonephritis and other autoimmune renal disease (ANA, anti–dsDNA, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, complement levels) was unremarkable. Evaluation for chronic and autoimmune liver disease, including hepatitis panel, QuantiFERON, CMV, EBV, HIV, α₁-antitrypsin, and ceruloplasmin, was negative.

Given elevated lipase and epigastric pain, acute pancreatitis was diagnosed. Investigations for causes of pancreatitis included autoimmune markers (negative), urine toxicology (negative), undetectable blood ethanol level, abdominal CT (only subtle gallbladder sludge vs small stones), and MRCP (unremarkable).

Right upper-quadrant ultrasound demonstrated isolated hepatofugal flow in the right portal vein suspicious for portal vein thrombosis, whereas the left portal vein had normal directional flow.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia was suspected due to recent hospitalization but was excluded when “testing for heparin-induced platelet antibody came back negative.” 2. Viral infections (CMV, EBV, HIV) were considered in light of multiorgan involvement but “laboratory studies for underlying infections such as Cytomegalovirus (CMV), Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) were negative.” 3. Autoimmune and other causes of glomerulonephritis were evaluated but autoantibodies (ANA, anti–dsDNA, p-ANCA, c-ANCA) and complement levels “did not show significant results.” 4. Autoimmune pancreatitis was excluded as testing was negative and no imaging abnormalities supported this diagnosis. 5. Alcohol-induced pancreatitis was ruled out by an “undetectable” blood ethanol level. 6. Gallstone pancreatitis was considered given gallbladder sludge but was unlikely since “computed tomography … was unremarkable except for subtle sludge vs gallstones” and “MRCP was unremarkable.”

title: Pediatric Pulmonary Epstein-Barr Virus-Positive Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt: A 22-month-old Caucasian girl presented with low-grade fever, cough, decreased activity, poor oral intake, and bilateral jaw-line swelling. She was born at term without complications; her maternal aunt had autoimmune disease. Vaccinations were incomplete (no MMR, varicella, or fourth DTaP). History included recurrent otitis media (with tubes) and eczema. Initial tests showed positive mumps IgM; elevated IgM, IgG, IgA, IgE, and IgG subclasses; positive rhinovirus and enterovirus; negative CMV, HIV, and hepatitis panels. She was leukopenic with cold-agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia. Exam revealed mild hepatosplenomegaly and small mobile cervical nodes. EBV serologies indicated acute infection, with plasma EBV load 9 000–20 600 copies/μL. Flow cytometry showed reduced B, T, and NK cells. Despite broad-spectrum antibiotics and symptomatic improvement, chest radiography and CT demonstrated persistent bilateral perihilar and right middle lobe infiltrates. One month later, she was readmitted with febrile neutropenia, rash, elevated soluble IL-2, but did not meet five of eight HLH criteria. Lymphocyte proliferation studies showed poor response to PHA but normal response to pokeweed mitogen.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lymphomatoid granulomatosis — “…lymphomatoid granulomatosis is also in the differential diagnosis … but it tends to have polymorphous lymphoid infiltrates and more prominent blood vessel wall invasion and occurs in adults and immunodeficient children.” 2. EBV-positive reactive lymphoid hyperplasias and polymorphic lymphoproliferations — “Although there are other EBV+ reactive lymphoid hyperplasias and polymorphic extranodal or nodal lymphoproliferations, the architecture of EBV+ DLBCL is almost always diffuse with large cell proliferation, mixed with bystanding T-cells and histiocytes, with geographic necrosis and occasionally angiotropism.”

title: Non-pancreatic pseudo cyst of oral cavity-case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 4-year-old boy was evaluated for a 1-year history of a slowly enlarging mass in the right cheek. He reported no pain, tenderness, dysphagia, dysphonia, dyspnea, trauma, or systemic symptoms. No relevant medical, family, or psychosocial history was noted.

On examination, there was a 3 × 2 × 1 cm firm, non-tender, non-fluctuant, non-pulsatile mass on the right cheek, extending from the right oral commissure to the pterygomandibular raphe. The overlying mucosa was normal in color, margins were distinct, and the lesion was freely mobile between the buccal mucosa and skin. Aspiration yielded no fluid. No submandibular or submental lymphadenopathy was present.

Ultrasonography confirmed the presence of a soft-tissue mass. Contrast-enhanced CT of the right cheek demonstrated a well-defined, hypodense lesion within the muscle layer. Complete blood count and metabolic panel were within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of odontogenic infections (e.g., buccal or masseteric space infections) — “unlikely in this case because the lesion has attained considerable size without any signs of infection/inflammation like malaise or fever.” 2. Consideration of mucocele or ranula — “Ranula has characteristic bluish-gray hue which was not present in this lesion, the lesion had normal overlying mucosal color.” 3. Consideration of lipoma — “appearance of lipoma is yellowish and nodular. … This lesion did not have such features hence this diagnosis is also unlikely.” 4. Consideration of vascular lesions (hemangioma or lymphangioma) — “A vascular lesion like hemangioma or lymphangioma have irregular mucosal surface and are lobulated. Hemangioma is reddish purple in color and lymphangioma is clear and translucent. This lesion did not have such features hence this diagnosis is also unlikely.” 5. Consideration of oral lymphoepithelial cyst — “Its size is less than one cm, smooth, whitish-yellow, firm, painless mass. … Due to the size, location, color, this diagnosis was also excluded.”

title: Acquired Hemophilia in an Elderly Patient with Carcinoma of the Ampulla of Vater
case_prompt:
An 81-year-old man presented to the emergency department with presyncope. Two months earlier he had undergone a Whipple procedure for pT2N1M0 carcinoma of the ampulla of Vater. His medical history also included hypertension. He was receiving prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin.  
On examination, he had ecchymoses and swelling of the right shin and both forearms. Digital rectal examination was negative for bleeding.  
Laboratory studies showed normocytic normochromic anemia, mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase, a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time, a normal prothrombin time, a normal fibrinogen level, and no schistocytes on peripheral smear.  
Ultrasound of the right shin excluded deep-vein thrombosis and confirmed a hematoma.  
Differential diagnoses included an adverse reaction to heparin use, antiphospholipid syndrome, and a bleeding disorder due to an acquired inhibitor in the clotting cascade.
