Contrast‐enhanced computed tomography revealed a moderate pericardial effusion and multiple large, bilateral, cavitary pulmonary lesions as well as splenic abscesses. She was treated empirically with broad‐spectrum antibiotics and her immunosuppressive therapies were discontinued, after which her clinical status deteriorated.

Mycobacterium fortuitum was isolated from a sputum sample. Bronchoalveolar lavage, transbronchial biopsy, and two lung wedge resections showed only abscess formation with no evidence of infection by stains, cultures, or molecular studies. An antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody test was negative. Despite initiation of broad‐spectrum antibiotics and systemic antifungal therapy, her condition continued to worsen.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Vasculitis — “An antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody test was negative.” 2. Aseptic abscess syndrome — “Microscopically, lung tissue has consistently shown numerous neutrophils and negative infectious stains and tissue cultures, which can be seen in both extracutaneous PG and aseptic abscess syndrome. However, the patient's history of cutaneous PG flares in parallel with the extracutaneous lesions suggests extracutaneous PG as the most likely diagnosis.”

title: Diagnosis and management of traumatically induced hemicrania continua and neuropathic pain secondary to facial gun shot wound
case_prompt:
A 53-year-old man was referred for rehabilitation of mandibular opening after life-saving surgery and reconstruction for a gunshot wound to the face. He had undergone palatal defect repair with a tongue pedicle flap and open reduction of a comminuted condylar fracture, followed by placement of a prosthetic left temporomandibular joint. His maximal interincisal opening improved from 5 mm to 28 mm with physical therapy.

During the initial evaluation, he reported a second complaint of headache. The headache was strictly left‐sided, located periorbitally and in the temporal region, described as a constant background ache that waxed and waned, with superimposed severe exacerbations occurring unpredictably. During these exacerbations, he experienced ipsilateral lacrimation, persistent rhinorrhea, and photophobia.

He also reported a burning and tingling sensation over the left zygomatic arch. On examination, the left forehead was flat with a lowered eyebrow. The left eyelid was ptotic, and anisocoria was present with a smaller left pupil. The left conjunctiva appeared injected. Allodynia and hyperalgesia were noted in the distribution of the zygomatic branch of the trigeminal nerve on the left side.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cluster headache — considered because of unilateral periorbital pain with autonomic features but excluded because cluster headache attacks are episodic rather than continuous: “with the exception of HC, in that they are typically episodic; HC is an uncommon, benign unilateral and continuous headache, with superimposed exacerbations of severe pain and autonomic features.” 2. Odontalgia — considered because trigeminal autonomic cephalgias often present as dental pain but excluded when ocular autonomic signs and continuous headache were identified: “Cluster headache (CH), chronic paroxysmal hemicrania (CPH) and HC often mimic anterior maxillary odontogenic pain leading to inappropriate dental procedures.” 3. Temporomandibular disorders and trigeminal neuralgia — considered due to masticatory pain and facial sensory changes but excluded because the pain was continuous with autonomic features rather than paroxysmal or movement‐related: “Differential diagnoses also considered in this, and similar cases are temporomandibular disorders and trigeminal neuralgia.” 4. Hemicrania continua — supported by the continuous unilateral headache with superimposed exacerbations and ipsilateral cranial autonomic features and the exclusion of other trigeminal autonomic cephalgias.

title: Pseudo-hypotension with acute pulmonary oedema due to simultaneous bilateral subclavian artery stenosis in a patient with coronary artery bypass graft surgery using bilateral internal mammary arteries: a case report
case_prompt:
A 75-year-old woman presented with acute severe dyspnea. Eight and thirteen years earlier, she had undergone coronary artery bypass grafting: the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) to the left anterior descending artery, the right internal mammary artery (RIMA) to an obtuse marginal branch, and a saphenous vein graft to the distal left circumflex artery. Her medical history included hypertension, diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 6.5%–6.7%), hyperlipidemia (LDL 100 mg/dL), and a bilateral neck bruit noted two years ago. MR angiography at that time showed <50% stenosis of both carotid arteries and 50% stenosis of both subclavian arteries. Ankle–brachial indices were elevated (ABI 1.62 on both sides), attributed to arterial stiffness, and she was asymptomatic without inter-arm blood pressure difference or arm claudication.

Six months before admission, she developed exertional chest discomfort. Two months later, her systolic blood pressure fell from 126/70 mmHg to 94/60 mmHg. ECG showed worsening ST-segment depression and QRS widening; echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 40% (previously >55%). Treatment with pimobendan led to slight improvement. One week before admission, her blood pressure measured 70/40 mmHg in the right arm and 80/50 mmHg in the left arm; antihypertensive medications were discontinued.

She was scheduled for cardiac catheterization but presented first with severe dyspnea. In the emergency department, respiratory rate was 30/min, heart rate 86/min, temperature 36.0°C, and bilateral arm blood pressures were difficult to measure. Chest radiography showed severe bilateral pulmonary edema. ECG demonstrated diffuse ST-segment depression with non-specific intraventricular conduction delay. Cardiac troponin I was positive; creatine phosphokinase rose from 66 U/L to 720 U/L. Renal and liver functions were normal.

She was intubated, admitted to intensive care, and started on intravenous dobutamine and furosemide. Dyspnea improved rapidly, and urine output was 950 mL in the first 4 hours and 50–100 mL/h thereafter. Despite vigorous treatment, systolic blood pressure remained low at 60–70 mmHg in both arms, but femoral pulses were easily palpable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pseudo-hypotension due to bilateral subclavian artery stenosis was suspected — “Bilateral SAS was suspected to be the cause of pseudo-hypotension, diffuse myocardial dysfunction and acute pulmonary oedema.” 2. Diffuse myocardial ischaemia from low perfusion pressure in the mammary grafts explained the LV dysfunction — “Diffuse myocardial ischaemia due to low perfusion pressure in the bilateral mammary arteries was considered the cause of left ventricular dysfunction and pulmonary oedema.”

title: Recurrent solitary fibrous tumor of lumbar spine with vertebral body involvement: imaging features and differential diagnosis with report of a case
case_prompt: A 73-year-old man with a history of treated prostate cancer presented with progressive left lower-extremity weakness and numbness over several years, chronic low back pain, and constipation. On examination, there was diminished sensation in the left groin, anterior thigh, and calf; muscle strength was 4/5 in the right lower extremity and 3/5 in the left lower extremity. He had a history of resection of a lumbar spinal tumor in 1992, originally diagnosed as a paraganglioma, followed by radiation therapy in 2002. Surveillance imaging suggested increase in the lumbar lesion over time. Computed tomography of the spine showed expansion of the spinal canal by an isodense soft-tissue mass with accompanying osseous destruction. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated the mass to be hypointense on T1-weighted and T2-weighted imaging, with homogenous enhancement on postcontrast T1-weighted images.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of mesenchymal chondrosarcoma for similar vascular pattern — “Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma may show a similar vascular pattern, however, would demonstrate cartilaginous growth.” 2. Consideration of meningioma on imaging — “A meningioma would usually be well circumscribed, nonerosive, with calcifications and would be more common in women.” 3. Consideration of chordoma on imaging — “Furthermore, a chordoma is most commonly seen in the sacrum with its presentation as an extraosseous and/or epidural soft-tissue mass with the destructive process centered in the bone.” 4. Consideration of aggressive hemangioma on imaging — “An aggressive hemangioma shows thickened vertical trabeculae within area of circumscribed osteolysis, with the lesion demonstrating avid enhancement with an associated epidural or paravertebral soft-tissue mass.” 5. Consideration of vascular metastases from renal or thyroid carcinoma — “Renal cell and thyroid carcinomas are known for causing vascular metastases; however, these are usually centered in the cancellous bone with involvement of the posterior elements of the vertebral body.” 6. Consideration of angiosarcoma as a rare vascular spinal tumor — “Differential diagnosis for an SFT based on radiographic presentation includes ... and rarely an angiosarcoma.”

title: Scrub typhus with hemorrhagic stroke: a case report
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old Nepali woman from the far western region presented with sudden onset left-sided weakness and difficulty speaking for 6 hours. Seven days earlier, she developed moderate-to-high–grade continuous fever without chills, accompanied by headaches, nonbilious vomiting, and generalized weakness. Despite 4 days of over-the-counter treatment, symptoms persisted, and during the last 3 days she developed shortness of breath and cough with mucoid sputum. She denied vision changes, altered mentation, chest pain, abdominal pain, or dysuria.

On examination, she was confused with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 14 (E4 V4 M6); temperature 102 °F; blood pressure 80/50 mmHg; heart rate 123/min; and oxygen saturation 78% on room air. She was pale and icteric, with bilateral chest crepitations. Head-to-toe inspection revealed a brownish-black scab on the right buttock. Pupils were equal and reactive; plantar responses were normal; and there was no neck rigidity.

Noncontrast CT of the head showed an acute right frontal lobe parenchymal hemorrhage with vasogenic edema and mild mass effect. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and features of pulmonary edema. Ultrasonography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed diffuse gallbladder wall thickening, borderline splenomegaly, and right-sided pleural effusion.

Laboratory studies on admission included: leukocyte count 13,200/µL (neutrophils 88%); hemoglobin 10 g/dL; platelets 460,000/µL; serum creatinine 1.7 mg/dL; total bilirubin 6.2 mg/dL (direct 2.7 mg/dL); AST 228.6 IU/L; ALT 72.3 IU/L; ALP 619.9 IU/L; arterial blood gas pH 7.26, pCO₂ 31 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 15.8 mmol/L, lactate 5.54 mmol/L.

Serological tests for dengue, leptospirosis, and Brucella were negative. Thick and thin blood smears for malaria and PCR testing for SARS-CoV-2 were negative. Sputum AFB staining and culture were negative for tuberculosis, and HIV serology was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Dengue fever considered because it causes an acute febrile illness and rash — “Serological tests were performed for dengue ... which were reported to be negative.” 2. Falciparum malaria considered in an endemic region causing fever and neurologic symptoms — “Thick and thin smears for malaria ... were negative.” 3. Leptospirosis considered due to multiorgan dysfunction in tropical exposure — “Serological tests were performed for ... leptospirosis ... which were reported to be negative.” 4. Brucellosis considered in undifferentiated febrile illness — “Serological tests were performed for ... Brucella ... which were reported to be negative.” 5. Tuberculosis considered with cough and constitutional symptoms — “Tuberculosis was excluded based on negative sputum acid-fast bacilli (AFB) staining and sputum culture results.” 6. HIV infection considered as a cause of immunosuppression — “Human immunodeficiency (HIV) serology was negative.”

title: Recurrent pneumothoraces with diffuse small ground‐glass and solid nodules
case_prompt:
A 50-year-old Chinese woman with a history of Graves’ disease presented with one week of dyspnea and cough without fever, chest pain, or hemoptysis. She was a non-smoker, with no weight loss or appetite change. Physical examination revealed a BMI of 17 kg/m2, blood pressure 172/102 mmHg, heart rate 97 bpm, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Chest examination showed reduced air entry on the right. Cutaneous examination disclosed bilateral small erythematous papules on the cheeks and nasolabial folds. There was no clubbing or lymphadenopathy.

A chest radiograph demonstrated a large right pneumothorax without mediastinal shift and diffuse reticular-nodular opacities in the left lung. A right‐sided chest tube was inserted with subsequent lung re-expansion and removal of the tube.

High-resolution CT of the chest revealed multiple round thin-walled cysts throughout both lungs, mild interlobular septal thickening, and numerous tiny (<4 mm) randomly distributed ground‐glass and solid nodules. Abdominal CT showed multiple fat-density lesions in both kidneys consistent with angiomyolipomas.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis — “In our patient, the cystic changes of diffuse and uniform thin‐walled cysts with the presence of renal angiomyolipomas are compatible with a clinical diagnosis of LAM.” 2. Miliary tuberculosis — “Radiological differentials of MMPH include miliary tuberculosis (TB) … Misdiagnosis of MMPH as miliary TB has been described as clinical differentiation is difficult.” 3. Atypical adenomatous hyperplasia/adenocarcinoma in situ — “Radiological stability over time and multicentric disease at onset favour MMPH over AAH/AIS.” 4. Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis — “Early‐stage PLCH tends to have irregular and bizarre shaped cysts with sparring of the lung bases and costophrenic angles, whereas the cysts in LAM are diffuse, round and uniform with thin walls.”

title: Aggressive vertebral hemangioma masquerading as neurological disease in a pediatric patient
case_prompt:
A 13-year-old boy with a history of asthma presented with a 3-month history of progressive lower extremity weakness and balance problems, resulting in multiple falls and the need to hold onto objects to walk. There was no history of trauma, intercurrent illness, or new medications.  
On examination, he had a positive Romberg sign, weakness of hip flexion and ankle dorsiflexion, intact reflexes, and no dysmetria or tremor.  
MRI of the spine showed a mass centered in the T10 vertebral body, extending into the posterior elements, epidural space, and paraspinal region. The lesion was heterogeneously T2 hyperintense with multiple serpentine T2 hypointense areas (suggestive of flow voids), demonstrated avid contrast enhancement, and showed facilitated diffusion. There was severe spinal canal narrowing with cord compression and T2 hyperintensity within the cord, as well as narrowing of the bilateral T10/T11 neuroforamina. No other spinal lesions were identified.  
Subsequent CT of the thoracic spine confirmed a T10 lesion with thickened, striated trabeculae, scattered areas of osteolysis, asymmetric enlargement of the T10 segmental arteries, and hypertrophy of paravertebral veins. CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no additional lesions.  
Given these findings, what is your leading diagnosis and what alternatives should be considered?
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lymphoma considered due to imaging mimicry — “Aggressive hemangiomas … have been described as being misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as lymphoma…” 2. Myeloma considered similarly — “Aggressive hemangiomas … misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as … myeloma…” 3. Metastasis considered similarly — “Aggressive hemangiomas … misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as … metastasis…” 4. Schwannoma considered similarly — “Aggressive hemangiomas … misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as … schwannoma…” 5. Chordoma considered similarly — “Aggressive hemangiomas … misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as … chordoma…” 6. Aneurysmal bone cyst considered similarly — “Aggressive hemangiomas … misdiagnosed preoperatively by imaging as … aneurysmal bone cyst.” 7. Ewing sarcoma considered in pediatrics — “Particularly in the pediatric population, it is important to note that Ewing sarcoma can have a similar imaging appearance.” 8. Neuroblastoma metastasis considered — “helping to delineate the lesion preoperatively from other more common pediatric vertebral lesions, such as Ewing Sarcoma or neuroblastoma metastasis.” 9. Malignancy excluded by diffusion imaging — “malignant vertebral lesions usually demonstrate a lower apparent diffusion coefficient values … The high apparent diffusion coefficient value in our patient's lesion helped us to differentiate it preoperatively from one of these malignant lesions.”

title: Acute toxic effects of areca nut on central nervous system and liver: A case report
case_prompt:
A 4-year-old girl was referred with gross hematuria and altered mental status after 6 hours of symptoms. Two days earlier she was well; she had sudden abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, and three episodes of vomiting. Her mother had given her two tablespoons of areca nut powder and ibuprofen syrup for presumed diarrhea and fever. Immediately thereafter, she became pale, developed cold extremities, gross hematuria, and two generalized tonic–clonic seizures (10 and 3 minutes in duration). On arrival, temperature was 39.4°C, heart rate 140/min, respiratory rate 40/min, blood pressure 90/50 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 85%. She was pale, febrile, had non-exudative conjunctivitis, and gross hematuria.  
Initial laboratory tests showed normal electrolytes except for potassium 3.8 mEq/L, C-reactive protein >150 mg/L, lactate dehydrogenase 3570 U/L, fibrinogen 611 mg/dL, a negative direct and indirect Coombs test, and sufficient G6PD activity. Venous blood gas showed pH 7.43, PCO2 14.3 kPa, HCO3 9.6 mEq/L. Echocardiography was normal. Renal function was normal (BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.42 mg/dL). Liver enzymes were elevated (AST 2670 U/L, ALT 970 U/L, INR 1.76) with normal total bilirubin, direct bilirubin, and alkaline phosphatase. Urinalysis showed 4+ blood and 2+ albumin. Stool examination revealed 3+ occult blood and numerous white blood cells. Blood cultures showed no growth. Virology tests for HBV, HCV, HAV, SARS-CoV-2, and HSV 1 and 2 were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of septic shock — “Due to high suspicion of a septic shock, intravenous hydration, and Vancomycin ... and Ceftriaxone ... were empirically prescribed,” but blood cultures showed no growth. 2. Consideration of hemolytic uremic syndrome — renal function tests were normal, “which ruled out the hemolytic uremic syndrome followed by Escherichia coli gastroenteritis.” 3. Exclusion of immune hemolysis — “the negative coombs’ test ... indicated non-immune hemolysis.” 4. Consideration of hepatic shock — “Because of the patient's unstable hemodynamics, the hepatic shock was considered one of the differential diagnoses for elevated liver function.” 5. Attribution to areca nut toxicity — literature reports “AN showed cytotoxic effects on red blood cells, resulting in hemolysis, hematuria, and anemia” and “AN was reported to be hepatotoxic, causing both cholestatic and hepatocellular hepatic injury and increased blood transaminases and alkaline phosphatase.”

title: Asymptomatic ventricular tachycardia: diagnostic pitfalls of Andersen–Tawil syndrome—a case report
case_prompt: A 9-year-old girl was referred for an irregular heart rate noted at an incidental well-child visit. An electrocardiogram showed a prolonged QTc of 485 ms and runs of non-sustained bidirectional ventricular tachycardia. There was no family history of arrhythmogenic disorders, congenital heart disease, or sudden death; the mother and maternal grandmother had syndactyly. Physical examination revealed short stature, low-set ears, mild micrognathia, wide-spaced eyes, dental abnormalities, and left iris heterochromia. Vital signs were age-appropriate. Holter monitoring revealed a ventricular ectopy burden of 40%. Laboratory studies, including a CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, cardiac enzymes, and thyroid function tests, were normal. Exercise stress testing showed no significant increase in ectopy from baseline at higher heart rates. Transthoracic echocardiography and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a structurally normal heart with no evidence of ventricular dysfunction.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Structural heart disease is unlikely — “A transthoracic echocardiogram and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed a structurally normal heart with no evidence of ventricular dysfunction.” 2. Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia is less likely given the absence of stress-induced arrhythmias — “Further exercise stress testing showed no significant increase in ectopy from baseline at higher heart rates.” 3. Andersen–Tawil syndrome is supported by the presence of characteristic dysmorphic features — “Physical examination revealed short stature, low set ears, mild micrognathia, wide spaced eyes, and dental abnormalities.”

title: Enamel hypoplasia with nonsyndromic oligodontia: A rare case report
journal: Journal of Conservative Dentistry and Endodontics
case_prompt: A 16-year-old female presented with dissatisfaction with her smile due to badly broken upper front teeth. Clinical examination revealed enamel chipping and dentin exposure in teeth 11, 12, 21, 22, 34, and 44, accompanied by generalized enamel opacities. A digital orthopantomogram showed congenital absence of mandibular anterior teeth (31, 32, 33, 41, 42, and 43). Intraoral periapical radiographs of teeth 12, 11, 21, and 22 displayed radiolucency affecting enamel, dentin, and pulp, along with widened periodontal ligament spaces. Radiographs of teeth 34 and 44 revealed radiolucency in enamel and dentin without periapical changes. Pulp viability testing showed negative responses for teeth 12, 11, 21, and 22, and positive responses for teeth 34 and 44.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of amelogenesis imperfecta — “Amelogenesis imperfecta was eliminated as it typically presents in a generalized and genetically derived manner.”

title: Cyclical sinus bradycardia and atrioventricular block induced by ticagrelor
case_prompt: A 75-year-old woman with a history of coronary artery disease (balloon angioplasty and stenting of the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries 7 years earlier), hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and obesity presented with chest pain. An electrocardiogram during pain showed diffuse ST-segment depression. She received a loading dose of ticagrelor 180 mg and aspirin 325 mg and underwent urgent coronary angiography, which revealed chronic total occlusion of the right coronary artery and severe stenosis of the left main and circumflex arteries. Two drug-eluting stents were placed with excellent angiographic results. Postprocedural left ventricular ejection fraction was 50% with mild mitral regurgitation. A few hours later, telemetry showed Cheyne–Stokes respiration without overt heart failure, marked sinus arrhythmia with sinus tachycardia during apneic phases and severe sinus bradycardia (<30 beats/min) during hyperpneic phases, and cyclical transient complete atrioventricular block with pauses up to 5 seconds. Work-up including electrolytes, creatinine clearance, liver enzymes, and thyroid function was unrevealing.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Heart failure–related Cheyne–Stokes respiration was considered because Cheyne–Stokes respiration frequently occurs in patients with congestive heart failure, but was excluded by absence of any evidence of heart failure — “Cheyne-Stokes respiration frequently occurs in patients with congestive heart failure … While our patient had no evidence of heart failure …” 2. Ticagrelor-induced bradyarrhythmia was supported by prior observations of ticagrelor-associated pauses and the drug’s adenosine-mediated effects on sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction — “Bradycardia related to ticagrelor was first described in a phase IIb dose-ranging study, where a post hoc analysis of cardiac arrhythmias revealed an unexpected increased incidence of predominantly asymptomatic ventricular pauses.”

title: Diabetes Myonecrosis: A Debilitating Complication in an Indigenous Young Woman With Long Standing Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
case_prompt:
A 24-year-old Indigenous Australian woman from a remote community presented with a 3-month history of left thigh swelling and pain associated with limitation of movement. She had type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 14 years with chronically poor glycaemic control (HbA1c, 12.4% on admission; historically as high as 20.9%) and multiple microvascular complications, including proliferative retinopathy, nephrotic-range proteinuria, peripheral neuropathy, and autonomic neuropathy. Other history was notable for acute rheumatic fever, syphilis, recurrent urinary tract infections, active smoking (20 cigarettes per day), and prior right femoral neck fracture with internal fixation. Her only medication was premixed insulin (degludec and aspart 14 units twice daily).

On examination she was pale, cachectic (weight 32.5 kg; BMI 12.9 kg/m2), and sinus tachycardic at 120 bpm. She had significant tender swelling of the left thigh without erythema, warmth, or skin changes; peripheral pulses were intact. Neurologic assessment was limited by pain but revealed stocking-distribution sensory neuropathy and reduced proximal left lower-limb strength. Visual acuity was counting fingers bilaterally; funduscopic exam showed severe proliferative diabetic retinopathy with macular oedema.

Laboratory studies revealed normocytic anaemia (Hb 90 g/L), thrombocytosis (649 × 10^9/L), leukocytosis (20.3 × 10^9/L), elevated neutrophils (17.43 × 10^9/L), ESR 120 mm/h, CRP 185 mg/L, ferritin 1290 µg/L, and creatinine clearance 50 mL/min. Creatine kinase and liver function tests were normal. Thyroid function was normal; morning cortisol was 528 nmol/L. Nutritional studies showed hypoalbuminaemia (<15 g/L), vitamin D 10.3 nmol/L, and vitamin C 12 µmol/L; coeliac serology was negative. Urinalysis indicated a urinary tract infection and albumin/creatinine ratio 840 g/mol. Renal ultrasound demonstrated cystitis; renal biopsy confirmed class III diabetic nephropathy. 

CT of the pelvis and left leg showed ill-defined diffuse low attenuation in the left mid-thigh muscle measuring 36 × 34 × 150 mm without masses or collections. A Doppler ultrasound excluded deep vein thrombosis. MRI revealed marked oedema of the left anterior thigh musculature (rectus femoris, vastus medialis, vastus lateralis, and adductor muscles) with muscle atrophy, fatty and fluid replacement, and subcutaneous tissue oedema; the posterior compartment was spared.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Deep vein thrombosis was excluded — “A Doppler ultrasound excluded a deep vein thrombosis.” 2. Nutritional myopathy was considered but felt unlikely — “it was thought to be less likely in light of clinical presentation and imaging features.” 3. Vitamin D deficiency–related myopathy was excluded — “However, this is typically more widespread and bilateral rather than unilateral, as with our patient.” 4. Diabetic myonecrosis best fit the presentation and imaging — “The clinical presentation and progress, as well as imaging features of our case, were in keeping with diabetic myonecrosis…”

title:
Non-communicated rudimentary uterine horn pregnancy and uterine rupture: A
case report
case_prompt:
A 35‐year‐old woman, 17 weeks’ gestation by last menstrual period, presented to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain that began 2 hours after defecating. She had confirmed pregnancy at 4 weeks’ gestation when she developed morning-sickness–type digestion discomfort. She reported no vaginal bleeding.

Her gynecologic history was significant for infrequent menstruation (4–5 days every 6 months), polycystic ovarian syndrome, left salpingectomy for tubal ectopic pregnancy 12 years ago, and seven in vitro fertilization–embryo transfer attempts; the seventh resulted in a pregnancy that ended in spontaneous abortion at 12 weeks, after which she discontinued fertility treatments and used no contraception.

On examination, her abdomen was rigid with rebound tenderness, especially on the left, where a tender mass appeared connected to the uterus. Initial laboratory results showed WBC 15.22 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 116 g/L, and serum amylase 41.9 IU/L. Transabdominal ultrasound revealed a viable 17-week fetus (fetal heart rate 155/min), a maximum vertical pocket of 6.8 cm, no free fluid, and a low-lying placenta with its edge near the internal cervical os.

Over the next 12 hours, her abdominal distention increased. Repeat labs showed WBC 13.23 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 98 g/L, and CRP 33.91 mg/L. On repeat ultrasound, the fetus’s heart rate was 163/min, but there was no detectable amniotic fluid, and a 2.0 cm low-echoic area was seen between the liver and kidney, consistent with new ascites.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Threatened abortion considered and excluded — “At this point, the patient’s symptoms did not fit with those of threatened abortion, which most often include vaginal bleeding and signs of inflammation, which were not present or supported by the amylase or other relevant lab results.” 2. Second lower placenta (placenta previa) considered — “Differential diagnoses we considered included a threatened abortion or a second lower placenta.” 3. Ovarian cyst or tumor rupture considered but deemed unlikely — “Although there was no imaging evidence to support an ovarian cyst, we could not entirely exclude ovarian cyst or tumor rupture based on the physical examination.”

title: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder co‐existing with antiphospholipid syndrome: A case report
case_prompt:
An African woman in her early 40s presented with 4 years of gradually progressive visual loss, beginning in the right eye and then involving the left, associated with eye pain that worsened with movement and throbbing bitemporal headache. There was no fever, seizures, or altered mental status. She was initially diagnosed with idiopathic intracranial hypertension despite a normal brain MRI and normal CSF opening pressure, and underwent ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement after acetazolamide failed.

One year after the shunt, she developed numbness in her lower and upper extremities. She noted progressive difficulty standing from sitting, climbing stairs, sitting unsupported, handling objects, writing, buttoning shirts, and combing hair. She also developed bladder and bowel incontinence. There was no history of joint pain, weight loss, skin changes, or preceding respiratory or gastrointestinal illness.

Her medical history included 6 years of hypertension, well controlled on medications. Obstetric history was notable for 11 spontaneous pregnancy losses before 24 weeks and one living child; menstrual history was otherwise unremarkable. There was no history of preeclampsia, thrombosis, or similar family illnesses.

On examination, she was alert with normal cognition and no meningeal signs. Cranial nerves were intact except for the optic nerves. Ocular motility was full; visual acuity was light perception only in both eyes, intraocular pressures were 17 mmHg (right) and 18 mmHg (left), pupils were dilated and nonreactive, and fundoscopy showed bilateral optic disc pallor with peripapillary atrophy and otherwise normal retinal background. Motor examination revealed normal bulk, strength 2/5 in upper limbs and 1/5 in lower limbs, brisk deep tendon reflexes, and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Sensory examination showed decreased pinprick, fine touch, temperature, and vibration below the C4 level; spine palpation was unremarkable. Other systems were normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension was considered because of the visual loss and headache — “Upon initial evaluation, IIH was considered as the cause of progressive visual loss and headache. However, the likelihood was downgraded following unfavorable response to treatment … and onset of new symptoms suggestive of transverse myelitis…”

title: Refractory Thyrotoxicosis in Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma Invading the Thyroid Gland
case_prompt: A 62-year-old Indian man with recently diagnosed p16-negative posterior oropharyngeal wall squamous cell carcinoma (stage IVb, T3N3bM0) and comorbid hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and ischemic heart failure (ejection fraction 23% with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) underwent elective feeding gastrostomy. His thyroid function was normal at initial cancer staging. Three weeks later, he developed fever, tachycardia, and stridor. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy showed near-complete oropharyngeal obstruction; an emergency tracheostomy was performed. Examination under anesthesia revealed rapid progression of the tumour into a circumferential oropharyngeal stricture. Despite 17 days of piperacillin-tazobactam, his fever persisted (maximum 39.6 °C) and heart rate remained 110–120 bpm. Extensive cultures (blood, urine, sputum, gastrostomy site), HIV and cryptococcal tests, echocardiography, and brain CT were negative. Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein 243.3 g/L and a white-cell count of 18,400/mm3 (75.1% neutrophils). Chest radiography demonstrated a small right pleural effusion. Neck CT revealed worsening pharyngeal disease with invasion into bilateral thyroid lobes and new bilateral lung nodules. On examination, there was central neck tenderness without a palpable goiter. Thyroid function tests showed triiodothyronine 8.0 pmol/L (reference 3.5–6.0), free thyroxine 54 pmol/L (reference 8.0–16.0), and thyroid-stimulating hormone 0.01 mIU/L (reference 0.45–4.5). Antithyroid peroxidase, thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor, and thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin antibodies were negative. Thyroid ultrasonography showed reduced vascularity without abscess formation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute suppurative thyroiditis was plausible in the context of fever and leukocytosis but was made unlikely by absence of an abscess on imaging and negative cultures — “Acute suppurative thyroiditis became a plausible differential, but the lack of an abscess on neck CT and sonography...and lack of bacterial growth on multiple blood cultures...also decreased the likelihood of acute suppurative thyroiditis.” 2. Graves’ disease was considered but excluded by negative autoantibodies — “Autoimmune thyroid antibodies including antithyroid peroxidase, thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibody, and thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin were negative.” 3. Thyrotoxicosis from direct tumour invasion was supported by CT evidence of thyroid infiltration and by analogy to follicular disruption — “We postulate that the mechanism of thyrotoxicosis in our patient could be follicular and stromal disruption of the thyroid parenchyma from local invasion by OPSCC.”

title: Successful radiofrequency ablation of junctional ectopic tachycardia in an adult patient
case_prompt: A 45-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of malaise and upper respiratory symptoms for which she had taken ibuprofen and pseudoephedrine. On initial assessment she was febrile (38.5 °C), hypotensive, and tachycardic with a heart rate of 250 bpm. Vagal maneuvers and escalating doses of intravenous adenosine did not affect her rhythm. At the community hospital her ECG showed a narrow QRS tachycardia up to 280 bpm. She received several liters of crystalloid and an infusion of IV diltiazem without sustained slowing, and six attempts at electrical cardioversion under sedation failed to terminate the tachycardia. Over the next hours, control was attempted with IV esmolol, digoxin, magnesium sulfate, and a 150 mg loading dose of amiodarone. As she developed increasing oxygen requirements and pulmonary edema, she was given IV furosemide and transferred to the cardiac ICU at a tertiary center. There she received a second amiodarone load, three more unsuccessful cardioversions, and boluses of lidocaine and procainamide with only transient rate slowing. With worsening respiratory status she was intubated, cooled to 35.5 °C, and placed on infusions of esmolol, lidocaine, and procainamide. Her heart rate slowed to 130 bpm and ECG demonstrated atrioventricular dissociation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Junctional ectopic tachycardia was determined to be the mechanism because of the incessant narrow-complex tachycardia with AV dissociation that was unresponsive to vagal maneuvers, adenosine, multiple antiarrhythmic drugs, and DC cardioversion — “The above findings strongly support a diagnosis of a focal JET. This also fits the clinical picture, with an almost incessant tachycardia unresponsive to vagal maneuvers, adenosine, multiple antiarrhythmic drugs, and DC cardioversion.”

title: Ultrasound diagnosis of a retroperitoneal fetus in fetu: A case report
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, presented at 37+4 weeks’ gestation after an obstetric ultrasound suggested a teratoma. The initial ultrasound at 37+3 weeks showed a fetal-like echo in the thoracic cavity of the fetus. On repeat examination, there was a live singleton fetus in cephalic presentation with a right retroperitoneal mass of mixed echogenicity. Two parallel fetal-like signals were seen within the mass: one exhibited a weak heartbeat, and the other was acardiac.  
Both parents were healthy, there was no history of twin gestation, and the first child had no congenital abnormalities.  
Ultrasonography revealed:  
• A 4.7 × 2.1 cm cystic-solid mass in the right retroperitoneum with intra-cyst septation and mixed echogenicity adherent to the cyst wall; no head, spine, upper limb, or heart echoes in this mass.  
• Continuous scanning showed one limb with skin covering, a long bone, distal footpad, and toe-like echoes. An umbilical cord–like vascular echo was connected to the mass, but color Doppler showed no blood flow.  
• A second 3.9 × 2.1 cm irregular mass posteriorly, lacking head, spine, or upper limbs; sagittal sections revealed a buttock-like contour, a limb with a proximal long bone and plantar echo, all covered with skin. A faint fetal heartbeat was detected in this mass; color Doppler demonstrated blood flow, and pulsed-wave Doppler showed a two-way arterial spectrum. Continuous fetal movements were observed.  
• The masses compressed the host fetus’s inferior vena cava and abdominal aorta, causing arch-shaped shifting; the intestines were displaced leftward, and the right kidney was displaced into the pelvis. Renal blood flow to the live mass derived from the host fetus’s abdominal aorta.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Teratoma considered and excluded because it “is sporadic bone mass or calcification without vertebral bones, limbs, or viscera.” 2. Neuroblastoma considered and excluded because it “is primarily characterized by a solid mass with no bones or other viscera.” 3. Meconium peritonitis considered and excluded because it “can manifest as an abdominal mass devoid of fetal-like structures.”

title: Postoperative Aseptic Intracranial Granuloma: The Possible Influence of Fluid Hemostatics
case_prompt:
A 71-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of persistent headache. Seven months earlier, she underwent right frontal craniotomy for a transitional (WHO Grade I) falx meningioma, which was completely excised (Simpson Grade I). Preoperatively, she received antihistamines and corticosteroids for poliallergic asthma. Hemostasis was achieved by filling the surgical cavity with absorbable hemostatic agents (bovine-derived gelatine with human-derived thrombin matrix and oxidized regenerated cellulose), and a dural graft was placed. Her postoperative course was uneventful. Early CT and MRI scans confirmed complete tumor removal. Blood tests showed elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (72.9 mg/L; normal <5 mg/L) but no fever, leukocytosis, or procalcitonin elevation. She was discharged on schedule.

On presentation, the surgical wound was fully healed. There was no fever, and hs-CRP remained slightly elevated (59.4 mg/L). Lumbar puncture yielded normal CSF both chemically and microbiologically. Noncontrast CT of the brain showed a 2.5 cm irregular, contrast-enhancing lesion along the right frontal falx, with surrounding edema and mass effect. MRI demonstrated that the lesion was isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted, diffusion-weighted, and FLAIR sequences. A relapse of meningioma was suspected, and surgical re-exploration was planned.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered infection — “the hypothesis of infection was not supported by the anamnesis … nor by any clinical signs or symptoms. Moreover, the negativity of procalcitonin firstly, and CSF examination secondary, seemed to controvert the elevation of hs-CRP.”

title: Bucillamine-induced Pneumonitis in a Patient with Rheumatoid Arthritis-associated Interstitial Pneumonia: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
An 81-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a 1-month history of progressive dyspnea. She had smoked 30 pack-years. Three months earlier, she had been diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis and started on bucillamine (200 mg/day) and prednisolone (4 mg/day), with improvement in her arthritis symptoms. Before starting bucillamine, fine crackles were noted over both lower lung fields, and a chest radiograph showed reticular shadows in the bilateral lower lung zones. Her medical history included angina pectoris and mitral valve replacement one year earlier. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis.

On examination, she was afebrile, with a respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute. Fine crackles were audible bilaterally; there were no signs of congestive heart failure. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (10,900/µL) with neutrophilia (81.5%), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (408 U/L), C-reactive protein (5.51 mg/dL), Krebs von den Lungen-6 (2,140 U/mL), surfactant protein-D (299.0 ng/mL), rheumatoid factor (352 IU/mL), anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody (≥1,200 U/mL), and hypoxemia (PaO2 87.2 mmHg on 3 L/min nasal oxygen). A lymphocyte stimulation test for bucillamine was negative (stimulation index, 168%). Brain natriuretic peptide, immunoglobulins, β-D-glucan, and other autoantibodies were normal or negative.

Pulmonary function tests revealed a vital capacity of 1.11 L (53.9% predicted), forced vital capacity of 1.08 L (52.4% predicted), FEV1 of 1.03 L (77.4% predicted), and DLCO of 2.23 mL/min/mmHg (17.7% predicted). Chest radiography demonstrated infiltrative opacities with reticular shadows in the bilateral middle and lower lung fields. High-resolution CT showed ground-glass opacities and consolidations in both lungs, along with reticular opacities, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis in the lower lobes, consistent with pre-existing usual interstitial pneumonia; note that ground-glass opacities and consolidations spared the lung bases. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid contained 2.45×10^5 cells/mL (44.0% macrophages, 25.5% lymphocytes, 26.0% neutrophils, 4.5% eosinophils; CD4/CD8 ratio 0.2), and cultures were negative. Transbronchial lung biopsy revealed alveolar organizing pneumonia, septal thickening, and inflammatory cell infiltration.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection was considered but excluded because “No microorganisms were detected in a BALF culture.” 2. Congestive heart failure was considered but deemed unlikely given that “No physical findings suggestive of congestive heart failure (pitting edema of the lower extremities and jugular venous distention) were observed,” and cardiac biomarkers were normal. 3. Acute exacerbation of pre-existing ILD was considered but judged less likely since “The most common histologic pattern of AE of chronic ILD is DAD; less commonly, patients have OP …” and the patient’s presentation showed an OP pattern temporally related to bucillamine initiation.

title: Cranial Nerve Palsies: Sarcoidosis to Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
case_prompt:
A 61-year-old African-American man presented with a four-day history of binocular diplopia, left eyelid droop, and numbness and tingling in both hands and feet. He also reported one month of profound fatigue and progressive proximal weakness, to the point of being unable to ambulate. His medical history was notable for biopsy-proven sarcoidosis treated with steroids in 1970, gout, dyspepsia, and mild obstructive pulmonary disease. He had lost 30 pounds over six months and had a chronic dry cough.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cranial-nerve testing revealed significant left eyelid ptosis (1 mm aperture), impaired adduction, abduction, and upward gaze bilaterally, partial right III and VI nerve palsies, and almost complete left III and VI nerve palsies. There was subtle bilateral facial weakness, decreased left facial sensation to pinprick, and proximal muscle atrophy with 4/5 strength in the shoulders and hips, 3/5 in lower extremities, preserved distal strength, decreased to absent reflexes bilaterally, and mildly decreased distal leg sensation. The abdomen was diffusely mildly tender; joints were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, normal calcium, electrolytes, renal function, HIV serology, vitamin B12, folate, muscle enzymes, and infectious serologies except mildly elevated AST and total bilirubin. ESR was 66 mm/hr, CRP normal, and urinalysis revealed 3+ proteinuria (normal six months earlier). Head CT was normal.

He was treated with five days of IVIG for a presumed Guillain-Barré–like syndrome. Chest CT showed multiple calcified nodules (some larger than prior studies) and a new right lower lobe infiltrate. Brain MRI/MRA (including orbits with gadolinium) was unremarkable. Nerve conduction studies and EMG revealed a diffuse, predominantly axonal, length-dependent sensory-motor polyneuropathy with myopathic features. Lumbar MRI showed multilevel degeneration. CSF analysis demonstrated albuminocytologic dissociation (protein 105 mg/dL). CT abdomen and pelvis showed minimal free fluid. Fine-needle aspiration of the lung infiltrate showed inflammatory cells and plasma cells without granulomas; cultures were negative. Muscle biopsy showed inflammatory myopathy with fiber atrophy and regeneration without vasculitis. Sural nerve biopsy revealed no acute abnormality but evidence of possible old vasculitis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraneoplastic syndrome was entertained due to weight loss and neuropathy but ruled out by negative malignancy workup, including normal abdominal imaging and lung biopsy — “fine needle aspiration biopsy of the lung infiltrate revealed an inflammatory infiltrate with increased plasma cells but no granulomas and all cultures were negative.” 2. Vasculitic neuropathy was considered based on neuropathy and biopsy findings but not confirmed as acute vasculitis — “a sural nerve biopsy was negative for any acute abnormality but revealed evidence of possible old vasculitis.”

title: Primary neuroendocrine tumor of the pineal gland: a case report
case_prompt:
A 53-year-old man with hypertension presented with five days of intractable vomiting, generalized weakness, ataxia, and severe headaches. On examination, he was lethargic, retching frequently, and had bilateral papilledema with impaired upward and downward gaze; gait could not be assessed safely. CT and MRI of the brain demonstrated hydrocephalus from obstruction of the third ventricle by a 2.5 × 3.0 × 3.2 cm hyperdense, contrast-enhancing mass centered in the pineal region with central calcification and mass effect on an edematous midbrain. 

He underwent endoscopic biopsy with third ventriculostomy and external ventricular drainage, followed by a subtotal resection via a supracerebellar infratentorial craniotomy. Histologic examination showed a highly cellular neoplasm of primitive round cells with finely dispersed chromatin, scant cytoplasm, brisk mitotic activity (20 per 10 high-power fields), focal papillary arrangements, and vague rosettes. Immunohistochemistry revealed strong positivity for synaptophysin, neuron-specific enolase, cytokeratin 8/18, CK AE1/AE3, and CAM 5.2; focal positivity for chromogranin, S100, and neurofilament; negativity for CK7, CK20, epithelial membrane antigen, thyroid transcription factor-1, glial fibrillary acidic protein, and parathyroid hormone; intact INI1; and a Ki-67 index of 60–70%. 

A PET/CT scan showed FDG avidity solely in the pineal region. A small, non–FDG-avid lesion in the left renal upper pole was noted incidentally.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of pineal parenchymal tumors — “The initial differential diagnosis included pineal parenchymal tumors, such as a pineoblastoma…” 2. Consideration of metastatic neuroendocrine carcinoma — “…as well as metastatic neuroendocrine carcinoma.” 3. Favoring neuroendocrine carcinoma based on dual marker expression — “…the presence of neuroendocrine markers along with cytokeratin positivity is consistent with a neuroendocrine carcinoma.” 4. Exclusion of a systemic primary by PET/CT — “PET/CT showed no primary lesion other than the pineal gland… a possible RCC was incidentally found, but this was unlikely to be the primary site due to the lack of FDG avidity.”

title: A rare and severe case of pronator teres syndrome
case_prompt:
A 57-year-old left-handed chef presented with a 5-year history of progressive weakness and decreased function in his left hand. Initial symptoms were mild numbness of the middle finger, treated conservatively as suspected carpal tunnel syndrome. Over several years the numbness progressed to the thumb and index finger, accompanied by grip and pinch weakness. Referral was prompted when he developed increasing forearm pain and complete loss of strength in thumb and index flexion, rendering him unable to work. He denied nocturnal symptoms and had no history of heavy lifting.

Medical history included smoking and left-sided male breast cancer treated by mastectomy, axillary node clearance, radiotherapy, and ongoing tamoxifen.

On examination, there was marked wasting of the thenar eminence and subtle forearm muscle atrophy. Sensation was reduced in the thumb, index, and middle fingers, and in the thenar triangle. Motor testing revealed 0/5 strength in flexor pollicis longus, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor digitorum superficialis of the index finger, with failure to form an “O-sign.” A Tinel sign was elicited 12 cm distal to the medial epicondyle. All provocative tests for carpal tunnel syndrome were negative.

Bilateral humeral radiographs showed no supracondylar processes, and none were palpable. Neurophysiology studies demonstrated no recordable median nerve motor responses on the left, with preserved sensory responses at the wrist. There was no evidence of widespread brachial plexus pathology or carpal tunnel syndrome, and findings indicated a severe left median mononeuropathy with acute and chronic denervation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Carpal tunnel syndrome was initially considered because of radial-digit numbness but was excluded when “All provocative tests for CTS were negative.” 2. Brachial plexus pathology was considered but excluded as “No indication of widespread brachial plexus pathology and nor CTS was found.” 3. Struthers’ ligament entrapment was considered but excluded as “On bilateral humeral plain radiographs, no supracondylar processes (Struthers’ ligament origin) were identified, nor were any such palpable.” 4. Anterior interosseous nerve syndrome was considered given the motor paralysis but was unlikely since it “classically presents with pure motor weakness, as the AIN provides no cutaneous innervation.”

title: A Nonfunctioning Pituitary Macroadenoma Presenting as Cyclical Vomiting and Severe Hyponatremia in a Young Female
case_prompt:
A 23-year-old Sri Lankan female law student presented with multiple episodes of nonbilious, nonprojectile vomiting over two days, associated with burning chest pain and regurgitation. She reported a 3-year history of similar cyclical vomiting episodes—each lasting 4–5 days with 4–6 daily episodes—followed by symptom-free intervals. These episodes were managed as gastroesophageal reflux disease with proton-pump inhibitors and prokinetics without full investigations. She denied abdominal pain, fever, weight loss, or bulimic behaviors; there were no signs of an eating disorder. She had reduced appetite, generalized weakness for 3 years, and secondary amenorrhea since age 20. She had psychosocial stressors, including a recent breakup. On exam, BMI was 16 kg/m^2, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, pulse 78 beats/min, and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed:  
- Serum sodium 110 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, chloride 74 mmol/L (repeatedly low)  
- Serum osmolality 223 mOsm/kg; urine osmolality 266 mOsm/kg; urine Na+ 14 mmol/L  
- Random serum cortisol 105 nmol/L (measured at 1 a.m.)  
- TSH 0.209 µIU/mL, free T3 1.32 pg/mL, free T4 0.98 ng/dL  
- FSH 1.4 mIU/mL, LH 0.4 mIU/mL  
- Serum prolactin 90.8 ng/mL after dilution  

She was euvolemic, denied polydipsia or polyuria, and had a normal urine output.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Gastroesophageal reflux disease was initially considered because of burning chest pain and regurgitation but was excluded since “managed as having gastroesophageal reflux disease with proton pump inhibitors and prokinetics as an outpatient without being investigated thoroughly,” yet episodes recurred. 2. Psychogenic vomiting related to stress was suggested because “She attributed its onset to the breakup of her relationship,” but organic causes were sought given persistent biochemical derangements. 3. Primary adrenal insufficiency was considered given her hyponatremia but was deemed unlikely when “Vomiting is an acute stress to the body. It usually causes high cortisol level in the body. This result contradicted it” (cortisol was low).

title: Navigating the Diagnostic and Management Challenges of Suspected Cardiac Amyloidosis in Resource‐Limited Settings: Ethiopia Experience
case_prompt:
A 54-year-old man presented with progressively worsening shortness of breath over 1 month, on a background of exertional dyspnea for 1 year and increasing fatigue. Recently, he developed orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and progressive bilateral leg swelling. He was treated with furosemide 40 mg twice daily and enalapril 5 mg twice daily with no improvement; enalapril caused dizziness and worsened fatigue. He has no personal or family history of hypertension or diabetes and denies syncope, neurologic symptoms, joint pain, or autonomic symptoms.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, pulse 110 bpm, respiratory rate 23 /min. Lung auscultation revealed bibasilar dullness and decreased air entry, more on the right. The jugular venous pressure was elevated; the precordium was quiet, and there was a systolic murmur at the left sternal border. The liver was enlarged 6 cm below the costal margin and tender, with shifting dullness; there was grade ++ bilateral pitting edema.

Laboratory tests showed normal renal function, electrolytes, liver function, and urinalysis. Hemoglobin was 11.7 g/dL (MCV 91, MCH 29). Serum troponin was elevated at 656 ng/L and 578 ng/L (normal < 100 ng/L). Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated hepatic congestion with hepatomegaly, ascites, and bilateral pleural effusions. Chest X-ray confirmed a right pleural effusion. ECG showed low-voltage limb leads with a pseudoinfarction pattern in precordial leads and ST-segment depression with T-wave inversions in V4–V6.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed biatrial enlargement and biventricular hypertrophy (IVS 22 mm, LVPW 21 mm, RV wall 14 mm) with interatrial septal thickening and granular “sparkling” myocardium. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 65% with no regional wall motion abnormalities. Doppler indices showed a restrictive filling pattern (E/A ratio 11, septal e′ 3, E/e′ 18) and atrial arrest. Mitral, aortic, and tricuspid valves were thickened; the IVC was plethoric, and there was minimal pericardial effusion.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hypertensive heart disease considered but excluded — “lack of a previous or current history of hypertension ... and the presence of a low voltage ECG instead of ECG features of left ventricular hypertrophy ... suggest that hypertensive heart disease is less likely in this case.” 2. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy considered but excluded — “presence of right ventricular hypertrophy ... and thickening of the interatrial septum ... make HCM less likely in this patient even without diagnostic imaging such as CMR.” 3. Cardiac amyloidosis considered most likely — “typical low voltage QRS with a pseudo infarction pattern, along with echocardiographic features such as LVH (ECG‐echocardiography discordance), biventricular hypertrophy, biatrial enlargement, interatrial septal thickening, myocardial sparkling, restrictive LV filling, and typical atrial arrest on echocardiography with persistent elevation of cardiac troponin ... strongly indicate cardiac amyloidosis.”

title: A Case Report of Peritoneal Tuberculosis: A Challenging Diagnosis
case_prompt:
A 16-year-old girl presented with a 7-day history of diffuse abdominal tenderness. She had no chronic illnesses, no alcohol or drug use, and no known tuberculosis exposures. Vital signs were normal. On examination, she had moderate ascites without other abnormalities.

Laboratory studies showed: 
- Normal blood counts and chemistries  
- CRP 39 mg/L (0–5)  
- ESR 42 mm/h (0–20)  

Chest radiograph was normal. Abdominal ultrasound revealed free fluid with septations, multiple small nodular implants on peritoneal surfaces (largest 16 mm), and a septated left ovarian cyst measuring 39 × 27 mm. Paracentesis yielded lymphocyte-predominant fluid without malignant cells, with a serum–ascites albumin gradient <1.1 g/dL.

Thyroid function tests were normal. Serum tumor markers were: 
- AFP 2.48 ng/mL (0–7)  
- CEA 0.39 ng/mL (0–3.8)  
- CA15-3 26.15 U/mL (0–26.4)  
- CA19-9 1.14 U/mL (0–27)  
- CA125 107.5 U/mL (0–35)  

Brucella and viral serologies were negative. Tuberculin skin testing was anergic, and QuantiFERON PCR and acid-fast staining of ascitic fluid were negative.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck, chest, and abdomen showed bilateral cervical, supraclavicular, paratracheal, subcarinal, anterior diaphragmatic, and cardiophrenic lymphadenopathy; nasopharyngeal soft-tissue thickening; omental nodularity with peritoneal implants; ascites; a 23 × 18 mm left ovarian cyst; and mesenteric lymph nodes. Ascitic fluid adenosine deaminase was elevated at 62 U/L (0–30). Abdominal MRI confirmed bilateral ovarian cysts, dilated fallopian tubes, nodular peritoneal opacities, and moderate ascites.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Brucellosis was tested for given systemic inflammation and ascites but excluded — “Brucella markers… were negative.” 2. Viral infection was excluded after negative serologic testing — “viral markers were negative.”

title: Germinoma with Involvement of Midline and Off-Midline Intracranial Structures
case_prompt:
An 18-year-old male was admitted with psychomotor excitement, polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, and a seven-month history of progressive right-sided hemiparesis with dystonia. He had two years of poor school performance, anxiety, and emotional lability. Seven months earlier, a nonenhanced head CT was normal.

On examination, he appeared pale with a BMI of 14.4 kg/m2 and blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg with a poor response to fluids. Sexual maturation was Tanner stage 3 with small testes (6 mL) and pubic and axillary hair stage G2. Neurologically, he had right hemiparesis with hyperreflexia and dystonia. Laboratory studies showed polyuria with low urine osmolality, hypernatremia, hypotension refractory to fluid resuscitation, and panhypopituitarism, leading to treatment with hydrocortisone followed by levothyroxine and desmopressin.

Brain MRI revealed a solid, homogeneously enhancing mass in the pituitary and hypothalamus with marked pituitary stalk thickening and absence of the posterior pituitary bright spot. There was T2 hyperintensity in the left internal capsule, corona radiata, caudate nucleus, and bilateral globus pallidus, with mild atrophy of the left internal capsule and ipsilateral cerebral peduncle. Differential diagnoses included nonadenomatous lesions of the pituitary stalk and ectopic germinoma. Chest CT and serum angiotensin-converting enzyme levels were normal. Skull and long-bone radiographs showed no lytic lesions. CSF examination revealed no atypical cells; CSF α-fetoprotein was negative and CSF β-hCG was slightly elevated.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pituitary adenoma was considered but DI makes this diagnosis unlikely — “It is important to emphasize that the presence of DI almost rules out a pituitary adenoma.” 2. Sarcoidosis was considered but ruled out by normal chest CT and ACE levels — “normal thoracic TC and angiotensin converting enzyme ruled out sarcoidosis.” 3. Langerhans cell histiocytosis was considered but excluded by normal skull and long bones radiographs without typical lytic lesions — “skull and long bones X-rays were normal, without typical lytic lesions of Langerhans cell histiocytosis.” 4. Metastatic disease or hematologic malignancy was considered but CSF cytology was negative for atypical cells — “The CSF examination did not show atypical cells.”

title: Surgical treatment of a distal oesophageal stricture by mucosal radial incision and dilation in a kitten with secondary megaoesophagus
case_prompt: A 7-month-old intact female Maine Coon kitten was evaluated for a 2-month history of severe regurgitation occurring seconds to minutes after eating or drinking, with episodes ranging from twice weekly to twice daily. Weight loss was noted over this period despite a normal appetite, water consumption, and activity level. Previous treatments—including maropitant, cimetidine, amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, and gastrointestinal diets—had no effect. The kitten had a history of chronic vomiting of undigested food and bile from age 3–5 months and a self-resolving episode of acute vomiting at 5 months. No regurgitation or vomiting occurred before 3 months of age. On presentation, the kitten was bright, alert, weighed 2.5 kg with a body condition score of 2/9, and had stunted growth; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Thoracic radiographs obtained before and after oral contrast demonstrated severe and diffuse oesophageal dilatation consistent with generalized megaoesophagus, with persistent contrast and aeric distension of the oesophageal lumen and apparent loss of continuity between the distal oesophagus and the stomach. No aspiration pneumonia was evident.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Peptic stricture — “We think it is most likely that the stricture in the cat presented herein was acquired, and of chemical origin, secondary to irritation from gastric acid in the context of chronic vomiting…” 2. Anesthesia-induced gastroesophageal reflux — “Other origins for oesophageal strictures in cats include gastroesophageal reflux (mostly occurring during anaesthesia owing to reduced cardial tonus)…” 3. Caustic drug injury — “Other origins for oesophageal strictures in cats include… oral administration of caustic drugs such as doxycycline or clindamycin…” 4. Foreign body lesion — “Other origins for oesophageal strictures in cats include… a foreign body causing mucosal lesions.” 5. Congenital stenosis or atresia — “A congenital stenosis could not be definitively ruled out. Partial oesophageal atresia or congenital stricture… seemed discordant with normal food intake in the first months of the kitten’s life and the late onset of regurgitations.” 6. Gastric cardia neoplasm — “Neoplasms of the gastric cardia are known to cause megaoesophagus in dogs…but would have been unexpected in such a young patient.”

title: A patient with Pfeifer-Weber-Christian Disease - Successful Therapy with Cyclosporin A: case report
case_prompt: A 64-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis (RF positive, anti-CCP negative), diagnosed in 2003, initially treated with methotrexate and low-dose prednisolone, presented in early 2007 with an RA flare treated successfully with prednisolone and leflunomide. Six months later, despite no joint pain or ultrasonographic evidence of synovitis, he reported fatigue, night sweats, a 10-kg weight gain, and persistent elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP). He then developed paresthesia and pressure pain in both lower legs; extensive evaluation—including echocardiography, sonography of joints, soft tissues, and abdomen; CT of the thorax; repeated blood cultures; bone marrow aspiration; and urine testing—failed to identify any infectious or malignant cause. Serologic testing showed normal ANA, ANCA, anti–dsDNA, complement C3/C4, anti-CCP, and anti-MCV levels; RF remained positive at baseline levels. A positron emission tomography scan demonstrated focal FDG uptake in the lower limbs without a vasculitic pattern. Treatment was switched to sulfasalazine and high-dose prednisolone with temporary CRP improvement, but one month later he returned with high fevers (up to 40 °C), CRP 220 mg/L, night sweats, and multiple 3–4 cm painful nodules on the inner thighs causing immobility. There were still no signs of synovitis. MRI of the lower limbs showed acute fasciitis. A painful nodule was excised, and histologic examination revealed lobular panniculitis with a mixed inflammatory infiltrate dominated by neutrophils.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Rheumatoid arthritis flare was unlikely — “physical examination revealed no signs of synovitis.” 2. Infection was excluded — “repeated blood cultures… not able to identify any infectious… cause.” 3. Malignancy or lymphoma was excluded — “bone marrow aspiration… not able to identify any… malignant cause.” 4. Erythema nodosum was excluded by histology — “PWCD has been classified as lobular panniculitis with mixed cell infiltrate, which contrasts findings in erythema nodosum classified as predominantly septal panniculitis.”

title: Perirenal schwannoma: a case report
case_prompt:
A 55-year-old man presented with lower urinary tract symptoms characterized by hesitancy, poor urinary stream, and urgency. He denied flank pain, colicky pain, haematuria, weight loss, or constitutional symptoms. His medical history was unremarkable, and he had no prior genitourinary disease. He was afebrile, with stable vital signs, and a physical examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed a serum prostate-specific antigen level of 5.1 ng/ml and a serum creatinine concentration of 86 μmol/l. Urine microscopy demonstrated no haematuria or atypical cells. Transrectal ultrasound–guided prostate biopsies revealed benign prostatic hyperplasia.

An abdominal ultrasound performed to evaluate his lower urinary tract symptoms incidentally revealed an exophytic lesion in the right kidney measuring approximately 3.0 × 3.2 × 4.2 cm. The lesion was homogeneous and hypoechoic with a well-defined margin, located at the lateral border of the ventral mid-zone of the kidney. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen confirmed a soft-tissue mass in the same location, measuring up to 2.17 cm in maximum diameter, with a small area of calcification; the lesion was exophytic and situated away from the renal hilum.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Synovial sarcoma — “This highlights the importance of using a panel of antibodies on handling a spindle cell lesion of the kidney to cover all of the possible differential diagnoses such as sarcomatoid carcinoma and synovial sarcoma in the context of the gross and histological features.” 2. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) — “Initial scepticism was expressed about the biological behaviour of cellular schwannomas, with some suggesting that it was in fact a low-grade malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST). Woodruff et al. stressed the benign clinical course of cellular schwannomas and the importance of distinguishing them from MPNST.”

title: Exercise intolerance — from spiroergometry to transdiaphragmatic myocardial punch biopsy: a case report of isolated cardiac sarcoidosis
case_prompt:
A 49-year-old amateur athlete presented with 3 weeks of exercise intolerance manifesting as exertional dyspnoea and dizziness. He denied syncope, had no family history of sudden cardiac death, and took no medications. He had traveled to Iran and, 3 months earlier, had an inguinal bullous skin lesion treated briefly with topical steroids without recurrence.

On examination: heart rate 61 bpm, blood pressure 122/77 mmHg, temperature 36.5 °C; cardiac and pulmonary exams were normal. Laboratory tests showed normal leukocytes (4.89 g/L) and CRP (0.8 mg/L). Resting ECG revealed an incomplete right bundle branch block and first-degree AV block; rhythm monitoring showed intermittent 2:1 AV block. During spiroergometry, exercise-induced high-degree AV block was documented.

Transthoracic echocardiography raised suspicion of inferobasal septal wall-motion abnormalities and, retrospectively, a mass; left ventricular ejection fraction was normal. Coronary angiography excluded coronary artery disease, and chest CT revealed no pulmonary pathology. Cardiac MRI demonstrated a 5 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm mass in the inferobasal septum with late gadolinium enhancement but no active perfusion. FDG-PET showed intense uptake in the inferobasal septum and no extracardiac uptake.

Further evaluation revealed normal sIL-2 receptor, ANA, ANCA, serum calcium, and ACE levels. Blood cultures (aerobic, anaerobic, mycobacterial) and serologies for Brucella, Bartonella henselae, Treponema pallidum, Borrelia burgdorferi, Coxiella burnetii, and HIV were negative. PCR for tuberculosis and mycobacterial cultures were negative. Dermatologic full-body exam showed only non-specific skin changes.

Because there were no extracardiac manifestations, transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography-guided endomyocardial biopsies were performed, but all samples were non-specific.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cardiac tumour was considered — “a tumour…However, no active perfusion of this particular site was seen in coronary angiography nor CMR.” 2. Infectious disease (including tuberculosis) was considered — “Travel history to Iran raised pre‐test probability for mycobacterial infection. However, polymerase chain reaction for tuberculosis and blood cultures for mycobacteria remained negative.” 3. Cutaneous sarcoidosis was considered — “Dermatologist‐performed full‐body skin examination revealed only unremarkable cutaneous lesions that were considered as non‐specific and not typical for sarcoidosis.” 4. Endomyocardial biopsy was attempted to differentiate these entities but remained non‐specific — “all samples remained non‐specific.”

title: A Rare Cause of Ascites-Disseminated TB with Peritonitis in a Middle-Aged Female
case_prompt: A 47-year-old previously healthy woman presented with four months of generalized body swelling, disproportionate ascites, loss of appetite, and weight loss. She denied fever, night sweats, jaundice, bleeding, cough, or TB exposure. There was no history of cardiac, renal, or liver disease; no alcohol use; and an HIV test was negative. On exam she was emaciated (BMI 18), afebrile, with moderate ascites, mild ankle edema, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies showed normochromic normocytic anemia, thrombocytosis, normal liver enzymes except mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT, normal INR, normal renal function, and a serum CA-125 of 175 U/ml (normal <46). Chest radiograph was normal; echocardiography showed normal cardiac structure and function without constrictive pericarditis. Transvaginal ultrasound revealed no ovarian pathology. Abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT suggested heterogeneous liver parenchyma, minimal surface nodularity, and moderate ascites without portal vein thrombosis or lymphadenopathy, raising concern for decompensated chronic liver disease. Workup for cirrhosis was unrevealing, and upper endoscopy showed no varices or portal gastropathy. Diuretics were started. Diagnostic paracentesis revealed exudative ascites with elevated inflammatory markers; cultures (bacterial, fungal), acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear, TB culture, and GeneXpert on fluid were negative, and cytology was negative for malignant cells. The tuberculin skin test measured 17 mm induration.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cryptogenic chronic liver disease — “the findings were in favour of possible decompensated chronic liver disease” … “the CLD workup did not reveal any potential aetiology.” 2. Constrictive pericarditis — “transthoracic echocardiography showed … no evidence of constrictive pericarditis.” 3. Congestive heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, and renal impairment — “Other causes of generalised oedema … were also excluded by history, physical examination, and basic investigations.” 4. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis — “There was no evidence of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.” 5. Malignancy — “ascitic fluid cytology was negative for malignant cells.”

title: Large left atrial thrombus resection in a patient in sinus rhythm without mitral valve disease: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt: A 63-year-old man with a history of duodenal ulcer repair and mesh insertion for an abdominal incisional hernia was admitted with fever and found to have pus in his abdomen on examination. He had no history of thrombotic events, smoking, drug use, or family history of coagulopathy. Laboratory tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 2.63 mg/dL, fibrinogen 493 mg/dL, and a normal antithrombin III level; blood cultures were negative. He was diagnosed with a mesh infection and received 4 weeks of antibiotic therapy before transfer for abdominal surgical debridement. Preoperative transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 39 mm mass in the left atrium adherent to the atrial septum, an ejection fraction of 56%, and no valvular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced CT also demonstrated a 39 mm left atrial mass with partial calcification. Brain MRI showed minuscule, scattered cerebral infarctions. Based on these findings, the mass was presumed to be a myxoma and surgical resection was scheduled.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered left atrial myxoma — “Myxomas appear as smooth-surfaced and attached to the atrial septum by the stalk in the fossa ovalis…They present calcification-associated heterogeneous areas of hyper-echogenicity.” 2. Excluded patent foramen ovale–related thrombus — “Patent foramen ovale also causes LA thrombus, but in such cases, the thrombus extends from the right atrium through the patent foramen ovale into the left atrium. This is not consistent with the present case.” 3. Excluded congenital coagulopathy — “Congenital coagulopathy is a potential cause but unlikely because of absent medical history and no family history of coagulopathy.” 4. Considered left atrial thrombus given embolic events — “LA thrombus, a common cause of cerebral infarction, is caused by blood stasis in the left atrium.”

title: An Enlarged and Infected Prostatic Utricle as a Rare Cause of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms in Adolescent Males
case_prompt:
A 13-year-old boy with mixed gonadal dysgenesis and a two-stage proximal hypospadias repair as an infant presented with 2 days of lower abdominal pain and dysuria. He had occasional vomiting, loose stools, and had had a fever 1 day earlier. In the ED, he was afebrile, with mild lower abdominal distension and suprapubic tenderness. Urinalysis showed turbid urine with too numerous to count white blood cells; a urine culture was sent. He was discharged on oral cephalexin for presumed UTI.

He returned 2 days later with persistent end-of-void suprapubic and right lower quadrant pain, dysuria, urgency, frequency, and foul-smelling urine. He denied fevers or chills; diarrhea had resolved. He reported decreased appetite and small, clumped stools but denied chronic constipation. His mother noted a small amount of urethral discharge. On exam, he was afebrile, with lower abdominal tenderness; genitourinary exam revealed a circumcised penis, an absent left testis, and a mildly tender right testis with normal lie and reflexes. Repeat urinalysis again showed too numerous to count white blood cells, though the previous urine culture had no growth. Peripheral white count was normal and CRP was 20 mg/dL.

Scrotal ultrasound showed a normal right testicle with normal flow. Abdominal ultrasound did not fully visualize the appendix and showed no secondary signs of appendicitis. Retroperitoneal ultrasound revealed normal kidneys and bladder but reported “circumferential wall thickening of the rectum with an air-fluid level.” The differential diagnosis included UTI, appendicitis, scrotal pathology, severe constipation, or gastroenteritis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial urinary tract infection was considered but “A urine culture had shown no growth, arguing against a bacterial urinary tract infection.” 2. Scrotal pathology was evaluated but “Scrotal pathology was ruled out by ultrasound.” 3. Gastroenteritis was plausible because of diarrhea and vomiting, but “the presence of significant pyuria and dysuria would be unusual.” 4. Appendicitis was considered but “While the absence of secondary signs of appendicitis was reassuring, appendicitis could not be ruled out.” 5. Severe constipation was on the differential but “He denied chronic constipation.”

title: Adenocarcinoma originating from presumed liver ectopic thyroid in a cat
case_prompt: A 15-year-old spayed female domestic shorthair cat was examined for a routine checkup. Physical examination, complete blood count, and serum biochemistry were unremarkable. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 9 × 7 mm heterogeneous hypoechoic nodule at the periphery of the quadrate liver lobe. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology showed numerous grouped epithelial cells with moderate to marked anisocytosis and anisokaryosis, round nuclei with reticular to coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and poorly basophilic cytoplasm. A presumptive diagnosis of epithelial neoplasia (carcinoma) was made, and metastatic disease was considered. Serial monitoring showed progressive growth of the nodule to 1.73 × 1.34 cm over 4 months, with a transient rise in AST and ALT. Laparoscopic liver biopsy was then performed; histopathology suggested lymphocytic cholangitis, and symptomatic treatment normalized transaminases, but the nodule continued to enlarge. Whole-body CT showed a diffuse heterogeneous liver parenchyma, especially in the quadrate lobe, without any other abnormalities. A partial lobectomy of the quadrate lobe was performed. Histopathology of the excised mass revealed multilobulated epithelial cells forming follicular structures containing dense eosinophilic, colloid-like material, merging into tubular and solid proliferations with nuclear crowding and mitotic figures. Immunohistochemistry showed diffuse cytoplasmic positivity for thyroglobulin in most neoplastic cells.
