Thyroxine (T4) and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) concentrations were within reference intervals. Re-evaluation of the CT images revealed no abnormalities in the orthotopic thyroid gland and no nodular lesions elsewhere.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary hepatic epithelial neoplasia was initially presumed given cytologic features — “Based on the ultrasound and cytological findings, a presumptive diagnosis of epithelial neoplasia (carcinoma) was made…” 2. Reactive inflammatory disease was diagnosed on laparoscopic biopsy — “After this, a histopathological diagnosis of lymphocytic cholangitis was made.” 3. Metastatic lesion was considered due to the malignant cytologic appearance — “the possibility of a metastatic lesion was considered.”

title: Whipple's Disease with Long-term Endoscopic Follow-up
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old man presented with a 3-year history of progressive anorexia, general malaise, and a 15 kg weight loss that left him unable to walk. His medical history was notable only for intestinal volvulus surgery at age 14 years. On admission, he was alert; temperature was 36.9 °C, blood pressure 124/86 mmHg, pulse 60 beats/min. He appeared cachectic (BMI 16.4 kg/m2). Neurologic, respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed:
- Hypoalbuminemia (2.1 g/dL) and low total protein (4.9 g/dL)
- Elevated C-reactive protein (2.75 mg/dL)
- Elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor (1,950 U/mL) and interleukin-6 (20.7 pg/mL)
- Hemoglobin 6.6 g/dL; other values notable for normal LFTs and renal function.

Abdominal CT demonstrated thickening of the small-intestinal mucosa and intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy. Upper endoscopy revealed yellow-white, shaggy mucosa in the duodenum; biopsy specimens showed no specific abnormalities. The patient received intravenous hyperalimentation without improvement. Given the high sIL-2R and IL-6 levels and lymphadenopathy, oral prednisolone (25 mg daily) was initiated, leading to improved appetite and weight gain. He was discharged on day 33. One month later, he lost his appetite again and was readmitted for further evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Inflammatory rheumatic diseases were considered in the differential — “The differential diagnoses include inflammatory rheumatic diseases, malabsorption with small intestine involvement (e.g., celiac disease, sarcoidosis, and lymphoma), Addison’s disease, and connective tissue disorders.” 2. Malabsorption syndromes (celiac disease, sarcoidosis, lymphoma) were in the differential — “…malabsorption with small intestine involvement (e.g., celiac disease, sarcoidosis, and lymphoma)…” 3. Addison’s disease was included among endocrine causes — “…Addison’s disease…” 4. Connective tissue disorders were considered — “…and connective tissue disorders.” 5. Mycobacterium avium complex was noted as a histologic mimic — “Mycobacterium avium complex, which also stains PAS-positive, should be considered as a possible differential diagnosis…”

title: Pericardial anisakiasis: unravelling diagnostic challenges in an unprecedented extra-abdominal manifestation: a case report
case_prompt:
A 26-year-old man presented with two weeks of progressive lower-limb edema and exertional dyspnea. He had chronic sinusitis and a family history of celiac disease. Two months earlier, he had eaten raw fish without gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable, with muffled heart sounds and pitting edema of the legs.

Laboratory tests showed eosinophilia (980/mm^3; normal 0–700), elevated total IgE, and C-reactive protein of 12 mg/dL (normal <5). ECG was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large pericardial effusion with “swinging heart,” ventricular interdependence, a dilated inferior vena cava, respiratory variation of mitral inflow of 25%, and an LVEF of 48%. Contrast CT of the chest and abdomen showed pericardial and pleural effusions and mediastinal lymphadenopathy up to 15 mm. 

He was started on colchicine and ibuprofen. Echo-guided pericardiocentesis drained 1150 mL of serous fluid. Fluid cultures were negative and cytology revealed normal lymphocytes, macrophages, and mesothelial cells, without eosinophils. His LVEF normalized and edema improved, but eosinophilia persisted (940–1100/mm^3) and a residual posterior effusion of 16 mm remained.

Cardiac MRI demonstrated pericardial and subepicardial late gadolinium enhancement and myocardial edema on T2 mapping, with an LVEF of 54%. PET-FDG scan showed intense uptake in the pericardium and mediastinal lymph nodes. 

Extensive testing was performed: serologies for viral and bacterial causes of pericarditis were negative; inhalant allergy tests and skin prick tests were negative; stool studies were normal; serologies for Toxocara canis, Strongyloides stercoralis, Ascaris lumbricoides, and Trichinella spiralis were negative. Echinococcus granulosus IgG was mildly elevated at 1:64 (positive >1:32), and Anisakis simplex IgE was 0.33 kUA/L (positive >0.10). Peripheral-blood and bone-marrow immunophenotyping and cytology showed expansion of normal eosinophils without malignancy. Autoantibodies for eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis were negative, and sinus imaging was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral or bacterial pericarditis considered and excluded — “All serology tests for viral and bacterial aetiologies of pericarditis ... yielded negative results.” 2. Allergic pericardial inflammation considered and excluded — “An allergic reaction was excluded due to unsuggestive serology for inhalants, negative skin prick tests, and lack of common allergic symptoms.” 3. Common parasitic infections considered and excluded — “Serology of Toxocara canis, Strongyloides stercoralis, Ascaris lumbricoides, and Trichinella spiralis were also negative.” 4. Onco-haematologic disease considered and excluded — “An onco-haematologic condition was excluded after negative results from peripheral-blood immunophenotyping and medullary-blood immunophenotyping and cytology—which showed an expansion of normal medullary eosinophils.” 5. Pericardial anisakiasis considered — “Upon confirmation of stable elevation of Anisakis-specific IgE titre and long-lasting eosinophilia, we suspected the patient had consumed contaminated fish, leading to potential pericardial anisakiasis.”

title: Frameshift mutation in exon 17 of PTCH1 gene in Nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome: A case report
journal: Journal of Oral and Maxillofacial Pathology : JOMFP
case_prompt:
A 14-year-old girl presented with a 3-week history of bilateral mandibular swelling and morning purulent oral discharge. She was otherwise well, with stable vital signs. On examination, there was bilateral facial swelling in the mandibular region, multiple dark brown nevi on the palms and soles (unchanged in size for ≥5 years), and clinical macrocephaly. Intraorally, there was bilateral buccal cortical expansion in the posterior mandible with moderate pus discharge adjacent to tooth 36; teeth were firm and without paresthesia.

Orthopantomogram showed expansile radiolucent lesions in the mandible bilaterally, extending from the first molar region to the coronoid processes, with impacted second and third molars displaced into the coronoid areas and cortical thinning and erosion at multiple sites. A smaller radiolucent lesion with an impacted third molar was seen in the maxilla. 

Incisional biopsy from multiple sites revealed a parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelial lining, six to eight cell layers thick, with surface corrugation and basal cell palisading, and a moderately collagenous fibrous capsule. The mandibular lesions were initially marsupialized; three months later, enucleation specimens showed similar histology plus budding of basal epithelial cells, keratin-filled daughter cysts, odontogenic epithelial islands, and focal crowding with mild atypia of basal and parabasal layers.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the head demonstrated calcifications within the falx cerebri. A chest radiograph was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Multiple odontogenic cysts (OKC vs dentigerous cyst) were considered based on imaging — “a differential diagnosis of multiple odontogenic cysts (OKC and dentigerous cyst) were considered…” 2. Dentigerous cyst was excluded and OKC was confirmed on histopathology revealing parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelial lining with basal cell palisading — “These exhibited features are characteristic of OKC.” 3. The combination of multiple OKCs in adolescence, falx cerebri calcifications, and palmar/plantar nevi raised the possibility of NBCCS — “Since the patient had falx cerebri calcifications, multiple nevi on palms and soles along with the presence of multiple OKCs, the possibility of NBCCS had to be considered.”

title: Oral focal mucinosis of the hard palate and gingiva
case_prompt: A 41-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a progressively enlarging oral lesion over 8 months. She had no significant medical history but recalled an oral surgery at the same site 8 years earlier (records unavailable). Intraoral examination revealed a well-defined, lobulated nodule measuring 3.0 cm, covered by normal mucosa, extending from the palatal gingiva of the right first and second molars onto the hard palate. The lesion was firm on palpation, painless, and slightly mobile. A removable partial prosthesis had caused mild trauma to the adjacent premolar region. Panoramic radiography was normal. An incisional biopsy was performed; the specimen was rubbery, soft to moderately firm, and gray-white in color.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Reactive gingival lesions (e.g., fibroma, epulis, pyogenic granuloma, peripheral ossifying fibroma) were considered, but “In our case, the location and the size of the lesion did not immediately favor such clinical hypotheses.” 2. Peripheral ossifying fibroma was less likely given its exclusive gingival origin and typical occurrence in younger patients: “POF is a fibro-osseous reactive lesion, exclusive to the gingiva, though it could expand to the adjacent structures, depending on the size.” 3. Pyogenic granuloma was ruled out because it forms a reddish-purple nodule prone to bleeding: “PG occurs both on the skin and mucosa... having a natural tendency to bleeding, distinguishing it from OFM.” 4. Peripheral giant cell granuloma was unlikely, as it demonstrates radiographic alveolar bone cupping, which was absent here: “a superficial ‘cupping’ representing alveolar bone resorption is often seen in the radiographs; this aspect is not found in OFM.” 5. Pleomorphic adenoma was considered for a palatal mass, but its usual dome-shaped contour and limited size on the palate conflicted with the lobulated appearance: “The lobulated appearance seen in the present case is not a common finding in OFM and it usually appears as a dome-shaped nodule.”

title: Intratympanic steroid treatments rescued recurrent hearing loss following COVID-19 vaccination and detection of an intralabyrinthine schwannoma
case_prompt: A man in his 20s with no prior hearing disorder was referred to the ENT clinic after two episodes of sudden left-ear hearing loss occurring within three months of receiving his second dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. On day 1 after vaccination, he developed progressive left-ear hearing loss lasting 10 days. Treatment with pseudoephedrine and fluticasone nasal spray for suspected allergic otitis media yielded no improvement. He then took cetirizine 10 mg daily from day 9 to 24, with near-normal hearing on an audiogram performed on day 26 (normal pure-tone averages, bilateral type A tympanograms, masked bone conduction). On day 31, he experienced recurrent left-ear hearing loss unresponsive to a 5-day course of cetirizine. A high-dose oral prednisone regimen (60 mg/day for 7 days followed by taper) was given for presumed Meniere’s disease but failed to improve his hearing. Sequential audiograms on days 44 and 60 demonstrated progressive low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the left ear and a marginal decline in word recognition. Videonystagmography on day 76 showed 93% left-ear caloric weakness and 50% directional preponderance to the right; optokinetic testing revealed asymmetrical, reduced left-sided responses. Additional blood tests—including complete blood count with differential, rheumatoid factor, comprehensive metabolic panel, and lipid profile—were all within normal limits, and anti–SARS-CoV-2 antibodies were positive.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Allergic otitis media with effusion suspected — “Subsequent treatments with pseudoephedrine and fluticasone propionate nasal spray for suspected allergic otitis media with effusion did not show any improvement.” 2. Autoimmune inner ear disease excluded — “additional blood tests were performed showing normal complete blood counts (with differential analyses) and normal rheumatoid factor results without detection of autoimmune response.” 3. Metabolic dysregulation excluded — “The comprehensive metabolic panel and blood lipid laboratory tests were also normal, largely excluding the possibility of metabolic dysregulation caused hearing damage.”

title: Clinicopathological Implications of Proteinuria after Long-Term Isolated Hematuria due to Thin Basement Membrane Nephropathy and Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old man was referred for evaluation of proteinuria. He had noted occult blood in his urine since age 24. At age 37, hypertension was diagnosed, and laboratory tests showed microscopic hematuria and proteinuria of 0.4 g/g creatinine. Over the next eight years, proteinuria gradually increased to 1.16 g/g creatinine, prompting hospital admission.

He denied episodes of gross hematuria or flank pain. He was born at term; birth weight was unknown. There was no family history of kidney disease. Medications included losartan (25 mg daily), amlodipine (5 mg daily), and febuxostat (20 mg daily). He weighed 98.7 kg and was 177 cm tall (BMI 31.5 kg/m^2) and had been overweight (BMI >25) since adolescence. Blood pressure was 140/78 mm Hg.

Physical examination was unremarkable. Urinalysis showed proteinuria of 0.7 g/g creatinine, 10–19 red blood cells per high-power field, and red-cell casts. Serum creatinine was 0.69 mg/dL, cystatin C 0.74 mg/L, albumin 4.1 g/dL, uric acid 6.3 mg/dL, LDL cholesterol 144 mg/dL, and HbA1c 5.5%. Estimated GFR was 97.6 mL/min/1.73 m^2. C-reactive protein, IgG, IgA, complement levels, and ANA were within normal limits. ECG and chest radiograph were normal. Renal ultrasonography showed bilaterally normal-sized kidneys with preserved cortical thickness.

With a clinical suspicion of IgA nephropathy, a kidney biopsy was performed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered IgA nephropathy because of the combination of microscopic hematuria and proteinuria — “With a clinical suspicion of IgA nephropathy, kidney biopsy was performed.”

title: Temporal horns subependymomas: A report of two cases of an intraventricular neoplasm in an atypical location
case_prompt:
A 59‐year‐old woman was referred after an incidental finding of a brain mass on CT performed for evaluation of labyrinthitis. She was asymptomatic, with no neurologic deficits. Her medical history included hypertension and diabetes mellitus; there was no history of seizures. Laboratory studies were unremarkable.

Noncontrast head CT showed a well‐circumscribed hypodense mass in the temporal horn of the right lateral ventricle, with tiny hyperdense foci suggestive of prior microhemorrhages. Brain MRI demonstrated a lobulated intraventricular lesion measuring approximately 4.0 × 2.8 × 2.7 cm. On T1‐weighted imaging, the mass was hypointense relative to gray matter; on T2‐weighted imaging, it was heterogeneously hyperintense, with mild adjacent temporal lobe hyperintensity. Susceptibility‐weighted imaging confirmed punctate hypointense foci within the lesion. There was no or only minimal enhancement after gadolinium administration. High‐resolution 3D‐CISS sequences revealed internal cystic components. Short‐echo‐time proton spectroscopy of the lesion showed a partially decreased N‐acetylaspartate peak, a normal choline peak, and an increased myoinositol peak.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ependymoma was considered because intraventricular tumors may share imaging features, but it was deemed unlikely given that “Intense enhancement is observed in ependymomas, as well as extraventricular extension to the brain parenchyma and the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF),” whereas this lesion showed minimal enhancement and no evidence of invasion. 2. Central neurocytoma was considered due to the intraventricular location and cystic components but was excluded because “subependymomas show a large MI peak (produced only by astrocyte cells), not seen in neurocytomas,” and our spectroscopy showed an increased myoinositol peak. 3. Pilocytic astrocytoma, chordoid glioma, myxoid meningioma, and oligodendroglioma were noted as potential diagnoses when cystic or microcystic degeneration is present, as “When there is microcystic degeneration, the list should contain pilocytic astrocytomas, chordoid gliomas, myxoide meningioma and oligodendrogliomas.” 4. The combination of minimal enhancement, lack of invasion, lobulated intraventricular location in a middle‐aged patient, and a spectroscopy profile of decreased NAA with elevated myoinositol most strongly supported a benign glial neoplasm—subependymoma.

title: Isolated left foot drop post aortobifemoral bypass surgery: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and a 36–pack-year smoking history presented with severe bilateral lower-limb claudication (walking tolerance ≤ 100 m) and a non-healing ulcer on the right fifth toe. He had no palpable femoral pulses, monophasic distal Doppler signals bilaterally, and was neurologically intact preoperatively with normal strength and sensation. CT angiography demonstrated bilateral external iliac artery occlusion with patent internal and common iliac arteries and collateralized flow to the femorals. 

He underwent an aortobifemoral bypass via a transperitoneal approach under general anesthesia. The procedure lasted 4 hours without intraoperative hypotension, inotropes, or leg compression. Post-bypass Doppler signals in the anterior and posterior tibial arteries were biphasic. 

Twelve hours postoperatively, he reported inability to dorsiflex his left foot. Examination revealed 0/5 strength of left ankle dorsiflexion and eversion, numbness over the dorsum of the left foot, an absent left Achilles reflex, and preserved warmth and biphasic distal pulses. Over the next two days, left ankle dorsiflexion improved to 2/5. 

A spine MRI showed no disc herniation, cord compression, or other abnormalities. A nerve conduction study revealed severe chronic sensory motor axonal peripheral polyneuropathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of an upper motor neuron lesion was noted because “Other less common causes include upper motor neuron lesions at the cerebral cortex and corticospinal tract levels.”

title: Mesalazine-induced myopericarditis: a case report
case_prompt:
A 22-year-old Caucasian man presented with sudden onset left-sided chest pain radiating to the shoulder tip. The pain was sharp, worsened by deep inspiration and lying supine, and was accompanied by dyspnea and fatigue. He denied cough, fever, or prodromal viral symptoms. 

His past medical history was notable for ulcerative colitis diagnosed at age 17, limited to proctitis until a recent flare extended to the splenic flexure. Twelve days before presentation, he had begun mesalazine 1.6 g twice daily alongside a tapering course of prednisolone 40 mg daily. He continued to have abdominal pain, increased bowel frequency, and bloody diarrhea on arrival. 

On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, and had normal oxygen saturation. Cardiorespiratory and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Chest radiography showed clear lung fields. 

Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 19.46 × 10^9/L, D-dimer 1.81 mg/L, and troponin-T 209 ng/L (reference <14 ng/L); serum electrolytes were normal. A 12-lead ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm with concave ST-segment elevation in leads V4–V6 and biphasic T-waves. Twenty-four hours later, he reported persistent chest pain and a repeat ECG showed dynamic T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4–V6, with a peak troponin-T of 242 ng/L. 

He was initially treated as a suspected pulmonary embolus and transferred to the cardiology ward. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a non-dilated left ventricle with severe global systolic and diastolic dysfunction (mitral annular plane systolic excursion 4 mm, E/e′ 13) and normal right ventricular function; there was no pericardial effusion. He was started on ramipril and bisoprolol, clinically stabilized, and was discharged with plans for outpatient cardiac magnetic resonance imaging and heart-failure clinic follow-up.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pulmonary embolism was initially considered because of chest pain, dyspnea, and elevated D-dimer — “He was initially treated as a suspected pulmonary embolus and transferred to the cardiology ward.”

title: An Unusual Cause of Lymphadenopathy: Rosai Dorfman Disease in a 7-Year-Old Female Zambian Child: Case Report and Literature Review
case_prompt: A 7-year-old female presented with a 12-month history of progressive bilateral neck swelling, intermittent fever, night sweats, and weight loss. She had no known tuberculosis exposure or similar family history. On examination, she was pale and undernourished (BMI 10.24 kg/m2) with multiple rubbery, mobile, non-tender lymph nodes in the submandibular, submental, and posterior cervical regions (largest 4 × 3 cm), as well as inguinal nodes; no hepatosplenomegaly was noted. Vital signs were normal. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (14.57 × 10^9/L) with neutrophilia, microcytic hypochromic anemia (hemoglobin 7.7 g/dL; MCV 64.7 fL), and thrombocytosis (974 × 10^9/L); ESR was 36 mm/h, CRP 641.8 mg/L, and ferritin 256 ng/mL. Peripheral smear demonstrated hypochromia, anisocytosis, microcytosis, rouleaux formation, and no blasts. Gene-Xpert testing on lymph node aspirate and sputum was negative for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, as was urine LF-LAM. Chest radiography revealed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. CT imaging of the neck, chest, and abdomen showed generalized lymphadenopathy without extranodal masses. She was started empirically on anti-tuberculous therapy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Tuberculous lymphadenitis was considered because of the clinical picture and local TB epidemiology but was excluded by negative molecular and antigen tests — “Lymph node aspirate and sputum were subjected to gene-Xpert for Mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) and were negative, lateral flow urine lipoarabinomannan (LF-LAM) for MTB was also negative.”

title: Advanced primary nonurachal adenocarcinoma of urinary bladder responding to modified FOLFOX6 and capecitabine: a case report
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old man presented with 4 months of urinary hesitancy, 4 days of gross hematuria, and mild back pain. During a planned transurethral procedure for presumed benign prostatic hyperplasia, a papillary tumor was found over the right half of the trigone and left bladder neck base, with invasion into the prostate. The tumor was excised as completely as possible. 

Histologic examination of the bladder and prostate specimens revealed adenocarcinoma morphologically similar to colorectal carcinoma. Immunohistochemical studies showed that the tumor cells were positive for cytokeratin 20 (CK20) and CDX2, and negative for cytokeratin 7 (CK7) and prostate specific antigen (PSA). β-catenin staining was strongly positive in the membranous and cytoplasmic portions of the carcinoma cells. 

A colonoscopy failed to detect any malignant lesions, aside from a hamartomatous polyp in the sigmoid colon.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Secondary colorectal adenocarcinoma — “β-catenin usually shows nuclear reactivity in colon cancer but membranous and cytoplasmic staining in primary bladder adenocarcinoma” and colonoscopy did not reveal any colonic malignancy. 2. Urachal adenocarcinoma — “nonurachal adenocarcinoma ... tends to arise from trigone and posterior bladder wall, in contrast to the rather specific location of urachal carcinoma on the dome and anterior wall.” 3. Prostatic adenocarcinoma invading the bladder — excluded by “negative for ... prostate specific antigen (PSA).”

title: Focal necrosis of the falciform ligament as a rare cause of abdominal pain: A case report
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of progressive, continuous epigastric and right upper quadrant pain radiating to both flanks and aggravated by eating. She also had multiple episodes of nausea and nonbloody vomiting. Her medical history was notable only for hypertension; she denied tobacco or alcohol use and had a remote cesarean delivery.

On examination, she was afebrile with normal vital signs. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, a positive Murphy’s sign, and normal bowel sounds. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with T-wave inversions in leads III and V3. Serial troponin levels were 0.017 ng/mL at presentation, 0.031 ng/mL at 2 hours, and 0.056 ng/mL at 10 hours; coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries.

Laboratory studies showed normal serum amylase, lipase, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin levels. Aspartate aminotransferase was 61 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 81 U/L, and gamma-glutamyl transferase 94 IU/L. The white-cell count was 15.8×10^3/µL, and the C-reactive protein level was 232 mg/dL.

Abdominal ultrasonography revealed diffuse reactionary gallbladder wall thickening (up to 9 mm) without gallstones, a thickened falciform ligament, and a normal extrahepatic and intrahepatic biliary tree. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a heterogeneous, hypodense soft-tissue mass in the fissure of the falciform ligament containing fat-attenuation areas and surrounding fat-stranding, with a normal pancreas and mild gallbladder wall thickening but no gallstones.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cholelithiasis — “gallbladder ... with no gallstones.” 2. Acute pancreatitis — “amylase, lipase ... within normal ranges.” 3. Acute coronary syndrome — “troponin levels progressively elevated ... prompting referral to cardiac catheterization, which showed normal coronaries.” 4. Necrotizing pancreatitis — “CT scan ... revealed ... a normal pancreas.” 5. Epiploic appendagitis — “If these findings appeared close to the colon, they raise the suspicion of epiploic appendagitis.” 6. Omental infarction — “omental infarction ... appears on CT scan as a soft tissue mass anterior to the right colon.”

title: Eosinophilic pulmonary granulomatosis resembling a pulmonary carcinoma in a dog in Hong Kong
case_prompt:
A 7-year-old male Bichon Frise was evaluated for a 4-week history of progressive cough and recent anorexia. The cough began intermittently but increased in frequency over several days, and the dog’s appetite declined. There was no prior respiratory disease. Vaccinations were current; no parasite prophylaxis for 18 months. On exam, the dog was bright and alert, with normal heart and lung sounds, but had lost 20% of body weight since his last visit 18 months earlier. Mild otitis externa was noted; otherwise the physical examination was unremarkable.

Hematology on day 1 demonstrated marked eosinophilia (7.9 × 10^9/l; reference range 0.06–1.23) and moderate basophilia (0.27 × 10^9/l; reference range 0.00–0.1). Serum biochemistry was within reference limits. An antigen test for heartworm and fecal parasite analysis were negative.

Thoracic radiographs showed a large soft-tissue opacity in the left cranial lung lobe with a mild generalized bronchiolar pattern and no pleural effusion. After 10 days of antibiotics and bronchodilators, eosinophilia resolved but the mass on radiographs was unchanged. Pulmonary neoplasia was suspected, and the dog was referred for advanced imaging.

Contrast CT of the thorax revealed a lobulated mass in the left cranial lung lobe measuring 7.6 × 2.1 × 5.8 cm, compressing and distorting adjacent bronchi with heterogeneous enhancement and fluid pockets. Irregular nodular thickening infiltrated and nearly occluded airways in the right cranial and middle lobes. The sternal and cranial mediastinal lymph nodes were moderately enlarged. Abdominal CT was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pulmonary neoplasia was considered because of the large lobulated mass on CT and lack of response to empiric therapy — “Based on the poor treatment response and the imaging findings, pulmonary neoplasia was suspected.” 2. Eosinophilic pulmonary granulomatosis was raised due to nodular lung masses with eosinophilic inflammation — “EPG … presents with nodular pulmonary infiltration and/or masses containing eosinophils…” 3. Heartworm-associated EPG was excluded by negative antigen test and history — “An antigen heartworm test … was negative … and fecal analysis was negative for parasites too.” 4. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia was less likely given the short duration of signs — “relatively young age of the patient, the lack of chronicity of signs…” 5. Lymphomatoid granulomatosis was considered among granulomatous diseases but lacks predominance of eosinophils on histology — “radiological appearance of lesions … were similar to … lymphomatoid granulomatosis where coughing, presence of masses, lymphadenopathy, and eosinophilia and basophilia have been also described.” 6. Eosinophilic bronchopneumopathy was excluded because nodules or masses are not features of that subtype — “Bronchointerstitial pattern on radiography, cough, and eosinophilia … could have been a differential diagnosis, however, no nodules or masses are reported in this subtype of ELD…”

title: Aseptic Meningitis and White Matter Disease in Childhood-Onset Neuropsychiatric Lupus
case_prompt:
A 10‐year‐old girl with a history of SLE diagnosed at age 6 (initial features: polyarthralgia, facial rash, oral ulcers; ANA 1:320; anti-dsDNA 21.3 IU/mL; normal complement; SLEDAI 9) had been well on hydroxychloroquine. She presented with a 2-day history of fever up to 39.5°C, headache, and repeated vomiting. On examination, she was alert, without focal deficits or seizures; Brudzinski’s sign was positive. 

Noncontrast head CT showed an extensive hypodensity in the left temporal lobe without edema or hemorrhage. Lumbar puncture revealed an opening pressure of 29 cm H₂O, CSF WBC 485 cells/mm³ (91.7% polymorphs), protein 1526 mg/L, and glucose 3.6 mmol/L; gram stain and CSF cultures were negative. EEG demonstrated focal slowing with a sharp wave over the left posterior temporal region without epileptiform discharges. 

Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and antivirals were started, but fever and meningism persisted. Blood tests showed CRP 14.7 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, C3 0.27 g/L, C4 < 0.1 g/L, ANA 1:2560, and anti-dsDNA > 300 IU/mL. Anti-NMDA, anti-NMO, and anti-MOG antibodies were negative, as were lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies. Multiplex PCR and antigen tests for common meningitis pathogens, plus specific viral, mycobacterial, and fungal studies, were all negative.

Brain MRI revealed T2-weighted hyperintensity in the left temporal subcortical and deep white matter with mild cortical swelling; susceptibility-weighted imaging showed petechial hemorrhages. MRA and MRV were normal, and MR spectroscopy showed no lactate peak.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection (bacterial or viral meningoencephalitis) — “Extensive microbiological workups for infective meningoencephalitis with multiplex polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for meningitis panels and latex agglutination were negative for common meningitis pathogens. Specific investigations … were negative. CSF culture was negative too.” 2. Multiple sclerosis or acute demyelinating encephalomyelitis — “The diagnosis of MS could not be substantiated at the time of presentation as it has to demonstrate dissemination in time and place.” 3. Ischemic neuropsychiatric lupus (vasculopathy or thrombosis) — “Magnetic resonance arteriogram (MRA) and magnetic resonance venogram (MRV) … did not reveal any aneurysm or stenosis … while the major dural venous sinuses were patent … making ischemic NPSLE less likely than inflammatory NPSLE.”

title: Reel syndrome, a diagnostic conundrum: a case report
case_prompt:
A 77-year-old overweight woman presented with a 4-hour history of abdominal pain and rhythmic abdominal wall and bilateral lower limb jerking movements after she forcefully pulled a blanket toward her with her left arm while supine. Her history was notable for mild cognitive impairment, a hiatus hernia, and Factor V Leiden deficiency. Three months earlier, she had undergone implantation of a dual-lead permanent pacemaker for recurrent syncope due to sinus node dysfunction.

On arrival, she denied chest pain, presyncope, or heart-failure symptoms. Examination revealed a pulsating contraction of the abdominal wall; the lower-limb jerking had ceased. Laboratory studies were unremarkable. A computed tomography aortogram excluded the suspected diagnosis of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. A chest radiograph demonstrated displacement of both pacemaker leads, with the atrial lead migrated to the superior vena cava and the ventricular lead within the right atrium. Electrocardiography showed intermittent atrial pacing, under-sensing of the ventricular lead, ventricular pacing spikes occurring after the QRS complexes, and absence of ventricular capture. The rhythmic 60-beats-per-minute abdominal contractions resolved after deactivation of the pacemaker.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm was initially suspected but then excluded — “computed tomography aortogram excluded the suspected initial diagnosis of probably ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm.”

title: “Acute onset tetraplegia associated with immune‐mediated thrombocytopenia and suspected secondary intraspinal hemorrhage in a dog”
case_prompt:
A 10-year-old female spayed Havanese (4.8 kg) was referred for acute onset of nonambulatory tetraplegia. One month earlier, the dog had left forelimb lameness that resolved with NSAIDs; two months earlier, hair thinning and pigment changes had resolved. The night before referral, the dog vocalized and was found nonambulatory. At the primary vet: heart rate 136 /min, grade II–III/VI left apical systolic murmur, pink mucous membranes, normal capillary refill. The dog was laterally recumbent with absent motor function and severely decreased pain sensation in all four limbs. A clinic hematology panel showed platelet count 0 × 10^9 cells/L (reference 1.86–5.45 × 10^9), and chemistry was unremarkable. 

On presentation (day 1) at the referral hospital, vital signs were normal. Neurologic assessment confirmed nonambulatory tetraplegia with severely decreased pain sensation, absent conscious proprioception, and no cervical or spinal pain; cranial nerves were normal and spinal reflexes were intact in all limbs, localizing to C1–C6. Petechiae and ecchymoses were noted on the ventral abdomen and inguinal area. Minimum database: PCV 49% (38.3–56.5%), TPP 5.2 g/dL (5.0–7.2 g/dL), lactate 1.46 mmol/L (0–2.0), glucose 101 mg/dL (68–104), systolic blood pressure 140 mmHg. Blood smear confirmed severe thrombocytopenia (<10 000/µL). Thoracic radiographs were unremarkable. Echocardiogram revealed myxomatous valve degeneration with trace regurgitations. Abdominal ultrasound showed a mildly hyperechoic liver. A rapid point-of-care tick-borne disease assay was negative; a comprehensive vector panel and external CBC were pending.

On day 2, MRI of the cervical spine (with and without contrast) under general anesthesia demonstrated a crescentic accumulation of material in the left dorsolateral spinal canal along C3, causing mild-to-moderate cord compression. The lesion was T1 hyperintense, T2 hyperintense, non-suppressing on FLAIR/STIR, without susceptibility on T2*, and showed mild peripheral contrast enhancement. Intramedullary hyperintensity extended from mid-C2 to cranial C5. Findings were most suggestive of intraspinal hemorrhage.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Intervertebral disk disease, trauma, fibrocartilaginous embolism, and spondylomyelopathy were considered as common causes of canine tetraplegia but were excluded by imaging demonstrating hemorrhage rather than these lesions — “Findings were most suggestive of intraspinal hemorrhage.” 2. Lower motor neuron diseases (tick paralysis, botulism, and polyradiculoneuritis) were considered but were unlikely given a central lesion with preservation of spinal reflexes — “Spinal reflexes were normal in all four limbs.” 3. Neoplasia was considered as a cause of cord compression and secondary thrombocytopenia but was not supported by unremarkable thoracic radiographs and abdominal ultrasound — “Thoracic radiographs, which were unremarkable, and an abdominal ultrasound revealed a slightly hyperechoic liver, suggestive of a mild chronic hepatopathy.” 4. Primary immune-mediated thrombocytopenia was considered the most likely etiology after exclusion of secondary causes, consistent with severe isolated thrombocytopenia and hemorrhagic signs in the absence of other abnormalities — “A diagnosis of primary ITP is typically made when no underlying cause for the immune‐mediated destruction is found.”

title: Entamoeba histolytica‐associated proctitis and ileitis mimicking Crohn's disease—A case report
case_prompt:
A 53‐year‐old man who has sex with men presented in August 2018 with rectal tenesmus, small‐volume frequent bowel movements, hematochezia, and mucous discharge. He denied dysuria, pollakiuria, or urethral discharge. He reported recent homosexual contact with a partner of Chinese descent. He had no relevant travel history.  
On examination, external ano‐genital inspection was normal. Vital signs were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed negative HIV, viral hepatitis, and Treponema pallidum serologies. Urethral and anal swabs were negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Trichomonas vaginalis. Serology for Chlamydia trachomatis was positive (IgG >200.0 AU/ml, IgA =58.8 AU/ml).  
Stool culture was positive for Brachypsira aalborgii. A 7‐day course of metronidazole led to transient symptom relief for three weeks.  
Proctoscopy revealed ulcerative proctitis with vulnerable mucosa. Confocal endomicroscopy showed mucosal edema, capillary leakage, and focal enterocyte destruction. Histopathology demonstrated mild active inflammation with polarized crypts and increased eosinophils; no malignancy. PCR of rectal biopsy was positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae, cytomegalovirus, and herpes simplex virus I/II.  
A 7‐day course of doxycycline (100 mg bid) resulted in improvement but not full resolution. After symptom recurrence, an additional 21‐day doxycycline course, single‐dose ceftriaxone, and two‐week metronidazole courses provided no sustained benefit. Follow‐up STI testing remained negative.  
By June 2020, with persistent ulcerative proctitis refractory to antibiotics, mesalazine and budesonide enemas were initiated and the patient was referred for further evaluation of suspected inflammatory bowel disease. Colonoscopy showed ulcerations in the rectum, cecum, ileocecal valve, and terminal ileum with a discontinuous distribution. Histopathology was nonspecific but compatible with IBD, leading to a presumptive diagnosis of Crohn’s disease.  
Hydro‐magnetic resonance tomography demonstrated contrast enhancement in the rectum and cecal region without fistula, stenosis, or additional small‐intestinal involvement; there was also increased prostate enhancement.  
Screening for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and Clostridioides difficile was negative. The patient received systemic glucocorticoids, then anti‐TNF‐α therapy (adalimumab), and subsequently anti‐IL‐12/23 therapy (ustekinumab) over 11 months, but clinical symptoms and elevated fecal calprotectin persisted.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Exclusion of Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Trichomonas vaginalis — “Urethral and anal swaps did not identify N. gonorrhoeae and Trichomonas vaginalis.” 2. Exclusion of cytomegalovirus and herpes simplex — “PCR for … cytomegalovirus, and herpes simplex I/II remained negative.” 3. Presumptive Crohn’s disease (IBD) — “Histopathologic findings were unspecific, but principally in line with an IBD. Crohn's disease was suspected due to the typical discontinuous inflammatory distribution pattern … hydro magnet resonance tomography confirmed contrast enhancement in the rectum and caecal region.” 4. Exclusion of tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and C. difficile colitis — “Following a negative screening for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, hepatitis B and Clostridium difficile.”

title: Heparin-induced thrombocytopaenia presenting as acute aortic mural thrombosis
case_prompt: A 77-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on letrozole and high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma treated six cycles of carboplatin presented with worsening exertional dyspnea. On examination she was hypoxic (oxygen saturation 84% on 2 L/min), and the remainder of her vital signs and chest examination were unremarkable. One month earlier, a routine CT staging examination had shown stable malignancy but new bilateral lower-lobe pulmonary emboli and left common femoral vein thrombosis; she was managed with once-daily therapeutic-dose dalteparin. On admission, a repeat CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis showed progression of pulmonary emboli with new thrombi in both main pulmonary arteries and multiple acute nonocclusive mural thrombi in the thoracic and upper abdominal aorta, despite therapeutic anticoagulation. Laboratory tests revealed a platelet count of 29×10^9/L (reference 160–370×10^9/L), having been 468×10^9/L three weeks earlier; the remainder of her blood tests were within reference ranges. A peripheral blood film showed platelet clumping and fibrin stranding, and repeat measurements confirmed genuine thrombocytopaenia.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sepsis — “The broad differential diagnoses of her thrombocytopaenia included sepsis, chemotherapy-induced bone marrow suppression, metastatic infiltration of the bone marrow, drug-induced or immune thrombocytopaenic purpura, haematinic-deficiency and HIT.” 2. Metastatic infiltration of the bone marrow — “The broad differential diagnoses of her thrombocytopaenia included … metastatic infiltration of the bone marrow …” 3. Drug-induced or immune thrombocytopaenic purpura — “The broad differential diagnoses of her thrombocytopaenia included … drug-induced or immune thrombocytopaenic purpura …” 4. Haematinic-deficiency — “The broad differential diagnoses of her thrombocytopaenia included … haematinic-deficiency …” 5. Prothrombotic malignancy-related cause — “A prothrombotic malignancy-related cause for the patient’s progressive arterial and venous thrombosis had been considered, but the worsening venous and new arterial thrombi following the administration of low molecular weight heparin treatment is considered a very rare entity in the context of malignancy.” 6. Heparin-induced thrombocytopaenia — “This new thrombocytopaenia, in the context of active LMWH therapy and progressive pulmonary and aortic thrombosis, triggered a suspicion of HIT.”

title: Streptococcus salivarius pneumonia-associated pneumomediastinum: a case report and literature review
case_prompt: A 36-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of persistent cough and sputum production that worsened with the onset of a sore throat 1 day before admission. He works in a barbecue restaurant and has smoked approximately 20 cigarettes per day for 10 years. Initial treatment with an herbal cough remedy failed to relieve symptoms. On examination, vital signs were stable. Subcutaneous emphysema was palpable in the neck, and wet rales were auscultated over the left lung. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 25.74×10^9/L (neutrophils 21.04×10^9/L, 81.8%) and a C-reactive protein level of 1.63 mg/L. Chest radiography demonstrated obstructive atelectasis in the left lower lobe. Computed tomography of the chest confirmed pneumomediastinum and left lower lobe atelectasis. Flexible bronchoscopy revealed congested mucosa in the left main bronchus and in the basal and dorsal segments of the left lower lobe, along with copious white, viscous secretions, but no airway perforation, neoplasm, or active bleeding.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral upper respiratory infection — “Due to the patient’s markedly elevated leukocyte count and the presence of pulmonary atelectasis, the clinical presentation was inconsistent with a viral infection. Consequently, we excluded the possibility of rhinovirus infection.”

title: EIF2B2 gene mutation causing early onset vanishing white matter disease: a case report
case_prompt:
A 4-month-old boy, born at 36 weeks’ gestation to third-degree consanguineous parents, was referred for clusters of brief events involving right-sided clonic jerks, chewing movements, and blinking that began at age 3 months after a febrile vaccination reaction. These episodes occurred up to 10 times daily, lasted seconds, and resolved spontaneously or with IV benzodiazepines. Parents also noted decreased feeding, loss of head control, and diminished reactivity.

Birth history was notable only for transient neonatal hypoglycemia; Apgar scores were 10 and 10. Growth parameters at presentation were below the 3rd percentile for weight (7.16 kg) and between the 10th and 25th percentiles for length and head circumference. Family history was unremarkable.

On neurologic examination, there was marked axial hypotonia, poor eye contact, weak sucking, and decreased social interaction. Vital signs, routine labs (CBC, liver and renal function), serum ammonia, and lactate were normal. An initial EEG showed recurrent slow waves in the right temporal region and asynchronous left temporo-occipital slow waves, occasionally followed by suppression of background activity; IV pyridoxine produced no change, and levetiracetam led to only partial seizure control.

Brain MRI at 3 months of age demonstrated a thinned corpus callosum, diffuse white matter hyperintensity on T2 and hypointensity on T1, and absence of myelination in the posterior limbs of both internal capsules. Based on imaging, a hypomyelinating leukoencephalopathy of genetic or metabolic origin was suspected. Complete metabolic screening—including plasma amino acids, urinary organic acids, tests for oligosaccharidosis, mucopolysaccharidosis, neurotransmitter defects, pyridoxine and pyridoxal-phosphate deficiency, primary hyperlactatemia, brain glucose transport deficiency, and peroxisomal disorders—was normal. Comparative genomic hybridization array revealed no duplications or deletions.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Demyelinating encephalomyelitis — “in patients presenting with rapid neurological deterioration it is important to exclude demyelinating encephalomyelitis and encephalitis.” 2. Encephalitis — “in patients presenting with rapid neurological deterioration it is important to exclude demyelinating encephalomyelitis and encephalitis.” 3. Mitochondrial leukoencephalopathies — “In patients with subacute or chronic neurological deterioration and an MRI showing diffuse cerebral white matter abnormalities, mitochondrial leukoencephalopathies are important disorders in the differential diagnosis.” 4. Non-ketotic hyperglycinemia — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as…non-ketotic hyperglycinemia…” 5. Classical phenylketonuria — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as…classical phenylketonuria…” 6. Mucopolysaccharidoses — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as mucopolysaccharidoses…” 7. GM gangliosidoses — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as GM gangliosidoses…” 8. Zellweger disease — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as…Zellweger disease…” 9. Adrenomyeloneuropathy — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as adrenomyeloneuropathy…” 10. Van der Knaap disease — “White matter abnormalities similar to VMD are described in metabolic disease such as Van der Knaap disease.” 11. Oligosaccharidosis — “metabolic investigations were also negative for oligosaccharidosis…” 12. Neurotransmitter deficiency — “metabolic investigations were also negative for…neurotransmitter deficiency…” 13. Pyridoxine and pyridoxal-phosphate deficiency — “metabolic investigations were also negative for pyridoxine and pyridoxal-phosphate deficiency…” 14. Primary hyperlactatemia — “metabolic investigations were also negative for…primary hyperlactatemia…” 15. Brain glucose transport deficiency — “metabolic investigations were also negative for…brain glucose transport deficiency…” 16. Peroxisomal deficiency — “metabolic investigations were also negative for…peroxisomal deficiency.” 17. Chromosomal duplications or deletions — “CGH array did not document anomalies such as duplications or deletions.”

title: Nodular Scleritis Associated with Herpes Zoster Virus: An Infectious and Immune-Mediated Process
case_prompt:
A 54-year-old Caucasian woman reported 4 days of right ocular pain and progressive blurred vision. On initial evaluation, best corrected visual acuity was 20/32 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left eye. Intraocular pressure measured 32 mmHg in the right eye and 16 mmHg in the left eye. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye showed marked ciliary injection, mild corneal edema with diffuse keratic precipitates, and an anterior chamber reaction (cell grade 2+, flare grade 0). Funduscopic examination was unremarkable. The left eye was normal. She was diagnosed with hypertensive anterior uveitis presumed herpetic and started on oral valacyclovir 1 g three times daily, topical dexamethasone every 2 hours, topical cyclopentolate, and brinzolamide twice daily, with progressive improvement of the uveitis.

Two months later, while on valacyclovir 500 mg daily, she developed a tender, reddish scleral nodule at the 11 o’clock position of the right eye. Biomicroscopy showed a clear cornea and anterior chamber, with normal fundus findings. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory therapy (ibuprofen 600 mg twice daily) produced no change. Laboratory studies revealed a normal blood cell count, normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal angiotensin-converting enzyme levels. Immunological tests were negative for rheumatoid factor, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and antinuclear antibodies, but positive for HLA-B27. An infectious workup—including tests for syphilis, tuberculosis, herpes simplex, and Borrelia—was negative. Therapy was switched to oral prednisolone at 1 mg/kg/day, which led to some improvement of the scleritis but plateaued over several weeks without further resolution.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Systemic autoimmune vasculitis or rheumatologic scleritis — “immunological study was negative for rheumatoid factor, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), and antinuclear antibodies (ANA)…” 3. Bacterial or spirochetal infectious scleritis (syphilis, tuberculosis, Borrelia) and HSV — “The infectious study, including syphilis, tuberculosis, herpes simplex, and Borrelia was negative.” 4. Trauma-associated infectious scleritis — “The infectious scleritis usually follows accidental or surgical trauma… In our case, the patient presented hypertensive anterior uveitis… preceding the scleritis.” 5. Varicella zoster virus scleritis — “This uveitis was probably associated with herpetic viral disease, supported by the response to valacyclovir…”

title: Central-variant posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome in an infant with mid-aortic syndrome: A rare case of symmetric basal ganglia lesions
case_prompt: A 4-month-old previously healthy girl was brought after a cardiac arrest of unknown cause following routine immunization. She was supported on venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation for three days. After decannulation, a head CT demonstrated diffuse cerebral edema and watershed infarctions; there were no basal ganglia abnormalities. She was diagnosed with hypoxic–ischemic encephalopathy. Further evaluation revealed left ventricular myocardial hypertrophy and end-stage renal failure requiring continuous hemodialysis. Approximately 10 days after resuscitation, marked systolic hypertension (100–130 mm Hg, above the 99th percentile for age and weight) developed and persisted despite maximal doses of calcium-channel blockers and α2-agonists. On day 16 after the arrest, brain MRI showed mild cerebral atrophy and T1 hyperintensities in the bilateral putamen and occipital cortices; T2, diffusion-weighted, and ADC sequences were normal. One month after the onset of hypertension, blood pressure peaked at 200 mm Hg. After five days of sustained severe hypertension, a noncontrast head CT demonstrated new, symmetric, swollen areas of hypodensity in the putamen and globus pallidus.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Neoplastic, infectious, toxic, and other inflammatory lesions were part of the broad differential but were not supported by imaging or clinical findings — “Differential diagnoses of infantile symmetric bilateral basal ganglia lesions are broad and include genetic, acquired metabolic or toxic, infectious, inflammatory, vascular, and neoplastic pathologies.”

title: Mycosis fungoides presenting as symmetric concentric patches mimicking figurate erythema
case_prompt: A 52-year-old white man presented with a 4-year history of recurrent, intermittent rash of symmetrically distributed, red annular patches on non–sun-exposed areas of the trunk and extremities. The lesions gradually expanded in concentric rings with scale over several months, were mildly pruritic, and associated with arthralgias during flares. His only medication was a proton pump inhibitor. Lyme disease testing was negative. Initial treatment with topical and systemic corticosteroids resulted in only temporary improvement. On examination, pink-red, symmetric, concentric annular patches with trailing scale involved 10% to 15% of his body surface area without lymphadenopathy. Laboratory investigations were within normal limits. A punch biopsy and a broad shave biopsy from the leading edge of a lesion showed a superficial perivascular and lichenoid lymphocytic infiltrate with exocytosis of small lymphocytes with mild nuclear contour irregularities, minimal spongiosis, vacuolar alteration at the dermoepidermal junction, and papillary dermal fibrosis. Immunohistochemical staining revealed a predominantly CD3⁺ infiltrate with an approximately equal ratio of CD4⁺ to CD8⁺ cells. Periodic acid–Schiff diastase staining was negative for fungi. No perivascular cuffing, interstitial lymphocytic infiltrate, or significant red blood cell extravasation was seen. Loss of CD7 on lymphocytes was estimated to be approximately 40%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Erythema gyratum repens was considered clinically because of the multiple concentric, almost targetoid rings — “presenting similarly to that of gyratum repens.” 2. Tinea corporis was excluded by negative fungal staining — “Periodic acid–Schiff diastase staining was negative for fungi.” 3. Mycosis fungoides was suspected based on lichenoid and epidermotropic lymphocytic infiltrates — “we observed some of these features, including epidermotropism, in our patient that raised the suspicion for MF despite clinical features that were unusual for this diagnosis.”

title: Cancer of the prostate presenting with diffuse osteolytic metastatic bone lesions: a case report
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old Namibian man was referred for evaluation of anemia, thrombocytopenia, and back pain. Seven months earlier, he developed insidious low back pain that gradually worsened, radiated to the lower limbs, and was aggravated by walking and relieved by rest. Four months before admission, he noted exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and palpitations. He also reported weight loss, fevers, malaise, and urinary symptoms including straining, frequency, and dysuria. He had no history of trauma, cough, gastrointestinal symptoms, smoking, or alcohol use.

On examination, he appeared pale and mildly wasted. The right shoulder joint was swollen, tender, and had a limited range of motion. There was percussion tenderness over the thoracic and lumbar spine without deformity. Digital rectal examination revealed an asymmetrically enlarged, nodular, and hard prostate with diffuse firm areas. Other findings were unremarkable.

Laboratory tests showed a normocytic, normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 5.4 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (42×10^9/L), a reticulocyte count of 101 500 cells/µL (index 1.1), hypercalcemia (2.9 mmol/L), elevated alkaline phosphatase (509 IU/L), and lactate dehydrogenase (547 IU/L). Renal and liver function tests were normal. Serum protein electrophoresis revealed no M-protein, and urine was negative for Bence Jones proteins. Urinalysis showed mild proteinuria and microscopic hematuria; urine culture grew Escherichia coli sensitive to cefuroxime. The prostate-specific antigen level was >100 ng/mL.

Radiographs of the right humerus demonstrated osteolytic lesions. Computed tomography of the spine, pelvis, and femur showed multiple osteolytic lesions. A lateral skull radiograph was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. The absence of skull or jaw lesions argued against multiple myeloma—“he had no lytic lesions in the skull bones or mandible.” 2. Negative serum protein electrophoresis and absence of Bence Jones proteins further excluded multiple myeloma—“serum electrophoresis was negative for M-protein and there were no Bence Jones proteins in the urine.”

title: Bilateral Lipid Keratopathy in the Setting of Brimonidine Tartrate Use
case_prompt:
An 86-year-old woman was referred for several months of worsening vision. She had cataract surgery two years earlier and a 25-year history of open-angle glaucoma treated for seven years with brimonidine tartrate 0.1% twice daily in both eyes and artificial tears. She denied contact lens use or lid disorders and had no history of corneal disease. She reported gradual vision decline since her cataract surgery, most noticeable over the past three months, and experienced photosensitivity in both eyes. She had no history of hyperlipidemia or systemic disease.

On examination, best-corrected distance visual acuity was 20/60 in the right eye and 20/70 in the left. Manifest refraction was +0.75–1.00 × 160° OD and +0.50–1.25 × 165° OS; intraocular pressures were within normal limits. Slit-lamp examination revealed dermatochalasis, superficial punctate keratitis, and bilateral peripheral corneal opacities from the 4- to 5-o’clock and 7- to 8-o’clock positions with overlying neovascularization. Crocodile shagreen was noted bilaterally, with no guttata or lattice lines. Dilated fundus examination showed a blunted macular reflex in both eyes and cup-to-disc ratios of 0.65 OD and 0.75 OS.

An infectious and autoimmune workup was performed, including CBC with differential, ESR, CRP, ANA, c-ANCA, p-ANCA, rheumatoid factor, anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La, PPD, interferon-gamma release assay, chest radiography, ACE and lysozyme levels, MRSA swabs, stool ova and cysts, Lyme titers, syphilis testing, and review by the primary care physician for systemic rheumatologic disease. All test results were negative or within normal limits, and there were no systemic symptoms to suggest autoimmune disease.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious keratitis — “All tests resulted as negative or within normal limits.” 2. Autoimmune-associated keratitis (e.g., RA, SLE, Sjögren) — “The patient's PCP reported no findings in the review of systems or on physical exam consistent with rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren syndrome, or other similar systemic diseases.” 3. Corneal arcus — “Corneal arcus, unlike LK, does not typically affect visual acuity as the central cornea is spared.” 4. Schnyder corneal dystrophy — “Bilateral corneal opacification may also be seen with Schnyder corneal dystrophy (SCD), a rare inherited disorder of the stroma.” 5. Corneal xanthoma and systemic lipid disorders (e.g., Tangier disease, familial HDL disorders) — “Although these are less common and lack neovascularization.” 6. Drug-induced lipid keratopathy from brimonidine use — “We have concluded that the BT is the most likely cause of this patient's lipid keratopathy.”

title: Metastatic right atrial mass in the presence of atrial septal defect: A rare clinical coincidence
case_prompt:
A 37-year-old woman presented 2 weeks after cesarean section with a 2-week history of cough, dyspnea, and head and neck swelling. Symptoms had begun in pregnancy and worsened over the last few days. The cough was nonproductive, with no hemoptysis. She had no history of cardiac or pulmonary disease or risk factors such as alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs.

On examination, heart rate was 110/min, blood pressure 110/75 mmHg, and she was afebrile. There was slight respiratory discomfort but no cyanosis. Lung fields were clear; heart sounds revealed wide fixed splitting of S2 without murmur. There was no clinical evidence of right heart failure.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a severely dilated right atrium and right ventricle, a nonmobile 6.5 cm² mass attached to the right atrial wall, and a secundum-type atrial septal defect measuring 2.44 cm with a left-to-right shunt (QP/QS 2.9). A large extracardiac mass (8.7 × 4.3 cm) anterior to the ascending aorta was also noted.

Chest CT and pulmonary CT angiography demonstrated a lobulated anterior mediastinal soft-tissue mass (105 × 46 mm), suggestive of thymoma, teratoma, thyroid goiter, lymphoma, or germ cell tumor, with extension of thrombosis into the brachiocephalic veins, superior vena cava (SVC), and right atrium, as well as multiple pulmonary nodules. Abdominal and pelvic CT were unremarkable. Serum alpha-fetoprotein was 5000 ng/mL; blood cultures were negative.

At this point, by considering the findings of physical and laboratory examinations, echocardiography, thoracic CT scan, and pulmonary CT angiography, a mediastinal malignancy with cardiac and lung metastasis was at the top of our differential diagnoses. Atrial thrombosis was another potential diagnosis due to the hypercoagulable state because of the post‐partum period and the mediastinal probable malignancy. On the other hand, the absence of fever, negative blood culture, and no history of IV drug abuse made endocarditis vegetation a less likely diagnosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Mediastinal malignancy with cardiac and lung metastasis was considered most likely — “a mediastinal malignancy with cardiac and lung metastasis was at the top of our differential diagnoses.” 2. Atrial thrombosis was considered due to the postpartum hypercoagulable state and the probable mediastinal malignancy — “Atrial thrombosis was another potential diagnosis due to the hypercoagulable state because of the post‐partum period and the mediastinal probable malignancy.” 3. Endocarditis vegetation was considered but deemed unlikely in the absence of fever, with negative blood cultures and no history of IV drug use — “the absence of fever, negative blood culture, and no history of IV drug abuse made endocarditis vegetation a less likely diagnosis.”

title: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome in a patient taking phenytoin and levetiracetam: a case report
case_prompt:
An 18-year-old African-American man presented with a 5-day history of pruritic, maculopapular rash, periorbital swelling, fever, and transaminitis. Five days before presentation, he noted pruritus and rash over his extremities that progressed over several days to involve his chest, back, and face. Thirty-five days earlier, he had begun treatment for new-onset seizures with phenytoin extended-release, 100 mg daily, and levetiracetam, 500 mg twice daily. He had no other significant medical history, drug allergies, or alcohol use. Review of systems was positive for non-productive cough, fever, and tea-colored urine, and negative for chest pain, abdominal pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, chills, or altered mental status.

On examination, his temperature was 40.2 °C, heart rate 88 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and blood pressure 110/55 mmHg. He was well developed and nourished, alert and oriented, and appeared uncomfortable but was not in distress. A fine exanthematous rash was present on the face and sun-exposed upper and lower extremities without involvement of the oral mucosa, palms, or soles. There was profound periorbital edema preventing eye opening. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly. Neurological examination showed no focal deficits.

Laboratory studies on admission:
• WBC 7.9 × 10^3/mm^3 (60% neutrophils, 8% lymphocytes, 4% eosinophils; absolute eosinophils 0.32 × 10^3/mm^3)  
• Free phenytoin level 0.4 µg/mL (therapeutic 1.0–2.0 µg/mL)  
• Basic metabolic panel within normal limits  
• AST 778 U/L, ALT 1274 U/L  
• Acetaminophen and salicylate levels undetectable  
• Serologies for hepatitis A, B, and C negative  
• Electrocardiogram and echocardiogram normal  
• EBV, CMV, and HIV testing negative  
• Antinuclear antibody test negative
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Toxic epidermal necrolysis/erythema multiforme were considered but the rash spared mucosal surfaces and palms/soles — “without involvement of the oral mucosa, palms, or soles.” 2. Viral exanthems (EBV, CMV, HIV) were considered but viral testing was negative — “EBV, CMV, and HIV testing were all negative.” 3. Autoimmune disease such as systemic lupus erythematosus was considered but ANA was negative — “as were the results of tests for antinuclear antibodies (ANA).” 4. Viral hepatitis was considered as the cause of transaminitis but hepatitis A, B, and C serologies were negative — “evaluation for acute and chronic hepatitis with serologies was negative for hepatitis A, B, and C.”
final_diagnosis: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms

title: Knobloch syndrome associated with Polymicrogyria and early onset of retinal detachment: two case reports
case_prompt:
A 7-month-old Hispanic male was referred for nystagmus. He was born at term with normal growth but exhibited motor and social developmental delay. Family history was notable for an older sister with visual abnormalities; his parents and two older brothers had no visual problems. 

On examination, he fixed and followed with the left eye but not the right. Cycloplegic refraction was −2.50 + 3.50 × 090° OD and −7.00 + 3.50 × 090° OS. Anterior segments were unremarkable bilaterally. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed a tilted optic nerve with trace pallor, a large posterior serous retinal detachment involving the macula with surrounding demarcation lines and a subretinal fibrotic band, and otherwise thin, atrophic retina without identifiable tears or holes. The left eye showed a tilted optic nerve, tessellated retina, RPE mottling, and central macular atrophy without detachment.

B-scan ultrasound of the right eye confirmed posterior subretinal fluid. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye demonstrated early and late hyperfluorescence consistent with pooling; the left eye showed RPE window defects and staining of drusen-like deposits. A full-field electroretinogram showed moderately to severely depressed responses from both the cone and rod systems. Optical coherence tomography of the right eye showed an elevated retina with subretinal fluid; the left eye showed RPE changes and thinning.

A hereditary retinal dystrophy panel covering ~180 genes revealed a mutation in CNGB3 associated with achromatopsia. This result, however, was inconsistent with the clinical presentation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cone-rod dystrophy was considered given combined cone and rod dysfunction on ERG — “full field electroretinogram... showed moderately to severely depressed responses from both the cone and rod systems.” 2. Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment was unlikely due to absence of tears or holes — “No retinal tears or holes were identified.” 3. Other hereditary retinal dystrophies such as Leber congenital amaurosis were part of the differential — “the differential diagnosis of KS includes but is not limited to... Leber congenital amaurosis...” 4. Syndromic causes including microcephaly-lymphedema-chorioretinal dysplasia syndrome were contemplated — “the differential diagnosis of KS includes but is not limited to... microcephaly lymphedema chorioretinal dysplasia syndrome...” 5. Stickler syndrome was also in the differential given high myopia and RD risk — “the differential diagnosis of KS includes but is not limited to... Stickler syndrome.”

title: A case report on transient cardiomyopathy with cytokine storm in SARS-CoV-2
case_prompt:
A 49-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 11.1% one month earlier), untreated hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, morbid obesity (BMI 42 kg/m^2), and obstructive sleep apnoea presented with 4 days of diarrhoea, nausea, vomiting, low-grade fever, and chills. He denied respiratory or chest symptoms. On first presentation, he was tachycardic (102 b.p.m.) but otherwise afebrile and normotensive; chest radiograph showed mild pulmonary congestion. He was tested for SARS-CoV-2 and discharged after intravenous fluids. 

One day later, he returned with fever (39.5°C), diarrhoea, dyspnoea, and severe cough. SARS-CoV-2 testing was positive. On examination, he was tachypnoeic (26 breaths/min), tachycardic (108 b.p.m.), with coarse bilateral breath sounds; cardiac exam was normal. Repeat chest radiograph showed patchy alveolar opacities superimposed on diffuse interstitial changes.

Laboratory results:
- WBC 5.8×10^3/µL, lymphocytes 0.6×10^3/µL  
- CRP 9.79 mg/dL, LDH 296 U/L, ferritin 482 ng/dL, IL-6 83.75 pg/mL  
- D-dimer <150 µg/L  
- Troponin I 0.018 ng/mL, BNP 25 pg/mL  

Arterial blood gas on nasal cannula showed pH 7.38, PaO₂ 66.2 mmHg, PaCO₂ 38.5 mmHg. Electrocardiogram on admission was normal sinus rhythm at 78 b.p.m.

He was admitted to the ICU for respiratory failure and treated with hydroxychloroquine. Despite therapy, inflammatory markers and oxygen requirements worsened, and he was intubated on hospital Day 6. Cardiac monitoring demonstrated episodes of premature ventricular complexes with ventricular bigeminy. 

On Day 7, transthoracic echocardiography revealed global left ventricular hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of 37% (previously 60–65% two months earlier) and markedly impaired global longitudinal strain averaging –1%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral myocarditis considered but less likely — “In the setting of normal troponin I and BNP through the course, viral myocarditis was also a less likely aetiology.” 2. Stress cardiomyopathy favored — “Although this patient’s BNP and troponin I did not increase, his transient cardiomyopathy was likely a case of global stress cardiomyopathy in the setting of cytokine storm.”

title: Acute presentation of a giant intrathyroidal parathyroid adenoma: a case report
case_prompt:
A 21-year-old Irish woman presented with a 3-week history of an enlarging, firm, painless right-sided neck mass. She denied dysphagia, stridor, fevers, weight loss, and hypercalcemic symptoms except mild constipation and two presyncopal episodes with exercise. Her medical history was unremarkable. Family history included sudden cardiac death in a maternal grandmother, two maternal aunts with hyperthyroidism and goitres, and early-onset hypertension in her mother.

On examination, vital signs showed a heart rate of 112 bpm and blood pressure of 116/80 mm Hg. There was a firm, non-tender swelling in the right thyroid lobe without lymphadenopathy. Thyroid and renal function tests were normal: serum creatinine 64 μmol/L and eGFR 124 mL/min/1.73 m2. Corrected serum calcium was elevated at 3.96 mmol/L (2.2–2.6), phosphate 0.35 mmol/L (0.8–1.5), and albumin 49 g/L. She received IV fluids and bisphosphonates; calcium fell to 2.46 mmol/L in 3 days. 

Neck ultrasound revealed a 5×4 cm solid and cystic nodule in the lower pole of the right thyroid lobe. Two days later, intact PTH was 138.4 pmol/L (1.6–6.9). Six days after presentation, calcium had rebounded to 2.73 mmol/L, vitamin D was low at 35 nmol/L, and alkaline phosphatase rose from 143 to 240 IU/L. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. 

Differential considerations included parathyroid adenoma, parathyroid hyperplasia, parathyroid carcinoma, or multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1. Tc-99m sestamibi single-photon emission CT/CT demonstrated a 7–10 cm tracer-avid lesion posterior to the right thyroid lobe. Twenty-four–hour urinary calcium was elevated at 9 mmol (2.5–7.5), and prolactin, urinary catecholamines, and metanephrines were normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Parathyroid hyperplasia considered due to PHPT presentation but less likely with a single gland lesion — “The differential diagnosis included parathyroid adenoma, parathyroid hyperplasia, and parathyroid carcinoma.”

title: Cutaneous Ulcers as Initial Presentation of Localized Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 57-year-old woman presented with recurrent fevers and painful leg ulcers for two months.
Eight months earlier she developed vertigo and a fullness sensation in her left ear; audiometry confirmed left sensorineural hearing loss.
Two weeks later she had fever with chills; chest radiograph showed a right‐lung infiltrate and ESR was elevated; her symptoms improved with oral antibiotics.
Subsequently she noted gingival hypertrophy. CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed subcutaneous thickening of the right buccal area, and she improved with ibuprofen.
Her history included hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. She denied recent travel but cared for sick pets.
On examination, temperature was 37.5°C, blood pressure 139/69 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, respirations 16/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Tender erythematous nodules were present on both lower extremities; otherwise normal.
Laboratory tests showed normocytic anemia with normal white‐cell and platelet counts, normal renal and liver function, and an unremarkable urinalysis.
Blood and urine cultures were negative. The test for latent tuberculosis by interferon gamma release assay (IGRA) was also negative. Serology for Brucella abortus and melitensis also came negative. Serological tests for connective‐tissue disease were negative as was the serum angiotensin converting enzyme level.
Abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple hypoechoic cystic lesions in both liver lobes. Contrast‐enhanced CT confirmed multiple oval hypoattenuating hepatic lesions.
Liver biopsy showed cholestasis and large irregular necrotic areas palisaded by epithelioid cells with granulomatous inflammation and plasma cells; acid‐fast bacilli culture remained negative at six weeks.
She continued to spike fevers up to 101°F, and her leg lesions progressed to central necrosis with purulent discharge. Pus cultures were negative for bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial growth.
Skin biopsy demonstrated surface ulceration, fibrinoid necrosis and vasculitis of superficial and deep dermal vessels with granuloma formation, neutrophilic infiltration, and debris. Immunohistochemistry and PCR for mycobacterial species on the biopsy were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Tuberculosis was considered but excluded because the IGRA was negative — “the test for latent tuberculosis by interferon gamma release assay (IGRA) was also negative.” 2. Brucellosis was considered but excluded by negative serology — “Serology for Brucella abortus and melitensis also came negative.” 3. Sarcoidosis was considered but made unlikely by a normal ACE level — “serum angiotensin converting enzyme level … was negative.”

title: Pitfalls in the diagnosis of a tumefactive demyelinating lesion: A case report
case_prompt:
A 20-year-old Caucasian woman with no past medical history presented with sudden onset of numbness and weakness of her left arm and leg. On neurologic examination, she had 4/5 strength of the left upper and lower extremities, no cranial-nerve deficits, no cerebellar signs, and cortical sensory loss to light touch and stereognosis on the left; pain and temperature sensation were preserved.

Noncontrast head CT showed a well-demarcated, 2.1-cm hypodense lesion in the right parietal lobe without surrounding edema. Brain MRI demonstrated a concentric mass in the right parietal white matter that was hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images. After gadolinium, the lesion enhanced inhomogeneously, forming alternating rings of enhancement and nonenhancement.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. An abscess was considered because “clinical differential diagnosis includes demyelinating diseases, neoplasms, and infections such as abscesses.” 2. Multiple sclerosis was considered since “such lesions with mass-like characteristics may be the presenting feature of multiple sclerosis (MS).” 3. Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis was excluded because “this type of concentric pattern has not been described in association with any other demyelinating/inflammatory diseases except BCS, and therefore, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis … were excluded in our case.” 4. Marburg variant of MS was excluded by the same reasoning: “… and therefore, … Marburg MS were excluded in our case.” 5. Low-grade glioma was considered because “the findings were not specific and were consistent either with an acute demyelinating lesion or with a low-grade glioma.”

title: Toxoplasmosis-associated IRIS involving the CNS: a case report with longitudinal analysis of T cell subsets
case_prompt:
A 37-year-old man presented with headache, psychomotor retardation, and left hemiparesis. Laboratory evaluation revealed HIV infection (CD4+ T-cell count 20 cells/μL; plasma HIV RNA 301,000 copies/mL) and positive serology for Toxoplasma gondii. A brain CT scan showed multiple scattered parenchymal lesions with perilesional edema, mass effect, and ring contrast enhancement. He received anti-toxoplasma therapy without corticosteroids, with clinical and radiologic improvement, and initiated HAART one month after diagnosis (regimen as per institutional protocol). Toxoplasmosis therapy continued (followed by suppressive dosing), and MAC prophylaxis was maintained.

Three months after HAART initiation, he reported abrupt onset of right lower-limb numbness that progressed over one month to involve his entire right lower limb and right hemi-abdomen. He denied fever or other systemic symptoms and affirmed full adherence to all therapies. On examination, mental status and cranial nerves were normal. Apart from his prior left hemiparesis, there was a distinct sensory level at T9 on the right, with loss of touch and pain sensation below this level; strength, proprioception, and reflexes in the affected areas were preserved. 

A follow-up brain CT scan revealed that some prior lesions had improved, others had slightly worsened with ring enhancement and edema, and several new lesions had appeared.
