MRI of the dorsal spinal cord demonstrated T2-weighted hyperintense lesions extending from T3 to T9, with a probable posterolateral lesion at T7–T8. At this time, CD4+ T-cell count had risen to 128 cells/μL and plasma HIV RNA had fallen to 121 copies/mL. CSF analysis showed 5 cells/μL, protein 58 mg/dL, glucose 51 mg/dL; Gram and acid-fast stains, microbiological cultures, cryptococcal antigen, VDRL, and PCR assays for JC virus, BK virus, cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex virus, human herpesvirus 6, varicella-zoster virus, and enterovirus were negative. CSF PCR for T. gondii was not performed. EBV PCR was positive, but a normal CSF lymphocyte phenotype and a whole-body PET scan showed no hypermetabolic cerebral or medullary lesions. HTLV-I/II serology was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Progression of toxoplasmosis infection — “The differential diagnosis between IRIS and a progression of toxoplasmosis infection is difficult, but if the last hypothesis was true, most probably a marked worsening of all the previous lesions would be present in the brain CT scan.” 2. Central nervous system lymphoma — “a normal CSF lymphocyte phenotype assay and a whole-body positron emission tomography (PET) scan revealed neither hyper-metabolic cerebral nor medullar lesions, which ruled out CNS lymphoma.” 3. HTLV-I/II infection — “Serological HTLV-I/II antibody assay was negative.” 4. Paradoxical immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome — “The clinical and laboratory information suggested a potential case of CNS-IRIS.”

title: Pediatric fibrocartilaginous spine embolism induced by trauma
case_prompt:
An 8-year-old previously healthy girl presented with sudden onset of mid–cervical back and neck pain followed by bilateral hand weakness. Earlier that evening, she had received repetitive kicks to her upper back while playing. She did not lose consciousness but noted progressive inability to move her hands later that night. By the following morning, grip strength was markedly diminished, making it difficult to pick up objects.

On arrival, vital signs were normal. She complained of neck and back pain but denied sensory changes. Neurologic examination revealed 0/5 strength in bilateral triceps, wrist flexors, wrist extensors, and intrinsic hand muscles; biceps and shoulder abductors were 5/5. Lower extremity motor and sensory function was intact. There were no bladder, bowel, or gait abnormalities; perianal sensation and rectal tone were normal.

A cervical spine MRI without contrast was obtained. Sagittal T1-weighted images showed a posterior disc protrusion at C5–C6. Sagittal STIR and T2 sequences demonstrated increased signal (edema) in the anterior spinal cord from C4 through C7 with minimal cord expansion. Axial T2 images localized the edema to the anterior two-thirds of the cord. Diffusion-weighted imaging revealed increased signal in the anterior cord spanning C4–C6 with corresponding signal drop-out on the apparent diffusion coefficient map, consistent with diffusion restriction.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fibrocartilaginous embolic infarction was suspected based on the combination of minor trauma, cervical disc protrusion, and acute MRI diffusion abnormalities in the anterior cord — “Given the above findings within the spinal cord in the presence of disc protrusion and history of trauma, fibrocartilaginous embolic infarction was strongly suspected.”

title: A novel presentation of Mycobacterium avium complex in a recipient of a lung transplant: a case report
case_prompt:
A 66‐year‐old African‐American woman, 4 years after bilateral lung transplantation for end‐stage pulmonary fibrosis, presented with 2 months of progressive dyspnea, wheezing, and 4 weeks of cough productive of scant blood‐tinged sputum. She denied fever or weight loss but reported nightly sweats for 6 months. Her immunosuppression regimen consisted of tacrolimus (goal trough 8–10 ng/mL) and prednisone 5 mg daily; azathioprine had been discontinued years earlier because of pancytopenia. Her medical history included chronic pancytopenia, stage IV chronic kidney disease, diabetes, hypertension, and gastroparesis. 

On examination, she was afebrile, respiratory rate 22/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air; focal end‐expiratory wheezes were heard over the left lung. Laboratory studies showed pancytopenia (WBC 0.9×10^3/mL, hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, platelets 134×10^3/mL) and elevated creatinine (2.37 mg/dL). CMV and EBV PCR were undetectable. 

Chest CT showed a 2.2×1.1×0.9 cm polypoid soft‐tissue lesion arising from the roof of the left mainstem bronchus at the anastomotic site, with severe luminal narrowing to 3 mm and tree‐in‐bud nodularity in the left lower lobe. 

Rigid bronchoscopy revealed a fibrinous mass protruding into the left mainstem bronchus proximal to the anastomosis. Multiple biopsies were obtained, and a bronchial wash was performed; samples were sent for histopathologic and microbiologic evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Squamous cell carcinoma was suspected because of the appearance of a new endobronchial mass in a transplant recipient — “there was a high concern for endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma” 2. Post‐transplant lymphoproliferative disorder was considered given long‐standing immunosuppression — “such as squamous cell carcinoma or an unusual presentation of post‐transplant lymphoproliferative disorder due to her chronic immunosuppression”

title: Hypoglycemia secondary to factitious hyperinsulinism in a foster care adolescent - a case report of munchausen syndrome in a community hospital emergency department setting
case_prompt:
A 19-year-old Hispanic woman with acne, asthma, and psychosocial distress presented to the ED with lightheadedness, generalized weakness, diaphoresis, diarrhea, and vomiting. 

First ED visit:  
• She reported a home glucose of 53 mg/dL, rising to 80 mg/dL after eating.  
• Triage vitals: BP 98/65 mmHg; HR 81 bpm; RR 18/min; SpO₂ 100% on room air; T 37.0°C.  
• Exam: well-developed, obese woman in no acute distress.  
• Point-of-care glucose was 60 mg/dL (confirmed by lab); other labs and examination were unremarkable.  
• She received IV saline, ondansetron, dietary advice for hypoglycemia, and was discharged.

Second ED visit (next day):  
• Found unresponsive at home with prehospital glucose 46 mg/dL treated with D50W.  
• Triage vitals: BP 122/75 mmHg; HR 100 bpm; RR 18/min; SpO₂ 100%; T 36.7°C.  
• Exam and labs again unremarkable. She was treated symptomatically and discharged.

Third ED visit (three weeks later):  
• Found unresponsive at work with glucose 23 mg/dL; EMS administered D50W to 172 mg/dL.  
• In the ED her glucose repeatedly fell despite multiple D50W boluses and escalating dextrose infusions (D5½NS then D10W), prompting ICU admission.  
• She had recurrent episodes of weakness, confusion, diaphoresis, palpitations, and altered mental status.  
• ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no acute changes.  
• Laboratory studies across visits showed hypoglycemia with otherwise normal hematologic, renal, and electrolyte panels (Table 2).  
• A standard drug screen was negative.  
• Further tests: hemoglobin A1C 5.1%; C-peptide 9.9 ng/mL; free insulin 370 mIU/L; total insulin 377 mIU/L.  
• Noncontrast head CT: no intracranial abnormalities.  
• Abdominal CT: right ovarian cyst; no pancreatic mass.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Insulinoma was considered but ruled out — “there was no pancreatic mass to suggest an insulinoma on CT of the abdomen.” 2. Intracranial lesion causing autonomic or neurologic dysfunction was excluded — “CT scan of the head without IV contrast … revealed no evidence of intracranial findings or suspicious intracranial mass.” 3. Drug- or toxin-induced hypoglycemia was excluded by toxicology screening — “Standard drug screen was done to rule out any symptoms from toxicity; however the results were negative.” 4. Surreptitious insulin administration was suspected due to biochemical findings — “Classic findings include a low blood sugar level, suppressed C-peptide level, and an inappropriately elevated insulin level.”

title: Conservative management of unicystic ameloblastoma in a young child: Report of two cases
case_prompt: A 9-year-old boy with no significant medical history presented for dental evaluation of carious teeth and a two-month history of painless swelling in the right posterior maxilla. On examination there was a non-tender, expansile swelling of the right maxillary posterior region associated with carious primary maxillary canine and first and second molars. Panoramic radiograph showed a well-corticated, unilocular radiolucent lesion measuring approximately 4 × 3 cm from the distal aspect of the permanent right lateral incisor to the mesial aspect of the permanent right second premolar. Aspiration of the cystic cavity yielded golden-yellow fluid. Based on the patient’s age, lesion location, and radiographic appearance, both an inflammatory dentigerous cyst and unicystic ameloblastoma were considered in the preoperative differential.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Odontogenic keratocyst was considered due to its unilocular radiolucent presentation but excluded because it typically demonstrates anteroposterior spread with minimal cortical expansion — “keratocyst usually spread anterio-posteriorly and seldom shows cortical expansion. On aspiration, keratocyst shows a large amount of keratin.” 2. Residual cyst was considered but excluded because these are associated with sites of extracted teeth, whereas no history of prior extraction in the lesion area was reported — “residual cysts are associated with missing teeth that have been extracted.” 3. Adenomatoid odontogenic tumor was considered given its cystic radiographic appearance but excluded because it predominantly occurs in the anterior maxilla, not the posterior region — “adenomatoid odontogenic tumors have a predilection for anterior maxilla.” 4. Central giant lesion was considered as a jaw lesion but excluded because it most often arises anterior to the first mandibular molar, not the maxillary posterior — “a central giant lesion often arises anterior to first mandibular molar.”

title: Extranodal Marginal Zone Lymphoma Presenting within the Meckel Diverticulum as Diverticulitis: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old man with a history of colonic diverticulitis presented with acute abdominal pain. On examination, his abdomen was distended with right lower quadrant tenderness. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed small-bowel loops dilated up to 4.3 cm in diameter, with a transition point in the mid-lower abdomen concerning for moderate enteric obstruction and asymmetric wall thickening of some ileal loops, along with a few borderline mesenteric lymph nodes. Diagnostic laparoscopy identified a nonperforated Meckel diverticulum in the mid-ileum that appeared prominent, inflamed, and indurated. 

Histologic examination of the resected diverticulum showed a true diverticulum lined by intestinal mucosa without ectopic gastric or pancreatic tissue. Microscopy revealed extensive infiltration of sheets of small lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm (monocytoid B-cells) forming lymphoepithelial lesions in the mucosa and submucosa and focally involving the serosal adipose tissue. In some areas, there were nodular proliferations with occasional reactive germinal centers and an expanded marginal zone. Immunohistochemical studies demonstrated that the lymphocytes were positive for CD20 with weak coexpression of CD43 and positive for BCL-2, and negative for CD10, Cyclin-D1, CD3, and CD5. The reactive germinal centers were BCL-2–negative and BCL-6–positive, with no evidence of follicular colonization by tumor cells. In situ hybridization for kappa and lambda showed scattered polyclonal plasma cells, and the Ki-67 proliferation index was less than 5%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Carcinoid tumor — “Malignant tumors arising from MD are rare and there are a few reported cases of carcinoid tumors…” Excluded because “microscopy revealed extensive infiltration of sheets of small lymphocytes…” 2. Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) — “Malignant tumors arising from MD are rare and there are a few reported cases of… gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST)…” Excluded because the lesion was composed of B-cells, not spindle cells typical of GIST. 3. Signet ring cell carcinoma — “…a few reported cases of… signet ring cell carcinoma…” Excluded because histology lacked malignant epithelial cells with signet morphology. 4. Adenocarcinoma — “…and adenocarcinoma” Excluded because there was no glandular neoplastic epithelium on microscopy. 5. Unclassified B-cell lymphoma — “…and one B-cell lymphoma with no further classification…” Excluded because the immunophenotypic profile and lymphoepithelial lesions indicated a marginal-zone origin. 6. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma — “Another differential diagnosis for GI ENMZL can be any primary GI non-Hodgkin lymphomas such as diffuse large B-cell lymphoma…” Excluded because the cells were small to medium sized with a low proliferation index. 8. Atypical MALT hyperplasia — “The molecular genetic testing is helpful to differentiate this type of lymphoma from atypical marginal zone hyperplasia of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue…” Excluded by the presence of lymphoepithelial lesions and expanded marginal zone beyond reactive hyperplasia. 9. IPSID (alpha chain disease) — “IPSID also known as alpha chain disease… most commonly involved small intestine.” Excluded because the plasma cells were polyclonal and there was no evidence of heavy-chain disease.

title: Unexplained proximal tibiofibular joint pain after high tibial osteotomy
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old man presented with diffuse left knee pain 3 years after a medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy (HTO) fixed with a TomoFix plate for varus-associated medial compartment overload. He described persistent anterior tibial and lateral knee pain; his preoperative medial joint pain had resolved. Two years after HTO the hardware was removed, but the pain continued.

On examination there were no signs of infection; the previous medial wound had healed. There was tenderness over the anterior and medial tibia and the lateral-posterior knee joint line, with mild hypesthesia around the scar. Vascular examination was normal. Varus stress and rotational provocation tests elicited severe pain that necessitated termination of testing. Active range of motion was 0° to 120°.

Standard non–weight-bearing anteroposterior and lateral radiographs of the knee showed a well-healed osteotomy site with a distally placed plate and generalized osteoarthritic changes (Kellgren–Lawrence grade II–IV), particularly in the patellofemoral joint. Long-leg alignment films showed the mechanical axis (Mikulicz line) passing through the lateral tibiofemoral compartment.

Because the cause of persistent pain remained unclear, a 99mTc-HDP SPECT/CT was obtained. There was no increased tracer uptake in the tibiofemoral joint or osteotomy site, but there was markedly increased uptake in the proximal tibiofibular joint. Axial CT images demonstrated that the proximal tibial screws were excessively long and protruded into the proximal tibiofibular joint, which also showed advanced degenerative changes.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Under-correction by osteotomy — “there was no increased uptake within the tibiofemoral joint, indicating a biologically well performed correction of the varus deformity.” 2. Nonunion at the osteotomy site — “The osteotomy site had healed indicating no tracer uptake.” 3. Tibiofemoral or patellofemoral osteoarthritis — “no tracer uptake within the tibiofemoral joint... Increased tracer uptake was seen in the proximal tibio-fibular joint.” 4. Iatrogenic proximal tibiofibular joint injury with resulting osteoarthritis — “the proximal screws were too long and placed within the pTFj,” correlating with focal tracer uptake and degenerative changes.

title: Management Challenge of Coexistence of Macrophage Activation Syndrome, Systemic Lupus Erythematosus, and Hepatitis B: Case Report
case_prompt:
A 42-year-old woman was admitted with a one-month history of fever, asthenia, and inflammatory polyarthralgia involving small, medium, and large joints. She later developed chest pain, a productive cough with whitish sputum (worse at night), progressive exertional dyspnea, and bilateral lower-limb edema.  

Her medical and family history were unremarkable except for obstetric history: five pregnancies, four live births, and termination at 21 weeks.  

On examination, temperature was 37.2 °C, heart rate 110 beats/min, blood pressure 180/100 mm Hg. She appeared fatigued and anorexic. Hands and feet showed intermittent acrosyndrome without ulcers. There was no synovitis or tenosynovitis. Findings included painful hepatomegaly, jugular venous distension, a positive hepatojugular reflux, bilateral pitting edema of the lower limbs, dullness to percussion and absent breath sounds at the right lung base, and mild ascites. No splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy was noted.  

Initial laboratory studies revealed:  
• Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, leukocytes 3.28 G/L, platelets 83 G/L  
• ESR 85 mm in first hour, CRP 96 mg/L  
• Creatinine 141 µmol/L; AST 84 U/L, ALT 90 U/L  
• Triglycerides 2.41 g/L; D-dimer 9300 ng/mL  
• Sodium 121 mmol/L; albumin 13.4 g/L; total protein 43 g/L  
• 24-h proteinuria 0.65 g; ferritin 2675.6 ng/mL  
• Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia; normal liver synthetic function; prothrombin time 100%; aPTT 53.7 s  

Serologic and microbiologic tests:  
• HBsAg positive; hepatitis C, HIV, and COVID-19 serologies negative; AFB smear negative  
• ANA 1:1280 (speckled); anti–double-stranded DNA 380 IU/mL  

Bone-marrow aspiration showed a hypercellular marrow with cell lysis and numerous macrophages.  

Chest radiograph demonstrated a moderate right pleural effusion and cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic index 0.6). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 13.2 mm noncompressive pericardial effusion and a dilated heart with an ejection fraction of 35%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of relapsing autoimmune disease — “the diagnostic hypotheses evoked were a relapsing dysimmune disease…” 2. Consideration of severe infection — “…a severe infection…” with negative bacteriologic and virologic studies except for HBsAg; “viral hepatitis B is an extremely rare cause of sHLH.” 5. Exclusion of hepatitis B as sole cause of MAS — “viral hepatitis B is an extremely rare cause of sHLH” and more likely a confounding or triggering factor rather than the primary driver.

title: Unravelling a mystery of hypokalemic hypertension- a rare case report of a reninoma
case_prompt:
A 19-year-old unmarried woman was found to have hypertension (200/120 mmHg) six months earlier, when she was hospitalized for a febrile illness. She has been on losartan 50 mg twice daily without blood-pressure control. She denies muscle weakness, polyuria, nocturia, weight gain, bruising, acne, hirsutism, episodic headaches, or rheumatologic symptoms. There is no family history of early hypertension or intracerebral hemorrhage. She takes no other medications or supplements.

On examination, her BMI is 22 kg/m2. Pulse is 88 beats/minute and regular. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable; there are no radio-radial or radio-femoral delays, murmurs, or bruits. Fundoscopy shows silver wiring of retinal vessels; other systems are normal.

Laboratory studies:
• Potassium 2.8 mmol/L (3.5–5.2) despite replacement  
• Sodium 136 mmol/L (135–145)  
• Bicarbonate 24.8 mEq/L (22–26)  
• Overnight dexamethasone suppression test <11 nmol/L (normal <50)  
• Aldosterone 49.7 ng/dL (1.76–39.2)  
• Plasma renin concentration 1275 μIU/mL (2.8–32.9)  
• Plasma renin activity 106.3 ng/mL/hr  
• Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) 0.5 (<10 suggests secondary aldosteronism)  
• TSH, 24-h urinary metanephrines, creatinine, ESR, and urine microscopy all normal  

Cardiac echocardiogram shows normal biventricular function without coarctation. Renal ultrasound shows normal renal size and parenchyma; renal artery Doppler is normal. CT angiogram excludes renal artery stenosis but reveals a 1.2 × 1 cm well-defined, exophytic, iso-dense lesion in the lower pole of the left kidney with delayed contrast enhancement.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Renovascular hypertension was excluded as “renal artery Doppler was also found to be normal. Lastly, her renal angiogram was also found to be normal, excluding a renal artery stenosis.” 2. Ectopic renin secretion from a paraneoplastic source was deemed unlikely given “the rarity of the ectopic renin-secreting lesion in this young female without any other clinical features suggesting an underlying malignancy.” 3. Other renin-secreting renal masses (Wilms tumour, renal cell carcinoma, oncocytoma) were considered less likely because “they are also known to secrete renin but are found to be less potent clinically.” 4. Reninoma was entertained on the basis that “because of this finding [renal lesion] in the background of her hypokalemic hypertension with secondary aldosteronism, the possibility of reninoma was entertained.”

title: Primary squamous cell carcinoma of the pancreas: a case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 70-year-old Caucasian man, a non-smoker, presented with a 3-month history of 12 kg weight loss and recent generalized pruritus and jaundice. Serum biochemistry and abdominal ultrasonography confirmed obstructive jaundice. Contrast CT of the abdomen showed a 4.6×4.1 cm mass in the head of the pancreas without radiologic evidence of distant metastases. He underwent ERCP with stent placement, followed two months later by a pylorus-sparing pancreaticoduodenectomy, with an uneventful recovery.

Gross examination revealed a 5.5 cm lobulated tumor in the pancreatic head extending to the superior mesenteric vessel margin; the entire lesion was submitted for histology. Microscopically, the neoplasm consisted of large nests of basaloid cells with central necrosis and scattered small foci of squamoid differentiation. There was no morphologic evidence of glandular or acinar formation. The tumor showed extensive intravenous and intralymphatic invasion, foci of perineural invasion, extension into the duodenum, ampullary region, pancreatic duct, and peripancreatic fat, and microscopic involvement of the superior mesenteric vessel margin. Metastatic deposits with similar morphology were identified in 6 of 43 regional lymph nodes.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Adenosquamous carcinoma was considered but excluded due to absence of glandular differentiation — “Adenosquamous carcinoma is a rare variant of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, showing both glandular differentiation and squamous differentiation. However, no glandular differentiation was seen in our tumor, despite extensive sampling.”
final_diagnosis: Squamous cell carcinoma of the pancreas

title: Negative-pressure-related diffuse alveolar hemorrhage after monitored anesthesia care for vertebroplasty: a case report
case_prompt:
A 68-year-old Asian woman with a history of osteoporosis was admitted for vertebroplasty of a compression fracture of the third lumbar vertebra. Her surgical history included appendectomy, thyroid nodule removal, and rotator cuff repair under general anesthesia. Preoperative evaluation was unremarkable except for grade I diastolic dysfunction on echocardiography. Vertebroplasty was performed in the prone position under spontaneous ventilation with sevoflurane sedation; there were no intraoperative events. 

In the post–anesthesia care unit, she had sudden hemoptysis—enough to drench four to five gauze pads—and dyspnea, with oxygen saturation falling to 80%, which improved to 93% with 5 L/min of oxygen by mask. She was hemodynamically stable, with coarse breath sounds bilaterally. Postoperative chest radiography showed new bilateral perihilar consolidation with air bronchograms. On transfer to our institution, she was afebrile; pulse, 82/min; respiration, 20/min; blood pressure, 120/75 mmHg; and oxygen saturation, 97% on supplemental oxygen. Computed tomography of the chest revealed diffuse ground-glass opacities and interlobular septal thickening in the central portions of both lungs. Bronchoscopy was not performed at the patient’s request.
diagnostic_reasoning: 2. Immunologic vasculitis or other non-immunologic causes (infection, malignancy, coagulation disorders, drug toxicity) were included in the differential for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, but perioperative cases are “mainly caused by high negative pressure due to acute airway obstruction.” 3. Sevoflurane-induced pulmonary toxicity was considered given reports of alveolar wall damage at high concentrations — “high concentrations of sevoflurane have toxic effects, increasing the risk of alveolar wall damage and, in several cases, inducing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.” 4. Negative pressure pulmonary hemorrhage was determined to explain the presentation via acute airway obstruction physiology — “Although most patients experience negative pressure pulmonary edema, hemorrhages can develop in the alveoli, and the destruction of pulmonary capillaries plays an important role in overt pulmonary hemorrhage.”

title: Urticaria multiforme in an adolescent: A rare benign rash not to be ignored
case_prompt: A 16-year-old adolescent presented with an extensive, pruritic rash that began abruptly 1 week earlier. The lesions were annular, arciform, or polycyclic, erythematous with an edematous peripheral border and a pale or ecchymotic center, distributed on the trunk, limbs, and face, with sparing of the palms and soles. Individual lesions were labile, resolving entirely within 24 hours, and dermographism was present. There was no edema of the extremities. There were no ulcerations of the oral or genital mucosa, and no target lesions. The throat was mildly erythematous. He was in good general condition and reported a 10-day history of cough and rhinorrhea. He had no significant past medical history, no known allergies, no recent medications, and no recent vaccinations. Laboratory studies revealed a C-reactive protein level of 50 mg/L (normal <5 mg/L) and a normal leukocyte count. Viral serologies for EBV, CMV, parvovirus, and hepatitis were negative, as was the antinuclear factor. A SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was also negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered erythema multiforme — “There is no ulceration of the oral or genital mucosa, nor are there any target lesions.” 2. Considered acute hemorrhagic edema of infants — “The extremities are not edematous.” 3. Considered pseudo‐serum sickness — “the pruritic and migratory nature of the lesions, as well as the presence of dermographism and the absence of residual pigmentation, are clinical criteria in favor of urticaria multiforme.”

title: An Interesting Cause of Hyperandrogenemic Hirsutism
case_prompt: A 68-year-old woman was referred for gradually increased facial hirsutism and deepening of the voice over 6 months. She had entered menopause at age 52 and had an unremarkable family history. Her medical history included hypertension managed with telmisartan and osteoarthritis treated with topical NSAID creams. On examination, blood pressure was 140/90 mmHg, pulse 84 beats/min, and temperature 36.9 °C. She had plethoric facies and terminal hair growth on the upper lip, chin, and upper abdomen (Ferriman–Gallwey score 4). There was no clitoromegaly or temporal balding. The remainder of her examination was normal. Laboratory tests showed polycythemia (hemoglobin 18 g/dL, hematocrit 54%), normal electrolytes and biochemistry, and a serum testosterone level of 25.40 nmol/L (normal range for women, 0.38–1.97 nmol/L). Further testing revealed a 17-hydroxyprogesterone level of 302.57 pmol/L and a cortisol level of 2.7 nmol/L after a 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test. Pelvic MRI showed ovarian dimensions of 22×12 mm and 22×14 mm without masses, and adrenal CT demonstrated normal gland size and configuration. Serial measurements on hospitalization days 4, 6, 8, and 10 showed a decline in testosterone levels to 12, 7.4, 4.3, and 2.1 nmol/L, respectively.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. PCOS was excluded because virilization symptoms and very high testosterone levels suggested against PCOS — “Virilization symptoms and very high testosterone levels suggested excluding PCOS.” 2. Ovarian tumor was suspected given the severity of virilization but was deemed unlikely by negative imaging, although imaging cannot fully exclude malignancy — “Negativity of ovarian imaging does not exclude ovarian malignancy.” 3. Exogenous androgen exposure was supported by the progressive decrease in testosterone levels during hospitalization — “This gradual decrease in testosterone levels was suggested to be due to exogenous androgen exposed.”

title: Thorahcic SMARCA4-deficient undifferentiated tumors with ganglioneuroma and enchondroma: implications for SLC7A11 and ARID1A expression: a case report
case_prompt:
A 43-year-old man presented with progressive respiratory distress. He was a heavy smoker. On admission, CT of the chest showed a 55 mm mass in the right upper lobe extending into the anterior mediastinum, with multiple enlarged regional lymph nodes. An ultrasound-guided lymph node biopsy was performed, and the initial pathological diagnosis was poorly differentiated non–small-cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma type. Despite systemic chemotherapy, the tumor was refractory, and the patient died six months after admission. 
At autopsy, the thoracic mass measured 10 cm and extended from the right upper lobe to the anterior mediastinum, with widespread lymph node metastases. On gross examination, the tumor was solid, opalescent, and necrotic. Microscopically, it showed sheets of monotonous epithelial-like cells with loose intercellular connections, distinct nucleoli, rhabdoid-like cells, frequent mitoses, and areas of necrosis. 
Immunohistochemical staining showed the tumor cells were negative for desmin, S100P, TTF-1, p40, CK7, CK20, SMARCA4, and SMARCA2, and positive for SOX2, CD34, p53, and AE1/AE3. Molecular testing for EGFR mutations and ALK and ROS1 rearrangements was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Exclusion of lymphoma, NUT carcinoma, germ cell tumor, neuroendocrine carcinoma, and melanoma — “Lymphoma, NUT carcinoma, germ cell tumor, neuroendocrine carcinoma, and melanoma are unlikely to be detected by immunohistochemistry.” 2. Lack of glandular or squamous differentiation argues against conventional NSCLC — “Furthermore, no differentiation into adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma was confirmed in any tumor section.”

title: Sweet syndrome: a lesson in the management of the cutaneous lesions of malignancy
case_prompt: An 81-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer and newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia, managed palliatively, presented with bilateral painful skin lesions of the thumbs. On admission she was febrile (38.2 °C). Examination revealed well-demarcated, tender, violaceous plaques on the dorsal and palmar surfaces of both thumbs, with pus at the left thumbnail base. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 19.8 × 10^9/L and a C-reactive protein level of 121 mg/L. She was started on intravenous clindamycin, acyclovir, and fluconazole. Over the next 2 days, the plaques extended to the bases of both thumbs and both index fingers. Wound swabs yielded no growth at 40 hours. Orthopaedic debridement removed necrotic fat tissue; histology demonstrated haemorrhage with fibrinopurulent exudate, florid reactive atypia and epidermal hyperplasia, and an acute inflammatory infiltrate without neoplastic cells. Subsequently, the lesions evolved into haemorrhagic bullae and new lesions appeared on the little fingers. Nonadjacent digit involvement and failure to respond to antibiotics suggested a noninfectious process.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraneoplastic pemphigus — typically involves mucosal surfaces and is refractory to steroids: “Paraneoplastic pemphigus preferentially involves oral mucosa and typically responds poorly to steroid therapy.” 2. Pyoderma gangrenosum — usually affects the trunk and lower extremities rather than hands: “Pyoderma gangrenosum … usually affects the trunk and lower extremities.” 3. Sweet syndrome — fits the abrupt febrile onset with tender violaceous plaques, neutrophilic histology, and steroid responsiveness: “This is not a vasculitis, but behaves similarly with abrupt onset of tender purple papules on the upper extremities that are accompanied by fever and respond very well to steroid therapy.”

title: “This Is Not the Original Timeline”: A Case Report of an Extended Dissociative Episode in a Healthy Young Male Accompanied with Severe Decline in Mental State
case_prompt:
A 21-year-old man was brought to the emergency department by his aunt after first presenting with sudden-onset severe anxiety and the sensation that he was dying. He has high-functioning autism and obsessive-compulsive disorder; there is no other psychiatric or medical family history. He reported using cocaine that day, but collateral history denied this. He was diagnosed with a panic attack and discharged. A few hours later he returned, agitated, pacing, and nonverbal.  

Initial vital signs and physical examination were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood count, liver function, renal profile, and inflammatory markers. A urine toxicology screen was negative. Notable findings included a low serum phosphate of 0.35 mmol/L and an elevated prolactin of 737 mu/L. Skull radiograph showed no fractures. MRI of the head was unremarkable. Lumbar puncture analysis returned with normal protein and glucose and was negative for bacteria or viruses.  

He was referred for psychiatric assessment and admitted to the acute psychiatric unit 9 days after initial presentation. On admission, he was severely disoriented to time, place, and person. He exhibited major thought block with markedly limited, stilted speech. He appeared dazed and had increased muscle tone, spastic movements, and preserved withdrawal to painful stimuli. His speech content was disjointed and bizarre; examples included “this is not the original,” “two people joined in the middle, different timelines,” and “I am not the soft science in the Jurassic thoughts.” He occasionally answered yes/no questions with great effort but could not recall his responses. Mental status examination showed a well-kept young man with poor eye contact and an intense stare, normal tone and volume of speech but extremely limited and incoherent content. Mood was euthymic, but thoughts were chaotic, and he lacked insight and capacity regarding his mental state.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious meningitis or encephalitis — “Both meningitis and encephalitis were easily ruled out with lumbar puncture showing normal protein and glucose levels.” 2. Pathological hyperprolactinaemia (eg, pituitary adenoma) — “A normal MRI scan showing no gross abnormalities would rule out any pathological cause.”

title: Elevated troponin level and nonspecific ST‐segment and T‐wave changes in a suspected acute pancreatitis patient, post‐SARS‐Cov‐2 infection: A case report
case_prompt:
A 62-year-old man presented to the emergency department with severe right upper‐quadrant abdominal pain, moderate epigastric pain, and chest pain. He reported a COVID-19 infection 14 days earlier, after which he developed mild right upper‐quadrant discomfort that progressed over 3 days to severe pain aggravated by eating and unrelieved by changes in position. The chest pain was described as atypical for cardiac origin and was accompanied by nausea and vomiting. He denied alcohol use, trauma, and had no known chronic diseases; family history was notable for cholecystectomy in his mother and sisters.

On arrival, blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, pulse 80/min, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 37°C, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure without peripheral edema, normal abdominal percussion, and a liver span of 14.5 cm at the midclavicular line.

Electrocardiogram showed bradyarrhythmia, T-wave inversion in lead V1, and ST‐segment elevation in leads V2 through V6. Chest and abdominal radiographs were unremarkable, with no evidence of pneumonia, air under the diaphragm, or biliary air; gallbladder sludge was noted. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated trivial mitral regurgitation and an ejection fraction of 55%.

Laboratory tests revealed elevated high-sensitivity troponin (0 h: 1547.6 pg/ml; 6 h: 1342.8 pg/ml), amylase 2420 U/L, lipase 987 U/L, AST 800 IU/L, ALT 760 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 361 IU/L, total bilirubin 3.27 mg/dl, direct bilirubin 2.37 mg/dl, blood sugar 203 mg/dl, and urea 68 mg/dl.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Myocardial injury secondary to non‐ischemic causes was excluded given the ECG and echocardiographic findings — “ST-segment changes in ECG are non-specific … thus myocardial injury is excluded.”

title: A Rare Presentation of Possible Disseminated Histoplasmosis with Adrenal Insufficiency Leading to Adrenal Crisis in an Immunocompetent Adult: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 42-year-old Sri Lankan shopkeeper with diet‐controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with three weeks of generalized malaise, nightly “feverishness,” evening sweats, anorexia, and 3-kg weight loss. He denied cough, hemoptysis, or tuberculosis contacts. There was nausea without vomiting or abdominal pain and no features of autoimmune disease. He had no significant environmental or travel exposures and no history of recurrent infections.

On examination he was afebrile, mildly pale, with mild nontender hepatomegaly but no lymphadenopathy, rashes, or splenomegaly. Blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg without postural drop. Initial labs showed pancytopenia (WBC 3.0×10^9/L, platelets 120×10^6/L, hemoglobin 10 g/dL), CRP 8 mg/L, ESR 60 mm/h, normal liver and renal profiles, and HbA1c 6.5%. Chest radiograph and echocardiogram were normal. Blood and urine cultures, Mantoux test, and bone marrow biopsy (including fungal and tuberculosis studies) were negative. Abdominal ultrasound showed mild hepatosplenomegaly, para‐aortic lymphadenopathy, and bilateral hypoechoic adrenal masses.

One week later he returned with three days of severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and postural dizziness. He was hypotensive (90/60 mmHg with a 30 mmHg postural drop), tachycardic (100 bpm), and dehydrated. Despite fluids his blood pressure remained low. Labs showed hyponatremia (114 mmol/L), hyperkalemia (5.0 mmol/L), random cortisol 1.3 µg/L, and improved blood counts. An ACTH stimulation test showed persistently low cortisol (<1 µg/L). CT of the chest and abdomen revealed bilateral, well‐defined, homogeneously enhancing adrenal masses (left 6.9×3.7×5.0 cm; right 5.3×3.4×5.4 cm), mild hepatosplenomegaly, and patchy para‐aortic lymphadenopathy.

Ultrasound‐guided adrenal biopsy showed necrosis with plasma cells, lymphocytes, and histiocytes. Gomori–Grocott methenamine silver staining demonstrated numerous fungal spores morphologically similar to Histoplasma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lymphoma and metastatic malignancy were considered causes of bilateral adrenal masses—“His adrenal biopsy did not show any histological evidence of tuberculosis, lymphoma, or disseminated malignancy.” 2. Direct visualization of yeast‐like spores by GMS favored Histoplasma—“With the Gomori–Grocott methenamine silver staining, there were numerous fungal spores which were morphologically similar to Histoplasma…”

title: Sight-Threatening Immune Retinopathy Developing Secondary to Durvalumab Treatment of Small-Cell Lung Cancer: A Case Report
case_prompt:
An 81-year-old Korean man was seen urgently for a 2-month history of a temporal C-shaped scotoma in his left eye. He had a 3-year history of neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD) treated with anti-VEGF in the right eye (last injection 9 months prior) and controlled nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy in both eyes. Other medical history included type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, and extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (T2b/3, N3, M1a) diagnosed 5 months earlier. He had received five cycles of durvalumab (1,500 mg every 3 weeks). Before cancer treatment, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left; intraocular pressures were normal. 

On presentation, visual acuity was 20/200 OD and 20/25 OS. The anterior segments were unremarkable. Fundus examination showed an atrophic macula in the right eye from prior nAMD and only mild pigmentary changes in the left eye. Fundus autofluorescence revealed broad C-shaped hypoautofluorescent areas with hyperfluorescent margins in both eyes. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) demonstrated loss of the peripapillary ellipsoid zone in the left eye corresponding to the visual field defect. Fluorescein angiography showed normal vascular perfusion.

Routine laboratory tests, including a CBC and renal function, were normal except for mildly elevated AST and ALT. Abdominal ultrasound showed moderate fatty liver. Thyroid function tests indicated subclinical hypothyroidism. Serologies for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis, and HIV were negative. F-FDG PET/CT revealed pleural seeding and multiple lymph node metastases. Brain MRI noted a left superior frontal gyrus metastasis, but these findings did not explain the patient’s visual field defects.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Neovascular AMD progression was unlikely in the left eye — “the right fundus photograph revealed an atrophic macula attributable to the nAMD, and there was no specific finding in the left eye other than mild pigmentary changes.” 2. Diabetic retinopathy flare was ruled out as the retinopathy was quiescent — “the nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy in both eyes … were under control.” 3. Retinal vasculitis from immunotherapy was considered but excluded by absence of leakage — “retinal vasculitis is a known side effect of durvalumab” and “Fluorescein angiography revealed normal vascular perfusion.”

title: Pervasive Refusal Syndrome in Autistic Spectrum Disorder
case_prompt: A 9-year-old Caucasian male with preexisting diagnoses of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and inattentive subtype attention deficit disorder (ADD) presented as an emergency to our inpatient service due to minimal dietary intake. He was detained under Section 2 of the Mental Health Act and admitted to a specialist psychiatric ward for children under 12. At presentation, his most prominent symptoms were refusal of food and fluids, mutism, school refusal, and self‐neglect, including refusal of personal care. His dietary restriction led to severe weight loss with a body mass index of less than 12 kg/m2 (<0.5th percentile), requiring nasogastric tube feeding. His sole method of communication was typing on an iPad, and his personal care was limited to wearing pajamas and a coat for several days without washing or changing. His mother reported incidents of urinary and fecal incontinence. He would not sleep in his bed but instead slept on the floor outside his parents’ bedroom in the “fetal position.” When family members attempted to touch him, he became physically aggressive and hit out at them. In May 2015, his stepfather gave him a haircut that he particularly disliked. Following this event, he became more controlling of his dietary intake and selectively mute with his stepfather, refusing to eat his food or speak to him.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. PRS over ASD alone — “a diagnosis of Pervasive Refusal Syndrome better describes the symptoms than ASD alone when the following present together: a significant change in functioning, significant level of severity … and an overt trigger that is perceived as stressful.” 2. ASD feeding selectivity considered but insufficient — “Children with an ASD diagnosis can develop different eating patterns … selective [eating] …; however, persistent refusal of all intake is not typical of baseline ASD traits.”

title: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia After Relapse of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia: A Case Report
case_prompt: A 51-year-old man with chronic myeloid leukemia (diagnosed in 2007) treated initially with imatinib then nilotinib (achieving a 4.5 log molecular response for 6 years) presented in March 2019 with 2 weeks of left upper quadrant abdominal pain, anorexia, and easy fatigability. He had elected a treatment-free remission in 2013. On exam he was pale with hepatosplenomegaly but no bleeding. Initial laboratory studies showed WBC 90.2×10^9/L (neutrophilia, eosinophilia, basophilia), hemoglobin 6.6 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, platelets 497×10^9/L, normal renal and liver function, and uric acid 745 μmol/L. Anemia workup demonstrated a positive direct antiglobulin test, mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and a normal reticulocyte count. Viral serologies (EBV, CMV, HIV) were negative. Peripheral blood smear showed 1% blasts consistent with CML and hypochromic microcytic anemia. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed CML relapse in chronic phase with erythroid hyperplasia. He was started on hydroxyurea and allopurinol and received 2 units of matched RBCs, but his hemoglobin rose only to 7.0 g/dL. Two weeks later he returned with increased fatigue and a hemoglobin of 5.8 g/dL. After transfusion of 2 additional units (hemoglobin 7.7 g/dL), repeat studies showed a hemolytic picture with elevated reticulocyte count, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and a positive direct antiglobulin test.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Anemia due to central marrow failure was considered but excluded because “the bone marrow biopsy showed proliferation of RBCs precursors.” 2. Transfusion‐related alloimmunization was considered but excluded because “DAT was positive prior to blood transfusion and the units were compatible with the patient’s blood.” 3. TKI-induced hemolysis, especially imatinib-associated autoimmune hemolysis, was considered but felt unlikely due to “the remote use of nilotinib” and lack of temporal relation. 4. Post-transplant immune reconstitution-related hemolysis was noted in other reports but excluded here since “the mechanism of hemolysis in those cases was believed to be linked to an immune reconstitution after transplantation.”

title: Severe thyrotoxicosis as initial presentation of gastric choriocarcinoma: a case report
case_prompt: A 62-year-old Hispanic woman with no significant past medical history presented with a 3-week history of progressive dyspnea, palpitations, profound weakness, new hot flashes, and right flank pain. Vital signs showed a heart rate of approximately 100 beats per minute and a temperature of 37.7 °C. Examination revealed pale conjunctivae without erythema or exophthalmos, a thyroid gland of normal size without nodules, a systolic tricuspid murmur, decreased basal breath sounds, and tender hepatomegaly extending 5 cm below the right costal margin. Neurologic examination demonstrated hyperreflexia and tremor. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 3054 U/L (122–222), alkaline phosphatase 434 U/L (40–129), elevated total and free thyroxine levels, and suppressed TSH. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed pulmonary hypertension (65 mmHg), right atrial and ventricular dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, preserved left ventricular function, and an atrial septal defect with right-to-left shunt. Contrast-enhanced 18 F-FDG PET-CT demonstrated hepatomegaly (22 cm) with multiple FDG-avid liver masses up to 12 cm (SUVmax 13.5), FDG uptake in the gastric antrum (SUVmax 9.4) without corresponding wall thickening, mesenteric lymphadenopathy, and small pulmonary infiltrates. A technetium-99m pertechnetate thyroid scan showed diffusely increased uptake, and assays for thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin and TSH receptor antibodies were negative. The patient was started on propranolol and methimazole for thyrotoxicosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Graves’ disease — “Graves’ disease was ruled out in the setting of no signs of Graves orbitopathy, no goiter, and negative thyroid stimulating antibodies.” 2. Gestational hyperthyroidism — “Biochemical hyperthyroidism during pregnancy occurs most commonly due to gestational hyperthyroidism (GH) or Graves’ disease… GH is generally mild, asymptomatic, and self-limiting.” 3. Factitious ingestion of thyroid hormones — “Thyrotoxicosis can occur in the context of increased thyroid function, release of preformed hormones, or even because of factitious ingestion of thyroid hormones.” 4. hCG-mediated thyrotoxicosis — “hCG is capable of inducing hyperthyroidism when hCG levels are extreme and sustained for several weeks.”

title: Orofacial granulomatosis affecting lip and gingiva in a 15-year-old patient: A rare case report
case_prompt:
A 15-year-old girl presented with a 2-year history of painless, gradually progressive swelling of the upper and lower lips and of the gums.
She had no relevant medical history and no history of allergy to foods, drugs, or cosmetics; no asthma or eczema; and no family history of similar findings.
On extraoral examination, there was diffuse, soft, nontender swelling of both lips with normal temperature, with mild fissuring and a cobblestone appearance of the upper lip.
Intraoral examination revealed enlargement of the gingiva extending from tooth 13 to 23, involving marginal, interdental, and attached gingiva to the mucogingival junction; the gingiva was red, smooth, shiny, and lacked stippling. Probing revealed bleeding on probing, mild local deposits, and false pockets without clinical attachment loss.
Panoramic radiograph showed no alveolar bone loss in the maxillary or mandibular anterior region.
Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, differential, platelet count, and serum angiotensin-converting enzyme level, were within normal limits.
Chest radiograph was unremarkable, and the Mantoux test was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sarcoidosis was considered but excluded — “The sarcoidosis was excluded on the basis of chest radiograph and serum angiotensin converting enzyme levels.” 2. Angioedema was considered but excluded — “Absence of allergic history to cosmetic, food, drug, atopic eczema or asthma was the criteria for the exclusion of angioedema.”

title: Primary Retroperitoneal Malignant Melanoma with Involvement of Iliac Artery and Vein
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a two-week history of left lower extremity swelling and pain that improved slightly with leg elevation and NSAIDs. She denied shortness of breath, recent trauma, fever, chest pain, or unexplained weight loss. Her medical history included inflammatory polyarthritis (on methotrexate and adalimumab), hyperlipidemia, hypertension, asthma–COPD overlap, osteopenia, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation managed with warfarin (therapeutic INR). She had a three–pack-year smoking history, quitting 40 years prior.

On examination, the left lower extremity had pitting edema; peripheral pulses were strong, and the abdomen was soft and nondistended. Laboratory studies showed hypokalemia (2.8 mEq/L), which was repleted, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 35 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 30.1 mg/L. Doppler ultrasound of the left leg was negative for deep venous thrombosis. Computed tomography angiography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a large heterogeneous left pelvic retroperitoneal mass centered at the left iliac chain, measuring 8.7 × 6.9 × 8.5 cm, with probable compression or occlusion of the adjacent iliac vein. Based on imaging, the differential diagnosis included sarcoma, lymphoma, ovarian neoplasm, or germ cell tumor.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sarcoma — considered given the imaging findings of a large heterogeneous retroperitoneal mass “Based on the imaging study, the differential diagnosis included sarcoma, lymphoma, ovarian neoplasm, or germ cell tumor.” 2. Lymphoma — included for similar reasons on imaging “Based on the imaging study, the differential diagnosis included sarcoma, lymphoma, ovarian neoplasm, or germ cell tumor.” 3. Germ cell tumor — considered because of a pelvic mass in a postmenopausal woman “Based on the imaging study, the differential diagnosis included sarcoma, lymphoma, ovarian neoplasm, or germ cell tumor.”

title: Successful management of an idiopathic first bite syndrome: A case report and review
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old man presented with 3 months of lower-jaw and submandibular pain occurring only with eating. He described acute, sharp, stabbing pain (8/10) over the lower jaw and submandibular glands, more pronounced on the right, triggered by the first bite of each meal, subsiding over subsequent bites and resolving minutes after the meal. Episodes lasted less than 2 minutes with no associated hoarseness, facial sweating, rhinorrhea, odynophagia, or dysphagia. Symptoms were independent of time of day or position. He had no history of surgery, trauma, underlying disease, medications, or family history of similar symptoms. Vital signs were normal. 

Head and neck examination revealed dentures in place, no ulcers, deformities, masses, or lymphadenopathy. The pharynx, tonsils, soft palate, tongue, parotid glands, nose, temporomandibular joint, and neck were all normal on inspection and palpation. 

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, viral serologies, fasting blood sugar, thyroid-stimulating hormone, inflammatory and rheumatologic markers, electrolytes, and renal function, were all within normal limits. 

Contrast and noncontrast CT scans from the skull base to the neck showed no space-occupying lesions, masses, or other pathologic findings.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Glossopharyngeal neuralgia was excluded—“Regional tenderness was absent, ruling out glossopharyngeal neuralgia.” 2. Laryngeal and hypopharyngeal disease and Frey syndrome were considered unlikely—“In the absence of hoarseness, dysphagia, and odynophagia, laryngeal and hypopharynx diseases and Frey syndrome were considered unlikely diagnoses.” 3. Salivary-gland tumors and other space-occupying lesions were excluded—“the CT scan…did not reveal any findings suggestive of head and neck space occupying lesion, mass…or any other pathologic findings.”

title: Bisoprolol Successfully Improved the Intraventricular Pressure Gradient in a Patient with Midventricular Obstructive Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy with an Apex Aneurysm due to Apical Myocardial Damage
case_prompt:
An 80-year-old man presented with dizziness. MRI of the head showed a high-density area in the pons, and he was diagnosed with cerebral infarction and started on ozagrel sodium and clopidogrel; his dizziness improved. Continuous ECG monitoring documented frequent nonsustained ventricular tachycardia and 7,888 premature ventricular contractions per day, all asymptomatic. He was referred for cardiovascular evaluation. 

On examination, his heart rate was 71 beats/min, blood pressure 109/72 mmHg, lungs were clear, and a fourth heart sound was present. Laboratory tests showed a D-dimer level of 5.70 μg/mL and a B-type natriuretic peptide level of 525.7 pg/dL. A chest radiograph showed no pulmonary congestion. ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevation in leads V5 and V6 and T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4–V6. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy and an apical aneurysm containing an intracavitary mass. Doppler echocardiography showed a paradoxical jet flow from the apex toward the base during early diastole, with midventricular peak velocities of 2.00 m/s in systole and 3.67 m/s in diastole; a pseudonormal transmitral flow pattern (E/A ratio 1.12); and an E/Ea ratio of 19.2. Enhanced CT confirmed an apical aneurysm with a 21×14 mm thrombus. Coronary CT demonstrated no significant stenosis. Serum amyloid A protein, angiotensin-converting enzyme, and α-galactosidase A levels were all within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ischemic cardiac disease was considered but excluded — “Coronary CT showed that there was no significant stenosis in the left or right coronary arteries, suggesting that ischemic cardiac disease was not the cause of the apex aneurysm.” 2. Infiltrative or storage diseases were considered but excluded — “The serum levels of amyloid A protein, angiotensin-converting enzyme, and α-galactosidase A were normal, which suggested the absence of amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, and Fabry disease.”

title: Chronic Constrictive Pericarditis
case_prompt:
A 52-year-old man presented with a 5-year history of progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, pleuritic chest pain, abdominal distension, and peripheral edema. One year earlier, coronary angiography for chest pain was normal. Six months before admission, thoracentesis revealed a lymphocyte-predominant exudative right pleural effusion; cytology was nondiagnostic, and pleural biopsy was nondiagnostic.

Over the past 10 days, he noted worsening abdominal distension and pain. On examination, blood pressure was 110/80 mmHg, pulse 78/min, jugular venous pressure was markedly elevated, and heart sounds were muffled. Right lung base breath sounds were diminished. Abdominal examination showed mild hepatomegaly, ascites, and peripheral edema.

Laboratory tests were within normal limits. Analysis of ascitic fluid demonstrated protein 4.1 g/dL and serum–ascites albumin gradient 1.6 g/dL. Abdominal ultrasound showed congestive hepatomegaly, mild splenomegaly, ascites, and signs of portal hypertension; upper endoscopy revealed no esophageal varices, and viral hepatitis serologies were negative. Chest and abdominal CT demonstrated right pleural effusion, pericardial thickening with calcification, ascites, and inferior vena cava dilation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignancy was considered given the exudative pleural effusion but was less likely after “lymphocyte-dominant and nondiagnostic cytology.” 2. Restrictive cardiomyopathy was distinguished from constrictive pericarditis by the principle that “discordance between changes in right and left ventricular systolic pressures during respiration, known as ventricular interdependence, reliably distinguishes CP from these other conditions.”

title: Isolated laryngeal tuberculosis complex infection: expect the unexpected
case_prompt: A 59-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of sore throat, cough, hoarseness, and a 10-kg weight loss. She was a never-smoker, had undergone left quadrantectomy for ductal carcinoma, and had been treated for stage II pulmonary sarcoidosis with hydroxychloroquine (400 mg/day) and oral prednisone (15 mg/day) for 5 years. Physical examination was unremarkable, and laboratory studies revealed a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. Flexible laryngoscopy showed a centimetric polylobulated lesion on the free border of the epiglottis, inframillimetric lesions on the laryngeal surface of the epiglottis, and a millimetric ulcerated area on the right arytenoid mucosa; vocal-fold and arytenoid motility were preserved, and neck examination was normal. Contrast-enhanced MRI of the neck demonstrated a small low-enhanced lesion of the epiglottic border without other laryngeal enhancements. Whole-body PET-CT revealed multiple ground-glass lung masses and bilateral nodules increased from prior imaging of known sarcoidosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Laryngeal carcinoma was suspected because of exophytic, multifocal lesions mimicking malignancy—“Due to the simultaneous involvement of different laryngeal subsites, in the suspicion of a malignancy, a contrast-enhanced MRI of the neck and a total body PET-CT scan were performed…” 2. Syphilis was included among granulomatous differential diagnoses—“…there are various granulomatous chronic diseases affecting the larynx to be considered, such as syphilis, sarcoidosis, Wegener granulomatosis and actinomicosis…” 3. Wegener granulomatosis was considered as another granulomatous laryngeal disease—“…such as syphilis, sarcoidosis, Wegener granulomatosis and actinomicosis…” 4. Actinomicosis was included among possible chronic granulomatous infections—“…such as syphilis, sarcoidosis, Wegener granulomatosis and actinomicosis…”

title: Cruciate Paralysis in a 20- year -old Male with an Undisplaced Type III Odontoid Fracture
case_prompt: A 20-year-old man fell and struck the back of his head. He reported severe neck and shoulder pain with difficulty raising both arms and in gripping objects. He was alert (GCS 15), with normal vital signs and no signs of head injury or respiratory distress. On cervical palpation, there was tenderness at the upper cervical spine and marked paraspinal muscle spasm. Neurologically, he had weakness of both arms (right worse than left) in the C5–T1 distribution with brisk reflexes but no sensory deficits. The lower limbs had brisk reflexes but normal strength. Cranial nerves were intact. An open-mouth odontoid radiograph and cervical CT scan showed an undisplaced type III odontoid fracture.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute central cord syndrome was considered because of the selective upper-limb weakness after neck trauma, but was less likely given the association with odontoid fractures: “Acute CCS is rarely associated with injury to the atlanto–axial complex.” 2. Cruciate paralysis was considered because it presents with arm paralysis and minimal or absent lower-limb involvement in the setting of upper cervical injury, and is often linked to axis fractures: “Cruciate paralysis has been closely associated with fractures of axis and/or atlas.”

title: Is it Lucio Phenomenon or Necrotic Erythema Nodosum Leprosum?
case_prompt:
A 38-year-old male cook presented with a 2-day history of blistering on both hands and feet. One week earlier, he had been admitted for epistaxis and nasopharyngeal myiasis and treated with gentamicin, crystalline penicillin, metronidazole, and turpentine oil instillations. Two days after starting antibiotics, he developed painless hemorrhagic edema and bullae over the dorsum of the left hand, which rapidly involved the right hand, both feet, and both earlobes. He denied fever, arthralgia, or other constitutional symptoms but reported a 1-year history of recurrent blistering of his hands and feet.

On examination:
• Multiple 1–2 cm hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding purpuric macules on the dorsa of both hands, feet, toes, and ears, which ruptured within 2 days to form deep irregular ulcers with angulated margins.  
• Diffuse facial infiltration with saddle-nose deformity; shiny infiltrated skin on the back with intact sensation.  
• Symmetrical, non-tender thickening of peripheral nerves; glove-and-stocking anesthesia distally.  
• Ulnar claw deformities bilaterally, resorption of the right little finger, pseudo-ainhum of the right fourth toe, and amputation of the left second toe.  
• Cranial nerves intact.

Investigations:
• Slit-skin smear from earlobes: globi present; bacteriological index 4+.  
• Hemogram: neutrophilic leukocytosis; ESR 56 mm in the first hour.  
• Liver and renal function tests: normal.  
• Urine porphyrins: negative.  
• Serologic tests: VDRL, HIV ELISA, and hepatitis B surface antigen negative.  
• Autoantibodies: serum cryoglobulins, rheumatoid factor (RF), and antinuclear antibody (ANA) negative.  
• Radiographs of the hands and feet: no evidence of osteitis or osteomyelitis.

A provisional diagnosis of diffuse non-nodular lepromatous leprosy with grade 2 deformities was made, and the differential diagnosis included Lucio phenomenon, drug-induced pseudoporphyria, drug-induced vasculitis, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, and necrotic erythema nodosum leprosum.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Drug-induced pseudoporphyria was considered but excluded because “Urine examination for porphyrins was negative.” 2. Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis was considered but excluded because “Serum cryoglobulins ... were negative.” 3. Drug-induced vasculitis was considered but made unlikely because “serum ... antinuclear antibody (ANA) were negative.” 4. Lucio phenomenon was deemed most probable given that “the classical angular infarcts and hemorrhagic blisters evolving into ulcers with angulated margins” occurred without fever or constitutional symptoms.

title: COVID-19 in Coincidence with Transient Distal Renal Tubular Acidosis in an Infant
case_prompt:
A 32-day-old girl was brought with a 4-day history of watery diarrhea (12–13 nonbloody, nonmucoid stools in 24 hours) and fever. There was no vomiting, poor feeding, or respiratory symptoms. She had contact with a COVID-19–positive case 4 weeks earlier. She was born at term, breastfed, and had normal growth parameters.

On admission she appeared toxic, tachypneic, and irritable. Temperature was 38.4°C, pulse 140/min, respiratory rate 30/min, and blood pressure 90/60 mmHg. Signs of dehydration included tenting skin, sunken eyes, and a soft fontanelle. Neurologic exam and development were normal.

Initial labs showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated ESR, CRP, ammonia, and D-dimer. A venous blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.22, HCO3– 11.5 mEq/L). Blood glucose and liver tests were normal. She received IV fluids and sodium bicarbonate.

On hospital day 2, repeat venous blood gas showed persistent metabolic acidosis (pH 7.26, HCO3– 11.5 mEq/L) with a normal anion gap (11 mEq/L). Chest CT, echocardiography, and an abdominopelvic ultrasound were unremarkable. Urine and stool cultures were negative; blood culture grew Klebsiella pneumoniae. SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR was positive, and COVID-19 IgM was elevated.

By day 4, diarrhea had improved but a venous blood gas still showed hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (pH 7.21, HCO3– 15 mEq/L, Cl– 115 mEq/L). Urine pH was 6.5 with a sodium of 105 mEq/L, specific gravity 1.012, and a positive urine anion gap. Serum potassium was 3 mEq/L, and ammonia was 235 mg/dL.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Diarrhea- and sepsis-induced acidosis considered — “metabolic acidosis could be justified due to the illness course and sepsis, but persistent hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, despite dehydration correction with typical findings…, encouraged us to diagnose dRTA.” 2. Liver dysfunction excluded — “normal liver enzymes, albumin, serum bilirubin, and partial thrombin time reasonably excluded liver dysfunction.”

title: Clinical Relevance of Pharmacogenetics in Serotonin Syndrome
case_prompt:
A 74-year-old man was brought to the emergency department with generalized shaking, tremors, clonus, and altered level of consciousness. He had been seen normal nine hours earlier by his spouse. His medical history included type 2 diabetes and remote traumatic brain injury (subdural frontal and temporal hemorrhage) from a fall 3 years ago; he had no history of seizures.  

Home medications were divalproex sodium 250 mg twice daily, quetiapine 25 mg twice daily, venlafaxine 37.5 mg twice daily, trazodone 50 mg as needed, donepezil 10 mg daily, insulin glargine 15 U daily, melatonin 3 mg nightly, magnesium oxide 400 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, pravastatin 20 mg nightly, and tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly.  

On arrival, his temperature was 39.7 °C, heart rate 120 beats/min, blood pressure 188/84 mmHg, and respiratory rate 23 breaths/min. Laboratory studies showed an elevated creatine kinase level of 6.34 mg/dL and a serum calcium level of 6.8 mg/dL. Examination revealed tremors, 3+ hyperreflexia in the lower limbs, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3. He was intubated and transferred to the medical ICU. His hyperthermia persisted despite antipyretics and cooling blankets.  

“Several etiologies for encephalopathy—hepatic, endocrine, metabolic, Wernicke's, seizures—were ruled out through laboratory, diagnostic, and microbiological testing.” “Viral and bacterial encephalopathy remained in the differential diagnosis, but was of low suspicion due to an unremarkable cerebrospinal fluid analysis.” Although he was at risk for seizures from his prior brain injury, “the neurology team conveyed a low level of suspicion for seizures due to a negative electroencephalogram (EEG) and a lack of tonic or clonic activity.”
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Other metabolic and toxic encephalopathies were excluded — “Several etiologies for encephalopathy—hepatic, endocrine, metabolic, Wernicke's, seizures—were ruled out through laboratory, diagnostic, and microbiological testing.” 2. Viral and bacterial encephalitis were considered but unlikely — “Viral and bacterial encephalopathy remained in the differential diagnosis, but was of low suspicion due to an unremarkable cerebrospinal fluid analysis.” 3. Seizure disorder was considered but excluded — “Although the patient was at risk for seizure disorder due to his history of TBI, the neurology team conveyed a low level of suspicion for seizures due to a negative electroencephalogram (EEG) and a lack of tonic or clonic activity.” 4. Serotonin syndrome was favored based on chronic serotonergic therapy — “Serotonin syndrome was of high suspicion as evidenced by the patient's home medication list that included divalproex sodium, venlafaxine, and trazodone.”

title: A case report of primary cardiac intimal sarcoma presenting with atrial fibrillation and a left atrial mass
case_prompt:
A 37-year-old woman was referred for management of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Her only prior surgery was a remote cholecystectomy, and her cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. An attempted pulmonary vein isolation ablation was aborted because of an extremely thick interatrial septum, and her atrial fibrillation was managed medically with propafenone.

Transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography revealed a sheet-like, clot-like mass lining almost the entire left atrium and extending into the left atrial appendage. A cardiac magnetic resonance study showed a sessile, sheet-like mass adherent to the septal side of the left atrial cavity, measuring 4.7 × 1.3 cm; it was isointense to myocardium on pre-contrast T1, mildly hyperintense on T2, and hyperintense on Short-T1 Inversion Recovery. These features were interpreted as most consistent with an atrial myxoma.

The patient underwent open surgical excision of the mass. Intraoperatively, the lesion was very fibrotic and densely adherent to the left atrial wall, extending toward the mitral leaflets and into the pulmonary vein orifices. A frozen section showed dense fibrous tissue with areas of fibrin undergoing organization, chronic inflammation, and reactive‐appearing stromal cells. No myxoma cells were identified, and the tissue was thought to represent an organizing thrombus.

Sixteen months later, she presented with severe back pain. Spinal MRI revealed aggressive lytic pathologic fractures of the T10 and L1 vertebral bodies with an associated paravertebral soft-tissue mass. Vertebral body biopsies demonstrated a malignant spindle cell tumour suggestive of smooth muscle differentiation; the tumour cells were positive for desmin and smooth muscle actin. Mammography, Papanicolaou testing, and CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis did not reveal a primary malignancy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Atrial myxoma was the leading consideration on imaging — “the conclusion was that the mass was most in keeping with an atrial myxoma.” 2. Organizing thrombus was favored intraoperatively and on frozen section — “revealed dense fibrous tissue, with areas of fibrin undergoing organization.” 3. Leiomyosarcoma was considered after vertebral biopsy given smooth muscle marker positivity — “the tumour cells were positive for muscle markers, desmin, and smooth muscle actin (SMA), pointing towards a leiomyosarcoma.”

title: Red flags for the differential diagnosis of granulomatous mastitis: a case report
case_prompt:
A 41-year-old G1P1 Hispanic woman presented with 3 months of gradually worsening left breast pain, swelling, chills, night sweats, skin discoloration, and a self-detected mass. Five years earlier she had a similar left-breast episode that resolved spontaneously. Three months before presentation, she was diagnosed elsewhere with mastitis and treated with meloxicam and trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole. She is on norgestimate/ethinyl estradiol and has no tobacco, alcohol, or family history of cancer.

On examination, vital signs were normal. The left breast showed a nodular mass in the outer quadrants with brownish discoloration and edema, but no nipple discharge, peau d’orange, or regional lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies (CBC, LFTs, renal function, urinalysis, serologies, lipid panel) were normal.

A 3D mammogram and targeted ultrasound were reported as BI-RADS 5, highly suspicious for malignancy. A percutaneous needle biopsy showed no invasive carcinoma but revealed fibrohistiocytic and florid inflammatory reactions with multinucleated giant cells. One week later she developed an abscess at the biopsy site; incision and drainage were performed, cultures were negative, and tuberculosis testing was negative. She remained on TMP–SMX and NSAIDs.

An incisional (wedge and Tru-Cut) biopsy of the breast mass showed granulomatous inflammation of the lobules with well-formed granulomas and multinucleated giant cells. Periodic acid–Schiff and Grocott’s methenamine silver stains were negative for fungi, a special acid-fast stain showed no organisms, and Gram stain showed no bacteria.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial mastitis and abscess were considered but unlikely given sterile cultures and poor response to antibiotics — “cultures were negative… she was kept on the antibiotic treatment regimen” and “antibiotic therapy usually fails” in sterile granulomatous disease. 2. Tuberculous mastitis was excluded by negative testing and stains — “Diagnostic testing for tuberculosis (TB) was negative” and “special stain for Acid-Fast Bacilli revealed no organisms.” 3. Periductal mastitis was less likely given lobulocentric granulomas rather than ductal involvement and absence of ductal discharge or infection.

title:
Fat Embolism Syndrome in Sickle Cell β-Thalassemia Patient With
Osteonecrosis: An Uncommon Presentation in a Young Adult
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 34-year-old African American man with hypertension and known sickle cell β-thalassemia presented with 2 days of diffuse body aches that worsened in his lower extremities to the point of being unable to get out of bed. He denied fever, chest pain, cough, abdominal pain, focal neurologic deficits, or recent trauma. One month earlier, he had MRI evidence of multiple bone infarcts in the pelvis and femur consistent with avascular necrosis, and hemoglobin electrophoresis at that time confirmed sickle cell β-thalassemia.

On admission he was afebrile, pulse 124/min, blood pressure 215/144 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. He was diaphoretic and mildly agitated; lung and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed WBC 10.8 ×10^3/µL, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelet count 61 000/µL, sodium 134 mEq/L, bicarbonate 17 mEq/L, AST 733 U/L, ALT 221 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 211 U/L, and total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL. Urinalysis and chest radiograph were unremarkable. 

On hospital day 2, he became obtunded and confused with a temperature of 40 °C, tachycardia, and respiratory rate of 35–40/min. Noncontrast head CT showed no hemorrhage but possible cerebral edema. Brain MRI revealed extensive punctate diffusion-restricted lesions in the basal ganglia, thalami, and bilateral white matter. Chest, abdomen, and pelvis CT showed a right lower lobe infiltrate. He developed acute respiratory failure and was intubated.

Hematology was consulted for worsening thrombocytopenia. ANA, ANCA, lupus anticoagulant, HIV, and hepatitis panels were negative. ADAMTS13 activity was 42% (normal >61%). Peripheral smear showed thrombocytopenia with leukocytosis and sickled cells; no schistocytes were seen. PT/INR and PTT were elevated; fibrinogen was normal. A CT angiogram of the head and neck was subsequently obtained.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. DIC considered and excluded — “Due to normal fibrinogen and the absence of schistocytes, disseminated intravascular coagulation was felt to be less likely.” 2. Ischemic hepatitis considered and excluded — “Blood pressures were normal to high negating ischemic hepatitis.” 3. Also, hemolysis usually does not cause such high transaminitis.” 4. Fat embolism syndrome supported by multiorgan involvement, acute bone marrow necrosis context, and MRI pattern of embolic microinfarcts — “Based on the presentation, with ongoing sickle cell thalassemia crisis, osteonecrosis, thrombocytopenia, tachypnea, and MRI pattern of infarctions, it was believed that his presentation was likely due to FES.”

title: Neurosyphilis Presenting as Asymptomatic Optic Perineuritis
case_prompt:
A 41-year-old man was referred for blurred optic disc margins and possible papilledema noted by his optometrist. He was visually asymptomatic. One month earlier, he had reactive but unexplained groin lymphadenopathy. He denied any prior sexually transmitted infections, genital lesions, penile discharge, or rash.

On examination, visual acuity was 20/25+ in the right eye and 20/20– in the left. Color vision was normal (11/11 OU). There was a trace afferent pupillary defect in the left eye. Intraocular pressures were 12 mm Hg OD and 18 mm Hg OS. Funduscopic examination revealed asymmetric disc edema, worse in the left eye. Optical coherence tomography showed an average retinal nerve fiber layer thickness of 188 µm OS with concentric elevation and 118 µm OD with inferonasal elevation.

Brain MRI and MR venography with and without contrast showed no mass lesions or venous occlusion. Lumbar puncture revealed an opening pressure of 18 cm H₂O, normal protein and glucose levels, and 72 white blood cells/mm³ with a lymphocytic predominance.

Two weeks later, the patient developed headaches and eye pain, especially when bending forward. Examination showed worsening disc edema in the right eye and enlargement of the blind spot on visual field testing. Orbital MRI demonstrated increased optic nerve sheath fluid posterior to the globes and contrast enhancement of the optic nerve sheaths, findings consistent with optic perineuritis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Papilledema was considered but excluded because “However, the normal CSF pressure and elevated CSF WBC count, along with the orbit MRI findings, suggested perineuritis.” 2. An intracranial mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis was excluded since “Brain MRI and MRV with and without contrast were unremarkable, without mass lesions or venous occlusion.” 3. Optic neuritis was distinguished from perineuritis because “Unlike optic neuritis, the optic nerve usually does not show prominent gadolinium enhancement.” 4. Optic nerve sheath meningioma was considered in the differential given that “optic perineuritis is a relatively uncommon inflammatory condition which may be mistaken for optic neuritis or optic nerve sheath meningioma.” 5. Idiopathic optic perineuritis was a possibility prior to identifying a cause, as “The condition is often idiopathic,” prompting evaluation for secondary etiologies.

title: Pediatric Porcelain Aorta Secondary to Gaucher Disease Type 3C With Successful Aortic Replacement Surgery
case_prompt:
A 13-year-old Mexican girl presented with a 6-month history of marked dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea. On examination she was tachycardic and tachypneic, with a suprasternal thrill; a right upper sternal, midsystolic grade V/VI murmur; and an apical, pansystolic grade III/VI murmur. She had significant hepatomegaly and splenomegaly.
