Her history was notable for hepatosplenomegaly and mildly impaired eye movements detected at age 2, but no comprehensive workup was performed; historical laboratory records showed anemia and thrombocytopenia.  

An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy. A chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed severe mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, an aortic valve Doppler mean gradient of 98 mm Hg, limited visualization of the aortic arch, and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35%. Cardiac computed tomography showed calcification of the aortic and mitral valves, concentric calcification of the aortic root, and ostial calcification of the coronary arteries as well as the brachiocephalic, left carotid, and left subclavian arteries. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed biventricular systolic dysfunction and aortic stenosis.  

Results of erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serum C-reactive protein, and autoantibody testing were normal. Ophthalmologic assessment demonstrated oculomotor apraxia.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Takayasu arteritis was initially suspected but then questioned — “first suspicion was Takayasu arteritis; however, the atypical course compelled us to rule out other disorders.” 2. Autoimmune aortitis (systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis) was excluded — “Systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis were also ruled out.”

title: An Autopsy Case of Antibody-negative Immune-mediated Necrotizing Myopathy with Severe Cardiac Involvement
case_prompt:
A 41‐year‐old man presented with progressive lower leg edema and dyspnea at rest. He reported two years of exertional dyspnea on light exercise but denied subjective limb weakness. His medical history was unremarkable, with no statin use or family history of cardiac or neuromuscular disease.

On examination, he had jugular venous distention and bilateral pitting edema. Lung auscultation revealed decreased breath sounds without crackles. Cardiac auscultation was unremarkable. He demonstrated proximal limb weakness (manual muscle testing score 3/5) and an unsteady gait.

Laboratory studies showed a serum creatine kinase level of 4409 U/L, CK-MB of 229 U/L (normal <12 U/L), brain natriuretic peptide 896.6 pg/mL (normal <18.4 pg/mL), and cardiac troponin I 240 pg/mL (cut-off 26.2 pg/mL). Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 64%), pulmonary congestion, and pleural effusion. Electrocardiography demonstrated sinus rhythm at 133 beats per minute, poor R-wave progression, and T-wave inversions in leads II, III, and aVF.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a dilated left ventricle (end-diastolic volume 120 mL, end-systolic volume 93 mL), diffuse hypokinesis, an ejection fraction of 20%, and moderate tricuspid and mild mitral regurgitation without regional wall motion abnormalities. He was diagnosed with acute heart failure, received intensive care (including catecholamines, intubation, and intra-aortic balloon pumping), and was treated with ACE inhibitors, β‐blockers, and amiodarone. He was discharged after 136 days, but his low ejection fraction and elevated CK, BNP, and troponin levels persisted, and his proximal muscle weakness progressed.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated endocardial late gadolinium enhancement in the anterolateral and inferior walls, with spotty transmural enhancement in the inferior wall. Coronary angiography revealed only moderate stenosis of the left anterior descending artery. Muscle MRI of the arms showed high-intensity signals in the biceps and triceps on short-inversion-time inversion recovery sequences.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ischemic cardiomyopathy was considered but angiography revealed only moderate stenosis — “We suspected ischemic cardiomyopathy as a cause of heart failure, but coronary angiography showed only moderate stenosis in the left anterior descending artery.”

title: Hepatocellular carcinoma with biliary and neuroendocrine differentiation: A case report
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old woman presented with several weeks of peri-umbilical and epigastric discomfort, anorexia, 10-pound unintentional weight loss, and night sweats. Her medical history included treated hypothyroidism, hypertension, chronic hepatitis C infection (genotype 1a), and a low-grade rectal neuroendocrine tumor resected in 2012 with negative re-excision margins in 2017. Physical examination revealed mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without palpable masses or hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed elevated serum chromogranin A at 450 ng/mL (normal < 93 ng/mL), α-fetoprotein (AFP) of 2198 ng/mL (normal 10–20 ng/mL), and AFP-L3% at 86.6%. Liver enzymes were mildly elevated (AST 72 U/L, ALT 86 U/L). 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed two hepatic lesions: a 2.2 × 2.1 cm peripherally enhancing mass with central hypodensity in segment 4, and a 1.4 × 1.0 cm low-density lesion in segment 7, with prominent portacaval lymph nodes. MRI with gadolinium confirmed a 2.7 × 2.5 × 2.3 cm T2-hyperintense lesion in segment 4 with peripheral rim arterial phase enhancement and persistent venous phase enhancement; the smaller lesion was too small for enhancement characterization. FDG-PET showed avidity in the segment 4 mass (SUV 6.6) only. Gallium-68 dotatate PET showed no uptake in the liver or lymph nodes. 

A CT-guided core biopsy of the segment 4 lesion demonstrated a high-grade malignant tumor with neuroendocrine morphology and synaptophysin positivity; Ki-67 index was 30%. Non-cirrhotic liver parenchyma was confirmed on biopsy. No extrahepatic disease was found on chest CT. The main differential diagnoses considered at this point were metastatic neuroendocrine tumor versus a primary hepatic malignancy, most likely hepatocellular carcinoma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Metastatic neuroendocrine tumor — “Given our patient’s history of a rectal carcinoid, this scan was performed in the context of detecting metastatic disease to the liver ... however, due to the possibility of a false negative result, a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor was not excluded.” 2. Primary hepatocellular carcinoma — “An increased serum AFP and HCV positivity raised the differential diagnosis of a primary HCC.” 3. Cholangiocarcinoma — “CC was not strongly considered pre-operatively, given the biopsy finding, imaging features, and tumor markers.”

title: Isolated conjunctival granuloma as a first manifestation of Parinaud's oculoglandular syndrome: A case report
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_prompt: A 67-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a 2-week history of a non-traumatic, painless, unilateral red eye and chemosis. On slit-lamp examination, there was a 7 mm × 4 mm conjunctival granuloma attached to the sclera with central epithelial necrosis; fundoscopy was normal. Ultrasound biomicroscopy with a 50 MHz transducer showed a high-reflectivity echo embedded in the conjunctiva, episclera, and superficial sclera, surrounded by a low-reflective granulomatous mass, and multiple pseudocysts. Topical gatifloxacin and prednisolone acetate were initiated. Etiological work-up revealed a mild leukocytosis; interferon gamma-release assay (QuantiFERON-TB®) and chest computed tomography were normal; treponemal and non-treponemal tests were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Tuberculosis was considered because necrosis in conjunctival granulomas often suggests TB — “interferon gamma-release assay (QuantiFERON-TB®, Cellestis, Carnegie, Australia), chest computed tomography (CT) … were normal.” 2. Local causes such as foreign bodies were considered but excluded by history and exam — “there were no factors, neither in the patient's history nor in the physical examination, that could explain local causes of conjunctival granuloma.”

title: An odontogenic cutaneous sinus tract in an unusual site with multiple misdiagnoses
case_prompt: A 21-year-old woman presented with a 1-cm tender, red, fluctuant nodule on her right cheek that had persisted for approximately 1 year. She had been treated by a dermatologist with oral azithromycin and intralesional triamcinolone without improvement. Another physician diagnosed an epidermoid cyst and recommended surgical excision, which she declined. Under suspicion of a parotid gland fistula, ultrasound imaging was requested. During a routine dental examination, a panoramic radiograph showed mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus adjacent to the palatal root of the right maxillary first molar. That tooth had received poor-quality endodontic treatment 4 years earlier, and the patient reported no history of abscess, pain, or tenderness since. On examination, a firm, cord-like tract was palpable from the mucogingival junction of that tooth, extending through the cheek toward the facial nodule.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acne lesion considered — “severe acne lesions resembling as observed in the present study, usually do not occur as a single lesion; whilst they are multiple nodulocystic lesions that appear mainly on the facial skin…”

title: An unusual presentation of acute abdomen: infarcted peritoneal cyst-a probable asbestos-related benign cystic mesothelioma
case_prompt:
A 42-year-old man presented with acute right lower abdominal pain of two days’ duration, associated with non-bilious vomiting, dysuria, and fever. The pain was aggravated by movement. He denied prior abdominal trauma or surgery and had no history of weight loss, diabetes, hypertension, tuberculosis, or pancreatitis. He worked in construction for 15 years, including daily handling of asbestos for more than 10 years.  
On examination, temperature was 39.0°C, heart rate 120 beats per minute, and blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg. There was distension and guarding in the right iliac and umbilical regions. Chest radiography and electrocardiography were normal. Sputum was negative for acid-fast bacilli.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, white-cell count 6400 per µL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 16 IU/L, aspartate aminotransferase 51 IU/L, lipase 149 IU/L, amylase 49 IU/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, glucose 146 mg/dL, and PaO2 54 mm Hg. Urine microscopy revealed numerous red blood cells.  
Abdominal ultrasonography demonstrated a well-defined intra-abdominal cyst with clear internal echoes in the pelvis, compressing and displacing the urinary bladder to the left; there was no free fluid. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed a solitary, well-defined intraperitoneal cyst in the pelvis containing low-attenuation fluid and surrounded by a 1-cm nonenhancing wall; it compressed the urinary bladder without communication to bowel or bladder. The pancreas and other abdominal organs were normal, and there was no ascites.  
An infected intra-abdominal cyst was diagnosed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lymphatic cyst — “Lymphatic cysts are rarely solitary, may have septa, could insinuate between tissues precluding a complete excision, may have milky chylous fluid or debris, imaging may show enhancement of the cyst wall and histopathology would show clear spaces with lymphatic elements.” 2. Pseudocyst — “As there was no evidence of pancreatitis, trauma or prior abdominal surgery, and the enzymes were in the normal range, and the imaging and visual examination were normal; a pseudocyst can also be ruled out.” 3. Benign cystic peritoneal mesothelioma — “Our case does not fit into any of the above categories described. Hence, it may be a case of a benign cystic peritoneal mesothelioma (BCPM), with asbestos as a probable cause.”

title: An Uncommon Cause of Hip Pain Originating From the Iliopsoas Muscle: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 43-year-old man presented with a 1-year history of left hip pain, worse with exercise and hip flexion, which had intensified over the past week. He also reported 2 months of malaise, anorexia, and a 6-kg weight loss in the previous month. He denied trauma and had no comorbidities.

On examination, flexion of the left hip beyond 40°—especially against resistance—elicited pain; there was no motor weakness, but hip flexion provoked numbness in the left lower extremity. The FABER test was positive, and gait was normal.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, electrolytes, and liver and renal function tests, were all within normal limits.

A pelvic radiograph showed no acute osseous abnormalities. Thoracoabdominal CT revealed a 64×29-mm noninvasive mass posterior to the psoas muscle containing amorphous calcifications. On follow-up pelvic radiograph, calcifications within the psoas compartment were confirmed. Abdominopelvic MRI demonstrated a 72×45×50-mm mass arising from the left iliopsoas muscle extending along fat planes; it was T1 hypointense in areas of calcification, T2 hyperintense, lobulated with sharp margins, and showed marked postcontrast enhancement.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of psoas abscess was low given the absence of laboratory evidence of infection — “the patient's laboratory findings did not suggest an infectious disease such as an abscess.” 2. Benign schwannoma was considered due to mass effect in the psoas compartment — “literature review revealed case reports suggesting psoas abscess, but which emerged as benign schwannoma after drainage and biopsy.” 3. A broad category of retroperitoneal tumors was entertained because of nonspecific imaging features — “Retroperitoneal tumors are difficult to diagnose because of the variations in pathology and the lack of specific radiological images.” 4. Various other malignancies were included in the differential of soft tissue masses — “Rhabdomyosarcoma must be differentiated from other tumors, especially Wilms tumor, Ewing sarcoma, neuroblastoma, liposarcoma, pheochromocytoma, osteosarcoma, acute myelocytic leukemia, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.” 5. Distinguishing among soft tissue sarcoma subtypes required histologic confirmation — “Although the differential diagnosis of soft tissue sarcomas can be made radiologically among the diseases mentioned above, it is difficult to distinguish among soft tissue sarcomas themselves. The gold standard of diagnosis is usually tissue biopsy.”

title: Diabetic Muscle Infarction of the Tibialis Anterior and Extensor Hallucis Longus Muscles Mimicking the Malignant Soft-Tissue Tumor
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old man with a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with oral hypoglycemic agents was referred for evaluation of a presumed soft-tissue tumor in his lower left leg. He had no known diabetic nephropathy, retinopathy, or sensory disturbances. He first noted a painless, palpable mass on the anterior aspect of his left lower leg 6 months earlier; it gradually enlarged and developed large blisters. 

On physical examination, there was swelling over the anterior lower left leg without tenderness. Laboratory tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 0.05 mg/L, a white-cell count of 4,580/mm3, a creatine kinase level of 164 IU/L, and a casual blood glucose level of 298 mg/dL. 

A radiograph of the lower left leg showed moderate soft-tissue swelling without bony abnormalities or soft-tissue calcifications. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a large mass (maximum diameter, 7.2 cm; length, 21.0 cm) with cystic areas within the tibialis anterior and extensor hallucis longus muscles; the mass and cysts did not enhance, while the anterior tibialis artery was well visualized. 

MRI demonstrated heterogeneous signal intensity of the mass on T1- and T2-weighted images and homogeneous signal of the cystic areas on both sequences; there was no perifascial, intramuscular, or subcutaneous edema. Fat-suppressed gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted images showed slight heterogeneous enhancement with focal hypointense nonenhancing areas. The ankle–brachial index was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignant soft-tissue tumor considered because the lesion was a painless, enlarging mass with blisters — “According to the clinical course and physical findings, the lesion simulated a malignant soft-tissue tumor more closely than a muscle infarction.” 2. Calcific myonecrosis excluded due to absence of calcifications on imaging — “Radiographs typically show a fusiform mass with peripherally oriented plaque-like amorphous calcifications. In our patient, the calcification was not seen by radiographs and CT, and so, we ruled out this disease.” 3. Cellulitis ruled out because there was muscle involvement rather than isolated subcutaneous swelling — “In cellulitis, subcutaneous swelling is seen but with no muscle involvement.”

title: A rare variant of mullerian agenesis: a case report and review of the literature
case_prompt: A 25-year-old Indian woman presented with sudden severe lower abdominal pain for 2 days. She denied fever, vomiting, bowel, or urinary symptoms. She reported onset of breast development at age 11 and pubic hair development at age 12 but has never menstruated. Since age 13 she has experienced infrequent dull cramping abdominal pain that became cyclic at 45–50-day intervals by age 16, managed with occasional analgesics. She is unmarried, sexually inactive, with unremarkable medical, surgical, and family histories. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable, with normal height, weight, and body habitus; Tanner stage 4 breasts and pubic hair; and mild right lower quadrant tenderness without guarding or rigidity. Pelvic ultrasound showed blood within the right uterine horn and multiple small ovarian cysts. Pelvic MRI without contrast demonstrated a bicornuate (likely unicollis) uterus with heterogeneous myometrial signal suggestive of adenomyosis in both horns, a single angulated cervix with suboptimal visualization of the uterocervical junction, a blind upper half of the vagina, and polycystic-appearing ovaries with multiple peripheral follicles and thickened stroma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Gonadal dysgenesis considered but unlikely because well-developed secondary sexual characteristics are present — “secondary sexual characteristics are not well developed owing to a lack of estrogen production by dysgenetic (streak) ovaries.”

title: Using diffuse weighted image and apparent diffusion coefficient in MRI for diagnosis of posterior ischemic optic neuropathy in a young male: a case report and literature review
case_prompt: A 30-year-old man presented with sudden onset of blurred vision and left-eye pain for 1 week. His medical history was notable for chronic renal failure attributed to childhood glomerulonephritis and hypertension, managed with furosemide, carvedilol, and nifedipine. He was highly myopic (–8.50 D OU) and on examination had visual acuity of 20/20 OD and counting-fingers OS. Color testing was 21/21 plates OD and 0/21 OS, and there was a strongly positive relative afferent pupillary defect in the left eye. Intraocular pressures were normal, and slit-lamp and fundus examinations were unremarkable bilaterally. Visual evoked potential showed normal response OD and a delayed response with small amplitude OS. Humphrey visual field testing showed general depression OS. He was admitted and treated with intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy (250 mg every 6 hours for 3 days) for presumed demyelinating optic neuritis. During admission, his blood pressure remained elevated (up to 190/127 mmHg) despite multiple antihypertensive agents. Laboratory studies showed a serum creatinine of 3.27 mg/dL, anemia, and hyperlipidemia (total cholesterol 259 mg/dL, LDL 170 mg/dL). Fluorescein angiography was deferred because of renal dysfunction. Brain and orbital MRI demonstrated a focal area of high signal intensity in the left optic nerve on diffusion-weighted imaging, with corresponding hypointensity on the apparent diffusion coefficient map. Additional findings included multiple periventricular white-matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR and scattered hypointense foci on gradient-echo sequences consistent with old microhemorrhages.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered inflammatory demyelinating optic neuritis because of acute unilateral vision loss, pain, and positive RAPD — “Acute ON due to inflammatory demyelination process is the most common optic neuropathy affecting young adults. It is characterized by acute unilateral vision loss, positive RAPD and pain with eye movement.” 2. Distinguished from demyelinating ON by MRI diffusion patterns: ischemia shows increased DWI and decreased ADC, whereas inflammation shows increased ADC — “Conversely, inflammatory demyelinating ON, often shows an increased value in ADC and DWI sequence compared to PION due to axonal disruption, which may differentiate these two diseases at an acute stage.” 3. Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy was excluded because the optic disc was normal without edema — “Anterior ION involves optic nerve head and presents with disc edema while PION involves retrobulbar optic nerve and shows normal optic disc.” 4. Other causes of optic neuropathy such as toxic optic neuropathy, compressive optic neuropathy, and macular or retinal lesions were considered and excluded by normal fundus examination and imaging — “The differential diagnosis of PION includes retrobulbar type ON, toxic optic neuropathy, compressive optic neuropathy and macular or retinal lesions.”

title: Epicardial cavernous haemangioma; A case report of a unique incidental finding
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old Mediterranean man presented with a 9-month history of intermittent palpitations and exertional dyspnea classified as New York Heart Association Class II. He had no cardiovascular risk factors, no family history of cancer or heart disease, and no other comorbidities. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits, heart sounds were unremarkable, and there were no skin lesions. A 12-lead ECG showed normal sinus rhythm at 62 bpm, with normal PR and QRS intervals.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a hyperechoic epicardial mass measuring 6 × 4 cm in the region of the inferior vena cava extending toward the right atrium and coronary sinus. Contrast-enhanced CT angiography demonstrated a hypodense heterogeneous mass measuring 7.5 × 6 × 5 cm, and it was suggested to represent a paraganglionic mass vascularized by the distal right coronary artery. Coronary catheterization could not exclude feeding vessels from the right coronary artery.

A conservative watch-and-wait approach was initially adopted. Six months later, cardiac MRI with contrast showed a prolonged T1 relaxation time of approximately 1751 ms (native myocardium ∼1240 ms; left ventricular blood 1774 ms), suggesting similarity to blood signal. T1 mapping showed isointensity to myocardium, T2 mapping showed a homogeneous hyperintense mass, and late gadolinium enhancement was heterogeneous. There was no significant change in size.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraganglionic mass considered because of vascular supply — “it was suggested to be a paraganglionic mass that was vascularized by the distal right coronary artery (RCA), which could not be ruled out during coronary catheterization.” 2. Malignant angiosarcoma considered due to typical right-atrium location and invasiveness — “The most important differential diagnosis was a malignant angiosarcoma, often located near the right atrium with extensive invasion of adjacent structures.” 3. Cardiac myxoma considered as a common benign tumour but less vascular — “Cardiac haemangiomas are often misdiagnosed as myxomas and must be differentiated from malignant angiosarcomas.”

title: Progression of a persisting mesenchymal hamartoma to intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma 24 years after the initial diagnosis: A case report
case_prompt: A 26-year-old woman presented with a 6-week history of dull right upper-quadrant abdominal pain. She was first diagnosed at 16 months of age with a 9 × 8 cm multiseptated cystic mass in the right hepatic lobe on ultrasonography and MRI, consistent with mesenchymal hamartoma of the liver. Resection was planned but deferred, and serial sonographic surveillance continued until age 10 years, after which she was lost to follow-up. On current presentation, vital signs were normal. Physical examination revealed mild right upper-quadrant tenderness without rebound or guarding. Laboratory evaluation showed normal liver enzyme levels except for a γ-glutamyl transpeptidase of 40 IU/L (reference, 8–35 IU/L). Serum tumor markers were: α-fetoprotein 1.13 ng/mL (0.89–8.78 ng/mL), carcinoembryonic antigen 1.3 ng/mL (0–5 ng/mL), and CA 19-9 1075 U/mL (0–37 U/mL). Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated interval growth of the right-lobe cystic mass to 12.9 × 8.3 cm and a new 6 cm peripheral enhancing region within its posterior aspect, with enlarged hepatoduodenal and portocaval lymph nodes. MRI and ^18F-FDG PET revealed heterogeneous internal signal intensity and focal hypermetabolism within the lesion, findings that could not exclude malignant transformation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Undifferentiated embryonal sarcoma considered given history of incomplete MHL resection — “Several studies have reported that UES can arise from incompletely resected MHL.” 2. Angiosarcoma considered as another malignant evolution of residual MHL — “development of undifferentiated embryonal sarcoma (UES) or angiosarcoma secondary to incompletely resected MHL.”

title: Bitemporal hemianopsia secondary to ischemic chiasmopathy following mechanical thrombectomy
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 60-year-old woman underwent surgical tricuspid valve replacement for symptomatic tricuspid regurgitation. Ten days later, she developed acute left-sided hemiplegia, dysarthria, right-gaze preference, and a left facial droop. Her history included idiopathic hypertrophic subaortic stenosis with prior pacemaker placement and postoperative atrial fibrillation treated with apixaban. Noncontrast CT and CT angiography of the head showed an occlusion of the right distal internal carotid artery and an acute right middle cerebral artery infarct in the insula and temporal lobe. She did not receive IV thrombolysis because of anticoagulation and underwent mechanical thrombectomy via stent retriever and suction, achieving complete revascularization.

The day after thrombectomy, she reported right eyelid droop, binocular diplopia, and loss of peripheral vision in both eyes. On examination, near visual acuity was 20/60 in each eye. There was no anisocoria or relative afferent pupillary defect. The right eyelid had 1 mm of ptosis, and there was mild limitation of abduction and supraduction of the right eye. Anterior segment examination showed mild conjunctival chemosis OD, and dilated funduscopic examination was normal bilaterally. Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits was unremarkable. Noncontrast CT of the head showed no change in the known infarct and no lesion involving the optic chiasm. MRI was not obtained because of her non-MRI–compatible pacemaker.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ischemic chiasmopathy considered — “A presumptive diagnosis of bitemporal hemianopsia following ischemic chiasmopathy following mechanical thrombectomy of the right ICA was made.”

title: Morphologically and immunohistochemically undifferentiated gastric neoplasia in a patient with multiple metastatic malignant melanomas: a case report
case_prompt: A 60-year-old man with a history of nodular cutaneous melanoma (left subcostal region, pT4a, Breslow thickness 8.3 mm, Clark level IV) underwent wide excision and axillary lymph node dissection (no nodal metastases), followed by six cycles of dacarbazine and IL-2. Four months after chemotherapy, CT showed multiple liver lesions and small pulmonary nodules; thoracotomy yielded only fibrotic tissue. Six months later, he developed recurrent subcutaneous masses, skeletal pain, and widespread metastatic lesions on bone scintigraphy, CT, and PET. He received interferon and temozolomide but developed worsening asthenia, pain, nausea, and vomiting. A CT scan revealed a confluent upper abdominal mass indistinguishable from gastric corpus and pancreas. Upper endoscopy demonstrated a 3 cm hard mass at the gastro-fundal junction covered by fibrin. Histology showed an undifferentiated neoplasm with fused tumor cells. Immunohistochemical stains for melanoma markers (S100, HMB-45, Melan A, tyrosinase), carcinoma (CK), gastrointestinal stromal tumor markers (CD-34, c-Kit), and lymphoma (LCA) were all negative, except for weak, focal vimentin expression.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary gastric carcinoma was considered but “The immunohistochemical stains for … carcinoma (CK) … were performed with negative results.” 2. Gastric lymphoma was considered but “The immunohistochemical stains for … lymphoma (LCA) … were performed with negative results.” 3. Other mesenchymal neoplasia (eg, sarcoma) was considered because of “a weak and focal expression of vimentin,” although “vimentin positivity … cannot distinguish from lymphoma or sarcoma.”

title: Transient unilateral brachial plexus block following landmark-guided superficial cervical plexus block: A case report
case_prompt:
A 61-year-old man with primary hyperparathyroidism due to a right parathyroid adenoma was scheduled for focused parathyroidectomy under general anesthesia. His medical history included asthma managed with long-term inhaled steroids leading to severe osteoporosis of the spine, and chronic left shoulder impingement with limited elbow extension from a remote injury. He had no hypertension, diabetes, smoking history, or cardiovascular disease. He weighed 64 kg (BMI 19.8 kg/m2) and was ASA class II. Preoperative airway examination showed Mallampati I without restriction or pain on neck extension, though a note advised careful extension due to osteoporosis. 

Anesthesia was induced with fentanyl, propofol, and remifentanil without neuromuscular blockers. Intubation with a conventional laryngoscope in slight neck extension revealed a Cormack–Lehane grade II view. The patient was supine with a small pillow under the shoulders for slight neck extension and arms alongside the body. A bilateral SCPB was performed using anatomical landmarks with 10 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine (5 mL per side). The 103-minute surgery was uneventful, and vital signs remained stable. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery. 

Shortly after transfer, painless left arm weakness was noted. Neurological examination 3.5 hours after surgery showed strength 2/5 in shoulder elevation, 4/5 in elbow flexion/extension, and 4/5 in finger abduction and wrist extension; fist closure was normal. There was no headache, altered consciousness, dysarthria, or leg weakness. Vital signs were normal. Given the mild symptoms and absence of signs suggesting stroke or cervical spine complication, no CT or MRI was performed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Temporary nerve damage from neck extension was considered but not confirmed — “However, we could not completely rule out temporary nerve damage through a slight extension of the cervical spine during intubation and surgery.” 2. Stroke was excluded by lack of cortical signs — “There was no headache, change of consciousness, dysarthria, or weakness of the ipsilateral leg…”

title: Charcot Spine and Parkinson's Disease
case_prompt:
– A 72-year-old man with a 12-year history of idiopathic Parkinson disease presented with progressively worsening low back pain.  
– Eight years earlier, he developed chronic back pain aggravated by movement, which became associated with bilateral radicular leg pain and weakness.  
– His past medical history included coronary artery disease, a pacemaker, and hemochromatosis.  
– Neurologic examination showed 3/5 strength in the right L4 and L5 myotomes and hypoesthesia in the L4 dermatome.  
– Electromyography demonstrated irritation of the right L4, L5, and S1 nerve roots.  
– He underwent L3–L5 laminectomies for presumed degenerative lumbar stenosis, with only transient relief; six months later, low back pain and right leg pain recurred with right foot drop.  
– Over the next three years, medical therapy and epidural corticosteroid injections failed to provide lasting benefit, and thoracolumbar kyphosis developed.  
– Plain radiographs revealed extensive destruction and collapse of the L2 vertebral body with paravertebral hypertrophic ossifications at the superior margin of the prior laminectomy.  
– Dynamic radiographs showed gross instability at L2–L3.  
– Computed tomography of the spine demonstrated severe lateral dislocation of the L2–L3 articulation and a reduction in the local kyphosis angle.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Degenerative lumbar stenosis was initially diagnosed because of neurogenic claudication symptoms and imaging, but “surgery had only a limited and temporary benefit.” 2. Neuropathic osteoarthropathy (Charcot spine) was suspected when “The progressive onset of associated destructive and constructive radiological phenomena provided arguments for an osteoarthropathy and raised suspicions of Charcot spine.”

title: A case of hypoparathyroidism, deafness, and renal dysplasia (HDR) syndrome with a novel frameshift variant in GATA3, p.W10Cfs40, lacks kidney malformation
case_prompt: A 24-year-old Japanese man was referred for evaluation of hypocalcemia. He was born at low birth weight (1114 g) with ptosis and joint contractures but without neonatal asphyxia or cerebral anomalies. At age 4, he was noted to have moderate bilateral sensorineural hearing loss requiring hearing aids. Beginning around age 10, he experienced facial spasms, tetany, and leg cramps, which improved during puberty but recurred in adulthood, prompting evaluation. Initial testing showed adjusted serum calcium 6.1 mg/dL (normal 8.8–10.1) and phosphate 5.4 mg/dL (2.7–4.6), and he was started on oral vitamin D and calcium, which normalized his calcium and relieved symptoms. There was no family history of parathyroid disease, tetany, deafness, kidney dysplasia, or congenital anomalies. On admission, while on supplementation his serum calcium was 8.3 mg/dL, phosphate 4.9 mg/dL, and intact PTH was low at 15 pg/mL (18.5–88.0). Other hormones were normal. Renal function was normal without proteinuria or hematuria. Neck ultrasound showed no thyroid or parathyroid abnormalities. Brain CT demonstrated multiple basal ganglia calcifications, and abdominal CT showed morphologically normal kidneys.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Kenny–Caffey syndrome was considered as a congenital cause of hypoparathyroidism — “Differential diagnosis of early‐onset hypoparathyroidism includes several congenital diseases, DiGeorge syndrome, Kenny‐Caffey syndrome, and a genetic abnormality in PTH or CaSR...” 2. Genetic abnormalities in PTH or the calcium‐sensing receptor were considered in the differential — “Differential diagnosis of early‐onset hypoparathyroidism includes ... a genetic abnormality in PTH or CaSR...” 3. HDR syndrome was suspected because of the combination of hypoparathyroidism and sensorineural hearing loss, “... deafness is almost always observed in HDR syndrome and more rarely in DiGeorge syndrome.”

title: Cystic dystrophy of the duodenal wall in heterotopic pancreas: an atypical case in non-alcoholic female
case_prompt:
A 59-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of progressive upper‐quadrant pain and postprandial vomiting. The pain became continuous, worsened after meals, and was relieved only by vomiting. She reported a 5-kg weight loss. There was no history of alcohol abuse. On examination, she had severe epigastric tenderness without abdominal distension. Laboratory tests showed an amylase level of 156 U/l (reference ≤100) and a CA 19-9 level of 52 U/ml (reference ≤37); complete blood counts were normal. 

Abdominal ultrasonography revealed normal abdominal parenchyma, a normal gallbladder, and no common bile duct dilation. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was unremarkable. 

Multidetector CT (portal phase) demonstrated cystic lesions encircling the second portion of the duodenum with circumferential duodenal wall thickening, and a soft‐tissue component in the pancreaticoduodenal groove abutting the pancreatic head; the pancreatic head and pancreatic duct appeared normal. Signs of inflammatory change, including fat stranding and fluid, surrounded the groove area. 

Contrast‐enhanced MRI confirmed intramural fluid‐signal lesions within the thickened duodenal wall and sheet‐like tissue in the groove with enhancement patterns similar to the pancreas. MR cholangiopancreatography showed a normal gallbladder and no dilation of the common bile duct or pancreatic duct.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Groove pancreatic carcinoma was considered; it was deemed less likely because “both duodenal wall thickening and cystic changes are findings extremely uncommon in pancreatic adenocarcinoma.” 2. Adenocarcinoma of the groove was further excluded as it “is homogeneously hypoenhancing compared with the pancreas,” unlike the heterogeneous, pancreas-like enhancement observed here. 3. Duodenal duplication cyst was considered but ruled out based on the intramural location within a thickened duodenal wall: “differentiating the duodenal cysts from duplication cysts is crucial to assess their location in the duodenal wall, which is also thickened.”

title: Malignant granular cell tumor of the multifidus muscle: Case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 69-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of mild lumbar and right hip pain without fever, chills, or weight loss. On examination, lumbar flexion was 60°, extension 10°, lateral bending 10° bilaterally; mild tenderness was noted near the L4 spinous process. Neurologic testing, including cervical reflexes, femoral nerve stretch, and straight leg raise (45° on the left, 50° on the right), was unremarkable except for hypoesthesia of the left lateral leg. Routine tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA, AFP, NSE, and others) were within normal limits.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the lumbosacral region revealed a well-demarcated, homogeneous soft-tissue mass (3.2 × 7.5 cm) within the right multifidus muscle. Noncontrast attenuation was 55 HU, with heterogeneous enhancement (arterial phase 61–85 HU; venous phase 67–95 HU) and a central low-density area. The mass compressed but did not infiltrate the sacrum; the adjacent bone showed compressive sclerosis without destruction.

MRI showed an intramuscular lesion from L4 to S3. On T1-weighted images, the mass was isointense to muscle with patchy hypointense areas. STIR images demonstrated slightly hyperintense signal with patchy low-signal foci. Post-contrast sequences revealed heterogeneous enhancement with lamellar nonenhancing regions.

At surgery, the tumor was found within the right multifidus muscle, firm, poorly circumscribed, infiltrating surrounding muscle but without bone destruction. Grossly, it measured 7.5 cm, was yellowish-gray and slightly firm.

Histologic sections showed large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm, marked nuclear pleomorphism with prominent nucleoli, and approximately 2 mitoses per 10 high-power fields; there was no definite invasion of nerves, vessels, or bone. Immunohistochemical stains were positive for S100 protein, SOX-10, MDM2, and Bcl-2, and negative for Desmin, MyoD1, Myogenin, CD99, Synaptophysin, CK-pan, CD34, ERG, and CD163. The Ki-67 proliferation index exceeded 10%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 2. Schwann-cell tumors such as malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor were less favored because the cells exhibited “a distinctive granular eosinophilic cytoplasm associated with typical nuclei,” a hallmark of granular cell tumor.

title: Thrombocytopenia‐associated multiorgan failure occurring in an infant at the onset of type 1 diabetes successfully treated with fresh frozen plasma
case_prompt:
A 13-month-old girl with no prior health issues was found unresponsive at home following 2 days of vomiting and 1 day of lethargy. Paramedics recorded a Glasgow Coma Score of 3, and she was intubated and transferred to a regional hospital. A noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. On admission she was hypothermic (core temperature 33.2 °C). Initial laboratory studies revealed:  
• Arterial pH 6.6, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L  
• Urine ketones positive; blood glucose 33 mmol/L  
• Platelet count 99 × 10^9/L; hemoglobin 106 g/L; creatinine 82 μmol/L  
• Fibrinogen, prothrombin time, and activated partial thromboplastin time within normal limits  

She was managed according to DKA protocols and transferred to a tertiary pediatric ICU. Over the next 48 hours, serum ketones normalized and blood glucose ranged 6–12 mmol/L, but metabolic acidosis persisted and she remained obtunded without fever. By day 7 she developed oliguria with urea 27.5 mmol/L and creatinine 175 μmol/L; renal ultrasound showed bilaterally enlarged kidneys, and she developed pulmonary edema with pleural effusions.  

By day 5, platelet count fell to 20 × 10^9/L and hemoglobin to 77 g/L despite transfusions. Blood film demonstrated red cell fragments and fibrin strands; reticulocyte count was 0.2%; coagulation studies remained normal; lactate dehydrogenase was elevated at 800 U/L.  
These biochemical features in conjunction with her clinical symptoms of renal failure and decreased neurological function led to a presumptive differential diagnosis of thrombocytopenia-associated multiorgan failure.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. TAMOF was considered because of “new onset thrombocytopenia, multiorgan dysfunction, and increased lactate dehydrogenase … and depends on the presence of red cell fragments (schistocytes)” supporting thrombotic microangiopathy. 2. DIC was excluded because “clotting studies indicated a normal fibrinogen and normal APTT values,” whereas DIC typically shows prolonged coagulation times and decreased fibrinogen.

title: Mandibular sporadic Burkitt lymphoma in an adult patient: A case report and review of the literature
case_prompt: A 49-year-old man presented with vague pain in the left mandibular area and lower-lip paresthesia of several weeks’ duration and occasional low-grade fevers. He was edentulous. On examination, there was a firm mass of the left edentulous mandibular ridge covered by intact mucosa. Cone-beam CT showed an ill-defined radiolucent lesion extending from the left alveolar crest to the inferior alveolar canal, with buccal cortical plate invasion. An incisional biopsy yielded tan, soft to elastic tissue fragments. Histologically, the lesion consisted of sheets of malignant small to medium-sized round cells with hyperchromatic, occasionally clefted nuclei, prominent nucleoli, scant cytoplasm, frequent mitoses, perineural invasion, and crush artifact. Immunohistochemistry revealed tumor cells positive for CD45, CD20, C-MYC, CD10, and BCL-6; scattered CD3 positivity; and negative staining for CD5, BCL-2, Cyclin D1, CD19, TdT, and MPO. The Ki-67 proliferation index was >95%, and in situ hybridization for Epstein–Barr virus was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. B-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma was excluded by the absence of TdT — “TdT negativity ruled out B‐cell lymphoblastic lymphoma.”

title: Warburg micro syndrome in siblings from India
case_prompt:
An 8-year-old boy born to consanguineous parents was evaluated for markedly delayed development. Antenatal and perinatal history were unremarkable; birth weight was 2.5 kg. At 4 months of age, a white reflex was noted bilaterally; lens aspiration for congenital cataracts yielded no visual improvement. The child has not attained neck control, vocalizes by turning his head to sound, and has a developmental quotient of 3.6%. There is no history of seizures.

On examination, he is wasted and stunted (weight 8 kg [<3rd percentile]; height 95 cm [<3rd percentile]; head circumference 45 cm [<3rd percentile]). Dysmorphic features include microcephaly with plagiocephaly, frontal hypertrichosis, microphthalmia, microcornea, broad nasal root, long philtrum, retrognathia, low-set prominent ears, high-arched palate, and poor dentition. There is bilateral undescended testes and micropenis. Musculoskeletal findings include bilateral simian creases, syndactyly of the second and third toes of the left foot, equinus deformity of the right foot, and scoliosis with left-sided concavity. Neurologic examination shows hypotonia of the upper limbs, hypertonia of the lower limbs, and brisk deep tendon reflexes. Pupils are atonic and unresponsive to light and mydriatics. Brainstem auditory evoked responses and visual evoked potentials were abnormal. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain revealed right frontal pachygyria, right ventriculomegaly, and hypoplasia of the corpus callosum with age-appropriate myelination.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Martsolf syndrome was considered because it shares craniofacial anomalies and short digital bones — “The main characteristics of the syndrome are craniofacial anomalies including microcephaly, maxillary retrusion, pouting mouth, malaligned teeth, and mildly dysplastic pinnae… The metacarpal and phalangeal bones are short.” 2. Martsolf syndrome was excluded due to the greater severity of the presentation — “Martsolf syndrome is less severe disorder. In view of the severity of disease, Martsolf syndrome is unlikely.” 3. Cerebro-oculo-facial syndrome (COFS) was considered based on ocular and facial findings — “COFS is characterized by blepharophimosis, camptodactyly, arthrogryposis, generalized osteoporosis, dysplastic acetabula, coxa valga, and vertical talus manifesting as rocker bottom feet.” 4. Congenital infections (e.g., toxoplasmosis, cytomegalovirus) were considered given cataracts and microcephaly — “Differential diagnoses for this condition are Martsolf syndrome, cerebro-oculo-facial syndrome (COFS), and prenatal infections (e.g., toxoplasmosis, cytomegalovirus).”

title: Sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma associated with WRN gene variant presenting as chronic dyspnea and pathologic cervical fracture: a case report and review of the literature
case_prompt: A 37-year-old white woman with no past medical history presented with 4 months of progressive dyspnea and back pain. Four months earlier, chest radiography and CT at an outside facility had shown right upper lobe consolidation, and she was treated for presumed pneumonia. Two months later, bronchoscopy with BAL and transbronchial biopsy revealed chronic inflammation without malignancy. On re-presentation, she complained of neck pain and was tachypneic on exam. Contrast CT of the chest showed near-complete collapse and consolidation of the right upper lobe with enlarged subcarinal and station 6 lymph nodes. Spine MRI demonstrated a 50% compression fracture at C7 and diffuse lytic vertebral lesions. CT of the abdomen revealed a solitary 1.5-cm hypodense lesion in the right hepatic lobe. A transthoracic biopsy of the right upper lobe showed interstitial fibrosis and lymphocytic infiltrates without malignancy. EBUS with transbronchial lymph node sampling and BAL cytology demonstrated poorly differentiated neoplastic cells but was insufficient for immunostains. A CT-guided liver biopsy revealed an undifferentiated epithelioid neoplasm forming nests and cords within a hyalinized sclerotic stroma, with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round‐to‐ovoid nuclei. Initial immunohistochemical stains showed weak synaptophysin positivity, membranous CD99, GATA-3 positivity in a subset, and weak PAX-8. The tumor cells were nonreactive to ER and SATB2 and negative for pankeratin, CK7, CK20, MOC-31, TTF-1, WT-1, HMB45, Melan-A, SOX-10, S100, Inhibin, SMA, MyoD1, CD117, CD45, CD30, and CD34. On expert review, the cells were reactive for MUC4, with preserved BRG1 and INI1 expression and negative claudin-4, ERG, CD31, CAMTA1, P40, ETV-4, and NUT.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Epithelioid synovial sarcoma was considered because initial histology and immunostains were inconclusive — “These ancillary studies were insufficient to rule out epithelioid synovial sarcoma…”

title: Steroid-responsive Encephalopathy Associated with Autoimmune Thyroiditis (SREAT) Presenting with Pure Cerebellar Ataxia
case_prompt:
A 21-year-old woman with a history of type 1 diabetes mellitus (onset at age 15 years) and hyperlipidemia presented with a 3-month history of gradually worsening difficulty walking. She and her family denied any cognitive complaints. On neurological examination, she was alert and oriented, with no overt encephalopathy. Her speech was normal. There was moderate-to-severe truncal ataxia and appendicular ataxia in both arms and legs. Eye-movement testing showed no square-wave jerks, mild left-beating gaze-evoked nystagmus on extreme left gaze, normal pursuit without saccadic intrusions, and normal saccades. Deep-tendon reflexes were normal throughout. There was no pes cavus.

Laboratory studies revealed an anti–thyroid peroxidase antibody titer of 1:1600 (normal <1:100) and a borderline anti–thyroglobulin antibody titer of 1:40 (normal <1:20). Thyroid function tests were within normal limits. Serum anti–glutamic acid decarboxylase, antinuclear antibody, anti-Ro, anti-La antibodies, and ceruloplasmin were all normal or negative. 

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed a protein level of 46 mg/dL, a glucose level of 112 mg/dL (serum glucose 196 mg/dL), 1 white cell per mm3, and 570 red cells per mm3. CSF oligoclonal bands, anti–aquaporin-4 antibodies, and a CSF paraneoplastic panel (including anti-Hu, anti-Ri, anti-Yo, anti–Purkinje cell cytoplasmic antibody type 2 [PCA2], anti-Tr, anti-MAG, anti-Ma, anti-GAD, anti–CRMP5, and anti-amphiphysin) were negative.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain was normal without evidence of cerebellar atrophy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration was evaluated but was excluded by a negative CSF paraneoplastic antibody panel — “CSF paraneoplastic panel including anti-Hu...anti-Yo...and anti-amphiphysin antibodies were all negative.” 2. Anti–GAD–associated ataxia was considered among autoimmune causes but was excluded by a negative serum anti–glutamic acid decarboxylase result — “Serum anti-glutamic acid decarboxylase (anti-GAD) was negative.” 3. Steroid-responsive encephalopathy with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT) was supported by high-titer anti–thyroid peroxidase antibodies in the appropriate clinical context and known responsiveness to steroids — “Elevation of anti-thyroid antibodies...is required to make the diagnosis...Patients typically show good response to immunotherapies, including steroids.”

title: Eosinophilic myocarditis: Case report and brief review of the literature
case_prompt: A 70-year-old woman with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, eczema, and obstructive sleep apnea presents with 4 days of fever and chills, with a maximum temperature of 104 °F, though she is afebrile on admission. Vital signs are normal. Initial laboratory studies reveal troponin 14 552 ng/L (0–30), C-reactive peptide 96.2 mg/L (< 8.0), ESR 23 mm/h (0–20), BNP 895 pg/mL (≤ 100), CK 2 584 U/L (25–190), and WBC count 8.7 × 10^3/µL (3.7–11.0). Peak absolute eosinophil count is 0.67 × 10^3/µL (≤ 0.50). Contrast CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis is unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiogram shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy. Cardiac MRI demonstrates diffuse mid-wall to near-transmural patchy late gadolinium enhancement of the left ventricle without subendocardial enhancement in a vascular distribution, consistent with nonischemic myocarditis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Myocardial infarction excluded — “No subendocardial enhancement in vascular territory distribution to suggest infarct.” 2. Nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy considered — “Nonischemic DCM can show a LGE in a mid-myocardial distribution not associated with a coronary artery, though most will not show LGE.” 3. Cardiac amyloidosis excluded — “Cardiac amyloidosis… has a circumferential LGE pattern affecting the subendocardium or less commonly transmurally, along with concentric hypertrophy seen on CMR.” 4. Cardiac sarcoidosis excluded — “Cardiac sarcoidosis can present with a LGE pattern at the midmyocardial wall or epicardium in a patchy distribution… though the pattern is variable and myocardial thickness can fluctuate based on disease activity.” 5. Eosinophilic myocarditis suspected — “in the setting of elevated eosinophil counts highly suggestive of eosinophilic cardiomyopathy.”

title: New-onset seizures: an unusual neurologic manifestation of rheumatoid arthritis
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old man with a history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate, hypertension, stable lung nodules, and a prior right thalamic ischemic stroke (treated with thrombolysis) presented after multiple witnessed seizures. The events began on awakening with headache and progressed to brief (<1 min) focal motor seizures of the left arm and leg, occurring every few minutes with preserved awareness; there was no incontinence, tongue biting, or postictal confusion. He reported left-sided tingling and numbness but denied visual, speech, or cognitive changes. He had been compliant with levetiracetam since a single seizure at the prior admission and had no further events until this presentation.

In the emergency department, he was hemodynamically stable. Examination revealed repeated left-sided shaking involving the neck and extremities with preserved consciousness, a left upper-extremity pronator drift, and 3/5 strength in the left lower extremity; the rest of the neurologic and general exam was normal. Lorazepam and a loading dose of levetiracetam controlled the seizures. 

Noncontrast head CT showed sulcal effacement with isodense material suggestive of a leptomeningeal process. EEG demonstrated cortical hyperexcitability with focal seizure activity over the right frontocentral and parasagittal regions. MRI of the brain ruled out acute infarction but revealed focal right frontal pachymeningeal and leptomeningeal enhancement.

Laboratory tests, including CBC, electrolytes, liver function, hepatitis B and C panels, vitamin D, HIV, angiotensin-converting enzyme, proteinase 3, C3, C4, extractable nuclear antigens, immunoglobulin G4, myeloperoxidase, rapid plasma reagin, and anti-dsDNA, were all negative. Rheumatoid factor was mildly elevated; anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody was >250 U/mL. 

Lumbar puncture showed normal cell counts, protein, and glucose. CSF studies—including rheumatoid factor, angiotensin-converting enzyme, Lyme serology, syphilis testing, Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR, adenosine deaminase, cytology, and flow cytometry—were negative, and bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures showed no growth. 

CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed only small stable pulmonary nodules; MRI of the spine was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ischemic stroke was considered but excluded because “MRI of the brain ruled out ischemic stroke but indicated focal right frontal pachymeningeal and leptomeningeal enhancement with concerns for possible malignancy.” 2. Malignancy was considered due to the MRI findings but excluded by negative CSF cytology and flow cytometry — “...cytology, and flow cytometry, yielded negative results.” 3. Infectious meningitis was excluded because “CSF bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures also showed no growth.” 4. Tuberculous meningitis was less likely since on MRI the “basal cisterns are typically unaffected, in contrast with TB meningitis.” 5. Neurosarcoidosis was excluded because “angiotensin-converting enzyme ... were all negative.” 6. Neurosyphilis was excluded because “rapid plasma reagin ... [was] negative.” 7. Carcinomatous meningitis was excluded by the negative “cytology, and flow cytometry, yielded negative results.”

title: Wound botulism caused by Clostridium subterminale after a heroin injection
case_prompt: A 33-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of progressive weakness and difficulty swallowing. He had daily heroin use administered by skin popping and untreated hepatitis C. On arrival, he could open his eyelids only 2–3 mm with marked ptosis, had diplopia, pooling of secretions after 1–2 words, dysarthria, difficulty holding up his head, and proximal upper-extremity weakness (4/5 bilaterally). Vital signs were blood pressure 136/80 mmHg, pulse 106 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, temperature 36.5°C, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Five deep-cavity abscesses over his hips and thighs were incised and drained, and wound cultures were obtained. Over the next few hours, his neck and extremity weakness worsened and breathing became very shallow, prompting intubation for airway protection and impending respiratory failure. A urine drug screen was positive for amphetamines, opiates, and acetone. The constellation of symptoms was suggestive of botulism, with differential diagnoses that included Guillain–Barré syndrome and myasthenia gravis. The patient denied ingestion of bulging canned food and recent travel.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Guillain–Barré syndrome considered — “Guillain-Barré syndrome is an autoimmune disease that demyelinates neurons and causes an ascending paralysis.” 2. Miller Fisher variant of Guillain–Barré syndrome considered — “However, the Miller Fischer variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome presents with a descending paralysis, closer mimicking that of botulism.” 3. Myasthenia gravis considered — “Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular disease that results in weakness of certain muscle groups, sometimes including facial and respiratory muscles.” 4. Brainstem stroke considered — “Bilateral or brainstem strokes can also cause similar symptoms such as facial drooping, limb weakness, and breathing difficulties.” 5. Wound botulism favored — “Therefore, the suspicion was for possible wound botulism.”

title: Histopathologically TMA-like distribution of multiple organ thromboses following the initial dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccine (Comirnaty, Pfizer/BioNTech): an autopsy case report
case_prompt: A 72-year-old woman with hyperthyroidism (controlled on oral therapy), a history of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in complete remission, and a penicillin allergy received her first dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine at 09:00 on day X. Pre-vaccination screening showed a temperature of 35 °C and no other abnormalities. Laboratory testing one month before vaccination revealed normal liver and kidney function and a normal blood count; absence of thrombocytopenia and anemia was confirmed 10 days before vaccination. She felt unwell immediately after vaccination but improved with rest and returned home. At approximately 16:00 on day X + 1, she developed fatigue, nausea, chest pain, and back pain. She was last known alive around 20:00 via social media activity. She was found deceased at home on day X + 2 with rigor mortis present, and a medicolegal autopsy was initiated to determine the cause of death.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) was unlikely as HUS thrombi are typically confined to the kidneys — “In HUS, thrombi are found primarily in the kidneys.” 2. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) was considered less likely because HIT primarily causes thrombosis in the lungs and gastrointestinal tract, not widespread microthrombi in arterioles — “the lung and gastrointestinal tract are reported as the main thrombosis sites in HIT.”

title: Experience of surgical treatment in a granular cell tumor in the ascending colon: a case report
case_prompt:
A 36-year-old man was referred to our hospital because of an abnormal finding on screening colonoscopy. He had a history of tuberculosis-related lung destruction with pneumothorax and an 8-year smoking history; there was no other relevant family or medical history. He had no gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, his vital signs were normal and laboratory tests, including alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, and carbohydrate antigen 19-9, were within normal limits.

Colonoscopy revealed an approximately 10-mm yellowish, sessile protruding lesion in the proximal ascending colon covered by intact mucosa without a rolling sign or cushion sign. Endoscopic ultrasonography showed a 10-mm homogeneous hypoechoic-to-isoechoic mass mainly in the mucosal layer with irregular extension into the submucosal layer; the muscularis propria appeared intact. On the basis of the EUS features, a colonic carcinoid tumor was initially suspected. Abdominal CT did not detect the lesion or any enlarged lymph nodes. An endoscopic biopsy specimen was consistent with a granular cell tumor, although the possibility of a malignant tumor could not be completely excluded based on the endoscopic evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Initial suspicion of carcinoid tumor — “the suspected diagnosis based on the EUS findings was a colonic carcinoid tumor.” 2. Consideration of lipoma — “yellowish hemispheric lesions in the mucosal or submucosal layer require a differential diagnosis that considers lipomas…” 3. Consideration of cyst — “yellowish hemispheric lesions in the mucosal or submucosal layer require a differential diagnosis that considers… cysts…” 4. Consideration of metastatic lesion — “yellowish hemispheric lesions in the mucosal or submucosal layer require a differential diagnosis that considers… metastatic lesions…” 5. Consideration of neurofibroma — “yellowish hemispheric lesions in the mucosal or submucosal layer require a differential diagnosis that considers… neurofibromas.” 6. Consideration of carcinoma — “some reports have indicated that GCT needs to be differentiated from a carcinoid tumor or carcinoma.” 7. Concern for malignant granular cell tumor — “the possibility of a malignant tumor could not be completely excluded based on the endoscopic evaluation.”

title: Leukemoid reaction in a patient with adenocarcinoma of the lung: a case report
case_prompt:
A 51-year-old woman with a history of long-term smoking presented with left hip pain. She denied cough, dyspnea, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Physical examination was unremarkable aside from hip tenderness. 

Laboratory tests revealed a leukocyte count of 21,200 cells/μL, hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelets 337,000/μL, and C-reactive protein 19.5 mg/L. A CT scan of the left hip demonstrated extensive osteolysis of the left acetabulum with suspected pathological fracture, confirmed on MRI. A CT-guided biopsy of the acetabular lesion showed poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, confirming metastatic disease. Immunohistochemical stains were pan-cytokeratin positive; TTF-1 and other lineage markers were negative. 

Subsequent CT imaging of the chest revealed a 2.3-cm right hilar mass encircling the right main bronchus, with enlarged mediastinal nodes and pleural, pericardial, and diaphragmatic metastases; abdominal imaging showed a 2.0-cm left adrenal lesion. Bronchoscopic biopsy confirmed a moderately to poorly differentiated lung adenocarcinoma. Molecular testing revealed wild-type EGFR and a KRAS mutation. 

The patient underwent radiotherapy to the hip lesion and concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin and vinorelbine) to the primary tumor and mediastinum. During treatment, she developed left flank pain due to adrenal mass growth, and her leukocyte count rose to 146,200 cells/μL. Differential showed neutrophils 85%, lymphocytes 1%, monocytes 2%, metamyelocytes 6%, and myelocytes 5%. CRP was 49.3 mg/L and procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL; she remained afebrile.

Bone marrow aspiration revealed massively increased, left-shifted granulopoiesis up to myeloblasts with displacement of erythropoiesis and normal megakaryopoiesis. Flow cytometry showed mature granulocytic cells with partial CD56 expression and no immature blasts. Cytogenetic analysis showed a normal female karyotype (46,XX). FISH excluded t(9;22) translocation, and PCR was negative for BCR-ABL and JAK2 V617F mutation. A CT-guided biopsy of the iliopsoas lesion, along with repeated blood and urine cultures, were sterile. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics did not reduce leukocytosis or inflammatory markers.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection or abscess formation was considered — “the differential diagnosis of abscess formation in the soft tissue metastasis in the iliopsoas muscle had to be followed up... however, this could not be confirmed by means of CT-guided biopsy. The biopsy specimen proved to be just as sterile as the repeatedly taken blood and urine cultures. At the same time, the empirically initiated antibiotic treatment... failed to affect either the leukocyte concentration or the C-reactive protein and procalcitonin to a clinically relevant degree.” 2. Paraneoplastic leukemoid reaction due to tumor-derived hematopoietic growth factors was favored — “the cause most discussed in the literature... is paraneoplastic production of hematopoietic growth factors... paraneoplastic production of growth factors by the tumor itself would mean permanent stimulation of these tumor cells and explain the high aggressiveness and the uncontrollable progression.”

title:
Extrahepatic portosystemic shunts as an unusual but treatable cause
of hyperammonemic encephalopathy in a noncirrhotic patient – a case
report
case_prompt:
A 79-year-old woman was admitted for a prolonged episode of confusion. Over the past months, she had recurrent episodes of confusion, disorientation, dysphasia, and fluctuating levels of consciousness; nonconvulsive seizures and status epilepticus had been considered, and antiseizure drugs including levetiracetam and gabapentin were ineffective. Intravenous valproic acid led to severe stupor that resolved after withdrawal. Her medical history included marginal zone lymphoma in remission (previous rituximab and bendamustine) and a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy 5 years earlier for intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm.

On examination, she was disoriented and dysphasic without focal motor or sensory deficits. Electroencephalography showed generalized, irregular delta-theta activity with triphasic waves. Laboratory testing revealed a serum ammonia concentration of 121 μg/dl (reference 19–55 μg/dl) despite normal liver and kidney function. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis and brain MRI were unremarkable. Hyperammonemic encephalopathy was suspected, and lactulose, rifaximin, and daily enemas were initiated. Serum ammonia levels decreased, and her clinical status improved under this regimen. Abdominal ultrasound with elastography and abdominal MRI showed only minor signs of chronic liver disease, without evidence of cirrhosis or significant portal hypertension.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of urea cycle disorders — “disorders of the urea cycle, which usually occur in children but might manifest in adult patients as well” 2. Hematological malignancies such as multiple myeloma or leukemia — “hematological diseases, such as multiple myeloma or leukemia … can lead to noncirrhotic hyperammonemic encephalopathy” 3. Increased muscle catabolism and refeeding syndrome — “increased muscle catabolism and refeeding syndrome” are known nonhepatic causes 4. Urease-producing microorganisms — “urease-producing microorganisms should also be considered in the differential diagnosis, especially in patients with anatomical alterations of the urinary tract” 5. Cryptococcal infection in immunosuppressed patients — “a recent case report highlighted cryptococcal infection as a rare cause of hyperammonemia in an immunosuppressed patient” 6. Post-bariatric surgery complications — “Hyperammonemic encephalopathy has also been described as a complication of bariatric surgery” 7. Drug-induced hyperammonemia, notably VPA — “intravenous administration of VPA was followed by severe clinical worsening … which in retrospect could have been due to a further, VPA-associated increase in serum ammonia levels”

title: Hodgkin Lymphoma Mimicking Lumbar Spine Tuberculosis
case_prompt:
A 48-year-old man with an 8-year history of type 2 diabetes managed with oral agents and a 30–pack-year smoking history, but no known exposure to tuberculosis, was referred for worsening low back pain. Four months earlier, he developed diffuse abdominal pain with altered bowel habits and insidious inflammatory low back pain, accompanied by night sweats and a 36-kg weight loss over 6 months without fever. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy without mediastinal or pulmonary lesions. A surgical biopsy of a retroperitoneal lymph node revealed rare epithelioid granulomas without caseous necrosis. Acid-fast bacilli were not detected in sputum, but an interferon-gamma release assay was positive, and the patient was started on a 6-month course of anti-tuberculous therapy.

After 4 months of treatment, he reported progressive low back pain radiating to the buttocks, now constant and disturbing sleep. On examination, he had lumbar spinal tenderness without neurologic deficit or palpable superficial lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis were unremarkable. MRI demonstrated an L4 vertebral body lesion (T1 hypointense, T2 and STIR hyperintense), disruption of the inferior L4 cortex with adjacent disc involvement, and a 46 × 32 mm paravertebral soft-tissue collection extending into the left psoas, along with persistent retroperitoneal lymphadenopathies.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Spinal tuberculosis was the leading consideration because “Given the clinical context of retroperitoneal lymph node TB, we first considered the case as vertebral tuberculous with psoas abscess.” 2. Lack of histopathologic/bacteriologic confirmation argued against TB: “the diagnosis of TB in our patient has not been based on histological and/or bacteriological evidence.” 3. Failure to respond to appropriate therapy further excluded TB: “although our patient initially received regular treatment for TB, he did not improve but worsened clinically.” 4. In the absence of detectable Mycobacterium, definitive differentiation relies on tumor cell morphology and immunophenotyping: “When the Mycobacterium cannot be detected, the differential diagnosis between TB and HL is based primarily on RS cell morphology and immunohistochemical profile.”

title: Cystic retroperitoneal dedifferentiated liposarcoma: A case report
case_prompt: An 86-year-old man presented with acute shortness of breath and several months of unexplained weight loss. He reported recent high-impact falls, having undergone right hip hemiarthroplasty for a femoral fracture. On examination he was hypoxic with peripheral edema but had no fever, night sweats, or other constitutional symptoms. Laboratory studies showed elevated alkaline phosphatase and D-dimer levels; hemoglobin was chronically low without an acute drop, and the white-cell count, neutrophil count, and C-reactive protein were normal. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a 13 × 12 × 24 cm heterogeneous complex cystic hypoenhancing lesion in the left retroperitoneum posterior to the left kidney, with attenuation values of 9–32 Hounsfield units, displacing the kidney, encasing the renal vasculature and collecting system, and causing mild hydronephrosis. MRI showed heterogeneous T2-weighted hyperintensity with multicystic areas; multiple T1-weighted hyperintense foci consistent with hemorrhage; chemical shift imaging demonstrated microscopic lipid content by signal loss on out-of-phase images in the superior portion of the cystic component; there was diffusion restriction in the peripheral solid regions; and post-contrast fat-saturated T1 images revealed avid enhancement of the thick peripheral walls and internal septations.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of chronic hematoma — “given the recent history of multiple falls, a complicated chronic hematoma was considered in the differential at the time.” 2. Consideration of abscess — “Differential considerations on CT included a complex hematoma, abscess, or a cystic tumor.” 3. Consideration of cystic tumor — “Differential considerations on CT included a complex hematoma, abscess, or a cystic tumor.” 4. Consideration of liposarcoma — “given the signal loss seen on the out-of-phase image, the first differential diagnosis considered is liposarcoma” 5. Consideration of lymphoma — “… followed by lymphoma …” 6. Consideration of peripheral nerve sheath tumor — “… or peripheral nerve sheath tumor.”

title: Case Report: The Rehabilitation of a Patient with Acute Transverse Myelitis after COVID-19 Vaccination
case_prompt:
A 55-year-old man with chronic hypertension and gout presented to the emergency department with acute-onset chest tightness and a burning sensation radiating from the chest into both upper extremities, accompanied by sensory impairment at the T4 (nipple) level. Minutes after arrival, he developed bilateral arm weakness. He reported receiving a fourth dose of the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine three weeks earlier. Cardiovascular and vascular etiologies were excluded on initial evaluation.

Over the next few hours, his left lower extremity strength declined from 5 to 2 on Lovett’s scale, followed by progressive weakening of the right lower limb (to 3/5) and both upper limbs (to 3/5). Cervical spine MRI revealed an inflammatory focus centrally at C5–C6 without contrast enhancement; brain and thoracic spine MRI were unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed elevated protein (0.91 g/L) and albumin (656.7 mg/L) levels without intrathecal oligoclonal band synthesis. CSF culture and PCR for pathogens were negative. Serum aquaporin-4 antibody was negative. Anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody titers were 1:10 on October 13 and <1:10 on October 18. A connective-tissue–disorder workup was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Multiple sclerosis — “MR images of the cerebrum and the thoracic spine acquired later showed no lesions.” 2. Higher dilution will result in a higher number of false positives…” 3. Infectious transverse myelitis — “CSF culture test and PCR test for the presence of pathogens in CSF were negative.” 4. Vascular causes (infarction, thrombosis) — “ED admission excluded cardiological and vascular causes.” 5. Connective-tissue–related myelitis (e.g., SLE, Sjögren’s) — “Connective tissue disorders were ruled out.”

title: Novel insights into retinal neovascularization secondary to central serous chorioretinopathy using 3D optical coherence tomography angiography
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 48-year-old man with a longstanding history of central serous chorioretinopathy presented with a sudden onset of tiny floaters in the field of view of his left eye. He had episodic blurred vision in both eyes. His past medical history was notable for ethanol-related liver cirrhosis with decompensation and hepatorenal syndrome; he had no history of diabetes mellitus or cardiovascular disease. Blood tests excluded diabetes and infectious causes of neovascularization. 

On ophthalmologic examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes, and intraocular pressures were normal. Slit-lamp examination showed clear lenses and no iris neovascularization or anterior segment inflammation. Funduscopy revealed normal optic discs but extensive pigment epithelial and retinal alterations in both maculae. 

In the right eye, there was a pigmented epithelial detachment and surrounding subretinal fluid near the temporal superior arcade, suggesting active choroidal leakage. In the left eye, there was a vitreous hemorrhage and a branched vascular proliferation projecting into the vitreous from the inferior temporal arcade in an area of retinal pigment epithelium atrophy; the remaining retinal vasculature and peripheral retina were normal. 

Spectral-domain OCT confirmed extensive photoreceptor and RPE degeneration in both eyes and the pigmented epithelial detachment with subretinal fluid in the right eye. Fundus autofluorescence showed large areas of reduced autofluorescence surrounded by increased autofluorescence (gravitational tracks) in both maculae, consistent with chronic CSCR. Fluorescein angiography demonstrated widespread RPE window defects, an active leakage point at the temporal superior arcade in the right eye, and an active vascular proliferation at the inferior temporal arcade in the left eye. Indocyanine green angiography of the left eye revealed multifocal confluent choroidal atrophy with reduced vessel density beneath the neovascular complex. En-face OCT angiography of the left eye confirmed a flow-positive vascular network protruding into the vitreous and underlying choriocapillaris and choroidal vessel voids, with surrounding retinal atrophy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of choroidal neovascularization — “At first sight, a choroidal neovascularization was suspected which is a common complication in chronic CSCR which could have penetrated the retina and into the vitreous. Surprisingly, however, the origin of the neovascularization was found to be retinal and its location preretinal instead of subretinal.” 2. Consideration of diabetic retinopathy — “No signs of diabetic retinopathy, vascular occlusive disease or peripheral capillary non-perfusion were observed during funduscopy or FFA.” 3. Consideration of retinal vein occlusion or other vascular occlusive disease — “No signs of diabetic retinopathy, vascular occlusive disease or peripheral capillary non-perfusion were observed during funduscopy or FFA.” 4. Consideration of other maculopathies (choroidal hemangioma, dome-shaped maculopathy, infectious maculopathy, age-related macular degeneration) — “Based on the described clinical and imaging findings, other potential differential diagnosis such as choroidal hemangioma, dome-shaped maculopathy, infectious maculopathy or age-related macular degeneration were excluded.” 5. Consideration of infectious causes of neovascularization — “Blood examination excluded other known causes of RNV such as diabetes or infections.”

title: Spontaneous cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhoea: a case report and literature review
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old Asian woman presented with a 3-month history of watery discharge from her left nostril and dull, throbbing headaches localized to the left side, which worsened when leaning forward or straining. The nasal discharge was clear, moderate in amount, nonfoul, and nonbloody. She denied fever, nasal obstruction, sneezing, itching, facial fullness, or other sinonasal symptoms. She had been diagnosed elsewhere with allergic rhinitis and treated with antiallergic medication without improvement.

Her medical history included an episode of sinusitis 5 years earlier and a childhood nasal injury at age 12. Surgical history comprised cesarean section, appendectomy, and sterilization. She had no other comorbidities, was a non-smoker, and did not drink alcohol.

On examination, she was afebrile with stable vital signs. Nasal inspection revealed a normal external contour and a reddish vestibule with clear left-sided discharge. The discharge could not be sniffed back and did not stiffen a handkerchief. There was no septal deviation, polyps, or mucosal abnormalities. Neurologic and other system examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed mild anemia (hemoglobin 10 g/dL) but were otherwise normal. The handkerchief test was positive. Analysis of the nasal fluid demonstrated a glucose concentration exceeding 30 mg/dL.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Allergic rhinitis was ruled out because “Allergic and vasomotor rhinitis were ruled out owing to the absence of characteristic symptoms.” 2.
