Vasomotor rhinitis was ruled out because “Allergic and vasomotor rhinitis were ruled out owing to the absence of characteristic symptoms.” 3. The finding of a “thin, watery, clear discharge that could not be sniffed back and did not stiffen a handkerchief” supported CSF rhinorrhea.

title: Corneal Graft Rejection after Descemet's Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty with Peripheral Anterior Synechiae
case_prompt:
A 66-year-old woman was referred for decreased visual acuity in her right eye. Three years earlier she had undergone bilateral laser iridotomy for primary angle closure and subsequently developed bullous keratopathy. On initial examination, the right cornea was edematous with Descemet’s membrane folds and a central corneal thickness of 702 µm. Anterior-chamber depths were 2.36 mm OD and 2.23 mm OS; the iridotomy site was patent without inflammation or hyperemia. Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was 20/40 OD; endothelial cell density (ECD) was unmeasurable OD and 1,773 cells/mm2 OS. 

The patient underwent phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implantation OD. Her BCVA was 20/50 at 1 week and 20/125 at 2 months with no further improvement, so Descemet’s membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) was performed. The graft was fully attached and corneal clarity improved except for progressive peripheral anterior synechiae at the temporal periphery. One month post-DMEK, BCVA was 20/20, ECD was 1,984 cells/mm2, and central corneal thickness was 477 µm. 

Oral prednisolone was discontinued at 1 month and topical 0.1% betamethasone was tapered from four times to once daily when the cornea was clear (BCVA 20/20, thickness 486 µm, ECD 1,114 cells/mm2). Fifteen months after DMEK, the regimen was changed to topical 0.1% fluorometholone once daily. Shortly thereafter, corneal edema recurred, with hyperemia, multiple Descemet’s membrane folds, diffuse, inferiorly located mutton-fat keratic precipitates on the graft, and anterior-chamber cells. BCVA worsened to 20/32, central corneal thickness was 704 µm, and ECD, which had been 980 cells/mm2 two months earlier, was now unmeasurable. 

Anterior-segment optical coherence tomography confirmed peripheral anterior synechiae in the recipient cornea. The patient declined PCR testing. Blood tests showed no elevation of antibodies against herpes simplex virus or varicella zoster virus.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Corneal endotheliitis from herpes simplex virus was considered as “a major consideration during differential diagnosis of graft rejection because it has similar clinical findings,” but herpes simplex virus infection typically shows “discoidal KPs.” 2. Corneal endotheliitis from cytomegalovirus was considered as “a major consideration during differential diagnosis of graft rejection because it has similar clinical findings,” but cytomegalovirus infection is characterized by “coin-shaped KPs.” 3. Viral infection was further made less likely by serology: “Blood tests did not reveal elevation of antibodies against herpes simplex virus or varicella zoster virus.”

title: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis - a crazy presentation of dyspnea
case_prompt: A 44-year-old man with a 50 pack-year smoking history was referred for 1 year of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fatigue, 10 kg weight loss, and recurrent “pneumonias.” He drank approximately 49 units of alcohol per week and worked in an office with no known inhalational exposures. On examination he had finger clubbing and bibasilar crackles. Spirometry showed an FEV₁ of 2.79 L (69% predicted), FVC of 4.04 L (81% predicted), and an FEV₁/FVC ratio of 69%. DLCO was 22% predicted and KCO was 32% predicted. Resting oxygen saturation on room air was 84–90%, and a 6-minute walk of 490 m caused desaturation from 89% to 78%. Lactate dehydrogenase was 339 U/L (reference 105–205 U/L). Serial chest radiographs showed a reticular pattern and bilateral infiltrates. High-resolution CT of the thorax revealed interlobular septal thickening with diffuse ground-glass opacities and areas of consolidation in a “crazy paving” pattern. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid appeared milky white, and microbiological studies of the lavage were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious pneumonia was deemed unlikely given “the long time span without clinical presentation of fever, sputum production, positive microbiology, and without typical findings on thoracic imaging.”

title: Role of integrated imaging in the diagnosis of an atypical and unresectable cardiac paraganglioma: a case report
case_prompt: A 19-year-old previously healthy male presented with chest pain and fever. Initial evaluation revealed an enlarged cardiac silhouette on chest radiograph, ST-segment elevation in leads aVF and III on ECG, and a 2.2 cm pericardial effusion on transthoracic echocardiography. He was diagnosed with acute pericarditis and treated with ibuprofen and colchicine; colchicine was continued after discharge. One month later, echocardiography showed persistent pericardial effusion (0.7 cm) and ongoing fatigue. Seven months after initial presentation, he experienced recurrent pericarditis with effusion and asthenia, prompting cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR). CMR demonstrated a 54×42 mm mass adjacent to the epicardial surface of the basal and mid anterior left ventricular wall that was hypointense with a peripheral hyperintense rim on T2-weighted images, hyperintense on T1-weighted images, and showed peripheral gadolinium enhancement with a nonenhancing central core; diffusion-weighted imaging showed no signal increase. Contrast-enhanced cardiac CT confirmed a hypervascular mass with a central hypodense scar and showed that the proximal left anterior descending artery coursed through the lesion without chamber invasion. Laboratory testing revealed marked elevations of plasma and urine 3-methoxytyramine and increased chromogranin A levels.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Angioma was considered because of a hypervascular cardiac mass but excluded due to its typical “slower and more homogeneous enhancement” — “angioma (which has slower and more homogeneous enhancement)”. 2. Myxofibrosarcoma was considered as a primary cardiac neoplasm but deemed unlikely given that it is “often already metastatic”. 3. Angiosarcoma was considered given its vascular nature but thought improbable because it “occurs mainly in the right atrium or pericardium”. 4. Idiopathic acute pericarditis was the initial diagnosis but excluded after imaging, since “These findings strongly suggested the presence of a primary cardiac neoplasm.”

title: Timolol-induced interstitial lung disease
case_prompt:
A 76-year-old Caucasian woman with hypertension and glaucoma presented with a three-month history of a productive cough and progressive dyspnea. She was initially treated for bronchitis with antibiotics and a methylprednisolone dose pack without improvement; allergy testing was negative. She denied fevers, chills, weight loss, chest pain, palpitations, hemoptysis, gastrointestinal symptoms, asthma, rash, arthritis, recreational drug use, bird exposure, or occupational exposures (silicon, beryllium, asbestos). Medications included timolol eye drops, losartan, and pantoprazole.

On examination, her blood pressure was 128/68 mm Hg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, temperature 98.8 °F, and oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Pulmonary exam revealed bibasilar rales; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood counts and chemistry panels. Blood cultures remained negative for 5 days; sputum cultures were negative for acid-fast bacilli, fungi, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Chest CT demonstrated a slight mosaic attenuation pattern suggestive of air trapping. High-resolution CT of the chest revealed ground-glass opacities and a mosaic pattern consistent with bronchial air trapping. Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) showed 89% neutrophils, 8% lymphocytes, 2% monocytes, and 1% macrophages. Transbronchial biopsy revealed focal giant cell reaction without malignancy; the CD4:CD8 ratio was 2.8:1. Pulmonary function testing showed an FEV1 of 75% of predicted and a decreased DLCO, consistent with restrictive physiology.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignancy was considered but excluded — “Transbronchial biopsy showed evidence of focal giant cell reaction and negative for malignancy.” 2. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis was considered but allergy testing was negative — “Patient underwent allergy testing which was negative.” 3. Occupational lung disease was considered but exposures were denied — “Patient denied ... exposure to pigeons or other birds, and exposure to silicon, berillym, or asbestos.”

title: Atypical skin manifestation in severe acute chikungunya infection in a pregnant woman: a case report
case_prompt:
An 18-year-old Thai woman, G1P0 at 31 weeks’ gestation, presented with 6 hours of fever and severely painful swelling of her wrists, hands, and feet. She was unable to make a fist and had difficulty walking. She also reported a headache but denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or myalgia. A pruritic, erythematous rash was noted on her abdomen. She lived in a community where neighbors had experienced fever and arthralgia in the past week. She was not on any medications, had no recent hospitalizations, and no family members were ill.

On examination, temperature was 38.1 °C, blood pressure 100/40 mmHg, pulse 126 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Maculopapular rashes were present on the forehead, cheeks, nose, and abdominal wall. There was arthritis of both wrists, ankles, and all metatarsophalangeal joints. Arthrocentesis of the left wrist yielded clear yellow synovial fluid with high viscosity; Gram stain showed no organisms.

Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; white cells 9300/mm3 (91% neutrophils); platelets 180 000/mm3; BUN 8.3 mg/dL; creatinine 0.38 mg/dL; total protein 6.2 g/dL; albumin 3.5 g/dL; globulin 2.7 g/dL; total bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL; direct bilirubin 0.1 mg/dL; AST 15 U/L; ALT 8 U/L; ALP 102 U/L; ESR 52 mm/h. Serology for dengue NS1 antigen and IgM and IgG antibodies was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial septic arthritis and sepsis were considered because of acute arthritis and hypotension—“initially diagnosed with viral arthritis, but septic arthritis could not be ruled out… Gram staining did not reveal the presence of organisms.”

title: Cerebral Toxoplasmosis as an Uncommon Complication of Biologic Therapy for Rheumatoid Arthritis: Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 62-year-old woman with severe rheumatoid arthritis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypothyroidism presented with two weeks of progressive temporal disorientation and unsteady gait. Her rheumatoid arthritis had been treated for 10 years with prednisolone (10 mg daily), leflunomide (20 mg daily), and weekly methotrexate (7.5 mg). Two months earlier, adalimumab (40 mg every two weeks) was added due to worsening joint pain and deformity.

On examination, she was afebrile. Neurologic assessment showed moderate left hemiparesis (Medical Research Council grade 3) without sensory deficits or cranial nerve abnormalities. Laboratory tests revealed a normal white-cell count (9100/µL) with neutrophilia (89%) and lymphopenia (7%), hyperglycemia (136 mg/dL), and an HbA1c of 6.9%. HIV testing was negative. Toxoplasma serology was IgG positive and IgM negative. Electrocardiogram and chest radiograph were unremarkable.

Noncontrast CT of the head demonstrated a focal hypodense lesion in the right basal ganglia with surrounding edema and mild midline shift. Brain MRI showed a heterogeneous T2/FLAIR lesion with peripheral gadolinium enhancement and perfusion‐weighted imaging revealed decreased cerebral blood volume in the lesion.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary central nervous system lymphoma was considered because it can present as a ring-enhancing mass in immunocompromised patients — “decreased CBV on perfusion-weighted MRI favor the hypothesis of a pseudotumoral mass (such as infection) instead of a brain tumor or metastasis.”

title: Colistin neurotoxicity mimicking Guillain-Barré syndrome in a patient with cystic fibrosis: case report and review
case_prompt:
A 38-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis, status post bilateral lung transplant and with stage 3B chronic kidney disease and CF-related diabetes, was transferred to the intensive care unit for acute-on-chronic hypoxemic respiratory and renal failure. Three weeks prior, she had been treated for acute-on-chronic sinusitis caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and Aspergillus fumigatus. Bronchoalveolar lavage cultures subsequently grew Stenotrophomonas maltophilia, and she was started on intravenous colistimethate. 

On the second day of colistin therapy, she reported new-onset numbness and tingling in her fingers, toes, and perioral region. Neurologic examination revealed intact cranial nerves, preserved sensation and proprioception, and full motor strength. Deep-tendon reflexes were decreased bilaterally at the knees and ankles but were normal in the upper extremities. Given a recent upper respiratory infection and the combination of subjective weakness, paresthesia, and diminished lower-extremity reflexes, Guillain-Barré syndrome was suspected. 

A lumbar puncture was performed. Cerebrospinal fluid contained 1 erythrocyte, no neutrophils, 71 lymphocytes, 29 monocytes, a glucose level of 81 mg/dL, and a protein level of 23 mg/dL.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Vasculitis and heavy-metal intoxication were considered as other mimics of GBS — “Other medical conditions can mimic the presentation of GBS, such as vasculitis, heavy metal and drug toxicities, including colistin.” 2. Drug-induced neurotoxicity emerged once medication review revealed recent intravenous colistin exposure and perioral tingling — “After review of the patient’s medication list…intravenous colistin was identified as a possible culprit.”

title: Q-uestioning the Diagnosis: An Educational Case Report
journal: Canadian Journal of Kidney Health and Disease
case_prompt:
A 52-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of microscopic hematuria and a rise in creatinine from a baseline of 59 to 94 µmol/L. Over three months, she had developed a bilateral petechial rash on her lower extremities, intermittent right knee swelling and pain, and a two-year history of subjective fevers and night sweats. A short course of oral prednisone produced transient improvement in her rash and systemic symptoms, but these recurred on tapering. She was restarted on prednisone 30 mg daily one week before nephrology consultation for concern of vasculitis or connective tissue disease.

Her medical history included a left femoral crossover graft (2005), gastroesophageal reflux, dyslipidemia, depression, and at least six months of microscopic hematuria with normal cystoscopy. Medications were rabeprazole, ASA, simvastatin, citalopram, and bupropion. On exam, she had palpable petechiae on her lower extremities and warm moderate effusions in the right knee and ankle; vital signs were normal.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 102 g/L, WBC 11.1×10^9/L, creatinine 94 µmol/L, ESR 115 mm/hr, CRP 94 mg/L, and rheumatoid factor 176 IU/mL. Urinalysis had trace protein, 58 RBCs/HPF with acanthocytes, and 7 WBCs/HPF. ANCA, C3, and C4 were normal. A skin biopsy demonstrated leukocytoclastic vasculitis with dermal capillary IgM and C3 deposition. Knee arthrocentesis was noninfectious and noncrystalline.

Serologies for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, and syphilis were negative; blood cultures were negative. SPEP showed no monoclonal protein; cryoglobulins and anti-CCP were negative. Kappa and lambda light chains were modestly elevated. ANA was weakly positive with RNP 1.1 AI, dsDNA was negative, and lupus anticoagulant was normal. Twenty-four–hour urine protein was 0.32 g. Renal ultrasound showed normal-sized kidneys.

Because her creatinine and symptoms initially improved on prednisone, biopsy was deferred. Upon prednisone withdrawal, her creatinine rose to 111 µmol/L and symptoms recurred, prompting a renal biopsy. Light microscopy revealed diffuse global mesangial hypercellularity. Immunofluorescence showed trace mesangial IgG and kappa, 1+ IgM, lambda, and C1q, and 2+ C3. Electron microscopy demonstrated mesangial electron-dense deposits. These findings were thought to be consistent with mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis, although her clinical and serologic profile was atypical.

She was treated with prednisone and azathioprine, then mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine, and rituximab in various combinations, each time showing creatinine improvement on steroids and relapse on tapering. Given her atypical clinical features and inability to wean prednisone, the lupus diagnosis was reconsidered. Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram, CT of the abdomen and pelvis, and blood cultures showed no infectious focus. She was referred for further infectious evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lupus nephritis was initially diagnosed because “pathology findings were felt to potentially be in keeping with mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis; however, it was acknowledged that clinical and laboratory findings supporting this diagnosis were lacking.” 2. Hepatitis B, C, HIV, and syphilis were excluded as causes because “additional investigation … included negative hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, and syphilis serology as well as negative blood cultures.” 3. Cryoglobulinemia was considered but ruled out as “cryoglobulins were negative.” 4. Monoclonal gammopathy was excluded since “serum protein electrophoresis did not show a monoclone.” 5. ANCA-associated vasculitis was deemed unlikely because “antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies … were within normal limits.” 6. Endocarditis and other standard infections were investigated with “repeat blood cultures” and a transthoracic echocardiogram, which “did not reveal an infectious source.” 7. A connective tissue disease or vasculitis was suspected clinically, prompting trials of prednisone, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine, and rituximab, but each time there was only “improvement … with immunosuppression … followed by relapse on tapering,” implying an atypical underlying process.

title: Large cervical endometrioid adenocarcinoma mimicking endometrial endometrioid adenocarcinoma: A case report
case_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of gradually increasing lower abdominal pain and irregular vaginal bleeding. She was postmenopausal (age at menopause, 56), with no significant medical, surgical, or family history. She had no history of HPV vaccination.

On examination, she was pale and underweight. Abdominopelvic CT without contrast showed an enlarged uterus. Laboratory evaluation revealed a white blood cell count of 32,200/mm3, a C-reactive protein level of 17 mg/dL, and hemoglobin of 5.5 g/dL; 6 units of red blood cells were transfused. Tumor markers were elevated: SCC antigen, 5.3 ng/mL (normal ≤2.5); CA-125, 112.7 U/mL (normal ≤35).

Speculum examination demonstrated a large bleeding tumor occupying the vaginal canal; bimanual exam revealed an enlarged, fixed uterus with the fundus midway between the symphysis pubis and the umbilicus. Contrast-enhanced CT showed multiloculated, thick-walled cystic lesions in the uterus and pelvic fluid suggestive of an abscess. Empiric antibiotics were begun.

Pelvic MRI revealed an 11×9-cm mass with central necrosis confined to the cervix; the endometrial cavity was spared and distended with fluid. There was no parametrial, vaginal wall, or rectal involvement on MRI; bladder mucosal invasion was not excluded.

Colposcopy-directed biopsy of the cervical lesion showed endometrioid adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry was diffusely positive for vimentin and estrogen receptor, almost negative for progesterone receptor, and patchy positive for p16. Tests for high-risk HPV (16 subtypes) and microsatellite instability were negative. Cystoscopy confirmed intact bladder mucosa.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Uterine fundus adenocarcinoma extending to the cervix — “imaging studies such as MRI are considerably useful in ruling out uterine fundus cancer from ECA by judging whether the endometrial cavity is spared and/or distended with secretions…”, which was noted in this case, making a fundal origin unlikely. 2. Uterine isthmus (lower uterine segment) adenocarcinoma — “Distinguishing endometrioid ECA from endometrioid uterine isthmus cancer is difficult even in hysterectomy specimens using immunohistochemistry… Therefore, for endometrioid carcinoma of equivocal origin… the tumor origin should be determined based on the clinical findings and course.”, favoring clinical and imaging data over overlapping immunoprofiles.

title: Borderline tuberculoid leprosy misdiagnosed as Cheilitis Granulomatosa Miescher: A case report
case_prompt: A 50-year-old woman presented with persistent swelling of her lower lip for 2 years. She had no history of tuberculosis or contact with patients with tuberculosis or leprosy. Routine blood biochemistry and hematological parameters were within normal limits. A slit skin smear examination, Mantoux test, and chest radiograph were unremarkable. On the basis of these findings and the clinical presentation, a diagnosis of cheilitis granulomatosa Miescher (CGM) was made, and multiple intralesional corticosteroid injections were administered into the lower lip without improvement. Four weeks later, she developed erythematous, non-pruritic, non-scaling plaques above her upper lip and on the right lower chin, each measuring approximately 1 cm × 1.5 cm. Systemic examination was otherwise normal. Incisional biopsy specimens from the lower lip and an erythematous plaque revealed granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes with periadnexal and perineural lymphocytic infiltration on histopathological examination.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cheilitis granulomatosa Miescher was initially diagnosed based on the isolated chronic lip swelling and normal early investigations — “Based on the clinical features and investigation, a diagnosis of CGM was made and multiple dosages of intralesional corticosteroid were injected into the lower lip.” 2. Melkersson–Rosenthal syndrome was considered because chronic lip swelling is part of its classical triad, but was excluded by absence of tongue and facial nerve involvement — “Our patient presented with isolated lip swelling, without tongue and facial nerve involvement.” 3. Tuberculosis was ruled out after the Mantoux test and chest radiograph were unremarkable — “Slit skin smear (SSS) examination, Mantoux test, and chest radiograph were unremarkable.”

title: Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV-1) Encephalitis Mimicking Glioblastoma: Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 60-year-old man presented with fever and confusion. One month earlier, he had been admitted for slurred speech, right hemiparesis, and weight loss; MRI at that time showed multiple left temporoparietal enhancing lesions, and biopsy diagnosed glioblastoma (WHO grade IV), for which he received radiation and temozolomide. His past medical history included diabetes mellitus.
On current admission, temperature was 39.7 °C, pulse 88/min; examination revealed left facial weakness and decreased strength in the right upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests showed WBC 0.4 K/mm3 (absolute neutrophil count 0.2 K/mm3), hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, and platelets 20 K/mm3; other routine tests were unremarkable. Chest radiograph suggested a possible right lower lobe infiltrate.
Noncontrast head CT demonstrated stable left frontal and corpus callosum abnormalities compared to prior MRI. He received platelet transfusion and was started on meropenem for febrile neutropenia; dexamethasone was tapered. After platelet count rose, lumbar puncture showed 0 WBC/mm3, 0 RBC/mm3, protein 100 mg/dL, glucose 33 mg/dL, LDH 38 IU/L, and negative Gram stain. EEG revealed generalized background slowing with left frontotemporal low-amplitude sharp waves. Repeat brain MRI with contrast showed a new signal abnormality in the right temporal lobe.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Multifocal or multicentric GBM was considered because such presentations have been reported and can mimic infection — “Since multifocal and multicentric glioblastomas (GBM) have been reported, HSE may be easily overlooked as a diagnostic possibility, as the clinical presentation of HSE may mimic GBM.”

title: Posterior fossa giant tumefactive perivascular spaces: 8-year follow-up in an adolescent
case_prompt: A 15-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of a 3-day history of dizziness, gait instability, nausea, and headache. On examination, he had mild diplopia on left lateral gaze and on convergence, an inability to sustain upward vertical gaze, mild right upper- and lower-extremity weakness (Medical Research Council grade 4), right-sided hypoesthesia, and mild gait instability without frank cerebellar signs. Brain MRI showed a well-defined, lobulated cystic lesion in the right middle cerebellar peduncle adjacent to the fourth ventricle, causing mild mass effect on the cerebellar vermis. The lesion contained multiple thin septations and was uniformly isointense to cerebrospinal fluid on all sequences, with no definite enhancement or diffusion restriction. There was mild surrounding edema in the right middle cerebellar peduncle. Single-voxel proton MR spectroscopy demonstrated unremarkable concentrations of N-acetylaspartate, choline, and creatine, with normal choline/creatine and choline/N-acetylaspartate ratios.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Abscess was considered — “There is no definite enhancement or diffusion restriction.” 2. Arachnoid cyst was considered — “Simple cysts of the cerebellum ... all reports identify a single large midline or paramedian cyst involving the cerebellar vermis.” 3. Age-related dilated perivascular spaces were considered — “perivascular dilations are relatively small, unclustered dilations that do not cause mass effect and appear symmetrically within the cerebral white matter, basal ganglia, and mesencephalon.” 4. Other cystic neoplasms and parasitic or developmental cysts were considered — “imaging features distinguishing GTPS from neoplasm or other disease are their isointensity to CSF on all sequences, no enhancement following contrast administration, no diffusion restriction, absence of calcification, and their characteristic locations.”

title: Electroencephalographic findings after convulsive seizures due to cerebral arterial air embolism secondary to lung cancer: a case report
case_prompt: A 55-year-old Japanese man with a six-month history of left hilar lung cancer (suspected squamous cell carcinoma based on sputum cytology and tumor markers) treated with radiotherapy and chemotherapy presented one year after diagnosis with fever and chest pain. On admission (day 1), he was hypotensive (BP 69/58 mmHg), tachycardic (146/min), tachypneic (24/min), and had an altered mental state. Chest CT showed a left hilar mass invading the left atrium and left main bronchus, with small low-density areas within the tumor and around the intestinal membrane. Echocardiography confirmed tumor invasion into the left atrium without vegetations. Blood cultures grew Staphylococcus aureus. He received noradrenaline and broad-spectrum antibiotics. On day 2, he developed acute difficulty speaking, left-sided weakness, and left unilateral spatial neglect, with an NIHSS score of 7. Head CT revealed multiple small low-density areas in the right cortex and bilateral subcortex interpreted as air emboli. Brain MRI showed faint hyperintensity along the right parietal cortex, subcortex, and left frontal cortex on diffusion-weighted images and small dot-like low signals in the right frontal cortex and left parietal subcortex on susceptibility-weighted images.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cerebral arterial air embolism — “multiple small low-density areas in the right cortex and bilateral subcortex, which were interpreted as air emboli.” 2. Ischemic stroke — “faint hyperintensity along the right parietal cortex, subcortex, and left frontal cortex on diffusion-weighted images.” 3. Brain metastasis — “ischemic stroke or brain metastasis was associated with cancer progression.” 4. Infective endocarditis — “no findings indicated infective endocarditis.”

title: Atypical or typical adrenocorticotropic hormone-producing pulmonary carcinoids and the usefulness of 11C-5-hydroxytryptophan positron emission tomography: two case reports
case_prompt:
A 63-year-old woman was referred with progressive generalized fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, insomnia, flushing, hypertension, impaired concentration, memory difficulties, and hypomanic symptoms. She reported a 5-kg weight loss over several months despite features of Cushing’s syndrome. Her history included breast cancer 16 years earlier and a superficial melanoma with no recurrence.

On examination she had moon facies, supraclavicular fat, a buffalo hump, chin hirsutism, and proximal myopathy. Laboratory studies showed a 24-hour urinary free cortisol of 1300 nmol (reference <183 nmol), serum cortisol levels of 600–1000 nmol/L without diurnal variation, and plasma ACTH levels of 30–45 ng/L throughout 24 hours. There was no suppression on 1-mg or 8-mg dexamethasone tests.

An MRI of the sella turcica showed no pituitary lesion. Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling after CRH stimulation demonstrated no central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient and no ACTH increase. Fasting CRH was normal at 3.5 pmol/L. Urinary catecholamines and hydroxyindoleacetic acid were normal. Chromogranin A was mildly elevated.

A CT scan of the neck, chest, and abdomen revealed only slight enlargement of the left adrenal gland with an attenuation of 25 Hounsfield units. A whole-body planar 111In-pentetreotide somatostatin receptor scintigraphy scan demonstrated no uptake typical of an ectopic ACTH source.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pituitary Cushing’s disease was excluded — “An MRI scan of her sella turcica found no pituitary tumor.” 2. A pituitary microadenoma was further unlikely because IPSS showed no central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient and no response to CRH — “Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling … revealed no central to peripheral ratio of ACTH, nor did ACTH increase during the CRH test.” 3. Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy failed to localize the tumor, acknowledging false negatives in small or receptor-poor lesions — “The scan demonstrated no uptake typical for an ectopic ACTH source.”

title: Cardiogenic shock in the context of newly diagnosed anomalous origin of the right coronary artery originating from the pulmonary artery: a case report
case_prompt:
A 76-year-old woman with hypertension and dyslipidaemia presented with two weeks of intermittent palpitations, orthopnoea, and NYHA class III–IV dyspnoea. On examination, her extremities were cool and clammy, jugular venous pressure was elevated to the angle of the jaw with a positive abdominojugular reflux, and auscultation revealed bilateral crackles. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs, but the rhythm was irregularly irregular at approximately 100 b.p.m. 

In the emergency department, she was found to be in atrial fibrillation with left bundle branch block on ECG and clinical evidence of decompensated heart failure, confirmed by chest X-ray showing pulmonary oedema and pleural effusions. Initial management included IV furosemide and rate control with metoprolol and digoxin. 

Over the next few hours, she became increasingly hypotensive (60/30 mmHg), tachycardic, diaphoretic, and developed anuric acute kidney injury and lactic acidosis. High-sensitivity troponin rose from 13 ng/L to 2040 ng/L. Labs showed worsening metabolic acidosis, elevated liver enzymes, and a lactate of 7.1 mmol/L. She was started on dual antiplatelet therapy and vasopressors for suspected acute coronary syndrome. Point-of-care ultrasound demonstrated significant biventricular systolic dysfunction, a plethoric non-collapsible IVC, and diastolic flattening of the interventricular septum. 

Differential diagnoses considered included acute coronary syndrome with RV infarction, underlying cardiomyopathy or pulmonary hypertension with decompensation, and massive pulmonary embolism. 

Given her hemodynamic instability, she was intubated and taken for emergent cardiac catheterization. Angiography showed very large, ectatic left main, LAD, and LCx arteries with no flow-limiting stenoses, and the RCA ostium could not be engaged. Contrast injection into the left system revealed retrograde filling of the RCA via multiple collateral vessels. On further review, it was felt that the RCA was emptying back into the pulmonary artery/right ventricular outflow tract.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute coronary syndrome with RV infarction was considered because of hypotension, troponin elevation, and RV dysfunction — “no flow-limiting stenoses were identified.” 2. Anomalous right coronary artery from the pulmonary artery was considered when the RCA ostium could not be engaged and contrast showed retrograde filling and drainage into the pulmonary artery — “it was felt that the RCA was emptying back into the pulmonary artery/right ventricular outflow tract.”
final_diagnosis: Anomalous right coronary artery from the pulmonary artery

title: Isolated Severe Stricturing Duodenal Crohn Disease in a Pediatric Patient
case_prompt: A 12-year-old African American girl with known iron deficiency anemia presented with 2 months of weight loss, decreased appetite, diffuse abdominal pain, and bilious vomiting. On examination, she had generalized abdominal tenderness and epigastric fullness. Laboratory studies showed thrombocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers. An upper gastrointestinal series revealed severe gastric distention with failure of contrast to pass beyond the stomach, consistent with gastric outlet obstruction. Magnetic resonance enterography demonstrated marked distention of the stomach and the first and second portions of the duodenum with decompression of the third portion; the remainder of the small and large bowel appeared normal. Upper endoscopy showed complete obstruction at the second portion of the duodenum. Biopsies from the stomach and proximal duodenum demonstrated chronic gastritis and mildly active duodenitis proximal to the stricture, without evidence of granulomas, viral inclusions, or malignancy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Gastrointestinal tumors were considered in the setting of obstruction but no malignancy was identified on biopsy — “There was no evidence of … malignancy.”

title: Severe functional ischaemic mitral regurgitation: is functional a misnomer for a dysfunctional valve? A case report
case_prompt:
A 63-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy and New York Heart Association class IV symptoms, on maximal medical therapy, was referred after 22 days in an intensive care unit for inability to wean from dobutamine support (INTERMACS III), with evidence of low cardiac output (acute on chronic kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and respiratory failure). 

On transfer, he was bed-bound, afebrile, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, heart rate 90 beats/min (on dobutamine), and oxygen saturation 95% on 40% FiO2 by facemask. Cardiovascular examination revealed a 2/6 apical systolic murmur and signs of volume overload with low output. 

His history included a myocardial infarction at age 48 (managed conservatively), coronary artery bypass grafting with left internal mammary artery grafts and left ventricular aneurysmectomy 6 months earlier, stage IV chronic kidney disease (peak creatinine 3.0 mg/dL, eGFR 21 mL/min/1.73 m²), and obesity (BMI 30.4 kg/m²). The most recent angiogram before surgery showed patent left main, mid left anterior descending artery occlusion, atheromatous circumflex and right coronary arteries, and 70% stenosis of the posterior descending artery. 

Laboratory tests on transfer showed B-natriuretic peptide 1328 pg/mL, INR 1.44, albumin 2.9 g/dL, total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase 114 U/L, urea 98 mg/dL, creatinine 2.3 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, high-sensitivity troponin 79 ng/L, and CK-MB 1.8 IU/L. Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly and interstitial pulmonary oedema. 

Transthoracic echocardiography was suboptimal. Transoesophageal echocardiography showed a dilated left ventricle (end-diastolic diameter 72 mm), ejection fraction ~20% on dobutamine, and a severe, centrally directed mitral regurgitant jet from A2–P2 attributed to annular dilatation and leaflet tethering (Carpentier types I and IIIb). The MR was considered disproportionate, with an effective regurgitant orifice area of 80 mm² and a regurgitant volume of 76 mL. Systolic pulmonary artery pressure was 60 mmHg. Left atrial haemodynamic tracing demonstrated giant V waves up to 65 mmHg (mean 32 mmHg).
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Severe ischaemic cardiomyopathy was considered the primary cause of decompensation — “The differential diagnosis for intractable heart failure was attributed predominantly to the underlying severe ischaemic cardiomyopathy …” 2. Stage IV chronic kidney disease was thought to contribute to low output and end-organ dysfunction — “The differential diagnosis for intractable heart failure was attributed predominantly to … stage IV chronic kidney disease …” 3. Primary (degenerative) MR was excluded based on echocardiographic features of annular dilatation and tethering without leaflet prolapse — “… severe … MR … attributed to annular dilatation and decreased leaflet mobility (Carpentier types I and III-b).”

title: Pigmented lesion in the anterior chamber angle following multiple trans-scleral diode laser photocoagulation for congenital glaucoma
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 4-year-old girl with bilateral congenital glaucoma underwent examination under anesthesia. Three years earlier, she had trabeculotomy and trabeculectomy in both eyes, and two years ago she suffered a retinal detachment in the right eye. The left eye had also undergone three sessions of transscleral cyclophotocoagulation. She was on topical timolol/dorzolamide twice daily, apraclonidine thrice daily, and latanoprost nightly in the left eye. There was no history of intraocular inflammation.

Visual acuity was count fingers in the right eye and no light perception in the left eye. Examination of the right eye revealed a quiet eye, horizontal corneal diameter 11.5 mm, deep anterior chamber, cataractous lens, corectopia, and chronic retinal detachment. Examination of the left eye showed a quiet eye with white conjunctiva, corneal diameter 12.5 mm with a faint diffuse scar, deep anterior chamber, clear lens, and a healthy optic disc (cup–disc ratio 0.5).

Gonioscopy of the left eye revealed several quadrants of densely hyperpigmented, dark brown, raised mounds within the trabecular meshwork, resembling iris tissue. No retrocorneal pigment deposition or iris transillumination defects were noted. Anterior segment OCT showed a deep anterior chamber, open angle recess, slightly posteriorly bowed iris, and confirmed elevated tissue within the angle. No ciliary body or iris masses were seen. Intraocular pressure in the left eye was 11 mmHg, and axial length was stable at 24.70 mm. Six months later, the appearance of the angle structures remained unchanged.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of pigment dispersion syndrome — “in our child, there was no iris atrophy or corneal endothelial pigment to suggest a pigmentary type of glaucoma.” 2. Consideration of uveitic hyperpigmentation — lack of any history or signs of intraocular inflammation made this unlikely. 3. Consideration of melanoma or iris/ciliary body tumor — “No ciliary body or iris masses were identified” on clinical exam or OCT. 4. Reasoning for proliferation rather than pigment deposit — “the lesions in the angle were raised and mound-like rather than flat, suggesting a proliferative lesion rather than purely collection of pigment,” and OCT showed a solid-appearing lesion.

title: A Rare Case of Docetaxel-Induced Hydrocephalus Presenting with Gait Disturbances Mimicking and Coexisting with Taxane-Associated Polyneuropathy: The Relevance of Differential Diagnosis, Clinical Assessment, and Response to Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of chronic progressive gait disturbances that began approximately 1 month after completing four cycles of docetaxel-based chemotherapy for breast cancer 2 years earlier. Before chemotherapy, she had walked normally. At the time of chemotherapy, she developed mild, distal extremity numbness that remained stable over 2 years. She denied headache, dizziness, speech changes, cognitive difficulties, visual loss, abnormal movements, or bladder or bowel symptoms, and there was no evidence of cancer relapse.

Electrophysiological studies were inconclusive but indicated polyneuropathy. Despite stable sensory deficits, her gait disturbance continued to worsen, prompting neurologic evaluation. On examination, she walked with short steps, rotational instability, and an increased base of support; the Romberg test was negative. There was mild impairment of superficial and deep sensation in both feet and hands. Cranial nerves, strength, deep tendon reflexes, cerebellar testing, and sphincter function were normal. Cognitive testing, including the Mini-Mental State Examination and verbal fluency, was within normal limits.

Brain MRI showed marked enlargement of the supratentorial and infratentorial ventricles without cortical atrophy and no evidence of metastatic or meningeal disease. A lumbar puncture was performed, and 30 mL of CSF was drained. Gait was assessed before and 24 hours after the tap: a 10-m walking test demonstrated a clear reduction in the number of steps and time to completion, and the number of steps needed for a 360° rotation decreased. CSF cytology was unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Polyneuropathy was initially suspected given docetaxel exposure and electrophysiological findings, but was deemed unlikely as “the gait disorder observed did not resemble a typical steppage polyneuropathic gait, but it was rather characterized by small steps and enlarged sustentation base and rotational difficulties, all features observed classically in patients with normal pressure hydrocephalus.” 2. Brain metastases were excluded because “there was no evidence of malignant brain or meningeal seed.” 3. Carcinomatous meningitis was ruled out as “Cytology of CSF was otherwise inconspicuous.” 4. Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration was considered but deemed unlikely since MRI and examination lacked cerebellar findings and “MRI is mandatory to confirm the diagnosis and rule out other causes of gait disturbances, such as brain metastasis or paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration.” 5. The ventricular enlargement represented true hydrocephalus rather than ex vacuo change, supported by the finding that “Brain MRI revealed a marked hydrocephalus with a dramatic enlargement of the supra- and infratentorial ventricular system and no cortical atrophy.”

title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis: A Case Report
case_prompt: A 54‐year‐old Hispanic woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and peripheral vascular disease presented with a 3‐day history of severe watery diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and altered mental status. Her medications included metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril, metoprolol, pravastatin, aspirin, hydrochlorothiazide, and nightly insulin detemir; she had not administered her prandial insulin for the preceding 24 hours due to poor oral intake. In the emergency department, her serum glucose was 47 mg/dL, for which glucagon was administered. She was hypotensive (70/39 mmHg), confused, and disoriented, with dry mucous membranes and decreased bowel sounds. Shortly after arrival, she became unresponsive and developed pulseless electrical activity; return of spontaneous circulation was achieved after 3 minutes of cardiopulmonary resuscitation, including intubation and mechanical ventilation. In the intensive care unit, her blood pressure was 98/37 mmHg on norepinephrine (0.3 µg/kg/min). Laboratory studies showed arterial pH 6.57, serum bicarbonate 2 mEq/L, anion gap 30 mmol/L, osmolar gap 21 mOsm/kg, lactate 16.3 mmol/L, hemoglobin A1c 7.3%, and creatinine 8.07 mg/dL (baseline 0.86 mg/dL two months earlier). A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed only nonspecific gallbladder wall thickening. Vasopressin was added to maintain a mean arterial pressure above 60 mmHg.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Severe sepsis and septic shock were initially considered because of her hypotension, acidemia, and metabolic derangement — “Originally, it was felt that her hypotension, acidemia, and severe metabolic derangement could be consistent with severe sepsis and septic shock.” 2. Cardiogenic shock from an acute coronary event was also considered given her cardiac arrest — “An acute coronary event leading to cardiogenic shock was also considered in the setting of her cardiac arrest.”

title: Upper Extremity Compartment Syndrome in a Patient with Acute Gout Attack but without Trauma or Other Typical Causes
case_prompt:
A 30-year-old right-hand–dominant Polynesian man (BMI 42.6) with a history of crystal-proven gout and poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presented with progressive right forearm, hand, and shoulder swelling and pain. Four days after two days of light weight exercise, he noted moderate forearm swelling and pain unrelieved by his usual oral anti-inflammatories. Four days later, during a long car trip, his right shoulder pain and forearm/hand swelling worsened. In the emergency department he received IV ketorolac, oral prednisone, and tramadol. The next morning he was admitted for presumed severe acute gout flare and emerging compartment syndrome of the right forearm and hand.

He was afebrile, blood pressure 140/73 mm Hg, heart rate 89 bpm. Examination showed tense volar forearm compartments, reduced finger sensation, decreased capillary refill, and severe pain with passive finger stretch, with overlying erythema suggestive of cellulitis.

Laboratory studies revealed serum uric acid 7.7 mg/dL, leukocytosis (13 800/μL), normal initial blood glucose (84 mg/dL), and mildly elevated CK (245 U/L). Tests for rheumatoid factor, HIV, and blood toxicology were negative. CT of the right upper extremity demonstrated fluid in the olecranon bursa and elbow joint; MRI of the shoulder was unremarkable. Aspiration of the elbow joint yielded monosodium urate crystals and a white-cell count of 91 650/mm3; wrist aspiration showed calcium pyrophosphate crystals with no bacterial growth. CT angiogram and Doppler ultrasound excluded vascular pathology.

With clinical signs of compartment syndrome and compartment pressures of 20–24 mm Hg in the volar forearm and 45–48 mm Hg in the carpal tunnel and palm, urgent fasciotomies of the volar compartments and carpal tunnel were performed. Intraoperatively the volar forearm muscles were tense, bulging, and contractile; there was no necrosis, pus, or odor. Small 0.25–0.5 mm tophi were noted in the synovial sheaths of the flexor tendons. Wounds were dressed with negative-pressure therapy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Tophi-induced reduced fascial compliance — “…studies showing that tophi do not accumulate significantly in the fascia and the fascia is not rendered thicker or stiffer by gout.” 2. Peripheral vascular disease leading to ACS — “…there was also no evidence that our patient had vascular disease.” 3. Saturday night palsy from toxic ingestion causing ACS — “…ruled out by our patient’s negative blood toxicology screen.”

title: Severe Hypercholesterolemia: A Unique Presentation of Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma in a Patient with Neurofibromatosis Type 1
case_prompt:
A 59-year-old Hispanic man with known neurofibromatosis type 1 presented with a 1-month history of episodic epigastric pain, pruritus, a 15-pound weight loss, and progressive jaundice. On examination he was deeply icteric; pulse 118 /min, respiratory rate 20 /min, blood pressure 116/80 mm Hg, temperature 98.3 °F. The abdomen was mildly distended with an enlarged, non-tender liver and widespread excoriations. His skin showed >10 cutaneous fibromas, multiple café-au-lait spots >1.5 cm, and extensive axillary freckling, meeting criteria for NF 1. 

Initial laboratory studies demonstrated a cholestatic liver profile with normal blood counts. Abdominal ultrasound showed marked intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary dilatation with the proximal common bile duct measuring 17 mm. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 4.5 × 5 × 3.4 cm hypoenhancing soft-tissue mass in the porta hepatis encasing the portal vein and common hepatic artery and extending toward the pancreatic head. MRCP confirmed an abrupt occlusion in the mid common bile duct; the pancreatic duct was normal. Additional imaging identified a 5 cm hypoenhancing splenic mass and a 4.5 × 4.5 × 2.8 cm retroperitoneal hypodense mass.

On hospital day 2, a lipid panel showed total cholesterol >1,000 mg/dL, HDL 19 mg/dL, triglycerides 233 mg/dL, and LDL >1,000 mg/dL; a repeat test confirmed these values. ERCP demonstrated a tight 6 cm proximal CBD stricture with marked biliary dilatation. After biliary sphincterotomy and placement of self-expanding metallic stents, the patient’s jaundice improved and serum cholesterol levels declined steadily.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pancreatic carcinoma — “Obstructive causes reported in the literature to produce hypercholesterolemia include pancreatic carcinoma…” 2. Primary biliary cirrhosis — “…and primary biliary cirrhosis, but it has not been known to be associated with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.”

title: Delayed diagnosis of avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder and autism spectrum disorder in a 14‐year‐old boy
case_prompt: A 14-year-old boy was referred for failure to gain weight and restrictive eating behaviors since early childhood. Parents described early breastfeeding difficulties and persistent aversions to certain textures and smells. His diet was limited to very plain and smoothly textured foods (for example, burgers without toppings, purees) and a narrow list of items (for example, Cheerios, chicken nuggets). He displayed rigid routines (insisting on the same seat at dinner, folding clothes in a particular way) and marked distress when routines were disrupted. He was anxious, with worsening anxiety after parental separation at age 5, and had inattention affecting school. His weight had tracked along the 3rd percentile until age 5, then plateaued for 8 years; bone age was delayed by one year. He was seen 2 months earlier by a pediatric endocrinologist, with no clear endocrine diagnosis. One month before admission, a psychiatrist’s formulation included sensory integration disorder, communication and other learning disorders, eating disorder, and autism spectrum disorder. Occupational therapy and psychoeducational assessments were recommended but not completed. On admission, weight was 24.4 kg (<0.01 percentile), height 143.5 cm (0.39 percentile), BMI 11.8 kg/m2 (≈68% of ideal weight). Laboratory studies showed no electrolyte abnormalities. The gastroenterology service did not find an organic cause for his failure to thrive.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Organic gastrointestinal or metabolic disease was evaluated — “the gastroenterology service was consulted and did not feel that his longstanding failure to thrive was organic in nature.” 2. Sensory integration disorder was in the differential — “psychiatrist’s formulation included a broad differential, including sensory integration disorder...” 3. Communication and other learning disorders were considered — “psychiatrist’s formulation included... communication and other learning disorders...”

title: Sporotrichoid lymphocutaneous pattern in a fish‐merchant under immunosuppressant medications: Clues to differential diagnoses
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old woman from Eastern Nepal presented with a 10-day history of painful erythematous nodules along the radial border of her left hand, forearm, and arm, and a 5-day history of swelling of the left hand. For the preceding 10 months, she had been treated with oral corticosteroids, methotrexate, and hydroxychloroquine for presumed pain in the left arm and forearm. She denied any antecedent trauma.

On examination, there were six tender, warm, erythematous nodules in a linear distribution along the radial border of the left upper extremity, with edema extending from the proximal third of the fingers to the wrist joint. Vital signs were within normal limits.

Laboratory investigations revealed a normal complete blood count, blood glucose, chest radiograph, urine microscopy, and serum cortisol. C-reactive protein was elevated; rheumatoid factor and serologies for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were negative. Liver transaminases were mildly elevated (1.6× upper limit of normal), and vitamin D level was low. A wound swab culture was sterile, and a microfilaria test was negative.

An incisional skin biopsy was performed and sent for histopathology and tissue culture for bacteria and fungi. Histopathological examination showed lymphohistiocytic infiltrates admixed with neutrophils and activated macrophages forming epithelioid aggregates and ill-defined granulomas, with acid-fast bacilli on staining. A slit-skin smear for Mycobacterium leprae and sputum examination for acid-fast bacilli were negative. Culture of the biopsy specimen yielded no growth.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Streptococcal infection was considered because of the acute lymphocutaneous nodules — “The diagnosis of sporotrichoid lymphocutaneous pattern secondary to infection (probably streptococcal infection or sporotrichosis or atypical mycobacteria) was made.” 2. Sporotrichosis was considered due to the linear nodular spread along lymphatics — “The diagnosis of sporotrichoid lymphocutaneous pattern secondary to infection (probably streptococcal infection or sporotrichosis or atypical mycobacteria) was made.” 3. Atypical mycobacterial infection was also considered early given chronicity and nodular lymphangitis — “The diagnosis of sporotrichoid lymphocutaneous pattern secondary to infection (probably streptococcal infection or sporotrichosis or atypical mycobacteria) was made.” 4. Leprosy was excluded by negative slit-skin smear — “the Slit skin smear for M. leprae and sputum for AFB were negative.” 5. Atypical mycobacteria other than M. marinum were less likely because there was no growth on standard culture media — “Culture revealed no growth.”

title: Drug-induced acute pneumonitis following initiation of flecainide therapy after pulmonary vein isolation ablation in a patient with mitral stenosis and previous chronic amiodarone use
case_prompt:
A 70-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic mitral stenosis status post percutaneous mitral valvotomy in 2012, hypertension, persistent atrial fibrillation, and prior amiodarone-induced hepatotoxicity underwent successful radiofrequency pulmonary vein isolation for symptomatic atrial fibrillation and was discharged off antiarrhythmics. Two days later, she was admitted in atrial fibrillation, converted to sinus rhythm with 360 J, and started on flecainide 100 mg every 12 hours. 

Approximately five hours after the first dose of flecainide, she developed fever (38.7 °C), chills, and a dry cough, and her oxygen requirement increased from 2 L/min by nasal cannula to high-flow nasal cannula. Laboratory studies revealed a leukocytosis of 12 000 cells/mm3. Portable chest radiography showed increased interstitial markings compared with baseline. She received 20 mg intravenous furosemide, and her symptoms initially improved.

Three hours after the second 100 mg dose of flecainide, fever recurred with worsening dyspnea, and her oxygen support escalated to bilevel positive airway pressure. Leukocytosis rose to 16 000 cells/mm3. Computed tomography of the chest demonstrated diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities predominantly in the upper lobes, multifocal perilobular consolidation in the lower lobes, and mild traction bronchiectasis and bronchiolectasis. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 65–70%, no mitral valve stenosis, and a small, collapsed inferior vena cava; serum brain natriuretic peptide was 186 pg/mL. Flecainide was discontinued.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cardiogenic pulmonary edema was considered — “Cardiogenic pulmonary edema was eliminated based on the absence of elevated filling pressures and failure to improve clinically with effective diuresis.”

title: Post-traumatic keratoacanthoma of the hand: A case report
case_prompt: A 59-year-old man with no significant medical or surgical history presented with a 6-week history of an enlarging, painful mass on the dorsum of his right hand. Six weeks earlier, he had sustained a penetrating injury from a galvanized culvert, resulting in a small laceration. Despite antibiotic therapy for presumed Staphylococcus abscess, the lesion continued to enlarge. On examination, there was a 2.5 × 2.0 cm solid, mobile mass over the dorsal right hand. The lesion was nonpulsatile, nonfriable, nonfluctuant, not adherent to underlying structures, and appeared limited to the cutaneous and subcutaneous tissues. There was no surrounding erythema, no expressible drainage, and no palpable lymphadenopathy. A central area of keratinaceous-appearing material was noted. Radiographs of the hand were unremarkable, and routine laboratory values were within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection was considered because of the penetrating injury and painful swelling but was excluded clinically — “the differential diagnosis was limited and included infection…however, the clinical examination was reassuring in terms of ruling out infection.” 2. Epidermal or epidermoid inclusion cyst was included in the differential given the history of trauma and keratinaceous core — “the differential diagnosis was limited and included…epidermal or epidermoid inclusion cyst.” 3. Keratoacanthoma was considered due to the rapid enlargement and central keratinaceous-appearing debris — “With the appearance of a central area of keratinaceous-appearing debris and rapid enlargement, a keratoacanthoma was also considered.”

title: Polycythemia vera with acute coronary syndrome and bleeding as initial presentation: A case report and literature review
case_prompt:
A 73-year-old man with a history of heart failure with recovered ejection fraction (60%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes mellitus presented with 2 days of severe, exertional, pressure-like chest pain rated 9/10. His physical examination was unremarkable. Vital signs were: heart rate 60 bpm, blood pressure 119/58 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. Initial laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 16 g/dL, platelet count 380 K/cm3, white-cell count 8.1 K/cm3, creatinine 2 mg/dL, high-sensitivity troponin >5000 ng/L, and B-type natriuretic peptide 2810 pg/mL. He was admitted for management of non–ST–elevation myocardial infarction and started on a heparin drip and antiplatelet therapy. 

On hospital day 2, he developed severe left lower abdominal and thigh pain. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed marked swelling of the left psoas and iliacus muscles with heterogeneous density, consistent with hemorrhage. Anticoagulation and antiplatelet agents were discontinued, and a CT angiogram revealed active contrast extravasation into the left psoas muscle. Interventional radiology performed angiography, which showed no arterial source of active bleeding. After stabilization, his hemoglobin rose to 18 g/dL.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia was considered given heparin use — “Heparin induced thrombocytopenia was also less likely as the platelet level remained normal.”

title:
Rapid Loss and Recovery of Vision Following Steroid Treatment in
Orbital Myositis: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old African American man with hypertension, COPD, mitral valve prolapse, CKD, osteoarthritis, PTSD, two prior strokes, nephrolithiasis, and chronic back pain presented to the emergency department with 24 hours of worsening headaches, retro-orbital pressure, decreased vision in the left eye, white flashes and floaters, and palinopsia in both eyes. He was afebrile, without leukocytosis, and other signs of infection.  
On ophthalmologic evaluation, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 OD and 20/60 OS. Extraocular movements were full, intraocular pressures were 20 mm Hg OD and 21 mm Hg OS, and there was a 1+ relative afferent pupillary defect OS. CT of the head was unremarkable. Slit-lamp and fundus examinations were normal except for 2+ nuclear sclerotic cataracts bilaterally. He was discharged with a presumptive diagnosis of migraine with aura.  

Ten days later, he returned with bilateral worsening vision loss, headaches, retro-orbital pressure, and periorbital swelling. Visual acuity was 20/150 OD and counting fingers at 1 m OS. Intraocular pressures were 26 mm Hg OD and 27 mm Hg OS. Extraocular movements were reduced in all gazes. Hertel measurements were 22 mm OD and 26 mm OS (base 118 mm).  
MRI of the orbits showed significant proptosis with straightened optic nerves and enlargement of all extraocular muscles, including their tendon sheaths. Serum IgG, ACE, TSI, TSH, and T4 levels were all within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Thyroid eye disease — “normal thyroid function tests and thyroid antibodies made this diagnosis less likely.” 2. Orbital cellulitis — “ruled out due to lack of fever, leukocytosis, or other signs of infectious etiology.”

title: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, primary varicella zoster virus coinfection, and a polymicrobial ventilator-associated tracheobronchitis in an adult immunocompetent male: a case report
case_prompt: A 48-year-old man with no prior comorbidities was transferred to the ICU after emergent craniotomy and intracranial pressure monitoring for traumatic epidural hematoma and subarachnoid bleeding. He had a 3–4-day history of fever and nonproductive cough following contact with a person known to have COVID-19; SARS-CoV-2 PCR on admission was positive. Seventy-two hours after ICU admission and decompressive craniectomy, he developed a maculopapular erythematous exanthem predominantly on the trunk and extremities, with sparing of mucosae, palms, and soles. Over the next 24–48 hours, the rash evolved into a papulovesicular eruption. Concurrently, he became hypotensive requiring high-dose vasopressors and developed progressive respiratory failure with rising oxygen requirements. Routine laboratory tests showed mildly elevated inflammatory markers and lymphopenia. Arterial blood gas on FiO2 0.55 revealed an oxygenation index of 490. Initial chest CT showed no infiltrates; on day 5, repeat CT demonstrated patchy ground-glass opacities and consolidations typical of COVID-19 affecting 23% of lung volume, along with small bilateral pleural effusions. At that time, his oxygenation index had fallen to 97.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Varicella zoster virus infection was considered given the development of a papulovesicular eruption — “However, because of the cutaneous manifestations, varicella zoster virus (VZV) was also considered.”

title: Two Cases of Delayed Diagnosis of Leprosy in Mauritania
case_prompt:
A 17-year-old farmer from Kankossa, Mauritania, presented with severe chronic arthralgia of both hands and bilateral perforating ulcers on the soles, evolving over 7 years. His symptoms began with hypopigmented maculopapular skin lesions and paresthesia at the finger and toe tips and on the plantar surfaces. He had seen multiple dermatologists and traditional healers without improvement despite analgesic, antibiotic, and topical therapies. 

On examination, there was distal phalangeal atrophy with severe hand deformities, hypochromic anesthetic macules on the skin, atrophy of the metatarsals and phalanges with marked foot deformity, and bilateral plantar perforating ulcers. Palpable thickening of the ulnar and popliteal nerve trunks was noted. Visual acuity was normal. Routine laboratory tests were within normal limits. Radiographs of the hands and feet demonstrated osteoporosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Rheumatoid arthritis or deforming polyarthritis was considered because of chronic joint pain and finger atrophy — “articular pain, atrophy of the fingers, and polymorphic cutaneous manifestations often evoke rheumatoid arthritis or deforming polyarthritis.” 2. Diabetic neuropathic ulcer was initially suspected due to the plantar perforations — “The perforating plantar ulcer in our first patient initially evoked a diabetic foot, but its bilateral character and hypertrophy of the ulnar and popliteal nerve trunks helped orient our diagnosis toward leprosy.” 3. Leprosy was confirmed by the combination of anesthetic hypochromic skin lesions and nerve thickening — “The hypochromic macules alone were not suggestive of leprosy, but their anesthetic character associated with nerve hypertrophy indicated leprosy in our first case.”

title: Lesions more than cerebellum in a puerpera of acute cerebellitis: concurrent encephalitis, not a subtype of ADEM
case_prompt:
A 29-year-old woman, 20 days postpartum after a cesarean delivery at 35.6 weeks’ gestation, presented with a 2-day history of fever (up to 38.5°C), body aches, sore throat, and a mild cough. On admission, her temperature was 39.8°C, pulse 104/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and blood pressure 104/68 mmHg. A complete blood count showed a white-cell count of 9.3×10^9/L with 78.3% neutrophils. She was treated with oseltamivir and paracetamol. 

On the third day of fever, she developed generalized convulsions and an altered mental state. Neurologic examination revealed dysarthria, nystagmus, and ataxia of the trunk and all extremities. Meningeal signs were absent.

On hospital day 4, brain MRI demonstrated well-defined lesions in the bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles and the splenium of the corpus callosum that were hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on diffusion-weighted imaging with low apparent diffusion coefficient values. There was no contrast enhancement. MR angiography and venography were normal.

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 29 white cells per mm^3; Gram stain was negative, and tests for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and VDRL were negative. Electroencephalography revealed diffuse slow waves with focal spike waves.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Other causes of reversible splenial lesions — “The most established and frequent causes of reversible focal lesions of the SCC are viral encephalitis, antiepileptic drug toxicity/withdrawal, and hypoglycemic encephalopathy... Reversible lesion of SCC was consistent with the infectious disorder and good recovery in this case.”

title: A Clear Cell Sarcoma Case: A Diagnostic and Treatment Challenge, with a Promising Response to Trabectedin
case_prompt: A 58-year-old woman with no significant medical history noted a left laterocervical mass enlarging over 3 months. CT imaging showed a 25 × 28 × 44 mm laterocervical mass. In September 2019, a core-needle biopsy was reported as paraganglioma. Octreotide scintigraphy revealed uptake in the left cervical region and in the body of the stomach; endoscopic biopsies of the stomach were negative. In October 2019, the cervical mass was resected, and the initial diagnosis was extraosseous Ewing sarcoma. Upon referral, an anatomopathological review described a well-demarcated tumor composed of oval cells with clear eosinophilic cytoplasm arranged in nests separated by thin fibrous septa. Immunohistochemistry was positive for S100, SOX10, synaptophysin, and CD57; negative for Melan-A, HMB45, MITF, CD99, and multiple other markers. FISH detected an EWSR1 rearrangement that next-generation sequencing confirmed as an EWSR1-ATF1 t(12;22) fusion. A diagnosis of malignant gastrointestinal neuroectodermal tumor (GNET) was rendered. In January 2020, PET-CT showed uptake in the left laterocervical surgical bed consistent with local recurrence.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraganglioma was initially diagnosed on needle biopsy, but this was reconsidered when the resected tumor showed a distinct histologic and immunophenotypic profile — “A core needle biopsy ... led to the diagnosis of a paraganglioma”; “An anatomopathological review revealed ... oval cells with a clear eosinophilic and poorly demarcated cytoplasm.”

title: Calcific myofibrosis due to pentazocine abuse: a case report
case_prompt:
A 42‐year‐old right‐handed man was admitted with a 6-year history of painless, progressive stiffness and wasting of the back and proximal limb muscles. He reported increasing difficulty with walking (short‐stepping gait), rising from supine, bending in any direction, lifting his arms, and abducting his thighs beyond 20°. He did not experience weakness, sensory changes, spasms, or pain.

Six years earlier, he began self-injecting pentazocine (60 mg daily, intramuscularly into buttocks and upper arms) combined with promethazine for abdominal pain and continued in a dependent fashion. He never injected the thighs, calves, abdomen, shoulder girdle, or forearms. He stopped the injections 6 months after symptom onset. He had a 13-year history of alcohol dependence but had been abstinent for at least 6 years. He was normotensive and non-diabetic.

On examination, there was marked wasting and woody induration of the deltoids, biceps, glutei, quadriceps, and paraspinal muscles. Active range of motion was markedly reduced: arm abduction to 45–50°, thigh abduction to 20°, elbow flexion to 15°, and thigh flexion to 10°. Distal joint movements were normal. Muscle power was full within the limited ranges, and sensation was intact. Other systems were unremarkable.

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, liver and renal function, serum calcium, phosphate, and creatine phosphokinase, were within normal limits. Roentgenograms of the lumbar spine, pelvis, thighs, knees, chest, and shoulders revealed multiple soft‐tissue calcifications with muscle hyperdensity. Electromyography of affected muscles was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ankylosing spondylitis was considered but excluded — “Ankylosing spondylitis was ruled out as there was no involvement of the joints.” 2. Stiff-man syndrome was considered but excluded — “Stiff-man syndrome presents with spasms and cramps, and usual presentation is after middle age. These features ruled out the possibility of this syndrome.” 3. Myositis ossificans was considered but deemed unlikely — “The possibility of myositis ossificans was unlikely, as the present case was of late onset and was characterized by the absence of skeletal abnormality.” 4. Hypoparathyroidism was considered but excluded — “Normal serum calcium and phosphate levels excluded hypoparathyroidism.” 5. Drug-induced calcific myofibrosis was favored — “Pentazocine-induced calcific myofibrosis was a strong possibility in view of the history of pentazocine abuse, calcified muscles and the clinical presentation.”

title: A case report of infective endocarditis in a 52-year-old female with unrepaired tricuspid atresia and d-transposition of the great arteries
case_prompt: A 52-year-old woman with known unrepaired Type II tricuspid atresia with d-transposition of the great arteries, hypoplastic right ventricle, unrestricted atrial and ventricular septal defects, and multi-level pulmonic stenosis presented with sudden-onset severe right upper quadrant abdominal pain without nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or dysuria. Two months earlier, she had flu-like symptoms treated with doxycycline, and she reported poor dentition with no dental care for two years. On examination, her blood pressure was 90/54 mmHg, she was afebrile, heart rate was 80 bpm, and oxygen saturation was 79% on room air. A grade-2 systolic ejection murmur was heard at the left upper sternal border. She had cyanotic fingers bilaterally but no Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, or splinter hemorrhages. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 17.9 g/dL and C-reactive protein 2.2 mg/dL; lactate, white blood cell count, and creatinine were within reference ranges. Computed tomography of the abdomen demonstrated an acute right renal infarct; cerebral computed tomography was negative. She was anticoagulated with heparin.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Embolic phenomenon from an intracardiac thrombus was considered due to the acute renal infarct and prompted heparin anticoagulation: “Computed tomography abdomen showed an acute right renal infarct… and she was anticoagulated with heparin.”

title: Plantar nerve neural fibrolipoma: rare neuroma arising from the plantar nerve of the heel
case_prompt:
A 46-year-old healthy woman was referred for evaluation of a painful soft-tissue mass on the right plantar midfoot. She first noticed the swelling 2–3 years earlier; it had enlarged and become increasingly painful over the past 4 months, and she described walking on a hard stone. There was no history of trauma or family history of similar problems. On inspection, the overlying skin was normal without signs of infection. Examination revealed bilateral planovalgus feet with bilateral hallux valgus (worse on the right), and a tender, subcutaneous mass on the right plantar midfoot associated with sensitivity and numbness.  

To provide temporary relief, she received an ultrasound-guided injection of 80 mg depo-medrone and 1 ml of 1% lignocaine into the second metatarsophalangeal region. Plain radiographs showed no bony injury or malignancy. Ultrasound demonstrated a well-defined, hypoechoic tender lesion within the plantar subcutaneous tissue, measuring approximately 17 × 7 × 11 mm, extending deep to but not arising from the plantar fascia. Contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a 2 cm × 5 mm tubular oblique lesion in the plantar subcutaneous fat and ruled out sinus tarsi syndrome or internal derangements. She was provided with orthotic medial arch support and referred to a sarcoma multidisciplinary team, which found no radiologic features of sarcoma. Despite these measures, she continued to experience sharp, stabbing pain (10/10) and elected to proceed with surgical excision biopsy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Plantar fibromatosis was excluded because “the lesion extended deep to the plantar fascia not arising from it, hence ruling out plantar fibromatosis.” 2. Sarcoma was considered but excluded as the sarcoma MDT concluded that “there were no radiological features of a sarcoma.” 4. Achilles tendonitis, retroachilles bursitis, and retrocalcaneal bursitis were ruled out because “the pain was focused on the plantar aspect of the heel and not primarily in the posterior heel.” 5. Sever disease (calcaneal apophysitis) was excluded given that “the patient was not an adolescent.” 6. Sinus tarsi syndrome and internal derangements were ruled out on MRI which “was also able to rule out sinus tarsi syndrome and any internal derangements.”

title: Cerebral granulomatosis as a manifestation of Crohn’s disease
case_prompt:
A 39-year-old right-handed woman was evaluated for recurrent seizures that began in early 2014. The initial events were dyscognitive and progressed to secondary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Some weeks before the first seizure, she developed subacute bifrontal headaches and noted progressive short-term memory impairment. In May 2015, her husband observed a transient personality change with disinhibited behavior, which did not recur. 

Her medical history was notable for Crohn’s disease diagnosed in 2006 by ileocolonoscopy, treated with steroids and mesalazine until 2008, then methotrexate (15 mg weekly) through 2016, with no gastrointestinal symptoms since 2013. She smoked 25 pack-years and had no other comorbidities, family history of neurological disease, or exposures. 

In early 2014, a transient dyscognitive event was interpreted as a transient ischemic attack, and a head CT excluded hemorrhage. By August 2014, after a secondarily generalized seizure, MRI showed multiple supratentorial Gd-enhancing cortical lesions. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed pleocytosis (14 cells/μl) with negative oligoclonal bands. Bronchoalveolar lavage and chest CT were normal. Laboratory studies showed normal levels of IL-2 receptor, ACE, neopterin, calcium, ANCA, ANA, and antiphospholipid antibodies; an interferon-gamma release assay was negative.

Two months later, MRI demonstrated progression of cortical lesions. Methotrexate was supplemented with prednisolone, leading to stable lesion load on MRI in February 2015. Seizures recurred intermittently without changes in therapy. In August 2016, MRI revealed increased bihemispheric cortical and deep gray matter hemorrhagic lesions, gadolinium enhancement in multiple cortical and limbic regions, and bilateral hippocampal atrophy with hyperintensity. Repeat CSF showed pleocytosis (8 cells/μl), elevated protein, and now positive oligoclonal bands. Extensive serologic and CSF testing for neural autoantibodies (including paraneoplastic and autoimmune encephalitis panels) was negative. Complement levels, rheumatoid factor, and immunoglobulins were normal. Neuropsychological testing showed depressive symptoms with attentional, executive, and memory deficits.

In September 2016, further evaluation included CSF testing for Borrelia burgdorferi, Treponema pallidum, PCR for HSV-1/2, VZV, Tropheryma whipplei, and HIV (all negative). Ultrasonography of extra- and intracranial arteries showed no large-vessel vasculitis. Sarcoidosis biomarkers, PR3-ANCA, p-ANCA, and blood cultures were negative. Fluorescein angiography of the retina excluded small-vessel vasculitis or Susac’s syndrome. Given the progressive cortical lesions and nondiagnostic noninvasive workup, a stereotactic brain biopsy was planned.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Large-vessel vasculitis was excluded by vascular ultrasound—“An ultrasonography of the extra- and intracranial arteries … showed no signs of a vasculitis of the large vessels.” 2. Susac’s syndrome and small-vessel retinal vasculitis were ruled out—“A fluorescein angiography … revealed normal results.”

title: Oral malignant melanoma: A case report with review of literature
case_prompt:
A 42-year-old woman presented with a painless, pigmented growth on the left anterior maxillary gingiva for 3 months. The lesion began as a pinpoint spot and gradually enlarged, extending to the palatal gingiva. She reported no systemic illness or head and neck trauma. Vital signs were within normal limits.

Extraoral examination revealed a tender, firm left submandibular lymph node fixed to the mandible and a separate 3 × 3 cm firm, non-tender, mobile right cervical lymph node. Intraoral examination showed a 2 × 3 cm sessile, rough-surfaced, black pigmented lesion on the left maxillary attached gingiva near the central and lateral incisors, extending to the hard-palate rugae and causing displacement of those teeth.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, biochemical tests, and urinalysis, were within normal limits. Intraoral periapical and panoramic radiographs demonstrated a radiolucent area with displacement of the left central and lateral incisors.
