Neck ultrasonography revealed a highly vascular mass with a low-velocity waveform indenting major vessels. Contrast-enhanced CT of the head and neck showed a well-defined, heterogeneously enhancing hypodense nodule in the left premaxillary region without bony erosion, and a 34 × 32 mm necrotic left level II cervical lymphadenopathy compressing the internal jugular vein.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Melanosis was considered as a benign cause of oral pigmentation — “Melanosis is a common benign condition in the oral cavity…” 2. Melanoacanthoma was considered but its benign nature and pattern of rapid centimeter-scale spread differed — “melano acanthoma is also a benign tumor, but it can spread up to centimeters in few weeks…”

title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and Hypertriglyceridemiainduced Pancreatitis: Can the Perfect Storm Happen Twice?
journal: Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives
case_prompt:
A 32-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and dyslipidemia presented with a 2-day history of continuous, progressive epigastric pain that began after a heavy meal. He described the pain as sharp (10/10), radiating to his back, and worsened by leaning back or lying down. He reported nausea and several episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting. He denied recent dietary changes, binge drinking, hospitalizations, or medication changes.  

Home medications included fenofibrate 48 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 30 units at bedtime, and insulin lispro 7 units three times daily. He admitted occasional noncompliance with his medications and poor adherence to a diabetic diet. He led a sedentary lifestyle.  

On examination, temperature was 98.9 °F, heart rate 91 bpm, blood pressure 127/84 mmHg, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/min. He sat leaning forward in mild distress. He was alert and oriented. Lung fields were clear. Cardiac examination was unremarkable. Abdominal examination revealed epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; there was no peripheral edema.  

Chart review revealed a hospitalization 2 years earlier for diabetic ketoacidosis complicated by hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis.  

Laboratory studies on this admission showed a normal complete blood count, blood glucose 256 mg/dL, an elevated anion gap >17, and a beta-hydroxybutyrate level of 74.5 mg/dL. Serum lipase was 257 IU/L. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a normal gallbladder and no dilation of the common bile duct.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of lactic acidosis — “The differential diagnoses for high anion metabolic acidosis would include lactic acidosis…” 2. Consideration of starvation ketoacidosis — “The differential diagnoses for high anion metabolic acidosis would include… starvation…” 3. Consideration of methanol or ethylene glycol ingestion — “…include methanol or ethylene glycol ingestion…” 4. Consideration of aspirin toxicity — “…and aspirin toxicity.” 5. Diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis — “Because of prior history of uncontrolled diabetes, diabetic ketoacidosis was the most likely diagnosis.” 6. Diagnosis of acute pancreatitis — “Because of the character of abdominal pain, and an elevated lipase level that was more than three times the upper limit of normal, a diagnosis of acute pancreatitis was made.” 7. Exclusion of gallstone pancreatitis — “Ultrasonography of the abdomen failed to reveal any evidence of cholelithiasis…” 8. Exclusion of alcohol-induced pancreatitis — “The patient has no history of significant alcohol abuse.”

title: Severe erytrodermic psoriasis in child twins: from clinical-pathological diagnosis to treatment of choice through genetic analyses: two case reports
case_prompt: A 3-year-old Caucasian sister and brother were referred for evaluation of severe, pruritic, nonexudative erythroderma affecting nearly the entire skin surface, ongoing since 8 months of age. At birth their skin and general health were normal, and both had grown regularly. On examination, both children had generalized scaling erythema with severe pruritus and onychodystrophy; the sister also had bilateral lower-lid ectropion. Their father and his twin offspring have plaque psoriasis, and their father's twin sister has psoriatic arthritis. Routine blood tests, metabolic panels and skin‐culture studies were all within normal limits. A punch biopsy of lesional skin was performed for further evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Psoriasis as a cause of childhood erythroderma — “As most of the times, psoriasis is the leading disease among the possible causes of pediatric erythrodermas.”

title: CT findings of pathology proven benign metastasizing leiomyoma
case_prompt: A 46-year-old woman presented for preoperative evaluation before a planned hysterectomy. She reported a 2-year history of dry cough and an unintentional 10-lb weight loss over the past year. Her medical history was unremarkable; she was a non-smoker with no history of cancer, recent travel, or significant occupational exposures. On examination, she was afebrile and denied dyspnea or shortness of breath on exertion. Pulmonary-function testing showed mild nonspecific restrictive and obstructive defects. A preoperative chest radiograph demonstrated innumerable nodular opacities throughout both lungs without lymphadenopathy or osseous lesions. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the chest revealed innumerable soft-tissue–density lesions of varying sizes throughout the lungs, the largest measuring 4 cm in the right infrahilar region, with no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusions, or bone lesions. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a large central pelvic soft-tissue mass measuring 17 × 15 × 13 cm with a necrotic component and multiple thin enhancing septations, as well as a 6.5-cm exophytic right adnexal lesion; there was no fat attenuation, calcification, lymphadenopathy, or osseous involvement.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary bronchogenic carcinoma was considered because “pulmonary nodules… are the most typical radiographic presentation of early lung cancer, and multiple pulmonary nodules of varying sizes may be the first sign of malignancy in a patient without a prior diagnosis” but was less likely given “this patient was not a smoker and did not have significant risk factors for lung metastases or primary bronchogenic malignancy.” 2. Pulmonary metastases from an occult primary tumor were considered since “multiple pulmonary nodules of varying sizes may be the first sign of malignancy in a patient without a prior diagnosis,” but no primary malignancy was known. 3. Metastatic leiomyosarcoma was included in the differential based on the presence of a large pelvic mass and pulmonary nodules, with “these findings… suggested a differential diagnosis of metastatic leiomyosarcoma versus benign metastasizing leiomyoma.” 4. Benign metastasizing leiomyoma was considered because this entity occurs in perimenopausal women with uterine leiomyomas presenting with pulmonary nodules, as noted: “these findings… suggested a differential diagnosis of… benign metastasizing leiomyoma.” 5. Pulmonary granulomas were part of the differential for multiple nodules, though “calcification is rare in the setting of BML,” and no calcifications were seen. 6. Nodular pulmonary sarcoidosis was considered, but “mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy is rare in the case of BML,” and no lymphadenopathy was present. 7. Rheumatoid nodules were listed among causes of pulmonary nodules (“...rheumatoid nodules...”); however, the patient had no history of rheumatoid arthritis. 8. Pulmonary amyloidosis was mentioned (“...and amyloidosis (patients with BML tend to be younger than patients for whom amyloidosis is a consideration)…”), but this patient’s age and presentation were more consistent with BML.

title: Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis of indeterminate cause: a fatal adult case
case_prompt:
A 41-year-old man presented with a 1-month history of daily high-grade fevers, night sweats, a 20-kg weight loss, anorexia, asthenia, progressive lower-limb weakness, and recent jaundice. His history included hypertension managed with captopril, remote heavy alcohol use, and active smoking. On examination, he was febrile (38.0 °C), slightly dehydrated, and icteric; blood pressure was 120/75 mmHg, pulse 106 bpm, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; BMI was 21.6. No lymphadenopathy was noted. Cardiac exam revealed a mild systolic murmur at the mitral area; lung fields were clear. Abdominal examination showed a liver edge 1 cm below the right costal margin and a tender spleen 5 cm below the left costal margin.  

Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 14.8 g/dL, WBC count 4.78×10^3/mm^3 with normal differential, platelets 91.7×10^3/mm^3; AST 443 U/L, ALT 404 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 719 U/L, γ-GT 368 U/L, total bilirubin 9.6 mg/dL, LDH 1274 U/L; INR 1.69; fibrinogen 67 mg/dL; triglycerides 617 mg/dL; ferritin >16 500 ng/mL. Serologic tests were negative for HIV, CMV (IgG/IgM), hepatitis B and C, syphilis, Schistosoma mansoni (IgM); EBV IgG was positive, IgM negative; toxoplasmosis IgG positive, IgM negative; rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies were negative.  

Upper endoscopy showed enanthematic pangastritis. Transthoracic echocardiography was normal. Abdominal ultrasound and CT demonstrated splenomegaly with multiple hypoechoic areas. Bone marrow aspiration was hypercellular with granulocytic predominance; biopsy showed megakaryocytic hyperplasia and a microgranuloma. Ziehl–Neelsen, Grocott, and PAS stains were negative. Immunophenotyping showed no evidence of lymphoma.  

Despite broad supportive care—including blood product transfusions—the patient remained febrile and developed progressive hepatic failure, acute renal failure requiring hemodialysis, and respiratory failure necessitating mechanical ventilation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Storage disorders (Niemann–Pick disease, Gaucher’s disease, Tangier disease) were considered — “the differential diagnosis of non-neoplastic proliferation of histiocytes…includes…storage disorders such as Niemann-Pick disease, Gaucher’s disease, and Tangier disease.” 2. Histiocytic sarcoma was considered but excluded — “In contrast to HLH, patients with HS present tumoral skin lesions… On the other hand, HLH is characterized by diffuse, rather than localized, histiocytic infiltration.”

title: Programmed cell death protein-1 inhibitor–induced granuloma annulare and hypertrophic lichen planus masquerading as squamous cell carcinoma
case_prompt:
A 79-year-old white man with PD-L1–positive stage IV non–small cell lung cancer began pembrolizumab, 200 mg every 3 weeks. His history included a 50-pack-year smoking history, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, hypothyroidism, and oral lichen planus managed with doxycycline, 20 mg daily, with good control. He had no prior cutaneous lichen planus or history of skin cancer.

Approximately 6 weeks after starting pembrolizumab, he noted a mildly pruritic, enlarging rash on his arms, legs, and upper back. On examination, there were innumerable hyperkeratotic, necrotic papulonodules and ulcerations on the extremities and scattered multicentimeter, serpiginous, nonscaly, erythematous annular plaques on the upper back and forearms. Concurrently, his oral lichen planus flared, and his doxycycline was increased to 40 mg twice daily. He denied genital lesions or systemic symptoms.

Punch biopsies of an extremity papulonodule and an annular plaque were performed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Annular lichen planus — “annular plaques on the back were most suggestive of annular lichen planus or GA…” 2. Sarcoidosis — “cases of sarcoidosis induced by pembrolizumab have been reported.” 3. Eruptive keratoacanthomas — “conditions more commonly seen in association with BRAF inhibitors, such as eruptive keratoacanthomas…” 4. Lichenoid drug eruption — “lichenoid drug eruption…” 5. Infection — “infection…” 6. Nodular vasculitis — “nodular vasculitis…” 7. Lymphomatoid papulosis — “lymphomatoid papulosis…”

title: Parkinsonism secondary to metastatic lesions within the central nervous system: a case report
case_prompt:
A 57-year-old man with recently diagnosed stage IV non-mucinous adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (multiple lung, liver, and nodal metastases) underwent palliative ileocolic bypass and was discharged in stable condition. Four days later, he was readmitted with fever and scrotal pain; a scrotal abscess was drained and antibiotics given. During the last three days of this admission, his wife noted that he developed small-stepped gait, slowed movements, clumsiness, and rigid posture. These neurologic signs worsened after discharge. 

On neurologic examination, he was mask-faced with slow eye movements and dysarthria. He had a resting tremor in both hands, bilateral bradykinesia, postural instability, a wide-based, festinating gait with freezing, absence of arm swing, and a bilaterally dystonic posture. Generalized hyperreflexia and plastic rigidity with cogwheeling were present; there were no cranial nerve palsies. Cognition was intact but responses were delayed. Family reported new nighttime urinary incontinence, and he was fully dependent for activities of daily living. He had no prior neurologic disease or hypertension. 

High-dose intravenous corticosteroid therapy was initiated, and an obvious improvement in the neurologic signs was observed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary brain lymphoma producing parkinsonism was considered — “Several cases of parkinsonism secondary to primary brain lymphoma have been described ... a diffuse B-cell lymphoma with selective infiltration and neuronal destruction of the substantia nigra was found post-mortem.” 2. Other intracranial tumors (astrocytomas, meningiomas, craniopharyngiomas, colloid cysts) were in the differential — “The most frequent causes of neoplastic parkinsonism are astrocytomas, meningiomas, craniopharyngiomas, colloid cysts and (less frequently) metastases.”

title: Significance of intra-operative cholangiogram and its application in suspected duodenal injuries – Case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old man presented with right upper quadrant pain and fevers up to 38 °C. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 17.9 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein of 425 mg/L, and normal liver-function tests. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated acute cholecystitis with an 8 mm stone impacted at the gallbladder neck and surrounding inflammation involving the duodenum and hepatic flexure. He received intravenous antibiotics, improved, and returned four weeks later for laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

Intraoperatively, dense adhesions of the omentum and transverse colon obscured the gallbladder and dissection was converted to open. The gangrenous gallbladder was separated from the duodenum, which showed patchy bile discoloration but no serosal tear. A subtotal cholecystectomy was completed without encountering a bile leak.

An intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) demonstrated an unexpected streak of contrast just inferior to the cystic duct. The duodenum was flushed with saline and pressurized methylene blue via the cystic duct catheter, with no visible leak. Metal clips were placed on the discolored duodenal areas. A repeat IOC performed 10 minutes after the first showed no contrast leak and the disappearance of the previously noted streak.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Duodenal fistula was suspected given the IOC finding of a contrast jet suggesting bile leak, but it was excluded by negative leak testing and resolution on repeat imaging — “methylene blue leak test failed to demonstrate the leak and a repeat IOC revealed disappearance of the suspicious jet.” 2. Extrahepatic bile duct duplication was mentioned as an extremely rare anomaly but was not supported by any imaging or intraoperative findings — “Differentials included duodenal fistula, diverticulum, retrograde flow into pylorus and the extremely rare extrahepatic bile duct duplication.”
final_diagnosis: Retrograde contrast reflux into the duodenum

title: Cavernous Hemangioma-Like Kaposi Sarcoma: Histomorphologic Features and Differential Diagnosis
case_prompt:
A 69-year-old man presented with a several-year history of purple spots on both feet that gradually increased in number and size. He had well-controlled hypertension for 10 years. Dermatologic examination revealed multiple purple-colored plaques and papules, 0.5–1.5 cm in diameter, some with crusted surfaces on the dorsum of the feet and around the ankles. He had no systemic symptoms, lymphadenopathy, or visceral involvement. Laboratory and serologic tests, including HIV-1 serology, were unremarkable. A punch biopsy was obtained from a lesion on the dorsum of his foot.

Histopathological examination of hematoxylin and eosin–stained sections showed a dilated, blood-filled vascular proliferation lined by flattened endothelium, extending from the papillary dermis under hyperkeratotic epidermis to the reticular dermis in a lobular pattern. A few thick-walled vessels were interspersed within predominantly thin-walled vascular spaces. Hemosiderin pigment was noted in vessel walls and surrounding stroma. Chronic inflammatory cells were present, and focal areas of immature vascular structures forming clefts, containing extravasated erythrocytes, and mild endothelial cytologic atypia were identified, especially in the papillary dermis. No spindle cell proliferation was observed.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Nodular (advanced-stage) Kaposi sarcoma — considered since nodular lesions can show similar dilated vascular formations but excluded here by lack of spindle cell proliferation: “spindle cell proliferation typical of advanced-stage KS is prominent.”

title: Revisiting hereditary tyrosinemia Type 1—spectrum of radiological findings
case_prompt:
An 18-month-old boy was evaluated for intermittent spiking fevers, constipation, and gradually progressive abdominal distension since 1 month of age. His birth history and immunizations were unremarkable. A sibling had died at age 2 with a similar illness.

On examination, he was below the fifth percentile for height and weight, jaundiced, and had abdominal distension. The liver and spleen were palpably enlarged.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.0 g/dL, white blood cell count 12,000/µL, alkaline phosphatase 2117 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 121 U/L, γ-glutamyl transferase 301 U/L, elevated total bilirubin, and prolonged prothrombin time/international normalized ratio. TORCH screening was negative. Serum α-fetoprotein was markedly elevated at 14,831 ng/mL.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a coarse, nodular-appearing liver with multiple small hypoechoic avascular nodules in a cirrhotic background. Contrast-enhanced CT confirmed multiple non-enhancing hepatic nodules on arterial, venous, and delayed phases. The portal vein was patent; splenomegaly was present without other signs of portal hypertension.

Both kidneys were bilaterally enlarged and markedly echogenic on ultrasound, with post-contrast CT demonstrating poor enhancement and loss of corticomedullary differentiation. A chest radiograph showed expansion of the anterior rib ends (rachitic rosary), and wrist radiographs demonstrated widened, flared growth plates with metaphyseal fraying consistent with rickets.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hepatocellular carcinoma or hepatoblastoma was initially suspected given the strikingly high α-fetoprotein level — “α feto protein level was strikingly raised in our patient and was clinically thought to have hepatoblastoma or hepatocellular carcinoma; however, imaging refuted this suspicion.” 2. Glycogen storage disease was considered due to cirrhosis and liver dysfunction — “These warning signs of cirrhotic liver with deranged liver function tests lead us to think about metabolic disorders like glycogen storage disease and Wilson’s disease.” 3. Wilson’s disease was included among hepatic metabolic disorders in the differential — “These warning signs of cirrhotic liver with deranged liver function tests lead us to think about metabolic disorders like glycogen storage disease and Wilson’s disease.” 4. Other inherited metabolic disorders (mitochondrial disorders, disorders of carbohydrate metabolism) were recognized as potential mimics — “Thus the differential diagnosis includes Wilson’s disease, mitochondrial disorders, and disorders of carbohydrate metabolism.”

title: Ossifying Fibromyxoid tumor of soft parts in head and neck: case report and literature review
case_prompt: A 32‐year‐old Asian man presented with a 1‐year history of a right neck mass. His past medical history was noncontributory. On extraoral examination, there was a soft, slightly movable mass in the right submandibular region with normal overlying skin; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Computed tomography of the neck revealed a well‐defined, mixed‐density lesion measuring approximately 5 × 3 cm adjacent to the right submandibular gland. Subsequent image segmentation suggested that the lesion contained components of fat, connective tissue, and osteoid. Primary differential diagnoses based on imaging included pleomorphic adenoma of the submandibular gland, teratoma, and osteo/chondroid lipoma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered pleomorphic adenoma — “primary differential diagnoses considered for this lesion included pleomorphic adenoma of the submandibular gland.” 2. Considered teratoma — “primary differential diagnoses considered for this lesion included … teratoma.” 3. Considered osteo/chondroid lipoma — “primary differential diagnoses considered for this lesion included … osteo/chondroid lipoma.”

title: Diffuse Spontaneous Laryngeal Hemorrhage with Trastuzumab
case_prompt: A 37-year-old woman with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer presented with a 2-week history of episodic hemoptysis, odynophonia, and odynophagia. She described awakening choking on blood and a sensation of “water in her throat” with cough. She also had recent rhinorrhea and cough. Her medical history included lung, neck, and bone metastases and chemotherapy-related iron deficiency anemia, menorrhagia, hematochezia, and thrombocytopenia. Initial therapy with docetaxel, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab was changed to trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), zoledronic acid, and tamoxifen; her last T-DM1 infusion was 26 days before presentation (total of 15 infusions). Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy revealed a friable, excoriated nasal septum; bilateral vocal fold edema with ulceration; epithelial bleeding on supraglottic and glottic surfaces; no submucosal hemorrhage; and no surrounding ecchymosis. CT imaging of the head, neck, and chest showed no parenchymal lung lesions or other sources of hemorrhage. The pulmonology team determined that this was not related to progression of metastatic lung lesions or lung-related hemoptysis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pulmonary hemorrhage from metastatic lung lesions was excluded — “CT scan was negative for contributing factors...the Pulmonology team determined that this was not related to progression of metastatic lung lesions or lung-related hemoptysis.” 2. Mechanical trauma to the larynx was considered but excluded — “no associated submucosal hemorrhage...with no surrounding ecchymosis.”

title: Cardiac relapse of extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma manifesting as incessant ventricular tachycardia: a case report
case_prompt:
A 39-year-old man presented to his general practitioner for a routine check-up. He had a history of extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma of the nasal type treated with chemotherapy and local radiation and assessed as in clinical remission two months earlier. During the examination, the physician noted a heart rate of 155 beats per minute. An ECG demonstrated a regular wide-complex tachycardia interpreted as a hemodynamically stable ventricular tachycardia with an apical-anterior-lateral exit. 

He was transferred urgently to the emergency department with ongoing VT. On arrival, he developed cardiogenic shock. One synchronized biphasic shock of 100 J failed to terminate the arrhythmia and induced ventricular fibrillation, requiring chest compressions and three defibrillations to restore an unstable sinus rhythm that soon degenerated back into VT. Intravenous antiarrhythmic therapy with ajmaline, landiolol, and electrolyte substitution was initiated, maintaining a ventricular rate of 120–150 beats per minute with brief sinus rhythm intervals.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 3 × 2 cm hyperechogenic zone in the apex of the left ventricle, regional hypokinesia in that area, and a small pericardial effusion. Coronary angiography showed no stenosing coronary artery disease.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ischemic ventricular tachycardia — “An ischaemic cause of the rhythmic instability was ruled out by coronary angiography.” 2. Idiopathic VT — “Differential diagnosis in a young male ... included ... idiopathic VT,” but the presence of a structural apical lesion made a purely idiopathic mechanism unlikely. 3. Primary cardiac tumour — “the majority of these primary tumours consists of myxoma located most likely in the left atrium,” making an apex mass an atypical presentation for a primary tumour. 4. Secondary cardiac tumour (metastasis) — “Although rare, lymphomas are reported to metastasize into the heart,” aligning with the patient’s lymphoma history and the apical mass.
final_diagnosis: Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma of the nasal type

title: Xanthoma Disseminatum In a Pair of Blind, Deaf Male Twins
case_prompt: Twin male patients, born after an uncomplicated pregnancy, presented with idiopathic, progressive loss of vision and hearing during early childhood, culminating in complete blindness and deafness by the first decade of life. There was no family history of similar cutaneous or audiovisual abnormalities. On examination, both brothers had multiple smooth, gelatinous papules and coalescing plaques on the face, numerous small erythematous papules on the buttocks, and scattered, well-defined red-brown waxy papules with coalescing patches on the lower extremities, accompanied by areas of mottled hypopigmentation over the knees and popliteal fossae. They denied arthritis, constitutional symptoms, abdominal or bone pain, and respiratory complaints. A punch biopsy of a knee lesion revealed a histiocytic cellular proliferation with plasma cells and multinucleated giant cells. Initial differential diagnoses included reactive histiocytoses (leishmaniasis, syphilis, atypical mycobacterial infection) and nonreactive histiocytoses (Rosai-Dorfman disease, xanthoma disseminatum, Langerhans cell histiocytosis). Empirical doxycycline (100 mg twice daily) was initiated without sustained clinical improvement, and therapy was discontinued after 6 weeks. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, metabolic panel, thyroid and liver function tests. A lipid panel demonstrated low triglycerides, normal cholesterol and LDL, and low HDL. Antinuclear antibodies and rapid plasma reagin testing were negative, and a urine Histoplasma antigen assay was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Reactive infections — “Reactive causes considered were lesions secondary to leishmaniasis, syphilis, or atypical mycobacterial infection.” 2. Syphilis — “Rapid plasma regain (RPR) were negative.”

title: Pituitary metastasis of salivary gland carcinoma mimicking hypophysitis: A case report and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 52-year-old Japanese man was evaluated for several months of progressive anorexia. His medical history was notable for sublingual adenocarcinoma treated with resection and radiation 6 years earlier, supraclavicular lymph-node metastasis resection and radiation 5 years earlier, and subsequent lung and bone metastases treated with platinum-based chemotherapy. He was currently receiving nivolumab. He had no history of smoking or significant family illnesses and was taking sertraline. 

On initial evaluation, laboratory testing demonstrated secondary adrenocortical insufficiency and secondary hypothyroidism without evidence of diabetes insipidus. Serum IgG4 was 52.0 mg/dL; angiotensin-converting enzyme, 13.6 U/L; soluble interleukin-2 receptor, 684 U/mL; and T-SPOT was negative. 

Magnetic resonance imaging showed an isointense, homogenously enhancing enlargement of the pituitary gland and stalk with extension into the cavernous sinus and the planum sphenoidale dura, without compression of the optic chiasm. No other intracranial lesions were identified. Autoimmune hypophysitis related to immune checkpoint inhibition was suspected, and the patient was started on glucocorticoids and thyroid hormone replacement. 

One month later, he returned with new right-eye visual impairment (visual acuity 20/200) attributed to invasion of the optic canal. Pulse corticosteroid therapy was administered as a diagnostic treatment. Repeat MRI showed no clear reduction in the pituitary lesion, and he subsequently developed a right oculomotor nerve palsy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Autoimmune hypophysitis was initially suspected because of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy and characteristic MRI findings — “Hypophysitis is characterized by diffuse enlargement of the pituitary gland and stalk, as well as homogenous contrast enhancement on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).” 2. The absence of additional intracranial lesions supported hypophysitis over metastatic disease — “The absence of other intracranial metastases and small suprasellar lesions supported the diagnosis of hypophysitis.” 3. Failure of the lesion to shrink after high-dose steroids made an inflammatory hypophysitis less likely — “However, there was no clear shrinkage of the pituitary tumor on MRI.” 4. Radiographic evidence of tumor invasion into the cavernous sinus and dura favored a metastatic process — “Tumoral extension into the cavernous sinus and the dura mater suggested pituitary metastasis rather than hypophysitis.”

title: Meropenem-induced thrombocytopenia: a paediatric case
case_prompt:
An 11-year-old previously healthy boy presented with 1 day of severe abdominal pain localizing to the right iliac fossa. He underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy for a perforated gangrenous appendix and was started on intravenous cefuroxime, gentamicin, and metronidazole. 

On postoperative day 5, his C-reactive protein remained elevated at 281 mg/L, and on day 8 he spiked fevers up to 38 °C. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 4 cm × 2 cm hypoechoic fluid collection in the right iliac fossa. Antibiotics were changed to intravenous cefotaxime plus metronidazole. CT on day 13 confirmed the persistent collection, which was drained. Culture of the fluid grew ESBL-producing, carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (resistant to ertapenem but sensitive to meropenem), Enterococcus faecalis, and Enterococcus avium. 

On day 20 postappendectomy, a PICC line was placed and therapy was switched to high-dose intravenous meropenem (40 mg/kg every 8 h) plus teicoplanin. He defervesced and serial imaging showed resolving collection.

On day 7 of meropenem administration (day 27 postappendectomy), he developed a rapidly spreading pruritic, non-tender maculopapular blanching rash with occasional petechiae over his trunk and upper limbs, and fevers up to 38 °C recurred.  
 
Complete blood count on that day showed a platelet count drop to 101 × 10^9/L from 322 × 10^9/L; by day 8 of meropenem it had fallen further to 14 × 10^9/L. Leukocyte and hemoglobin levels were stable. Coagulation studies (aPTT ratio, INR, fibrinogen) were within normal limits. Repeat abdominal CT showed improvement of the fluid collection. The PICC line was removed; line tip and blood cultures were negative, as were stool and urine cultures. Serial viral serologies and molecular tests for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and parvovirus remained negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Spurious thrombocytopenia ruled out — “spurious thrombocytopenia, which occurs due to clumping of platelets during analysis, was confidently ruled out by low platelet count being repeatedly confirmed on serial blood counts.” 2. Viral infection excluded — “viral causes were excluded on the basis of negative viral serology and molecular diagnostic testing for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus and parvovirus.”

title: Late-onset asystolic episodes in a patient with a vagal nerve stimulator
case_prompt:
A 46‐year‐old woman with intractable complex partial seizures presented with increasing episodes of sudden falls over the past 2 years. She had been diagnosed with epilepsy at age 21 and, despite maximal antiepileptic drug therapy, continued to have monthly seizures. A left‐sided vagal nerve stimulator had been implanted in 2004, with a generator change in 2012; over the past 2 years, the output current had been gradually increased to control breakthrough seizures.  

Her new spells occurred randomly, without prodrome or postictal period, were not position related, and were not accompanied by urinary or bowel incontinence. She reported brief loss of consciousness and an average of two falls per month, resulting in injuries. This pattern differed from her prior episodes of blank staring and automatic behavior.  

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cardiopulmonary auscultation was unremarkable, and there were no signs of heart failure. Brain MRI showed left mesial temporal sclerosis and increased signal in the posterior right frontal region. Three prolonged EEG studies demonstrated spikes in the F7–T3 distribution, consistent with her known hemispheric epileptiform focus. A Ziopatch monitor disclosed no arrhythmias; ECG showed normal sinus rhythm; echocardiography and myocardial perfusion stress testing were unremarkable.  

The VNS settings were adjusted (decreased current strength and increased OFF time), but her spells persisted. A LINQ implantable loop recorder was placed, and on interrogation several sinus pauses of 10 to 12 seconds were documented. Review of her records showed that these pauses corresponded exactly to the times of her falls and injuries.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Drop attacks and complex partial seizures were considered because of her epilepsy history — “The description of her spells was felt to be most consistent with cardiac syncope; however, differential diagnosis included drop attacks and complex partial seizures.” 2. Ictal bradycardia syndrome was considered because temporal lobe epilepsy can slow cardiac function and lead to asystole — “The diagnosis of ictal bradycardia syndrome was also considered, as it can cause epileptic discharges that slow the cardiac function and lead to asystole in patients with temporal lobe epilepsy.” 3. Excessive vagal nerve stimulation leading to sinus node depression and asystole was the leading diagnosis given the timing of events and device settings — “The leading diagnosis was that the excessive vagal nerve stimulation caused depression of the sinus node and led to profound bradycardia and asystole.”

title: Clinical Diagnosis of an Autoimmune Encephalitis Presented as a Manic Episode with Psychotic Symptoms: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 32-year-old man was brought by his wife to the emergency department with a 2-week history of intermittent disorientation, nervousness, pressured speech, emotional lability, sexual disinhibition, decreased appetite, unquantified weight loss, and insomnia. The onset of symptoms coincided with a recent dental procedure. 

Initial evaluation included complete blood count, electrolytes, renal and liver panels, coagulation studies, urinalysis with drug screen, ECG, and noncontrast head CT; all were normal. He was diagnosed with a hypomanic episode and discharged on olanzapine and lorazepam. 

Three days later, he returned with worsening paranoia, third-person imperative auditory hallucinations, severe irritability, and aggression. He was involuntarily admitted. Repeat blood tests remained normal, and he was started on olanzapine, lorazepam, and lithium. 

On hospital day 2, he had a witnessed coma with loss of consciousness and sphincter relaxation. He was intubated in the ICU; head CT remained normal. After stabilization, he returned to the acute mental health unit where he was disoriented in time and space, with formal thought disorder, incoherent speech, psychomotor agitation, and dysphoria. His regimen was changed to aripiprazole, valproic acid, dipotassium clorazepate, and levetiracetam. 

Over the next five days, disorientation persisted, speech became further disorganized with thought blocking, and soliloquies appeared. On day 7, neurology ordered an EEG (showing normal background, no epileptiform changes) and brain MRI (no abnormalities). Routine labs were unchanged. 

On day 8, he developed a low-grade fever (37.4°C), hypertension, sinus tachycardia, and fluctuating consciousness progressing to stupor, chewing movements, and minimal response to pain. New labs showed leukocytosis (15.28 × 10^3/µL) with neutrophilia, INR 1.19, and D-dimer 4902 ng/mL. Blood and urine cultures later returned negative. CT pulmonary angiography revealed a segmental thrombus in the left lower-lobe artery. 

Although there were no meningeal signs, the fever and altered consciousness prompted lumbar puncture, which showed:  
• Red cells: 2000/mm^3  
• White cells: 47/mm^3 (10% polymorphonuclear, 80% monocytes)  
• Protein: 0.27 g/dL  
• Glucose: 93 mg/dL (blood 115 mg/dL)  

He was provisionally diagnosed with limbic encephalitis of probable infectious or paraneoplastic origin.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary affective (hypomanic) disorder was initially favored because “Given the normality of the results of the complementary tests, the patient was diagnosed as having a hypomanic episode…” 2. Neuroleptic-induced encephalopathy was considered for the acute coma since “This episode was classified as a coma, probably secondary to taking neuroleptics.”

title: Generalized lichen spinulosus and secondary follicular mucinosis
case_prompt:
A 21-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of dry skin and generalized spiny skin lesions. She had been treated empirically for eczema with a medium-strength topical corticosteroid without improvement. She had no other medical problems, medications, atopy, allergies, or family history of similar skin findings.

On examination, she had multiple minute, digitate, folliculocentric hyperkeratotic papules symmetrically involving the face, neck, trunk, and upper extremities. There was no perilesional erythema, palmoplantar keratoderma, scarring, alopecia, or signs of nutritional deficiency.

A 3-mm punch biopsy from the back showed keratin plugs in hair follicle infundibula, sparse perifollicular lymphocytes, and cystic spaces in the outer root sheath of hair follicles and sebaceous glands. Alcian blue–periodic acid–Schiff stain highlighted mucin within these spaces. Immunohistochemistry demonstrated CD3- and predominantly CD4-positive small T lymphocytes, few CD8-positive T cells, and inconspicuous CD20-positive B cells and CD30-positive cells. T-cell receptor gene-rearrangement testing failed for technical reasons.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Eczema was considered because of xerosis and spiny lesions, but “did not improve on a medium-strength topical corticosteroid.” 2. Keratosis pilaris atrophicans was excluded because “there was no secondary scarring or alopecia in affected areas.” 3. Non-follicular and localized forms of multiple minute digitate hyperkeratosis were ruled out as “these forms lack folliculocentric involvement and generalized symmetry.”

title: Postpartum Endogenous Candida Endophthalmitis
journal: Middle East African Journal of Ophthalmology
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old woman with insulin-treated diabetes mellitus presented 4 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with a 1-week history of progressive vision loss in her right eye. Vital signs were stable. Visual acuity was 20/80 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left. Intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg OD and 12 mmHg OS. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye revealed fine keratic precipitates, 3+ anterior chamber cells, and a clear lens. Dilated fundus examination showed 1+ vitreous haze, a hyperemic optic disc, and multiple yellow-white retinal infiltrates. The left eye examination was normal. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye demonstrated diffuse retinal vascular leakage and optic-disc staining. Optical coherence tomography showed a dry macula. 

She was admitted with a provisional diagnosis of right acute nongranulomatous panuveitis, probably representing atypical toxoplasmosis. A uveitis workup—including complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, purified protein derivative skin test, chest CT, syphilis and toxoplasmosis serology, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, and urine analysis—was entirely negative. Hemoglobin A1c was 6.7%. Empirical therapy with clindamycin, co-trimoxazole, and systemic prednisolone resulted in only transient mild improvement, followed by persistent inflammation. Repeat toxoplasmosis serology remained negative. 

Further history revealed persistent vaginal discharge during the peripartum period without fever. A gynecologic evaluation including a vaginal swab showed heavy growth of Candida albicans. Infectious disease consultation prompted blood and urine cultures, contrast CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and cardiac echography, all of which were unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Atypical toxoplasmosis was initially considered given the presentation of acute panuveitis — “Patient was admitted with a provisional diagnosis of right acute nongranulomatous panuveitis probably representing atypical toxoplasmosis.” 2. Common infectious and noninfectious causes of uveitis were excluded by laboratory and imaging studies — “a uveitis workup was performed ... which all came to be negative.” 3. Systemic foci of infection, including endocarditis and visceral abscesses, were ruled out — “blood and urine culture; CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrasts; and cardiac echography were performed, which all came to be unremarkable.”

title: Reduced corneal endothelial cell density after toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS) caused by inadvertent intraocular ointment migration: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt: A 71-year-old Colombian man underwent uneventful phacoemulsification and in-the-bag intraocular lens implantation in the right eye. Preoperatively, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 OD and 20/20 OS, with no other ocular abnormalities or systemic disease. On postoperative day 1, uncorrected visual acuity in the right eye was 20/40, intraocular pressure was 10 mmHg, and slit-lamp examination showed diffuse conjunctival hyperemia, anterior chamber cells, mild Descemet folds, medium keratic precipitates, and a poorly defined intraocular foreign body at 12 o’clock. The cornea was otherwise clear. He was treated with dexamethasone eye drops hourly, ciprofloxacin eye drops four times daily, and dexamethasone–tobramycin ointment at bedtime. One week later, visual acuity improved to 20/25, and inflammation decreased to 1+ cells. Ciprofloxacin was stopped at two weeks, steroids were tapered over one month, and ointment continued for one month. Each time steroids were tapered, inflammation recurred and improved upon restarting therapy. Removal of the foreign body was recommended but declined by the patient due to good vision and minimal symptoms. Over six months, topical steroids were modulated to control the inflammation. Nongranulomatous anterior uveitis was considered the leading diagnosis. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 24 mm/h; C-reactive protein and antinuclear antibodies were within normal limits, and rheumatologic diseases were excluded on laboratory and physical evaluation. At six months after surgery, further evaluation of the anterior chamber foreign body was planned with gonioscopy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered nongranulomatous anterior uveitis — “Nongranulomatous anterior uveitis was the main differential diagnosis.” 2. Ruled out rheumatologic disease — “Rheumatological diseases were discarded after laboratories (besides ESR) and physical exam was normal.”

title: A case of neuroleptic malignant syndrome induced by perospirone
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old woman with a 17-month history of schizophrenia was admitted for adjustment of antipsychotic medication. Her regimen had been switched from risperidone to perospirone, and over three weeks the perospirone dose was increased to 40 mg/day. Shortly after reaching this dose, she developed tremor, muscle rigidity, akathisia, heavy sweating, difficulty swallowing and speaking, tachycardia, fluctuating blood pressure, a fever of 39.8 °C, restlessness, and confusion.  
Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 18.3 × 10^9/L, serum sodium 129 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, creatine phosphokinase 813.2 U/L (normal 26–140 U/L), and myoglobin 486 µg/L (normal 12–76 µg/L). A lumbar puncture revealed normal cerebrospinal fluid. Neuroimaging was unremarkable. Electroencephalography demonstrated diffuse slow waves, consistent with metabolic encephalopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral encephalitis was considered because of fever and altered mental status, but “examination of the extracted cerebrospinal fluid found no abnormalities.” 2. Malignant hyperthermia was considered due to hyperthermia and rigidity, but “malignant hyperthermia usually develops during surgery (caused by an inhaled anesthetic) in individuals with a history of primary musculoskeletal disorders.” 3. Non-convulsive seizures or structural brain lesions were considered because they can mimic NMS, but “neuroimaging did not identify any abnormalities” and “EEG demonstrated diffuse slow waves, consistent with metabolic encephalopathy.” 4. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome best fits the presentation of “severe muscle rigidity and elevated body temperature after the recent use of antipsychotic medications in an individual who concurrently exhibits … diaphoresis, tremor, tachycardia, … raised leukocyte level, and laboratory evidence of muscle damage.”

title:
Langerhans cell histiocytosis: unusual dorsal spine localization in
an adult male
case_prompt:
A 50-year-old Caucasian man presented with a dull, long-standing pain in the dorsolateral region of the left chest. He denied trauma, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. On examination, he had limited movement at the waist without motor or sensory abnormalities; the neurological examination was otherwise normal. Laboratory tests—including blood cell count, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein—were all within normal limits. Chest radiographs were unremarkable. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax showed a widening of the left T10 costovertebral joint with scalloping and mild sclerosis of the left aspect of the T10 vertebral body, partially involving the left pedicle and adjacent costal bone. FDG PET-CT revealed no significant tracer uptake at this site. MRI of the thoracic spine demonstrated non-specific changes at the left T10 costovertebral junction, including bone marrow edema in the T10 vertebral body, pedicle, and adjacent costal bone (low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted fat-saturated images) with non-specific contrast enhancement and no spinal canal invasion.

A fluoroscopy-guided needle biopsy of the T10 vertebral body yielded fibrous/myxoid tissue with reactive lymphocytes and plasma cells and rare histiocytic elements resembling a non-specific chronic osteomyelitic pattern. A CD1a immunostain was performed to further characterize the lesion.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection (e.g., tuberculosis) was considered because of costovertebral joint involvement—“Differential diagnosis included other conditions that can involve costovertebral joints as infections (e.g. tubercolosis)…In the majority of these cases, however, associated imaging findings are present.” 2. Inflammatory arthropathy (spondyloarthritis) was considered due to MRI findings similar to costovertebral inflammation but excluded because “lab data were normal, without any signs of inflammation.” 3. Primary tumors and metastases were considered for a focal bone lesion but excluded as “normal PET/CT scan was of great value in excluding the majority of tumoral entities, narrowing the differential diagnosis.”

title: Isolated Horner syndrome as a rare initial presentation of nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a case report
case_prompt: A 33-year-old Thai man with a heavy smoking habit presented with a 3-week history of left eyelid droop without other complaints. Visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally; intraocular pressure and ocular motility were normal. Examination revealed mild ptosis of the left upper eyelid and anisocoria, with the left pupil measuring 2 mm versus 3 mm on the right in bright light, and 2.5 mm versus 4 mm on the right in dim light. Both pupils reacted briskly to light without an afferent defect. Anhidrosis was noted on the medial aspect of the left forehead. A 10% cocaine test increased the pupillary difference to 2.5 mm (6.0 mm on the right, 3.5 mm on the left) and produced right upper-eyelid retraction. Neurologic examination was otherwise unremarkable. A provisional diagnosis of left-sided, painless, isolated Horner syndrome was made, and MRI and MRA of the neck and brain plus CT of the chest were scheduled.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cluster headache, cavernous sinus lesion, and Raeder paratrigeminal neuralgia were included in the painful postganglionic differential — “Differential diagnoses of painful postganglionic Horner syndrome always include life-threatening carotid artery dissection, cluster headache, a cavernous sinus lesion, and Raeder paratrigeminal neuralgia.”

title: Fifty‐eight‐year‐old female with abdominal migraine: A rare cause of episodic gastrointestinal disturbance in adults
case_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman presented with multiple episodes of coffee-ground emesis over 24 hours, preceded by nearly 20 episodes of greenish, nonbloody emesis beginning 3 days earlier. She reported a 7-month history of similar 2- to 3-day episodes of moderate, continuous, dull, midline abdominal pain associated with nausea, anorexia, and a mild headache without visual changes or aura; these episodes required three prior hospital admissions. Between admissions she was symptomatic enough to need assistance with activities of daily living. She denied fever, chills, bowel‐habit changes, weight loss, or skin findings. Her medical history included coronary artery disease treated with a drug-eluting stent 2 months earlier; she was on aspirin and ticagrelor. Prior outpatient therapies (omeprazole, ondansetron, sucralfate, meclizine, scopolamine, topiramate) had failed. 

On examination, her abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and tender in the epigastric and periumbilical areas without guarding or rebound. Initial laboratory studies showed potassium 3.0 mEq/L, glucose 264 mg/dL, anion gap 20 mEq/L, venous pH 7.412, HCO3 18.6 mmol/L, and beta-hydroxybutyrate 2.25 mmol/L. Management for presumed starvation ketosis and possible diabetic ketoacidosis corrected her metabolic abnormalities. Low hemoglobin and coffee-ground emesis raised concern for an upper GI bleed, so aspirin was held and intravenous fluids and antiemetics were administered.

A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a small hiatal hernia and no acute changes in the bowel or mesentery. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed no ulcers, strictures, or active bleeding, and biopsies were negative for malignancy. A gastric-emptying study was normal. Brain MRI demonstrated only a stable chronic cerebellar infarct without new findings. Blood cultures and urinalysis were normal, and chest radiography showed no pulmonary process. Cardiac evaluation revealed a normal ECG and negative troponins.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Gastrointestinal causes — “A computed tomography (CT) scan of her abdomen and pelvis showed ... no acute changes in the bowel and mesentery. It did not show any ulcers, strictures, or signs of UGI bleed, and subsequent biopsies were negative for any malignancies.” 2. Irritable bowel syndrome — “IBS was ruled out since the patient moved her bowels regularly without any constipation or diarrhea.” 3. Diabetic gastroparesis — “Diabetic gastroparesis was suspected, but follow‐up nuclear motility scan and gastric emptying studies were normal.” 4. Neurologic etiologies — “MRI of the brain showed only showed a stable chronic cerebellar infarct with no evidence of hydrocephalus, mass effect, acute infarct, or hemorrhage.” 5. Myocardial infarction — “she was worked up for a myocardial infarction but her EKG was normal and troponins were negative.”

title: Two Cases of Oculofaciocardiodental (OFCD) Syndrome due to X-Linked BCOR Mutations Presenting with Infantile Hemangiomas: Phenotypic Overlap with PHACE Syndrome
case_prompt:
A 10-year-old female was born full term and noted on day 4 of life to have bilateral congenital cataracts, which were removed by anterior vitrectomy at 5–6 weeks of age, with secondary membranous cataract removal two months later. At 3 months, she was referred for elevated intraocular pressure (30 mm Hg OD); examination revealed a hemangioma lateral to the right eyebrow, a posterior scalp protuberance consistent with a hemangioma, bilateral multifocal congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium, and left microphthalmia. Despite maximal medical therapy, she required a Baerveldt anterior chamber shunt and pars plana vitrectomy in the left eye at 17 months, with additional vitrectomies for inflammation; intraocular pressure has since remained controlled on topical therapy in both eyes.

Cardiac evaluation at 1 month identified an atrial septal defect and possible Wolff–Parkinson–White pattern on ECG. At 11 months, neurology assessment of a 5 × 6 cm parietal scalp vascular lesion raised suspicion for PHACE syndrome. MRI/MRA at 12 and 25 months showed a large right parietal scalp hemangioma (initially 3.9 × 2.3 cm, later involuting to 1.4 cm thickness) and a smaller right frontal scalp lesion, both without intracranial arterial communication, plus an aberrant right subclavian artery. Growth and development were otherwise unremarkable, with no endocrinopathies, hearing loss, or structural brain anomalies.

At 9 years, genetic consultation rendered a definite clinical diagnosis of PHACE syndrome based on two forehead/scalp hemangiomas and the presence of arterial, cardiac, and eye anomalies. During routine ophthalmic follow-up, the combination of congenital cataracts, glaucoma, bilateral CHRPE, and parental report of long dental roots, one missing tooth, and delayed primary tooth loss prompted consideration of an alternative diagnosis and molecular testing.
diagnostic_reasoning: 2. Overlapping features such as microphthalmia, ocular coloboma, and posterior fossa anomalies did not distinguish PHACE from BCOR‐related syndromes: “Microphthalmia does not assist in differentiating BCOR-microphthalmia syndromes from PHACE, as it is a minor diagnostic criterion for PHACE, and ocular coloboma can also be a feature of either PHACE or BCOR.” 3. Dental anomalies in PHACE (enamel hypoplasia) lack the radiculomegaly and delayed eruption characteristic of OFCD: “the only well documented tooth anomaly associated with PHACE is enamel hypoplasia … but OFCD dental anomalies are not so distinctive that they provide high specificity for that diagnosis.”

title: A case report of isolated arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy: phenotypes, diagnosis, and treatment
case_prompt:
A 54-year-old man presented with recurrent palpitations and retrosternal pain radiating to the back, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. On initial admission, his heart rate was 130 b.p.m.; blood pressure and other vital signs were stable, and physical examination was unremarkable. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, or cardiovascular disease, and no family history of syncope, coronary heart disease, malignant arrhythmia, or sudden death.

At a local hospital, ECG showed ventricular tachycardia at 213 b.p.m. Troponin and myocardial enzyme levels were normal. Coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated thinning and bulging of the left ventricular (LV) apex with localized dyskinesia. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) confirmed abnormal systolic activity at the LV apex; the right ventricle was normal. Non-coronary obstructive acute myocardial infarction was initially considered, and he was treated with metoprolol, rosuvastatin, and amiodarone, with symptomatic improvement.

One year later, he experienced recurrent palpitations with hypotension (85/56 mmHg) but no chest pain or syncope. ECG again revealed ventricular tachycardia at 156 b.p.m. He declined implantable cardioverter-defibrillator implantation and was managed medically with beta-blocker and amiodarone.

Two months later, he returned with chest pain, palpitations, and diaphoresis. On ECG, there was T-wave inversion in leads V1–V4 (depth >1 mm, deepest ∼5 mm in V1–V2). During palpitations, ECG showed paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia of left bundle-branch-block morphology with negative precordial concordance and a notched, wide QRS, suggesting an inferoapical origin. Transthoracic echocardiography now demonstrated left atrial enlargement and an LV apical aneurysm. CMRI revealed LV apical dilation, akinesis with paradoxical systolic motion, increased fat signal at the apex on fat-suppressed T1-weighted images, and increased adipose signal with late gadolinium enhancement at the LV apex on fat-suppressed T2-weighted and gadolinium sequences. Cardiac enzymes remained within normal limits (troponin 0.007 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 85.5 pg/mL, CK-MB 9 U/L, CK 82 U/L).
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Non-coronary obstructive acute myocardial infarction — “Non-coronary obstructive acute myocardial infarction was considered.” and “Coronary angiography showed normal coronary arteries.” 2. Pseudoaneurysm — “Other conditions that can present without pouches in the apical structure, including pseudoaneurysm, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, and ventricular aneurysm, also require further differential diagnosis.” 3. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy — “Other conditions that can present without pouches in the apical structure, including pseudoaneurysm, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, and ventricular aneurysm, also require further differential diagnosis.” 4. Ventricular aneurysm (post-infarction) — “Other conditions that can present without pouches in the apical structure, including pseudoaneurysm, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, and ventricular aneurysm, also require further differential diagnosis.” 5. Post-myocardial infarction — “Post-myocardial infarction, congenital anomalies, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiac sarcoidosis should also be included in the differential diagnosis.” 6. Congenital anomalies — “Post-myocardial infarction, congenital anomalies, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiac sarcoidosis should also be included in the differential diagnosis.” 7. Pulmonary hypertension — “Post-myocardial infarction, congenital anomalies, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiac sarcoidosis should also be included in the differential diagnosis.” 8. Cardiac sarcoidosis — “Post-myocardial infarction, congenital anomalies, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiac sarcoidosis should also be included in the differential diagnosis.” 9. Dilated cardiomyopathy — “Dilated cardiomyopathy and cardiomyopathy associated with neuromuscular diseases should be excluded due to the overlap in genetic background.” 10. Neuromuscular disease–associated cardiomyopathy — “Dilated cardiomyopathy and cardiomyopathy associated with neuromuscular diseases should be excluded due to the overlap in genetic background.” 11. Sarcoidosis — “Sarcoidosis and myocarditis can also cause life-threatening arrhythmias and should be excluded via genetic analysis.” 12. Myocarditis — “Sarcoidosis and myocarditis can also cause life-threatening arrhythmias and should be excluded via genetic analysis.”

title: Primary membranous glomerulonephritis with negative serum PLA2R in haemophilia A successfully managed with rituximab – case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 48-year-old man with moderate haemophilia A, transfusion-acquired HIV (on maraviroc/efavirenz/raltegravir; CD4+ 1200 cells/mm3; undetectable viral load) and treated genotype 1a hepatitis C virus infection (completed ledipasvir/sofosbuvir 2 years earlier) was referred for evaluation of new-onset proteinuria. He has portal hypertensive changes with splenomegaly, oesophageal varices and mild thrombocytopenia (106×10^9/L). He reports one year of foamy urine but is otherwise asymptomatic. Physical examination shows palpable splenomegaly without peripheral oedema.  
Laboratory studies reveal:  
• 24-h urine protein 4.35 g; microscopy 24×10^6 red cells  
• Serum creatinine 98 μmol/L; albumin 51 g/L  
• ANA 1:160, homogeneous; ANCA, dsDNA, anti-GBM negative  
• Serum anti-PLA2R and cryoglobulins negative; SPEP without monoclonal bands  
Renal ultrasonography shows normal-sized kidneys, preserved corticomedullary differentiation, bilateral simple cysts, and no hydronephrosis or calculi.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of lupus nephritis — secondary MN can be associated with SLE, but although ANA was mildly elevated, “double stranded DNA … negative.” 2. Need for histological confirmation — “definitive diagnosis is challenging given the bleeding risk associated with renal biopsy,” yet tissue evaluation is required to distinguish between these possibilities.

title: Granulocyte colony stimulating factor-associated aortitis evaluated via multiple imaging modalities including vascular echography: a case report
case_prompt:
A 52-year-old woman with a history of ovarian cancer completed six cycles of postoperative chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin. After her 5th and 6th cycles, she developed myelosuppression and received filgrastim (75 µg/day) for 3 and 4 days, respectively. On the night after her last filgrastim dose, she developed high fever. 

She was prescribed acetaminophen and levofloxacin as an outpatient but remained febrile and was admitted on day 4. She had no cough, chest pain, abdominal pain, rash, or visual symptoms. Her temperature was 38.0 °C, blood pressure 92/50 mmHg, pulse 70 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. The head and neck were without bruit or tenderness, chest and cardiac auscultation were unremarkable, and the abdomen was soft without tenderness. There were no skin lesions or peripheral edema. 

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 8780/µL, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, platelet count 70,000/µL, and C-reactive protein 19.39 mg/dL. Serum ferritin was elevated (883 ng/mL), β-D glucan was normal, and blood cultures were negative. Serologic tests including STS, tuberculosis interferon-γ, and viral panels were negative. Autoimmune serologies were negative (MPO-ANCA <1.0 U/mL, PR3-ANCA <1.0 U/mL, fluorescent ANA negative, IgG4 21 mg/dL). 

Electrocardiography, echocardiography, and chest radiography were unremarkable. Abdominal aortic ultrasonography demonstrated circumferential wall thickening with a surrounding hypoechoic rim. Contrast-enhanced CT showed new soft-tissue stranding around the aortic arch and abdominal aorta compared with pre-chemotherapy images. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI revealed thickening and enhancement of the aortic wall and periaortic tissue. These imaging findings suggested active inflammation of the aorta.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious aortitis was considered given persistent fever and periaortic inflammation but was excluded by “Negative bacterial, fungal, and viral test results were observed, and a broad-spectrum antibiotic treatment did not completely improve her condition.” 2. Syphilitic and tuberculous aortitis were considered and excluded by serology: “STS Negative; TbIFN-γ Negative.” 3. Systemic lupus erythematosus was considered but excluded by “FANA Negative.” 4. Behçet disease and Cogan syndrome were listed among other vasculitides under “Other differential diagnoses include … Behçet disease, Cogan syndrome …” 5. Malignancy with periaortic involvement was considered among “Other differential diagnoses include … malignancy,” but imaging lacked mass lesions.

title: Fusarium proliferatum-induced chronic lip ulcer: successful treatment with itraconazole: a case report
case_prompt: A 41-year-old Iranian woman with no known immunodeficiency presented with a 10-month history of slowly enlarging, crusted, painful ulcerative lesions on her lower lip. She had no history of smoking, alcohol, opium use, trauma, cosmetic lip tattooing, or burns. She had intermittent bronchitis treated with a corticosteroid nasal spray. On examination, there was an ill-demarcated, tender, thick-crusted ulcer measuring 3 × 1 cm on the lower lip, which discharged fluid when pressed. There were no signs of onychomycosis or interdigital intertrigo; she was afebrile, with no lymphadenopathy, and the remainder of her exam was unremarkable. HIV antibody, hepatitis B surface antigen, and hepatitis C antibody tests were negative. An upper endoscopy performed for dysphagia was normal. Despite local wound care, topical antifungal and antimicrobial therapies, and empiric immunosuppressive and antibiotic treatments, the ulcer worsened. Clinical differential diagnoses included squamous cell carcinoma, other malignant lesions, ulcerative leishmaniasis, ulcerative mycobacterial infection, erosive lichen planus, immunobullous disorders, and opportunistic fungal infection. Two incisional biopsies of the lip lesion showed parakeratotic hyperkeratosis, severe acanthosis, spongiosis, exocytosis of inflammatory cells in the epidermis, and a mixed dermal infiltrate without evidence of malignant transformation. Direct immunofluorescence was negative for immunobullous diseases. A direct smear for leishmaniasis was negative, and the nitroblue tetrazolium test was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Other malignant lesions were also considered but ruled out by the same histopathologic finding: “There was no evidence of … malignant transformation.” 2. Ulcerative leishmaniasis was in the differential but was excluded when “The direct smear for leishmaniasis was negative.” 3. Ulcerative mycobacterial infection was considered: “clinical differential diagnosis of … ulcerative mycobacterial infection was considered.” 4. Erosive lichen planus was considered as a cause of chronic ulceration: “clinical differential diagnosis of … erosive lichen planus was considered.” 5. Immunobullous disorders were suspected but excluded because “Direct immunofluorescence evaluation was negative for immunobullous diseases.”

title: Extraparenchymal neurocysticercosis in the United States: a case report
case_prompt: A 26-year-old Caucasian man with a history of diabetes mellitus presented with a two-month history of diffuse headache, progressive nausea, and vomiting. He denied fever, chills, or photophobia. He was born in Mexico and had lived in California for eight years. On examination, his temperature was 37 °C; he had mild neck stiffness, but neurologic and ophthalmologic examinations were otherwise normal. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 6500/mm3 (75% neutrophils, 15% lymphocytes, 8% monocytes), serum glucose of 90 mg/dL, and normal electrolytes and liver–renal function tests. Noncontrast head CT revealed dilated lateral and third ventricles consistent with hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure of 400 mmHg, 330 white blood cells/mm3 (14% neutrophils, 45% lymphocytes, 38% monocytes, 3% eosinophils), protein 84 mg/dL, glucose 43 mg/dL, and 3 red blood cells/mm3. Gram stain and India ink of cerebrospinal fluid were negative, and bacterial, viral, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures were negative. Empiric antimycobacterial therapy was initiated for presumed tuberculous meningitis. MRI of the brain demonstrated hydrocephalus with dilation of the lateral and third ventricles, stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct, absence of meningeal enhancement, and a subtle cystic area in the region of the aqueduct.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial meningitis was excluded — “A Gram stain … of CSF were negative and final … bacterial … cultures were negative.” 2. Viral meningitis was excluded — “final … viral … cultures were negative.” 3. Fungal meningitis was excluded — “final … fungal … cultures were negative.” 4. Mycobacterial meningitis was excluded — “final … mycobacterial cultures were negative.”

title: A case report of a childhood scurvy musculoskeletal manifestation: Radiologic findings and diagnostic implications
case_prompt: A 6-year-old boy was brought for evaluation of 2 months of persistent, throbbing right thigh pain and progressive swelling. The pain worsened at night and during inactivity, and he had difficulty walking for weeks. Parents noted generalized lethargy and low-grade fever for 2 weeks before presentation. There was no history of trauma or family history of cancer. Dietary history revealed minimal fruit and vegetable intake. On examination, he appeared irritable and malnourished (weight <1st percentile). There were hemorrhagic findings on the gingiva and labia. Both hip and knee joints were held in a semi-flexed position, with pain on attempted extension. The right thigh was edematous but not erythematous; a firm, tender mass extended across the mid-thigh. Laboratory studies showed WBC 11,740/µL (83.5% neutrophils), hemoglobin 4.8 g/dL, hematocrit 16.3%, normal MCV, MCH, and MCHC, and platelets 751,000/µL. Coagulation studies, metabolic panel, and electrolytes were normal. Given the clinical presentation of a painful thigh mass, the orthopedic team considered a primary bone neoplasm. A radiographic assessment of the right femur and a fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB) were requisitioned to substantiate this.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Primary bone neoplasm was considered because of persistent thigh pain, swelling, and a palpable mass — “the orthopedic consultant postulated a primary bone neoplasm as a differential diagnosis.” 2. Nutritional deficiency–related bone disorder was suggested by the patient’s diet — “the child's dietary recall highlighted a significant deficit in the consumption of fruits and vegetables.”

title: Non-classical Celiac Disease as an Unrecognized Cause for Post-operative Knee Swelling following Patellofemoral Arthroplasty: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 34-year-old woman with a history of recurrent patellar dislocations and progressive right anterior knee pain underwent patellofemoral arthroplasty with concomitant medial patellofemoral ligament reconstruction. The surgery was uneventful. Four weeks postoperatively, she noted an erythematous, blanchable rash around the arthrotomy incision, associated with warmth, swelling, and stiffness limiting range of motion to 0–30°. She was afebrile, and vital signs were normal. A 7-day course of trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole was initiated for presumed skin and soft-tissue infection. Two days later, she returned with increased pain, persistent rash with mild urticaria and warmth, tenderness along the joint line, and a trace effusion; the surgical incision sites were well healed. She was referred to the emergency department, where she received intravenous vancomycin and diphenhydramine. Laboratory tests showed normal inflammatory markers, and MRI of the knee showed no evidence of infection; the rash and swelling subjectively improved.  

Over postoperative weeks 7 through 21, she had recurrent episodes of knee warmth, swelling, a reticular rash over the limb, and throbbing pain rated up to 10/10 with activity, significantly impairing daily activities. At week 14, she presented to the emergency department with fatigue and headache; workup for infection and thromboembolism was negative. Outpatient allergy evaluation considered implant allergy, mastocytosis, and hereditary angioedema; patch testing was negative, and serum C4 complement and tryptase levels were within normal limits. Vascular surgery evaluation for May–Thurner syndrome and popliteal entrapment included MRI angiography and abdominopelvic CT with contrast, which demonstrated patent vasculature. On further questioning, she reported intermittent bloating, fatigue, and headaches. Allergy specialists recommended maintenance of a rigorous food diary before pursuing further testing.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cellulitis or skin and soft-tissue infection — “inflammatory markers and MRI were negative for infection with subjective improvement of erythema, urticaria, and swelling.” 2. Venous thromboembolism — “Following negative infectious and thromboembolic workup in the ED, she was referred to outpatient allergy and vascular surgery.” 3. Implant allergy — “Patch testing was negative.” 4. Mastocytosis — “blood levels of … tryptase were within normal limits.” 5. Vascular compression (May–Thurner syndrome, popliteal entrapment) — “advanced imaging … demonstrated patent vasculature.”

title: Ménétrier’s disease presenting as recurrent unprovoked venous thrombosis: a case report
case_prompt: A 40‐year‐old man with a history of juvenile polyposis syndrome (post–partial colectomy) and alcohol use disorder in remission presented in June 2018 with sudden‐onset right calf pain, swelling, and erythema. Venous ultrasound showed an acute, occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal, tibial, and peroneal veins. He was started on rivaroxaban. One week later, he returned with 3 days of melena. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 5.3 g/dL and MCV 55.7 fL; he received 3 units of packed red blood cells. Anticoagulation was held, and abdominal CT showed transmural thickening of the gastric antral and pyloric walls protruding into the duodenal bulb, multifocal bilateral segmental and subsegmental pulmonary emboli, and a nonocclusive thrombus from the right renal vein into the suprarenal IVC. An IVC filter was placed. Upper endoscopy demonstrated diffusely irregular, raised gastric mucosa throughout the gastric body, resembling a soft carpeted mass. Biopsy revealed prominent foveolar hyperplasia, focal granulation tissue, ulceration, reactive glandular changes, and chronic active inflammation, with no evidence of dysplasia or malignancy. Immunostaining was negative for Helicobacter pylori. A probable diagnosis of Ménétrier disease was established, and he was discharged on high‐dose oral pantoprazole.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Gastric carcinoma considered because of mass‐like thickening and raised gastric mucosa — “the gross appearance of our patient’s gastric lesion was initially most suggestive of malignancy …” 2. Malignancy excluded when biopsy showed no dysplasia or malignancy — “however, despite the suspicious gross appearance, there was no evidence of dysplasia or malignancy.”

title: Successful treatment of suspect Babesia‐induced ARDS in a dog using lung‐protective positive‐pressure ventilation and neuromuscular blockade
case_prompt:
An 11‐week‐old intact male mixed‐breed dog was referred for severe anemia and suspected babesiosis. Three weeks earlier, he had been transported from Texas. One week after transport, he developed acute lethargy and fever (40.4°C). In‐house CBC showed severe microcytic, normochromic, non‐regenerative anemia (HCT 0.147 L/L; RI 0.373–0.617 L/L) and severe thrombocytopenia (23 × 10^9/L; RI 148–484 × 10^9/L). He received 15 ml/kg pRBC over 4 h and was discharged on omeprazole, sucralfate, and amoxicillin/clavulanate. The next day, PCV was 23%. Eight days later, he was lethargic with PCV 15%; a blood smear was suspicious for Babesia, and clindamycin was started. When he became too weak to stand, he was referred.

On presentation, the dog was nonambulatory, pale, mildly icteric, and splenomegaly was noted. Weight was 5.75 kg; temperature 38.2°C; heart rate 150 beats/min; respiratory rate 60 breaths/min. Point-of-care testing showed PCV 14%, total protein 5.4 g/dL, and compensated metabolic acidosis with lactate 5.2 mmol/L. Ultrasound revealed volume contraction, no effusions or pulmonary B-lines, and subjective splenomegaly. He was blood‐typed (DEA 1.1 positive), cross-matched, and transfused with 14 ml/kg pRBCs. Cytologic evaluation demonstrated intra-erythrocytic piroplasms consistent with Babesia canis. He was treated with IM imidocarb and IV fluids.

After the first transfusion, PCV remained 13% and lactate rose to 8.2 mmol/L. He received diphenhydramine for suspected transfusion reaction and an additional 13 ml/kg pRBCs. Fourteen hours after admission, his respiratory rate exceeded 100 breaths/min; he was placed in an oxygen chamber, then developed diarrhea and hypersalivation treated with atropine. Tachypnea worsened further, necessitating emergent endotracheal intubation. During intubation, a large volume of serosanguineous/icteric fluid was produced through the endotracheal tube. Initial manual ventilation with 100% FiO₂ yielded SpO₂ values of 88%–92%, after which he was transitioned to mechanical ventilation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. ARDS considered — “Diagnostic considerations included ARDS or transfusion‐related acute lung injury (TRALI).” 2. TRALI considered then deemed less likely — “transfusion‐related acute lung injury cannot be ruled out, although a blood type and crossmatch were performed prior to transfusion.”

title: Longitudinal observation of ten family members with idiopathic basal ganglia calcification: A case report
case_prompt:
A 65-year-old woman was admitted for evaluation of progressive forgetfulness that had been present for five years. She reported difficulty preparing meals, repetitive conversations, and repeated purchasing of identical items. Neuropsychological testing showed an MMSE score of 19/30, an HDS-R score of 20/30, and a Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale–Revised total IQ of 87 (verbal IQ 83, performance IQ 92).

Her past medical history included uterine myoma at age 42 and cerebral aneurysm clipping at age 58. She graduated from junior high school and had worked in agriculture, construction, and food service. There was a family history of brain calcification: one brother had mental retardation with documented calcifications and another brother had alcoholism; her parents were asymptomatic.

On examination, there were no pyramidal or extrapyramidal signs, and the Albright sign was negative. Laboratory studies showed normal levels of thyroid hormones, parathyroid hormone, serum calcium, serum phosphate, and cerebrospinal fluid constituents. A treponemal hemagglutination assay was negative.

Noncontrast cranial CT revealed symmetric calcifications in the globus pallidus, pulvinar thalami, right frontal subcortical white matter, and the occipital cortex–white matter junction. T1-weighted MRI showed small patchy hyperintensities in the globus pallidus and pulvinar thalami, and T2-weighted MRI demonstrated corresponding hypointensities in the globus pallidus. Brain perfusion SPECT demonstrated decreased perfusion in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, and right frontal lobe.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Exclusion of metabolic causes — “the levels of thyroid hormones, parathyroid hormone (PT), serum calcium, serum phosphate and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) were all in the range of normal values.” 2.
