Exclusion of infectious causes — “the Treponema pallidum haemagglutination assay test was negative.” 3. Exclusion of hypoparathyroidism and pseudohypoparathyroidism — “The Albright sign was negative. There was no evidence of hypoparathyroidism and pseudohypoparathyroidism.” 4. Exclusion of toxic and traumatic causes — “the diagnosis of IBGC relies mainly on the visualization of bilateral calcification in the basal ganglia … and the absence of metabolic, infectious, toxic, or traumatic causes.”

title: Diffuse panbronchiolitis in a Samoan man
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old Samoan man was referred for evaluation of a 2-month history of persistent productive cough and worsening exertional dyspnea. Over the preceding 3 months he had noted subjective fevers and a 5-kg weight loss. He had chronic sinusitis and had been diagnosed with asthma in adolescence; treatment with inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators yielded no improvement. He was a lifelong non-smoker, worked in a mattress factory, and had no known risk factors for tuberculosis or immunosuppression.

On examination, he was afebrile, with an oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Auscultation revealed coarse crackles predominantly at the lung bases, without wheezing; there were no signs of right heart failure.

A chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral nodular interstitial infiltrates in the mid and lower lung zones. High-resolution computed tomography of the chest showed extensive tree-in-bud nodularity in all lobes, most pronounced in the lower lobes, along with bronchial wall thickening and early bronchiectasis. Spirometry revealed a fixed moderately severe airflow obstruction (FEV₁ 55% predicted, FEV₁/FVC 63%).

Sputum cultures grew Haemophilus influenzae; mycobacterial cultures were negative. An autoimmune panel, including rheumatoid factor and cold agglutinins, was within normal limits.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lack of response to asthma therapy makes asthma less likely — “He had been started on inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators with no improvement in his symptoms.”

title: Renal Replacement Lipomatosis Presenting in the Setting of Ureteral Stricture with Absence of Renal Calculus Disease
case_prompt: A 51-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus status post pancreas transplant presented for evaluation prior to kidney transplant and right native nephrectomy. She had developed a right ureteral stricture managed by a nephrostomy tube and nephroureterostomy stenting every two months, with progressive decline in right renal function and recurrent complicated urinary tract infections. Her past surgical history included left native nephrectomy at age 2, for unclear reasons. She denied any history of renal calculi. Preoperative contrast-enhanced CT and MRI of the abdomen demonstrated an atrophic right kidney with extensive lipomatous proliferation of the renal sinus fat and marked inflammation within the collecting system. A percutaneous drainage tube was in place.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis was considered because of chronic infection and parenchymal destruction — “XGP is characterized by destruction of the renal parenchyma and replacement by lipid-laden macrophages, whereas renal lipomatosis is a fatty proliferation of the renal sinus with atrophy of the renal parenchyma.” 2. Renal lipoma was considered due to the presence of a fatty mass within the kidney — “Renal lipomas tend to be small and originate in the fat cells within the renal capsule.” 3. Renal liposarcoma was considered as a fat-containing neoplasm — “Renal liposarcomas arise peripherally in perirenal fat within Gerota's fascia or within the renal capsule and may distort the kidney but usually do not invade the adjacent renal parenchyma.” 4. Angiomyolipoma was considered because of adipose tissue, vascular, and smooth muscle components — “The presence of smooth muscle fibers, thick-walled blood vessels, and coexpression of melanocytic and smooth muscle markers by immunohistochemistry in myoid and lipoid components in angiomyolipoma … is absent in renal lipomatosis.”

title: Treatment of Generalized Anxiety Disorder with Gabapentin
case_prompt:
A 59-year-old Caucasian woman with major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder presented after discontinuation of benzodiazepines. Her medical history includes Hashimoto’s thyroiditis on levothyroxine, primary Sjögren’s syndrome, and autoimmune diabetes with peripheral neuropathy. She has no tobacco, alcohol, or illicit-substance use. Family history is positive for mood and anxiety disorders.

She has previously failed or not tolerated multiple antidepressants and anxiolytics, including citalopram, paroxetine, duloxetine, amitriptyline, nortriptyline, mirtazapine, bupropion, aripiprazole, trazodone, and pregabalin. Cognitive behavioral therapy trials were unsuccessful. At age 52 she was on clonazepam up to 1 mg twice daily, but benzodiazepines were tapered three months before presentation, and her anxiety recurred. Her symptoms included persistent and excessive worry, restlessness, feeling on edge, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, insomnia, dysphoric mood, diaphoresis, palpitations, tremulousness, and muscle tension. She developed suicidal ideation and required two psychiatric hospitalizations over one month. Benzodiazepines were ultimately discontinued and she remained off them for 10 months.

During her most recent hospitalization, gabapentin was started at 300 mg three times daily for benzodiazepine withdrawal. She reported sedation but felt calmer and noted dissipation of anxiety. The dose was increased to 600 mg three times daily, and she was discharged.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Generalized anxiety disorder was considered given her pervasive worry and somatic symptoms — “We considered generalized anxiety disorder…anxiety due to another medical condition, such as thyroid disease, medication-induced mood, and anxiety disorder from prolonged benzodiazepine withdrawal.” 2. Panic disorder was included in the differential because of palpitations and tremulousness — “We considered generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder, MDD, obsessive compulsive disorder, anxiety due to another medical condition, such as thyroid disease, medication-induced mood, and anxiety disorder from prolonged benzodiazepine withdrawal.” 3. Major depressive disorder was evaluated due to dysphoric mood and suicidality — “We considered…MDD…” 4. Anxiety due to another medical condition (thyroid disease) was considered given her hypothyroidism — “We considered…anxiety due to another medical condition, such as thyroid disease…”

title: Recurrent lymphocytic meningitis and progressive dementia: manifestations of relapsing polychondritis: a case report
case_prompt:
A 71-year-old man was admitted with bitemporal and frontal headache radiating to the mandible, gait difficulties, low-grade fever, disorientation, transient loss of sphincter control, and prostration. His medical history included hypertension treated with losartan, and three prior episodes of meningitis (first at age 58 diagnosed as tuberculous meningitis without microbiological confirmation; second and third at ages 66 and 70, both aseptic lymphocytic meningitis) followed by secondary progressive cognitive decline. At age 56, he was diagnosed with relapsing polychondritis, fulfilling Michet’s criteria for auricular, nasal, and laryngotracheal chondritis, but he had not received follow-up or treatment.

On examination, he was drowsy and disoriented; temperature was 100.2 °F, and other vital signs were normal. A deformity was noted in the right auricle. Laboratory studies showed leukocytes 13,400/µL (neutrophils 10,600/µL, lymphocytes 1,900/µL), hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelets 350,000/µL, C-reactive protein 1.14 mg/dL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 10 mm/h; electrolytes were normal. Blood and urine cultures were negative.

Contrast-enhanced brain MRI demonstrated supratentorial and infratentorial nodular leptomeningeal enhancement, enlargement of the supratentorial ventricular system, and foci of subcortical and periventricular nodular enhancement. Lumbar puncture revealed lymphocytic pleocytosis similar to previous episodes. Microbiological studies and PCR of cerebrospinal fluid for multiple pathogens, including herpesviruses, were negative, ruling out Mollaret meningitis. CSF cytology and flow cytometry were negative for malignant infiltration, and chest and abdominal CT scans showed no suspicious lesions. Antinuclear antibodies were 1:160 with a homogeneous pattern; specific tests for systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, vasculitis, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, and Sjögren’s syndrome were negative. Studies for autoimmune encephalitis were not available.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious meningitis — “Microbiological studies and PCR of cerebrospinal fluid for multiple microorganisms were negative, including herpes viruses (Mollaret meningitis was ruled out).” 2. Neoplastic leptomeningeal disease — “Cytology and flow cytometry of the cerebrospinal fluid were negative for neoplastic infiltration, and there were no suspicious lesions of neoplasia in chest or abdominal tomography.” 3. Autoimmune encephalitis — “Studies for autoimmune encephalitis were not available.”

title: A Challenging Case of Oral Ulcers and Gastrointestinal Bleeding: Crohn's or Behçet's Disease
case_prompt:
An 8-year-old boy of Brazilian descent with autism presented with an 8-month history of painful oral ulcers. He had a one‐time perianal ulcer, folliculitis on his scalp and buttocks, intermittent fever, and bloody stools. He had no genital ulcers. He was initially treated as PFAPA with steroids, but symptoms recurred when steroids were tapered. Family history was noncontributory.

On examination, he had multiple large oral ulcers on the tongue, palate, and lips. Abdominal examination was normal, with no tenderness or masses; there were no perianal ulcers, arthritis, or other skin findings. An extensive workup for systemic lupus erythematosus, herpes simplex virus, Epstein–Barr virus, tuberculosis, cyclic neutropenia, primary immunodeficiencies, and periodic fever syndromes was unremarkable. Ophthalmological examination was negative, with no pathergy. Buccal biopsies showed chronic inflammation with granulation tissue, no granulomas, and grew Fusobacterium periodontium.

Prometheus IBD sgi testing demonstrated a pattern consistent with Crohn’s disease (positive anti-A4-Fa2 IgG, anti-Fax IgG, ATG16L1 heterozygous, NKX2-3 heterozygous, ICAM-1, SAA 179, elevated CRP; ASCA and ANCA negative). Multiple endoscopies and colonoscopies with ileal intubation were negative. Capsule endoscopy, CT of the abdomen, and MRI enterography showed no abnormalities.

Despite treatment with steroids, colchicine, infliximab, and methotrexate—later escalated to high-dose infliximab—the patient’s bloody stools worsened and he developed severe malnutrition (BMI z-score −2.38). At month 18, a fourth colonoscopy revealed large, round ulcers in the terminal ileum with granulation tissue and chronic inflammatory infiltrates, but no granulomas or cobblestoning.

Treatment was switched to ustekinumab, after which gastrointestinal symptoms improved. At month 19, he developed bilateral hip effusions and later presented with headaches, emesis, and fevers. A lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure of 39 cm H2O; brain MRI/MRA/MRV revealed extensive thrombosis of the superior sagittal sinus, straight sinus, and proximal right transverse sinus. A hypercoagulability panel was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Crohn’s disease was initially favored based on IBD sgi testing — “Prometheus IBD sgi testing showed a pattern consistent with CD…” 2. Intestinal tuberculosis was excluded by negative workup — “there are other clinical conditions… such as intestinal tuberculosis, but these were ruled out.” 3. Primary immunodeficiencies and periodic fever syndromes were excluded by initial testing — “initial workup was largely unremarkable… including… primary immunodeficiencies [and] periodic fever syndromes.” 5. Behçet’s disease was supported by mucocutaneous lesions, round ileal ulcers, treatment-refractory GI disease that improved on ustekinumab, and cerebral venous thrombosis — “the decision was made to decrease inflammation and treat it as Behçet's disease since gastrointestinal symptoms had improved… which decreased the likelihood of CD as the underlying cause.”

title: Fibrinogen deficiency in a dog - a case report
case_prompt:
A 1.5-year-old male German Pointer weighing 27 kg was referred for a 10-day history of right third eyelid protrusion and a locked-jaw syndrome with extreme pain on mouth opening. The signs began after the dog was struck on the head by a ball and had remained stable. Three months earlier, the dog had presented in shock with head wounds suggestive of a snake bite; laboratory tests then revealed markedly increased prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). Despite symptomatic treatment, PT and aPTT remained moderately elevated. One month later, a gingival wound required surgical suturing to stop bleeding, with PT >35 s (RI 12–19 s) and aPTT >200 s (RI 75–105 s).

On admission, the dog had exophthalmos of the right eye with third eyelid protrusion, reluctance to head palpation, and severe pain on opening the mouth. Ocular ultrasonography suggested a retrobulbar cellulitis without evidence of a foreign body. Because of the bleeding history, invasive sampling was deferred pending laboratory results.

An extensive hemostatic profile showed: buccal mucosal bleeding time 3 min 10 s (RI 3–5 min); platelet count 175,000/μL (RI 64,000–613,000/μL); PT 15.0 s (RI 7.1–9.0 s); aPTT 19.3 s (RI 12.8–17.2 s); and fibrinogen 0.34 g/L (RI 1.30–4.80 g/L). D-dimers and antithrombin were within reference intervals, and fibrinogen/fibrin degradation products were in the gray zone (5–20 μg/mL; RI <5 μg/mL). Repeated coagulometric measurements and a Millar heat-precipitation assay confirmed severe hypofibrinogenemia with an almost undetectable precipitation band.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — “The absence of clinical signs of disease conditions that are associated with disseminated intra-vascular coagulation (DIC) together with the absence of any other laboratory signs of hemostasis system activation (normal platelet count, DDi and AT) was not consistent with DIC.” 2. Dysfibrinogenemia — “However, because the fibrinogen concentration was similarly decreased with both coagulometric method and heat precipitation method, we concluded it was hypofibrinogenemia.” 3. After exclusion of all acquired causes — congenital fibrinogen deficiency was considered.

title: Case report of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome with retinal arterial and venous occlusion treated with eculizumab
case_prompt:
A 26-year-old African American woman presented with 3 days of progressive abdominal and pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Two months earlier, she had been hospitalized for possible rheumatic fever with polyserositis and cardiac tamponade requiring pericardial drainage. Medications included colchicine, lisinopril, proton-pump inhibitors, ranitidine, tramadol, penicillin G, and albuterol; she had recently quit smoking. Family history was significant for hypertension.

On examination, she was tachycardic (112 beats/min) with a 2/6 systolic murmur. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (41×10^9/L), hemoglobin 107 g/L, hypoalbuminemia (28 g/L), and serum creatinine 53 µmol/L. Urinalysis revealed 1+ protein. Abdominal CT demonstrated colitis and mild ascites. A presumptive diagnosis of sepsis related to Clostridium difficile colitis was made, and she received IV metronidazole, oral vancomycin, and IV fluids.

Four days later, serum creatinine rose to 159 µmol/L, leukocyte count to 60×10^9/L, and platelet count fell to 71×10^9/L; hemoglobin remained stable. She was evaluated for possible acute tubular necrosis and vasculitis. A peripheral smear then showed schistocytes and LDH was elevated at 3.69 µkat/L. A diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) was presumed, and she was started on high-dose steroids and daily plasma exchange. Dialysis and mycophenolate mofetil were also initiated. She subsequently developed seizures treated with levetiracetam.

Shortly thereafter, she reported vision loss. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed bilateral disc, macular, and retinal hemorrhages, central retinal artery and vein occlusion, and vitreous hemorrhage with a best-corrected visual acuity of 1–2 feet. Outpatient dialysis and plasma exchange were continued three times weekly without improvement. On September 2, her ADAMTS13 activity was found to be 61%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sepsis — “Sepsis, possibly related to Clostridium difficile colitis, was presumed.” 2. TTP — “A diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) was presumed.”

title: CT perfusion: stroke, seizure or both?
case_prompt: An independent octogenarian with hypertension, coronary artery disease, and prior Bell’s palsy was last seen well at 10 :00 AM but found ‘confused’ at 12 :40 PM with new speech disturbance, right facial droop, and right‐sided weakness. On arrival at 1 :40 PM, his NIH Stroke Scale score was 19, with severe aphasia, impaired command following, visual inattention, near‐unintelligible speech, and right hemiparesis. Blood pressure was 155/88 mm Hg. Baseline blood tests and electrolytes were unremarkable; finger‐prick glucose was normal. Noncontrast CT of the head showed chronic small‐vessel ischemic changes and arterial calcification without hemorrhage or acute infarct. Intravenous alteplase was administered 26 minutes after referral. During infusion, CT angiography revealed no large‐vessel occlusion. CT perfusion imaging demonstrated relatively reduced time to peak and time to maximum, and increased relative cerebral blood volume and flow in the left hemisphere compared with the right, findings interpreted as compatible with seizure activity.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Total anterior circulation stroke was the leading diagnosis given the acute density of the hemiparesis, facial weakness, visual inattention, dysarthria, and dysphasia — “dense hemiparesis, facial weakness, visual field deficit, hemi-inattention, dysarthria and dysphasia all pointed to an acute vascular event.” 2. The CT perfusion pattern of hyperperfusion was more consistent with seizure than with ischaemic core and penumbra — “CT perfusion scan was more in keeping with a seizure.”

title: Rare variant of metaplastic carcinoma of the breast: a case report and review of the literature
case_prompt: A 53-year-old woman with no family history of cancer presented with a lump in her left breast. Physical examination revealed a movable, painless, 3 cm nodule between the upper and lower inner quadrants of the left breast, without inflammatory signs or nipple retraction; the axillae were clinically negative. Mammography demonstrated a nodular lesion with irregular contours and peripheral calcification, classified as BI-RADS 4. Ultrasonography confirmed a 32 mm hypoechoic nodule with irregular, polylobed margins in the same location. An ultrasound-guided core-needle biopsy was performed. Histopathological examination showed spindle cell proliferation without hemorrhage or necrosis. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed positivity for cytokeratin AE1/AE3 and smooth muscle actin, a Ki-67 labeling index of 25%, and nuclear p63 expression. The pathology report concluded a spindle cell carcinomatous proliferation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fibromatosis was considered because it is a benign spindle cell lesion expressing actin, but excluded as “in contrast to SCC, CKs are not expressed.” 2. Primary pure spindle cell sarcoma was considered as a malignant spindle cell neoplasm but excluded since “IHC examination shows a lack of epithelial components in most pure spindle cell sarcomas of the breast.” 3. Malignant phyllodes tumor was considered given a large fibroepithelial mass but excluded as “Generally, the diagnosis of MPT does not require the use of immunohistochemistry.” 4. Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor was included among spindle cell lesions in the differential but excluded by the presence of epithelial markers: “SCC must be considered in the differential diagnosis of other spindle cell lesions (benign and malignant entities), such as fibromatosis, primary low-grade sarcoma, inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor, and malignant phyllodes tumors.”

title: Consecutive bilateral decompression retinopathy after mitomycin C trabeculectomy: a case report
case_prompt:
A 54-year-old woman with primary open-angle glaucoma and poor adherence to topical therapy was referred for surgical management. She had a history of severe obesity (BMI 37 kg/m2) and well-controlled insulin-dependent diabetes. Preoperative best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes. Untreated IOPs were consistently in the mid-30s mmHg; with tafluprost, her IOPs were 18 mmHg OD and 16 mmHg OS. Optic discs showed a temporal notch OD and were otherwise unremarkable.  

She underwent an uneventful mitomycin C–augmented trabeculectomy in the right eye. On postoperative day 1, her IOP was 8 mmHg, the bleb was functioning, and the anterior chamber was well formed; fundus examination was normal.  

Two weeks later, she complained of blurred vision in the right eye. Her best-corrected visual acuity was 20/32, and IOP was 10 mmHg off medications. Fundoscopy revealed multiple superficial, flame-shaped hemorrhages radiating from the optic disc with disc edema and no choroidal effusion. Optical coherence tomography showed macular folding with a small neurosensory detachment. Fluorescein angiography demonstrated macular microaneurysms with dye leakage, peripapillary hemorrhages, and late optic disc leakage without areas of nonperfusion or neovascularization.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Decompression retinopathy was suspected because it presents after an acute lowering of IOP with retinal hemorrhages, optic disc edema, and macular changes — “Our patient had all the described characteristics of DR, including retinal hemorrhages, optic disc edema, macular edema, and decreased visual acuity.” 2. Valsalva retinopathy was considered but deemed unlikely given that it typically causes pre-retinal hemorrhages centered on the macula, may involve vitreous hemorrhage, and is usually bilateral — “Typical ocular findings are classically described as pre-retinal hemorrhages with predilection for the macula and possible vitreous hemorrhages. Additionally, this condition is usually simultaneously bilateral.”

title: A case of fulminant perimyocarditis leading to extensive ECMO treatment and spinal injury resulting in paraplegia
case_prompt: A 28‐year‐old woman with mild asthma presented with 2 days of fever to 39.5°C, headache, fatigue, and pleuritic chest pain radiating to the back. On examination, she was febrile, with a heart rate of 71 beats/min, blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg, normal perfusion, and unremarkable heart and lung auscultation. The initial ECG showed a new left bundle branch block and first‐degree AV block. Laboratory studies revealed a CRP of 145 mg/L, a leukocyte count of 10.6 × 10^9/L with eosinophilic predominance, and a troponin-T of 5423 ng/L. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal right‐sided chambers but regional wall motion abnormalities in the LV apical region, without pericardial effusion or valvular abnormalities. CT angiography excluded aortic dissection and pulmonary embolism. A presumptive diagnosis of perimyocarditis was made, and in anticipation of coronary angiography she received aspirin, ticagrelor, and fondaparinux. During transfer she developed complete AV block with a junctional escape rhythm of 20–25 beats/min and was started on isoproterenol. Coronary angiography revealed normal vessels. Empiric benzylpenicillin, gentamicin, and doxycycline were initiated for suspected Lyme‐associated perimyocarditis. Overnight, she experienced repeated asystolic pauses up to 9.5 seconds despite escalating isoproterenol, atrial flutter, and increasing ventricular ectopy. The next morning her fever peaked at 39.8°C, systolic blood pressure fell to 70–85 mm Hg, oxygen requirements increased, oliguria developed, lactate rose, and pH dropped to 7.23. Repeat echocardiography showed an LVEF <25% without chamber dilatation, and transfer for VA‐ECMO support was arranged.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hypersensitivity myocarditis — “However, the presence of a modest peripheral eosinophilia does maintain the possibility of a hypersensitivity myocarditis syndrome.” 2. Other causes of lymphocytic myocarditis — “Lymphocytic myocarditis can be caused by a viral infection (including Coxsackie B, Coxsackie A, Echovirus, Adenovirus, Influenza, Epstein-Barr virus, or Human Immunodeficiency Virus), bacterial infection (including Borrelia burgdorferi, Corynebacterium diphtheria, and bacterial endocarditis), autoimmune diseases (including rheumatoid arthritis, lupus and inflammatory bowel disease) and toxins (including drugs such as cocaine and cyclophosphamide).”

title: Acute obstruction of a mechanical aortic valve in a young woman: case report and review of the literature
case_prompt:
A 27-year-old woman with Marfan syndrome underwent ascending aortic replacement and mechanical aortic valve insertion 8 years earlier. She presented with a 2-week history of rhinorrhea, progressive dyspnea, and central chest pain radiating to the back. Over the preceding two nights she developed night sweats but had no fever or rigors. Her only medication was warfarin, and outpatient records suggested that her international normalized ratios had been within the therapeutic range.

On arrival at the tertiary cardiac ICU, she was fully conscious but in cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Respiratory rate was 50 breaths/min with coarse crepitations over all lung zones and oxygen saturations of 85–88% on supplemental oxygen. She was tachycardic (sinus rhythm at 145 beats/min) with a blood pressure of 109/65 mmHg. Laboratory tests showed an INR of 1.3, APTT ratio 0.95, troponin I 4289 ng/L (normal <50), ALT 115 U/L (normal 0–40), CRP 67 mg/L, white blood cell count 28.2×10^9/L (neutrophils 25.4×10^9/L), hemoglobin 146 g/L, and platelets 233×10^9/L.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated severely restricted excursion of the bileaflet aortic prosthesis with no obvious thrombus or vegetations, a peak transvalvular gradient of 128 mmHg, and a hyperdynamic, non-dilated but impaired left ventricle. Chest radiography showed bilateral pulmonary edema.

The differential diagnosis was thrombosis, pannus formation, or—least likely—infective endocarditis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pannus formation was considered because of the long interval since valve implantation and its ability to obstruct leaflets despite adequate anticoagulation — “Pannus is a fibro-connective tissue ingrowth from the sewing ring that occurs many years after valve implantation.”

title: A case report of iatrogenic deterioration of yet undiagnosed Rhombencephalitis; always be careful with corticoids
case_prompt: A 67-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer treated three years earlier presented with progressive perioral numbness and dizziness of 16 days’ duration. She was initially treated as an outpatient for presumed vertebrobasilar stroke with carbasalate calcium. On admission, MRI of the brain showed intact cerebral vasculature but diffuse fluid-attenuated inversion recovery hyperintense lesions around the fourth ventricle extending into the pons and medulla oblongata. A lumbar puncture revealed 240 cells/μL (30% lymphocytes) and a CSF glucose of 3.7 mmol/L; blood and CSF cultures were negative. Empiric acyclovir and dipyridamole were started for presumed herpes encephalitis with secondary vasculitis, but the patient deteriorated. A second CSF sample showed 102 cells/μL (96% lymphocytes) and a glucose of 3.2 mmol/L. Initial Borrelia serology was negative, but on day 15 IgG seroconversion (negative IgM) combined with a recent tick bite led to a presumptive diagnosis of Lyme disease; ceftriaxone and dexamethasone were administered. Four days later the patient developed spiking fever, complete left hemiparesis, and cranial-nerve palsies. Three weeks after admission she was transferred to our center. On arrival she was somnolent (GCS 14), febrile (40.2 °C), hypertensive (167/70 mmHg), tachycardic (100/min), and tachypneic (25/min), with a full left-sided hemiparesis. Rapid deterioration necessitated ICU transfer and intubation (GCS 7). A repeat CSF sample showed 23 cells/μL and a glucose of 3.4 mmol/L.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cerebrovascular accident was excluded because MRI angiography showed preserved vasculature — “the initial diagnosis of CVA in the vertebrobasilar region was rejected, because the Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) showed fully intact vasculature.” 2. Herpes encephalitis with secondary vasculitis was considered based on imaging and CSF pleocytosis but deemed unlikely after lack of improvement with acyclovir — “With these results, Herpes encephalitis with secondary vasculitis was considered for which acyclovir and dipyridamole was started… Unfortunately, the patient deteriorated, despite the initiated therapy.” 3. Lyme disease was diagnosed on IgG seroconversion and tick-bite history but was ruled out after poor response to ceftriaxone and dexamethasone — “Lyme’s disease was diagnosed… Hence, intravenous therapy with ceftriaxone and dexamethasone was initiated. However after four days, the situation worsened dramatically.”

title: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with uncommon cardiac invasion
case_prompt:
A 26-year-old immunocompetent woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive shortness of breath, facial and upper-limb swelling, and mild lower-limb edema. She denied weight loss, night sweats, or fever. On admission, blood pressure was 85/60 mm Hg, heart rate 116 beats/min, respiratory rate 30 breaths/min, temperature 36.3 °C, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Examination revealed facial swelling, jugular venous distention of 6 cm above the sternal angle, upper-limb swelling, mild lower-limb edema, distant heart sounds, a diastolic murmur best heard at the left lower sternal border, and decreased breath sounds with dullness to percussion at both lung bases.  
Chest radiograph showed bilateral pleural effusions, a widened mediastinum, and hilar congestion. Electrocardiography revealed sinus rhythm, a Q wave in V1, and low‐voltage QRS complexes without electrical alternans. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large right atrial mass obstructing the tricuspid valve and severe pericardial effusion. Pericardiocentesis yielded 1000 mL of serous fluid, with glucose 86 mg/dL, negative acid‐fast bacilli stain, and no malignant cells. Bilateral thoracenteses removed 1 L of serous transudative fluid from each side.  
Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed a rounded right atrial mass measuring 6.3 × 6.4 cm obstructing the tricuspid valve. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed a large soft-tissue filling defect nearly occluding the right atrium and distal superior vena cava, a 7 × 4 cm anterior mediastinal mass, and a heterogeneous 13 × 13 × 7.5 cm pelvic mass likely arising from the right adnexa.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of atrial myxoma — “Initial clinical picture before CT scan was compatible with many differential diagnosis. It includes atrial myxoma as our initial thought…” 2. Consideration of rhabdomyosarcoma — “Initial clinical picture before CT scan was compatible with many differential diagnosis. It includes … rhabdomyosarcoma…” 3. Consideration of atrial thrombus — “Initial clinical picture before CT scan was compatible with many differential diagnosis. It includes … atrial thrombus…” 4. Consideration of lymphoma — “Initial clinical picture before CT scan was compatible with many differential diagnosis. It includes … lymphoma.” 5. Absence of systemic B symptoms argued against malignancy — “Other signs and symptoms suggestive of malignancy such as weight loss, night sweats, and fever could be presented. However, it was absent in this case which guided us to think of a benign tumor rather than malignancy.”

title: Low-Grade Uterine Adenosarcoma with Overexpression of MDM2 and CDK4 by Immunohistochemistry: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old woman presented with postmenopausal bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasonography revealed a mass extending from the uterine body into the cervical canal. Her medical history included appendectomy and cholecystectomy. Laboratory studies showed a cancer antigen 125 level of 113 U/mL (normal 0–35 U/mL), cancer antigen 19–9 of 63.7 U/mL (normal 0–37 U/mL), and lactate dehydrogenase of 277 IU/L (isoenzymes LD1 32%, LD2 33.8%, LD3 20.6%, LD4 7.2%, LD5 6.4%). 

Endometrial biopsy demonstrated spindle-shaped cell proliferation covered by non-atypical epithelium. Immunohistochemical staining of the stromal cells was positive for h-caldesmon, α-smooth muscle actin, STAT6, and MDM2, weakly positive for CDK4, and negative for CD34, CD99, and ALK. 

Pelvic MRI showed an 11 × 5 × 7 cm heterogeneous high-intensity mass within the uterine cavity extending into the cervix without myometrial invasion; it exhibited low signal intensity with areas of slight T1 high intensity, low apparent diffusion coefficient (0.97 × 10^−3 mm^2/s), inhomogeneous post-contrast enhancement, and high diffusion-weighted signal. Whole-body 18F-FDG PET-MRI demonstrated increased FDG uptake in the uterine mass (SUVmax 6.2). 

Differential diagnoses based on the imaging and biopsy findings included uterine sarcoma, carcinosarcoma, and adenosarcoma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor was considered since ALK can be positive in these tumors, but “ALK … was also negative.” 2. Carcinosarcoma and other uterine sarcomas were included in the imaging differential because the mass’s MRI and PET characteristics raised suspicion of malignancy: “Differential diagnoses based on the imaging findings included uterine sarcoma, carcinosarcoma, and adenosarcoma.”

title: Oral Cysticercosis in a Pediatric Patient: A Rare Case Report with Review
journal: International Journal of Clinical Pediatric Dentistry
case_prompt:
A 10-year-old boy was evaluated for a swelling on the dorsal surface of the tongue that had appeared 3 years earlier as a small, localized lesion and gradually increased to 1.5 × 1.5 cm. He reported no pain but had difficulty eating. There was no history of fever or other medical problems. On examination, the lesion was an oval, firm, nonmobile nodule with a smooth, nonulcerated surface.

Differential diagnoses included mucocele; benign mesenchymal tumors (lipoma, fibroma, hemangioma, lymphangioma, granular cell tumor); parasitic cyst; and minor salivary gland adenoma.

Fine-needle aspiration cytology was performed with a 22-gauge needle, yielding 1 ml of clear fluid containing pearly white flakes. Smears stained with May–Grünwald–Giemsa and hematoxylin–eosin showed a mixed inflammatory infiltrate composed of numerous eosinophils, plasma cells, and palisading histiocytes.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lipoma — “The lesion presented in this case as a firm nodule, thus making lipoma ... as less likely diagnosis.” 2. Hemangioma — “The lesion presented in this case as a firm nodule, thus making ... hemangioma as less likely diagnosis.” 3. Lymphangioma — “Although tongue is the most common intraoral site for lymphangioma, it is a less probable diagnosis as they usually are present since birth or appear at a very early age. The histopathologic findings were also not in favor of either hemangioma or lymphangioma.” 4. Granular cell tumor — “The histopathologic picture found in the presented case did not resemble the one described for granular cell tumor. Thus, granular cell tumor was also ruled out as the diagnosis for this case.”

title: Primary syphilis chancre masquerading as a frenulum tear, the essence of histology: a case report
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old Chinese man presented with a superficial penile wound. He reported noticing the lesion a few days earlier immediately after masturbating, when he felt a sudden pain. He also experienced pain at the site during erections. His medical history was notable only for a penicillin allergy; he had no chronic illnesses. He was a man who has sex with men, with 3–5 predominantly protected, casual anal‐sex partners per year. His last HIV and syphilis tests 6 months ago were both nonreactive, and his most recent sexual encounter was 3 months ago.

On examination, there was a 5‐mm shallow, partial‐thickness tear at the ventral midline of the frenulum of the penis. The lesion had regular borders, a yellow base with minimal discharge, and no surrounding erythema. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no other genital lesions.

The initial clinical impression was a frenulum tear from overzealous masturbation. A frenulectomy was offered, and the patient elected to have a circumcision at the same time. Under local anesthesia, a dorsal slit circumcision and removal of the remnant frenulum were performed. The circumferential wound was closed, and the frenulum site was left to heal by secondary intention. Excised tissue, including the foreskin and remnant frenulum, was sent for histologic examination.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Trauma was considered because of the history of overzealous masturbation and the presence of pain — “the lesion reportedly manifested shortly after a traumatic incident of overzealous masturbation and the presence of pain… directed us towards considering the wound more traumatic in nature.”

title: Intermittent spontaneous CSF rhinorrhea exacerbated by hormonal fluctuations associated with menstrual cycles
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old obese woman with chronic migraines, recurrent sinusitis, and allergic rhinitis presented for evaluation of a possible sinus infection. She reported a 2-month history of copious clear rhinorrhea, purulent postnasal drainage, and cough. She also experienced self-limiting episodes of clear nasal discharge at the onset of her menstrual cycles, each preceded by severe menstrual migraines. 

A 2-week course of antibiotics, antihistamines, and steroid nasal spray provided mild relief of her cough, but the clear rhinorrhea—more copious on the left—persisted. A subsequent 4-week trial of ipratropium nasal spray yielded little benefit. Several months prior to the current symptoms, she had developed a chronic severe pounding headache in the frontal and bitemporal regions, accompanied by tinnitus and visual disturbances; these symptoms resolved with the onset of her copious clear rhinorrhea.

Anterior rhinoscopy was unremarkable, and the patient declined nasal endoscopy. Computed tomography of the sinuses revealed an isolated air–fluid level in the left sphenoid sinus without masses, nasal obstruction, or obvious skull base defects. The patient noted that the nasal drainage worsened with lifting, bending over, and upon waking in the morning. This history and imaging prompted evaluation of the nasal fluid for cerebrospinal fluid.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of spontaneous CSF rhinorrhea because of the presentation of clear unilateral nasal discharge exacerbated by posture and menstrual cycle — “Copious clear unilateral rhinorrhea … poor response to antihistamines and ipratropium nasal spray; can be exacerbated by menstrual cycle.” 2. Allergic rhinitis was deemed unlikely given the unilateral nature and lack of response to allergy treatments — “Bilateral symptoms, seasonal variation, good response to steroid nasal spray and antihistamines.” 3. Chronic rhinosinusitis was less likely due to imaging and treatment failure — “Bilateral symptoms, usually responds well to antibiotics and steroids.” 4. Traumatic CSF rhinorrhea was excluded on the basis of no history of head trauma or prior surgery — “Recent head trauma, history of sinus or neuro‐cranial surgery.” 5. Primary nasal or nasopharyngeal tumor was considered improbable given the absence of masses, epistaxis, and typical risk factors on imaging — “Responds poorly to antibiotics, usually elderly, history of Epstein–Barr virus or HPV, smoking, excess alcohol consumption.”

title: Diagnosis and management of temporomandibular joint dysfunction before surgery: a case report
case_prompt: A 68-year-old woman was diagnosed with sigmoid colon cancer and was scheduled for laparoscopic colectomy the next day. On the evening before surgery, she reported left preauricular pain and difficulty opening her mouth. On examination, the maximum interincisal opening was 13 mm. Palpation of the temporomandibular joints revealed no pain or erythema, and there was no joint clicking. Schuller’s radiographic views of the left temporomandibular joint showed that the mandibular condyle did not translate anteriorly in the open position; there was no evidence of bony ankylosis or mandibular fracture. Dental examination revealed tooth mobility and loss from periodontal disease, with deviation of the occlusal plane.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Temporomandibular joint disorder considered given limited translation on imaging — “the mandibular condyle has not moved forward even in the open position.”

title: New onset ANCA-associated vasculitis in an adolescent during an acute COVID-19 infection: a case report
case_prompt:
A 17-year-old previously healthy male presented with a one-week history of fevers to 102 °F, drenching night sweats, nonproductive cough, nasal congestion, and chest tightness. One week before symptom onset, he had exposure to a COVID-19–positive contact, and nasopharyngeal PCR testing at symptom onset returned positive for SARS-CoV-2. Over the next seven days, he continued to have fevers, weight loss of 5 lb, and worsening fatigue. On illness day 7, he developed acute shortness of breath, lightheadedness, two episodes of hemoptysis, and recorded an oxygen saturation of 86% at home. He was taken to a community hospital, where he was febrile to 101.2 °F with otherwise normal respiratory rate, heart rate, lung examination, and an oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Repeat SARS-CoV-2 PCR remained positive.

Chest radiography revealed a 5-cm left upper-lobe mass and a 3-cm right paratracheal mass. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, electrolytes, liver enzymes, and kidney function. Chest CT demonstrated multiple bilateral cavitary lung lesions, the largest measuring 6.5 cm in the left upper lobe. He was transferred on illness day 8 to a tertiary pediatric center.

On arrival, he was afebrile, with 98% oxygen saturation on room air and an unremarkable examination. SARS-CoV-2 PCR remained positive. Initial laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 2.91 × 10^3/μL, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, platelets 369 × 10^3/μL, creatinine 0.74 mg/dL, ALT 35.8 U/L, C-reactive protein 4.52 mg/dL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/hr. Urinalysis was negative for blood, protein, and white cells.

Broad infectious coverage with vancomycin and ceftriaxone was initiated, and over 48 hours, testing for HIV, tuberculosis, Aspergillus, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, and blood cultures were all negative. Echocardiography was normal, and upper and lower extremity Doppler ultrasounds showed no deep venous thrombosis. Evaluation for multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children—including ferritin, D-dimer, fibrinogen, troponin levels, and echocardiography—revealed only mild elevations of ferritin, D-dimer, and fibrinogen but normal troponin, hemodynamics, and down-trending fevers. Concern for MIS-C decreased, and the patient improved clinically. He was discharged home on illness day 11.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious causes (bacterial, mycobacterial, fungal) were considered and excluded — “patient had testing return negative for HIV, tuberculosis, aspergillus, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, and blood cultures remained negative throughout his hospital course…” 2. Embolic phenomena were considered given cavitary lesions but were excluded — “…normal echocardiogram performed and upper/lower extremity venous Doppler ultrasounds were negative for deep venous thrombosis.” 3. Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) was considered but deemed unlikely — “…normal troponin levels, normal echocardiogram, lack of hypotension, and downtrend of fever curve without treatment, concern for MIS-C decreased substantially.”

title: Graft Loss Following Onset of Schizophrenia Long After Liver Transplantation
case_prompt:
A 41-year-old Japanese woman with a history of Wilson disease and orthotopic liver transplantation at age 21 presented in January 2018 with several months of insomnia followed by persecutory delusions (“I am being watched”) and auditory hallucinations (“I hear a voice telling me what to do”). She exhibited bizarre behaviors, such as attempting to strip her parents because she believed they were infected. In early March 2018, she was involuntarily hospitalized for psychomotor agitation and psychotic symptoms.

On examination, she had jaundice and bilateral lower-limb edema. Laboratory tests showed WBC 3650/µL, hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, hematocrit 26.1%, platelets 76 000/µL, prothrombin time 19.7 seconds, albumin 2.7 g/dL, total bilirubin 10.0 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 2.6 mg/dL, AST 56 U/L, ALT 54 U/L, ALP 1193 U/L, cholinesterase 85 U/L, BUN 20.8 mg/dL, CRP 0.90 mg/dL, serum copper 47 µg/dL (normal 68–128), ceruloplasmin 13 mg/dL (normal 21–37), tacrolimus level <2.0 ng/mL, and mycophenolate mofetil level <0.4 µg/mL. Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly.

Acute graft rejection due to nonadherence with tacrolimus and mycophenolate mofetil was diagnosed, and she received two days of methylprednisolone. A psychiatric liaison team administered risperidone up to 4 mg/day orally and intravenous haloperidol for agitation.

Brain MRI showed only a small high-intensity focus in the corpus callosum; there was no evidence of pathological copper deposition. There was no abnormality in copper metabolism, and tacrolimus trough levels had not increased prior to symptom onset and remained low thereafter. No other medical condition or substance use disorder was identified.

Therefore, schizophrenia was suspected, although the duration of psychotic symptoms had not yet met DSM-5 criteria.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Wilson disease recurrence — “no abnormalities, including pathological copper accumulation … no evidence of abnormal copper metabolism … psychosis due to the recurrence of Wilson disease … was ruled out.” 2. Tacrolimus-induced psychosis — “Tacrolimus-induced psychosis was also ruled out because the trough tacrolimus concentration had not increased before onset of the psychosis, and the psychosis persisted after the trough value decreased.” 3. Substance-related or other medical causes — “No evidence of other medical conditions or a substance use disorder that could cause psychotic symptoms was found.”

title: Clinical, imaging, and biofluid correlates of Lyme polyradiculitis in a case report of neuroborreliosis
case_prompt:
A man in his 60s with hyperlipidaemia and rosacea presented in July with 1 month of progressive bilateral leg weakness, paresthesias, lumbar back pain, constipation, and urinary retention. He had been active in golf and pickleball but now had difficulty standing without falling. One month earlier, he had paroxysms of sharp, electric shock–like pain radiating from the back to his legs, worse at night. He denied recent travel or recognized tick bites; he noted small, self-resolving, raised erythematous rashes on his leg and abdomen several weeks before.

Outpatient evaluation 4 weeks after symptom onset showed elevated AST (69 U/L) and ALT (101 U/L), CRP 54 mg/L, and EBV IgM+/IgG+; viral hepatitis panel was negative. Two weeks later, CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed distension of the bladder and colon without obstruction; labs normalized except for persistent bladder retention.

At admission one week later, he had new-onset paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (converted to sinus rhythm), AST 41 U/L, ALT 78 U/L, CRP 10 mg/L, normal troponins, and BNP 454 pg/mL. Neurologic examination showed:
  – Strength: hip flexors/extensors 2/5, knee flexors/extensors 4/5, ankle and foot muscles 5/5  
  – Sensation: decreased in medial/lateral thighs to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception; intact in lower legs/feet  
  – Reflexes: patellar 1+ bilaterally, left ankle 1+, right ankle 0  
  – Positive Romberg sign; gait unsteady and broad  
  – Cranial nerves and upper limbs normal; no meningeal signs

Contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine showed enhancement of bilateral cauda equina nerve roots from L1 to L5 without cord or vertebral abnormalities. Brain MRI with contrast suggested bilateral cranial nerve VII enhancement. Lumbar puncture revealed normal glucose (67 mg/dL), elevated protein (149 mg/dL), and pleocytosis (114 cells/mm3) with lymphocytic predominance.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Neuroborreliosis was considered given the combination of lumbosacral polyradiculitis and cranial nerve involvement — “Differential diagnosis included polyradiculitis due to neuroborreliosis …” 2. Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy (AIDP) was considered because of nerve root enhancement and lower motor neuron signs but was less likely given the pleocytosis — “AIDP shows CSF albuminocytologic dissociation … while Lyme polyradiculitis has dually elevated CSF protein and white blood cells” 3. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) was considered less likely based on the acute time course — “… or less likely to be chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) …”

title: Corticosteroid-Induced Liver Injury in Adult-Onset Still’s Disease
case_prompt:
A 29-year-old Taiwanese woman with a family history of Sjögren’s syndrome presented with two weeks of daily fevers, arthralgias, sore throat, and a macular rash. Initial evaluation at a district hospital included negative viral tests and bacterial cultures. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis (13 060/μL with 85.5% neutrophils) and marked hyperferritinemia (12 585 ng/mL). Antinuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor were negative. Gallium-67 inflammation scanning and abdominal CT demonstrated multiple enlarged lymph nodes; an excisional lymph node biopsy excluded lymphoma. She met Yamaguchi criteria for adult-onset Still’s disease. 

Because of a history of NSAID allergy, on hospital day 8 she received intravenous betamethasone (8 mg/day), which was transitioned to oral prednisolone (20 mg/day) two days later. Her fevers and arthralgias resolved. On day 10, ALT was 150 U/L and AST was 119 U/L; by day 15, levels peaked at ALT 1473 U/L and AST 722 U/L. Under suspicion of drug-induced liver injury, all medications were stopped. Viral and autoimmune hepatitis were excluded by serological tests, and liver enzymes declined. Corticosteroids were resumed on day 17, and she was discharged on prednisolone 20 mg/day. 

Five days after discharge, she was readmitted with acute severe hepatitis (ALT 1652 U/L; AST 766 U/L). Prednisolone was halted immediately while silymarin and lactulose were maintained.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. AOSD-related liver injury was unlikely because “liver dysfunction was not observed until the administration of corticosteroids and spontaneously resolved after the removal of the medication.” 2. Macrophage activation syndrome was excluded since “young AOSD patients with cytopenia were more likely to suffer from acute severe hepatitis related to reactive MAS with high mortality,” and this patient had leukocytosis rather than cytopenias. 3. Viral and autoimmune hepatitis were ruled out as “viral and autoimmune hepatitis were both excluded again by serological tests.” 4. Drug-induced liver injury from corticosteroids was confirmed when “corticosteroid-induced liver injury was confirmed based on the clinical findings and the positive prednisolone re-challenge.”

title: Congenital Benign Intrahepatic Cyst with Cystobiliary Communication: An Intraoperative Diagnostic Enigma
journal: Journal of Indian Association of Pediatric Surgeons
case_prompt: A 15-month-old boy was brought for evaluation of a right upper quadrant abdominal mass. The mass had been first noted at age 3 months and had rapidly increased in size over the preceding month, during which he also had decreased feeding and intermittent vomiting. On examination, there was a nontender, firm, smooth, rounded mass occupying the right hypochondrium, epigastrium, and right iliac fossa. Laboratory studies showed elevated liver transaminases (AST 319 U/L, ALT 204 U/L) and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (687 U/L); bilirubin, synthetic liver function tests, and α-fetoprotein were normal. A triple-phase abdominal CT scan showed a 15×10×11-cm hypodense cystic lesion in liver segments 4, 5, and 8, with irregular margins in the cranial segments, a few peripheral septations with poor enhancement, and no calcifications. The left portal vein and left hepatic duct were compressed, with resultant atrophy of the left lateral segment and left intrahepatic biliary dilation. T2-weighted MRI confirmed a uniformly hyperintense solitary cyst in the same central segments. The child was explored with preoperative diagnosis of a mesenchymal hamartoma.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Mesenchymal hamartoma was considered based on the imaging appearance in a young child — “The child was explored with preoperative diagnosis of a mesenchymal hamartoma.”

title: Sylvian fissure lipoma associated with fusiform aneurysm in the middle cerebral artery trifurcation: A case report and literature review
case_prompt:
A 42-year-old woman was referred to the emergency department after an out-of-hospital brain MRI was interpreted as suspicious for a right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm and late subacute subarachnoid hemorrhage in the right Sylvian fissure. She reported chronic, intermittent headaches responsive to analgesics and short-term memory problems of unclear duration. On examination, she was in good general condition with only moderate headache; the remainder of her medical and family histories was unremarkable.

Review of her MRI revealed a lesion in the right Sylvian fissure with a dilated right MCA passing through it. The lesion was non-homogeneously hyperintense on T1-weighted spin echo, T2-weighted fast spin echo, and FLAIR sequences; susceptibility-weighted imaging showed hypointensity with blooming artifacts, and diffusion-weighted imaging showed no diffusion restriction.

Emergency head CT angiography demonstrated an unruptured fusiform aneurysm of the right MCA at the M1–M2 junction with trifurcation of the M2 branches. A well-defined, non-enhancing, extremely hypodense lesion measuring 10 × 13 × 13 mm (−90 to −100 HU) was noted in the right Sylvian fissure, encasing the distal M1 and proximal M2 segments; there was no evidence of blood or calcification.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Late subacute hemorrhage was argued against when diffusion-weighted imaging showed no restriction — “DWI shows strong diffusion restriction in late subacute hemorrhage and loss of signal in lipoma.”

title: Extensive Macular Atrophy with Pseudodrusen in a Japanese Patient Evaluated by Wide-Field OCTA
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old Japanese man was referred for progressive vision loss in his right eye. He noted night blindness in his 50s, particularly difficulty driving through tunnels, without glare or photophobia. At age 69, he underwent bilateral cataract extraction with only transient visual improvement. His only other medical history was excision of a fingertip squamous cell carcinoma at age 54. He had no family history of eye disease and nonconsanguineous parents.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 0.08 in the right eye and 0.8 in the left. Anterior segments and intraocular lenses were unremarkable; intraocular pressures were 14 mm Hg (right) and 13 mm Hg (left). Fundoscopy and fundus autofluorescence revealed well-demarcated, symmetrical macular atrophy with a greater vertical than horizontal diameter, extending to and beyond the vascular arcades. Yellowish-white, net-like pseudodrusen were widespread throughout both retinas. Ultra-wide-field imaging confirmed extensive pseudodrusen beyond the arcades but showed no mid-peripheral hypoautofluorescence or peripheral paving-stone degeneration.

Swept-source OCT demonstrated extensive outer nuclear layer atrophy, loss of ellipsoid and cone outer segment termination lines replaced by subretinal hyperreflective material, and marked choroidal thinning. Fluorescein angiography showed window defects without leakage or staining. Indocyanine green angiography delineated choroidal vessels in the atrophic areas without evidence of neovascularization. Wide-field OCT angiography revealed preserved superficial and deep capillary plexuses and foveal avascular zone, with attenuation of choriocapillaris and deeper choroidal vessels in the atrophic regions. Kinetic and static perimetry showed paracentral scotomas with unreliable fixation. Electroretinography demonstrated an electronegative bright-flash waveform, normal rod response, cone response, and 30 Hz flicker, and preserved on and off responses on long-flash testing. Equatorial OCT identified pseudodrusen beneath the retinal pigment epithelium protruding into the outer nuclear layer, consistent with reticular pseudodrusen.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of diffuse trickling geographic atrophy (a severe form of dry AMD) — “In contrast to EMAP, patients with diffuse trickling geographic atrophy frequently have severe vascular or cardiac disorders; however, our patient had no preexisting medical conditions.” 2. Consideration of retinitis pigmentosa — “No obvious degeneration or low autofluorescence was observed in the mid-periphery, which is the area most likely to be affected first in retinitis pigmentosa.” 3. Consideration of choroidal neovascularization — “IA showed clearly delineated choroidal vessels in the degenerated area but no sign of CNV,” and “no suspicious signals for CNV were detected in the avascular slab.”

title: Intramural cyst originating from Luschka’s duct in the gall bladder: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A woman in her seventies with a history of appendectomy, hypothyroidism, and cataracts underwent endoscopic papillary balloon dilatation and later sphincterotomy for recurring choledocholithiasis. She presented for surgical management of recurrent gallbladder stones. On examination, she had no jaundice or anemia; her abdomen was soft and nontender. Laboratory tests showed no inflammation, normal hepatobiliary enzymes, and normal levels of carcinoembryonic antigen and CA 19-9. 
Abdominal CT demonstrated a low-attenuation area within the gallbladder wall, suggesting a cyst, with attenuation values lower than the gallbladder lumen. DIC-CT revealed no contrast filling of the cystic area, and ultrasound showed bile sludge but no wall thickening apart from the cyst and a 15 mm gallstone. 
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy was performed without difficulty, and the gallbladder appeared to have a normally reticulated mucosal surface. On the serosal (liver) side of the gallbladder fundus, a slightly elevated, whitish submucosal lesion was noted. On gross sectioning, multiple small cysts (3–5 mm) were visible within the gallbladder wall at that site, containing clear serous fluid without bile. 
Histologically, the cysts were located in the subserosal layer, each lined by a monolayer of cuboidal to low columnar cells without atypia, with small round nuclei and clear or weakly eosinophilic cytoplasm. No Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses or mucosal invaginations were seen around the cysts. The cysts were surrounded by dense fibrous tissue lacking elements of the usual gallbladder fibromuscular layer and without a muscularis propria. Scattered small tubular structures lined by low columnar epithelium were present around the cysts.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. RAS-derived cyst excluded — “no RAS lesions were observed around the cysts, which were located only in the subserosal layer” 2. Luschka duct cyst considered — “According to Cureton and Newcombe, the cyst derived from Luschka’s duct can be identified based on four features…The cysts in the present case…were located in the subserosal connective tissue on the hepatic side…The small ducts were scattered around the cysts”

title: Imperforate anus with a rectovestibular fistula and pseudotail: a case report
case_prompt:
A term (39+1 weeks) 3580 g Caucasian girl was transferred on day 1 of life for imperforate anus and a perineal mass. The pregnancy and prenatal ultrasound at 21 weeks were unremarkable. Apgar scores were 9 and 9. Physical examination revealed a soft, skin-covered, midline mass (3 × 1 cm) arising from the perineum posterior to the vagina, a sacral dimple, normal female genitalia, and no anal opening. Neurologic and the remainder of the examination were normal. The infant did not pass meconium on day 1. She was started on ampicillin and gentamicin. 

On day 2, mild abdominal distention developed and flecks of meconium appeared in the diaper. Inspection revealed a small opening in the posterior vaginal vestibule; on probing, meconium passed through a vestibular fistula, partially decompressing the abdomen. 

Anteroposterior and lateral radiographs of the sacrum and coccyx showed normal bony structures without soft-tissue calcifications. A prone cross-table lateral pelvic radiograph demonstrated a column of air in the rectum and sigmoid colon terminating just below the caudal sacrum. Echocardiogram and ultrasound of the spine and retroperitoneum showed no cardiac, vertebral, or renal anomalies; the spinal cord terminated normally at L2 with normal cauda equina motion and no tethered cord. 

Ultrasound of the perineal mass measured 2.5 × 3.6 × 1.3 cm, with internal vascular flow and possible presacral extension, suggesting a presacral teratoma. Serum alpha-fetoprotein was appropriate for age. MRI revealed a pedunculated, predominantly fat-intensity mass with a single central vessel, no presacral component, and a normal sacrum; the rectum and vagina converged just above the perineum. 

A contrast study through the vestibular fistula demonstrated a long, narrow fistulous tract to a distended colorectal pouch located approximately 3 to 3.5 cm from the skin.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Presacral teratoma was initially considered because of vascular flow and possible extension on ultrasound — “suggestive of a presacral teratoma, which warranted more urgent resection.” 2. A malignant teratoma became less likely given normal neonatal serum AFP — “serum alpha-fetoprotein levels were not inappropriately elevated for a newborn.” 3. MRI excluded a presacral mass by showing no presacral component — “The structure did not have a presacral component.” 4. Chordoma was unlikely due to the absence of soft-tissue calcifications on radiographs — “normal bony structures without adjacent soft tissue calcifications.”

title: Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor Mimicking Acute Septic Arthritis of the Hip: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 12-year-old boy presented with a 5-day history of atraumatic, progressively worsening right hip pain. He and his parents recalled a possible low-energy fall around the onset of pain, but could not definitively link the two. He was initially evaluated at an outside emergency department, where his erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, white blood cell count, and hip radiographs were normal, and he was diagnosed with transient synovitis. The following night he awoke with severe hip pain and was unable to bear weight. He returned to the outside emergency department, was given additional nonsteroidal antiinflammatories, and was referred to an orthopedic surgeon due to concern for septic arthritis.

On arrival at our pediatric emergency department, he reported severe right hip pain that prevented weight bearing and woke him from sleep. He was afebrile. Examination revealed that he held the hip in flexion, abduction, and external rotation. Within a moderate arc of motion he had minimal discomfort, but internal rotation, especially with the hip flexed, reproduced significant pain. He was tender over the medial groin and adductor tendons. His temperature was 37.0 °C. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 6050/mm3, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 6.6 mg/dL. Radiographs of the hip showed evidence of an effusion, confirmed by ultrasonography. An ultrasound-guided arthrocentesis yielded grossly bloody joint fluid with a white-cell count of 54 999/mm3, a red-cell count of 7000/mm3, and a negative Gram stain. Given equivocal findings, an MRI of the right hip with and without contrast was obtained. MRI demonstrated a 2.5 × 2.1 × 1.4 cm heterogeneous intra-articular mass in the anterior-inferior joint space that was hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted images, with enhancement, a moderate joint effusion, and synovitis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Transient synovitis was initially diagnosed but then reconsidered when the patient developed severe, progressive pain and inflammatory markers rose — “He was subsequently diagnosed with transient synovitis.” 2. Hemorrhagic synovial fluid suggested a nonseptic etiology, consistent with neoplastic synovitis — “arthrocentesis from previously reported cases of TGCT typically yielded bloody fluid ... most consistent with hemorrhagic synovial fluid.” 3. MRI signal characteristics raised suspicion for tenosynovial giant cell tumor rather than other mass lesions — “Imaging demonstrated a heterogeneous intra-articular mass ... suggestive of localized/nodular TGCT.” 4. Synovial sarcoma and other malignancies had to be excluded before definitive therapy — “recognizing the potential implications should a sarcoma diagnosis render.” 5. Other rheumatologic or orthopedic conditions are often misdiagnosed in pediatric TGCT due to equivocal presentation — “most of the cases were misdiagnosed as various other rheumatologic or orthopaedic conditions ... failure to consider TGCT.”

title: Acquired hemophilia in a 7‐year‐old girl successfully treated with recombinant FVIIA and steroids: A case report
case_prompt:
A previously healthy 7-year-old girl with no personal or family history of bleeding presented to the pediatric emergency department with recurrent self-limiting epistaxis. Examination by otolaryngology showed a bleeding varicose vessel on the nasal septum, and oral tranexamic acid was prescribed. One week later, she was readmitted with frequent episodes of epistaxis and new-onset fatigue. Blood tests showed microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL; MCV 59.4 fl), a normal platelet count (320 × 10^9/L), and an isolated prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) of 116 seconds. Iron supplementation was started, and she was discharged.

A few days later, she was admitted to the pediatric hematology–oncology service. Hemoglobin level had increased with reticulocytosis. The aPTT of 116 seconds persisted and did not correct on a 1:1 mixing study after two hours of incubation at 37 °C. Assay of clotting factors revealed a factor VIII activity of 0.7%, with normal levels of other clotting factors and a normal lupus anticoagulant screen. Tests for immunoglobulins, autoantibodies, antimycoplasma antibodies, and thyroid function were all negative. Antistreptolysin O titer was elevated at 1002 U/mL, with a negative throat swab for group A Streptococcus.

One week later, she returned with an acute, painful swelling of the left thigh, associated with severe anemia (hemoglobin 5 g/dL) and tachycardia requiring red-cell transfusion. On examination there was a large hematoma extending from the left hip to the knee, causing difficulty with ambulation. Repeat coagulation studies showed an aPTT of 117 seconds, factor VIII activity of 0%, and a factor VIII inhibitor titer of 8.9 Bethesda units.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Confirmation of an inhibitor-mediated process — “Subsequent finding of extremely low or absent factor VIII levels together with the presence of its inhibitor remains necessary in order to confirm the diagnosis.” 2. Diagnosis of acquired hemophilia A — “Clinical, anamnestic, and laboratory diagnostic criteria confirmed acquired hemophilia.”

title: Neuronal intranuclear inclusion disease misdiagnosed as Parkinson’s disease: a case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_prompt:
A 68-year-old woman was admitted with a 2-week history of limb weakness and progressive difficulty walking. She had no relevant medical, family, or psychosocial history. On examination, temperature was 36.2 °C, heart rate 65 beats/min, and blood pressure 132/82 mmHg. She had a paucity of facial expression, normal cranial nerves, and muscle strength of 4/5 in all limbs. She exhibited an asymmetric resting tremor and rigidity in her limbs, diminished bilateral knee reflexes, and a positive right Babinski sign. Brain diffusion-weighted MRI showed obvious high-intensity signals around the bilateral lateral ventricles and corpus callosum.

Review of her records revealed that at age 62 she developed a resting tremor in her right hand, which extended to the left hand at 63, accompanied by bradykinesia, increased muscle tone, urinary incontinence, poor sleep quality with occasional shouting, and an MDS-UPDRS score of 88. She was diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease and treated with levodopa and pramipexole, improving to 73 on the MDS-UPDRS. At age 65, selegiline was added without benefit (MDS-UPDRS 85), and she subsequently developed memory loss, visual hallucinations, and an MMSE score of 8/30.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Parkinson’s disease was considered because her extrapyramidal symptoms and positive response to levodopa supported the diagnosis — “All of the aforementioned features supported a diagnosis of PD.” 2. Parkinson’s disease became less likely when she experienced cognitive impairment, which is rarely observed in PD — “she experienced cognitive impairment, which is rarely observed in PD.”

title: Wernicke encephalopathy in a patient after liver transplantation: A case report
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old man underwent orthotopic liver transplantation for decompensated cirrhosis secondary to chronic hepatitis B. Three days before transplantation through postoperative day 22, he received total parenteral nutrition without a specific thiamine supplement. His immediate postoperative course was unremarkable, and immunosuppression included tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone.

On postoperative day 15, he became irritable, raving, and lethargic, and he developed progressive dysarthria, difficulty grasping objects, and memory loss. There was no nystagmus, diplopia, paresthesia, tremor, or incontinence. He had no recent alcohol use or psychiatric history.

His weight was 56.0 kg (preoperative 54.8 kg), height 172 cm, BMI 18.9. Temperature 36.8 °C, heart rate 82/min, respiratory rate 16/min, blood pressure 108/68 mmHg. Pupils were equal and reactive.

Laboratory studies: WBC 8.83 × 10^9/L (87.8% neutrophils), RBC 2.63 × 10^12/L, hemoglobin 81 g/L, platelets 83 × 10^9/L. ALT 13.3 U/L, AST 23.3 U/L, albumin 39.8 g/L, total bilirubin 17.8 µmol/L. Renal function, electrolytes, coagulation, blood ammonia, and arterial blood gas were normal. Random glucose ranged from 4.9 to 8.4 mmol/L; tacrolimus trough was 8.1 ng/mL.

Brain MRI showed symmetrical high T1- and T2-weighted signal intensities in the thalami and pons, and high T2 FLAIR signal in the paraventricular regions.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Wernicke encephalopathy was considered based on characteristic MRI lesions and risk factors — “The brain magnetic resonance imaging ... suggested WE, even though the plasma level of thiamine was not tested…” 2. Hepatic encephalopathy was included in the differential because of his history of liver failure — “Besides, the differential diagnoses of WE after liver transplantation includes ... hepatic encephalopathy ...” 3. Adverse effects of anti-rejection drugs were considered as a cause of neurologic changes — “Besides, the differential diagnoses of WE after liver transplantation includes ... adverse effects of anti-rejection drugs ...” 4. Cerebral vascular accident was considered given acute focal changes but imaging did not demonstrate infarction — “Besides, the differential diagnoses of WE after liver transplantation includes ... cerebral vascular accident ...”

title: Late Presentation of McArdle's Disease Mimicking Polymyalgia Rheumatica: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old man presented with severe acute‐onset myalgia affecting his hips and shoulders. Three months earlier, he had been diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. He described proximal myalgia and stiffness in the upper limbs more than the lower limbs; the pain was severe enough to disrupt sleep and had no diurnal variation. His primary‐care physician had prescribed morphine and, subsequently, a 5-day course of prednisolone 30 mg daily, which produced significant symptomatic improvement. 

On examination, he had nonspecific myalgia and arthralgia but no neurological deficit or other systemic findings. His medical history included fatty liver disease and type 2 diabetes; he was not taking metformin or atorvastatin. He was a retired executive, a nonsmoker, and drank little alcohol; there was no relevant family history.

Laboratory studies showed normal or negative results for complete blood count, PSA, immunoglobulins, renal function, vitamin D, and bone profile. He had mild chronic elevations of alkaline phosphatase and γ-glutamyl transferase. Inflammatory markers were elevated (CRP 103 mg/L [< 5], ESR 45 mm/hr), and creatine kinase was 811 IU/L (30–200). Immunological tests (ANA, ENA) and viral serologies (hepatitis B and C, HIV, dsDNA), as well as an expanded myositis autoantibody panel, were negative. MRI of the spine was normal; upper‐limb muscle MRI showed nonspecific atrophy without edema.

A working diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica was made, and a tapering prednisolone regimen (initial dose 20 mg) was started. An initial EMG was limited by pain but suggested a possible symmetrical myopathic disorder. A muscle biopsy was offered but deferred when his symptoms resolved on corticosteroids.

Fourteen months later, off steroids, he reported episodic brief myalgias, considered baseline. After strenuous activity, his CK rose to 3473 IU/L while CRP was < 5 mg/L. A repeat EMG demonstrated an active symmetrical myopathic process with short‐duration motor-unit potentials and fibrillation potentials in the right deltoid, with a proximal-to-distal gradient of severity. A muscle biopsy was then performed to further evaluate the myopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Polymyalgia rheumatica — “The main differential was PMR due to the age of onset as well as a response to steroids. However, his ongoing symptom after the steroid taper and a persisting CK elevation with a normal CRP led to other diagnostic considerations.” 2. Inflammatory myopathy (eg, polymyositis) — “Immunological tests including ANA, ENA, and extended-spectrum myositis autoantibody screen were negative.” 3. Mitochondrial myopathies — “Other mitochondrial myopathies such as Leigh syndrome and oxidation defects lead to symptoms that appear after prolonged exercise,” unlike this patient’s immediate onset of myalgia.

title: A case of giant cell tumor of the breast, clinically suspected as malignant breast tumor
case_prompt:
A 74-year-old Japanese woman noted a tender lump in her right breast and was referred after a core needle biopsy raised suspicion for metaplastic carcinoma. She had no family history of breast or ovarian cancer. On examination, there was a hard, tender 25-mm mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast and a palpable right axillary lymph node. Mammography demonstrated an indistinct mass on mediolateral oblique and craniocaudal views. Ultrasound showed an 18 × 16 mm irregular hypoechoic lesion with suspected ductal extension toward the nipple and multiple enlarged level I–II axillary lymph nodes. Contrast-enhanced MRI identified a 17 × 17 mm indistinct mass with surrounding non-mass segmental enhancement extending 74 mm toward the nipple without chest wall or skin invasion. Clinical and imaging findings strongly suggested invasive carcinoma with axillary metastases.

Core needle biopsy of the breast lesion revealed oval and spindle-shaped mononuclear cells admixed with multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells, stromal hyalinization, lymphocytic infiltration, and focal hemorrhage without necrosis. The multinucleated giant cells stained strongly positive for CD68; mononuclear cells were weakly CD68-positive and negative for estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and HER2. Few non-epithelial atypical cells were seen in a breast duct, but no atypical epithelial cells were identified. Fine-needle aspiration of an axillary lymph node yielded only normal lymphocytes. To address the discrepancy between the clinical suspicion of malignancy and the benign-appearing biopsy, a vacuum-assisted ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed again, yielding identical histologic and immunohistochemical findings.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Breast carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells was considered but excluded — “Few non-epithelial atypical cells were observed in the breast duct, but no atypical epithelial cells consistent with breast cancer were detected.”
final_diagnosis: Giant cell tumor of the breast

title: Phenotypic homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia successfully treated with proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 inhibitors
case_prompt:
A 29‐year‐old woman was referred for evaluation of recurrent cutaneous xanthomas. She had developed xanthomas before the age of 10 years, which were resected multiple times during her teenage years. On re-presentation she had nodular xanthomas over the elbows, palpebral xanthomas, and thickened Achilles tendons, but no palmar xanthomas or corneal rings. She smokes 10 cigarettes per day and drinks minimal alcohol. Her mother died suddenly at age 60 (possible premature coronary artery disease), and her sister had childhood xanthomas and persistent hypercholesterolemia (LDL‐C ~140 mg/dL) despite statin therapy. 

On examination: temperature 36.0°C, blood pressure 108/68 mm Hg, pulse 60/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Laboratory studies showed: total cholesterol 429 mg/dL, LDL‐C 357 mg/dL, HDL‐C 45 mg/dL, triglycerides 136 mg/dL, lipoprotein(a) 20.2 mg/dL, sitosterol 3.9 μg/mL, lysosomal acid lipase activity 18.1 μM/h/disk (normal ≥14.9), thyroid‐stimulating hormone 1.53 μIU/mL, free T3 2.1 pg/mL, free T4 0.99 ng/dL, total bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 30 U/L, HbA1c 5.7%, albumin 4.1 g/dL. Urinalysis was normal. 

Radiography of the Achilles tendons showed thickness of 15 mm. Coronary computed tomography angiography revealed no coronary artery disease, and there was no evidence of aortic stenosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sitosterolemia was excluded because “the patient exhibited normal sitosterol … levels.” 2. Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency was excluded because “the patient exhibited … normal … lysosomal acid lipase activities.”
