title: Dermatitis herpetiformis presenting as pseudovasculitis
case_prompt:
A 47-year-old man with a 7-year history of dermatitis herpetiformis presented with a 1-month history of a generalized petechial rash and tender swelling of the left arm, which worsened over the past week after he discontinued dapsone and relaxed a gluten-free diet. He reported his typical pruritic papulovesicular rash on the scalp and arms, but noted new flat, bright red lesions causing severe burning pain. His long-term medications included omeprazole and lisinopril-hydrochlorothiazide.

On examination, erythematous papulovesicles were seen on the scalp, upper arms, and elbows. Well-demarcated erythematous macules coalesced into larger patches on the upper back, buttocks, and trunk. Purpuric macules and patches were present at the periphery of erythematous patches on the anterior shins, knees, and thighs, with scattered petechiae on the distal fingertips. A large tender ulcer with purulent discharge and yellow crusting was noted on the left extensor forearm.

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 28.1 K/µL. A culture of the left forearm wound grew methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. A skin biopsy from the left anterior thigh demonstrated a subepidermal blister with aggregates of neutrophils within papillary dermal tips and pronounced dermal hemorrhage, but no evidence of leukocytoclastic vasculitis. Direct immunofluorescence revealed granular IgA deposition in the papillary dermal tips.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Active cutaneous vasculitis considered — “the histopathologic diagnostic criteria for active cutaneous vasculitis include angiocentric or angioinvasive inflammatory infiltrates, fibrinoid necrosis, and infiltration of vessel wall by an inflammatory infiltrate... Secondary changes such as red blood cell extravasation... can be suggestive, but not diagnostic, for active vasculitis” and absence of “leukocytoclastic vasculitis” on biopsy. 2. Erythema elevatum diutinum considered — “the morphology and the distribution of our patient's cutaneous findings may be similar to that of EED” but excluded because “lesions in EED tend to be asymptomatic” and DIF findings argue against EED, as “no such evidence of leukocytoclasis was found… other than a nonspecific dermal hemorrhage,” and DIF showed granular rather than linear IgA deposits. 3. Pseudovasculitis recognized — “these secondary changes in the absence of histopathologic evidence of vasculitis should direct one to include pseudovasculitis in the differential diagnosis.”

title: Extensive abdominal wall ulceration as a late manifestation of antiphospholipid syndrome: a case report
case_prompt:
A 58-year-old woman with a 27-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus and secondary antiphospholipid syndrome presented with painful, non-healing ulcers. Her initial lupus manifestations included recurrent pneumonitis, malar rash, and biopsy-proven lupus nephritis. She had recurrent deep venous thromboses at diagnosis, was positive for lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 3 months apart, and was anticoagulated with warfarin targeting an INR of 2.0–3.0. Additional thrombophilia testing (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, protein C/S, antithrombin III) was negative.

Her comorbidities included obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, hypertension, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, stage 3B chronic kidney disease, and previous endometrial cancer in remission. Medications were warfarin, hydroxychloroquine, atenolol, and analgesics.

Two months before admission, she noted small bruises at sites of abdominal ultrasound probe pressure, which progressed to ulcerative lesions on the anterior abdominal wall. Wound swab grew multidrug-resistant Enterobacter cloacae, and she was treated with ciprofloxacin and wound dressings. Her INR was 2.5 on admission, and over the prior 8 months had ranged between 2.3 and 3.6. She was afebrile and hemodynamically stable and had five ulcers on the anterior abdominal wall; the largest had irregular margins, surrounding erythema, and a clean base.

She was discharged on antibiotics and warfarin. Eleven days later, she returned with worsening pain and non-healing ulcers. Examination showed progression of the largest abdominal ulcer (11 × 7 cm) with black eschar and yellow exudate, plus new ulcers on the left breast and interscapular area. Wound cultures grew methicillin-resistant Staphylococci; blood cultures were sterile. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis, rising CRP, stable renal function, and mild worsening of anemia. Serologies were positive for lupus anticoagulant but negative for cardiolipin IgG, anti–β2-glycoprotein I, cryoglobulins, ANA, ENA, and ANCA; complement levels were mildly elevated. 

A skin biopsy from an abdominal ulcer showed ischemia and necrosis of subcutaneous fat, granulation tissue in the dermis, and thrombo-occlusive material but no evidence of vasculitis or calciphylaxis. A second biopsy revealed numerous microthrombi in small subcutaneous vessels and a recanalizing thrombus in a medium-sized vein, with no vasculitis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Warfarin-induced skin necrosis was considered but deemed unlikely because warfarin skin necrosis “usually occurs in the setting of protein C deficiency and usually develops within the first few days of starting warfarin,” whereas this patient had been anticoagulated for years. 2. Calciphylaxis was excluded since it “usually [occurs] in the setting of end-stage kidney disease and is usually not seen with moderate chronic kidney disease, as in the present case,” and biopsy lacked calcium deposits. 3. Vasculitis (including pyoderma gangrenosum) was unlikely because biopsy showed “no evidence of vasculitis” and “the absence of perivascular infiltrate excludes true vasculitis.” 4. Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome was ruled out as she had “no evidence of multiorgan failure with a rapid clinical deterioration.” 6. Persistent microvascular thrombosis from antiphospholipid syndrome remained: biopsies showed “numerous microthrombi in small vessels” consistent with APS-related cutaneous microthrombosis.

title: Parasellar meningioma presenting by stroke and bilateral occlusion of the internal carotid arteries: A case report
case_prompt:
A 30-year-old right-handed man presented with acute weakness of the right upper limb that progressed over 2 days to involve the right lower limb. He denied headache or seizures. On examination, he had right upper-motor-neuron facial paresis, right hemiparesis (MRC grade 2/5 in the hand, 3/5 in shoulder, toes, ankle; 4/5 in knee and hip), right hemihypoalgesia, diminished right tendon reflexes, and a right Babinski sign. Other cranial nerves and the fundus were normal. His NIH Stroke Scale score was 12, and his modified Rankin Scale grade was 4. 

Noncontrast CT of the brain showed a left fronto-temporo-parietal hypodense area consistent with an MCA-territory infarction. MRI of the brain demonstrated a 4.1 × 4.0 × 3.5 cm solid right parasellar lesion extending into the right sphenoid sinus and greater wing of the sphenoid, expanding the cavernous sinus, and attenuating the right internal carotid artery. A smaller left parasellar extension was noted. There was no suprasellar extension; the pituitary stalk and optic chiasm were normal. The lesion enhanced uniformly and strongly after gadolinium administration.

Magnetic resonance angiography revealed absence of both internal carotid arteries, with the anterior and middle cerebral arteries supplied via enlarged posterior communicating arteries; vertebral and basilar arteries were normal. Digital subtraction angiography confirmed occlusion of both ICAs at their origins, faint left terminal ICA flow via ophthalmic collaterals, and a parasellar blush from the right internal maxillary artery; external carotid, common carotid, and subclavian arteries were normal. 

Thyroid, reproductive, growth, cortisol, and prolactin hormone levels were within normal limits. Automated perimetry showed mild generalized depression of retinal sensitivity bilaterally with significant visual field defects. Transesophageal echocardiography and the coagulation profile were unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Pituitary adenoma was considered but excluded because “Meningioma differs from pituitary adenoma by its differential uniform enhancing pattern and normal hormonal profile.” 2. Other parasellar tumors were considered but deemed less likely because meningiomas have the unique ability “to encase the ICA, narrow its lumen, and compromise the cerebral blood blow.” 3. Stroke due to cardioembolic or hypercoagulable states was considered but excluded as “Transoesophogeal echocardiography and patient’s coagulation profile were unremarkable.”

title: Autoimmune sensorineural hearing loss as presenting manifestation of paediatric Behçet disease responding to adalimumab: a case report
case_prompt:
A 15-year-old Italian boy presented with a six-month history of recurrent episodes of fever, vomiting, dizziness, headache, tinnitus, and ataxia. He had no significant past medical or family history. On day 7 after symptom onset, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain was normal. A vestibular viral neuritis was diagnosed, and oral steroids were given with partial improvement.

On admission he was afebrile (37 °C), normotensive, and alert. He had persistent dizziness and horizontal nystagmus aggravated by head movement. He was unable to sit or stand unassisted. Romberg’s sign and the head-thrust test were positive. There were no visual deficits, meningeal signs, or pathological reflexes, and tympanic membranes were normal.

Laboratory studies showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 40 mm/h, C-reactive protein of 4.5 mg/dL, and neutrophilic leukocytosis. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 3 nucleated cells per field, normal glucose and protein levels, and negative tests for Herpes simplex virus 1–2, enterovirus, and Borrelia burgdorferi. Cultures of CSF, blood, urine, and throat swabs were negative. Serologic tests for varicella-zoster virus, Borrelia burgdorferi, cytomegalovirus, and Epstein–Barr virus were negative. Anti-nuclear and anti-cochlear antibodies were absent. HLA-B52 haplotype was detected.

Audiometry demonstrated bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Chest radiography, abdominal ultrasonography, and repeat brain MRI were unremarkable. Electroencephalography was normal.

An autoimmune audio-vestibular disease was suspected, and methylprednisolone (2 mg/kg/day) was initiated.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious etiologies were considered and ruled out by CSF and microbiologic studies: “Cultures of CSF, blood, urine and throat swabs showed negative results. Varicella-zoster, Borrelia Burgdorferi, Cytomegalovirus and Epstein-Barr virus serology were also negative.” 2. Sarcoidosis and other systemic inflammatory disorders were excluded by imaging: “Chest x-ray, abdomen ultrasound and brain MRI excluded any abnormality.” 3. Autoimmune inner ear disease (AIED) was considered given the bilateral sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo: “In every patient who has progressive bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with no other explainable cause, AIED should be considered…”

title: Management of Uveitis-Glaucoma-Hyphema Syndrome Caused by Soemmering Ring Cataract: Case Report and Literature Review
case_prompt:
A 77-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of recurrent episodes of transient vision loss in her right eye over the past 6 months. Each episode lasted 2–4 hours and occurred several times per week. She denied any headache or migrainous symptoms. Her past medical history was notable for hypertension, dyslipidemia, and thyroidectomy on levothyroxine; her ocular history was significant only for uncomplicated phacoemulsification with in-the-bag intraocular lens (IOL) implantation in the right eye 16 years ago.  

On initial examination, visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes. Intraocular pressure (IOP) was 18 mm Hg OD and 14 mm Hg OS. Humphrey visual field testing (24-2) showed a mean deviation of −2.83 dB OD and −0.05 dB OS. Optical coherence tomography revealed ganglion cell layer thickness of 64 μm OD and 67 μm OS, and retinal nerve fiber layer thickness of 89 μm OD and 87 μm OS. Slit-lamp examination showed mild posterior capsule opacification but was otherwise unremarkable.  

Given the transient monocular vision losses, a vascular workup for amaurosis fugax was performed, including C-reactive protein, MRI of the head and orbits, transthoracic echocardiography, carotid Doppler ultrasound, and 24-hour Holter monitoring; all results were within normal limits.  

On follow-up, IOP in the right eye had risen to 32–38 mm Hg (21–23 mm Hg OS). Gonioscopy revealed blood in the right anterior chamber angle without iris transillumination defects. Cerebral angiography was then obtained. Results were negative for any dural or carotid–cavernous fistula. Ultrasound biomicroscopy of the right eye demonstrated a circumscribed mass contacting the posterior surface of the iris.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Vascular (amaurosis fugax) cause considered — “All were within normal limits.” 2. Carotid–cavernous/dural fistula considered — “angiography … ruled out a dural fistula.”

title: A rare cause of lateral facial swelling
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old man presented with an asymptomatic, diffuse swelling on the right side of his face and neck. He reported similar, smaller swellings over the past 3–4 years that would spontaneously regress. He could not relate episodes to trauma, meals, dental pain, or systemic symptoms.

On examination, a 7 × 3 cm firm, non-tender, mobile swelling extended from the midface to the right submandibular region and from the oral commissure to the anterior masseter border. The overlying skin was normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy was palpable. Oral opening, salivary duct flow, cranial nerves, and neck movement were normal. There was no trismus, dysphagia, or dyspnea.

Initial differential diagnoses included buccal‐space infection, dermoid or epidermoid cyst, branchial cleft cyst, lipoma, vascular malformation, recurrent lymphadenitis or sialadenitis, salivary tumor, and cyst/pseudocyst of salivary origin. The absence of inflammatory signs and normal intraoral findings argued against infection.

Fine-needle aspiration yielded yellowish mucoid fluid. Microscopy showed cystic macrophages in a mucoid background, culture was negative, and the fluid had a high amylase content. Ultrasonography reported 7–9 mL of slightly echogenic fluid with irregular margins anterior to the right mandibular angle, with no calcifications.

Contrast-enhanced MRI of the face revealed a well-defined, lobulated 5.5 × 4.5 × 2.5 cm cystic lesion that was hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, with thin peripheral enhancement and a few septations. The lesion lay anterior to the right submandibular gland, lateral to the mylohyoid muscle, and extended over the masseter, without invasion of the parapharyngeal or sublingual spaces. The submandibular and parotid glands appeared normal. There was no “tail sign” toward the sublingual gland.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection was unlikely — “Absence of inflammatory signs and normal intraoral findings excluded chances of infection.” 2. Tumor of salivary origin was less likely — “The recurrent nature of the swelling … nullified the tumerous etiology.” 3. Vascular malformation was excluded by aspirate — “The clinical picture of aspirate reduced the probability of vascular anomaly … to a great extent.” 4. Salivary retention cyst (true cyst) versus extravasation was considered given high amylase, but lack of epithelial lining (not yet histologically confirmed) leaned toward extravasation — “Aspirate had high amylase content. This information was more suggestive of a salivary cause for the lesion.” 5. Plunging ranula (sublingual gland origin) was excluded by imaging — “The typical ‘tail sign’ of a plunging ranula was not seen.” 6. Localization to submandibular gland on MRI focused the diagnosis on a submandibular mucous extravasation phenomenon.

title: Advanced coats-like retinopathy as the initial presentation of Familial Retinal Arterial Macroaneurysms
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_prompt: A 7-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of intermittent exotropia in his left eye. His medical history was notable for mild supravalvular pulmonary stenosis discovered on echocardiography during a routine exam. Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 in the right eye and 3/200 in the left eye. Intraocular pressures were normal in both eyes, and anterior segment examination was unremarkable. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed subretinal hard exudates at the superotemporal arcade with minimal foveal involvement and clinically visible aneurysms in the midperiphery. The left eye showed a subtotal exudative retinal detachment with marked vascular telangiectasia superotemporally. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye highlighted subtle beading of a major arterial trunk, mid-peripheral and peripheral aneurysms, and capillary dropout in all quadrants. Fluorescein angiography of the left eye demonstrated peripheral aneurysms and areas of non-perfusion.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Familial retinal arterial macroaneurysms was considered because it is “a rare autosomal recessive disease characterized by retinal arterial beading, aneurysm formation along the major arterial trunks, hemorrhage and exudation.” 2. Coats disease was considered given the “telangiectatic vessels, capillary non-perfusion, aneurysmal dilatations, and exudation,” but bilateral involvement is rare and “usually raises the suspicion of a systemic disease associated with Coats’-like retinopathy.”

title: Multicentric reticulohistiocytosis with prominent skin lesions and arthritis: A case report
case_prompt:
A 38-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of a nonpruritic rash and 1 year of bilateral knee pain. The rash began as small red macules on the right thigh and progressed to brownish-red papules on both thighs, neck, shoulders, back, chest, abdomen, and arms. Treatments for presumed eczema had no effect. Over the subsequent year he developed firm, smooth, brownish-red nodules (2–8 mm) on the dorsal hands and “gooseneck” deformities of the fingers, with difficulty making a fist. He also reported worsening knee pain, swelling, and limited squatting but denied fever, myalgia, oral ulcers, or weight loss.

On examination, he had densely distributed brown-red maculopapular lesions behind the ears, neck, shoulders, chest, back, waist, abdomen, arms, and inner thighs. Multiple 2–8 mm hard, nontender nodules were present on the dorsal hands, with limited interphalangeal motion and flexion deformities of digits 2–5. Thumb interphalangeal joints were swollen bilaterally. Knee joints were swollen and tender with a reduced range of motion.

Laboratory studies showed normal liver function, biochemistry, lipids, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and tumor markers. ANA, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANCA, anti-MPO, and anti-PR3 were negative. Platelet and monocyte counts were normal. Immunoglobulin G and C3 were within reference ranges.

Radiographs of the hands revealed narrowing of interphalangeal and intercarpal joint spaces, reduced bone density, and cystic translucent areas. Left radial–carpal joint space was narrowed. Knee MRI demonstrated degeneration of the menisci, osteochondral injury of the patellofemoral surface, possible synovitis, and joint effusion. Chest CT showed bilateral interstitial inflammation, interlobular septal nodules, fibrous foci in the lower lobes, and cystic foci in the right subscapularis muscle.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Dermatomyositis was considered due to skin lesions and joint involvement but was excluded on the basis that MRH lesions are papular nodules and macules rather than heliotrope rash or Gottron papules, and there is no proximal muscle weakness — “Unlike dermatomyositis, the main skin lesions in MRH are papular nodules and macules, whereas Gottron papules, heliotrope rash, Gottron signs, … are observed in dermatomyositis” and “the musculoskeletal symptoms of MRH are featured as arthritis, whereas proximal muscle weakness is observed in dermatomyositis.”

title: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (central diabetes insipidus) with partial empty sella: a case report
case_prompt:
An 18-year-old woman presented with a 4-year history of severe, global headaches (10/10) that worsened over the past 2 years. Eighteen months ago, she developed polydipsia of approximately 15 L per day with matching polyuria, waking 10 times per night to urinate. She also reported secondary amenorrhea for 18 months, a weight loss of 10 kg over the past year, and intermittent epigastric discomfort. She denied trauma, surgery, radiation exposure, fever, cough, or osmotic diuretics. Menarche occurred at age 13. She had no personal or family history of diabetes mellitus or other endocrine disorders. Medications included occasional antacids and analgesics.

On examination, vital signs were normal; BMI was 16 kg/m2. Axillary and pubic hair were normal, and the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Initial labs: urine specific gravity 1.005; random urine osmolality 150 mOsm/kg. After overnight water deprivation, serum osmolality rose to 298 mOsm/kg while urine osmolality remained low at 210 mOsm/kg; urine output was 3 L over 8 h and urine specific gravity 1.006. Desmopressin challenge by IV was unavailable; however, on low-dose oral desmopressin, her urine specific gravity normalized to 1.020 and urine osmolality to 600 mOsm/kg.

Additional labs: βhCG 1.2 mIU/mL, AFP 7 ng/mL, IgG4 28 mg/dL, soluble IL-2 receptor 203 U/mL; anterior pituitary hormones (TSH, T3, T4, prolactin, cortisol) were within normal limits.

Brain MRI showed partial filling of the pituitary fossa by CSF with a flattened pituitary gland measuring 3.68 mm, consistent with a partial empty sella. There was no evidence of intracranial hypertension. Pelvic ultrasound was normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Exclusion of pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma) — “Lambert et al. hypothesized that an empty sella may be due to necrosis of a previous pituitary adenoma, most commonly a prolactinoma, but again in our patient, we did not suspect this from the clinical presentation as the prolactin level was normal.” 2. Exclusion of intracranial mass lesions — “There is no mass on the MRI that suggests the possible differential diagnosis like craniopharyngioma, germinoma, pinealoma, or a metastasis, lymphomas.” 3. Exclusion of idiopathic intracranial hypertension — “There is no sign of intracranial hypertension.”

title: IgG4-related focal retroperitoneal fibrosis in ureter suggestive of colon cancer recurrence and resected laparoscopically: a case report
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old man presented for routine follow-up of sigmoid colon cancer. Two years earlier, he had undergone laparoscopic sigmoidectomy for a pStage I, moderately differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma with submucosal and venous invasion. His father died of gastric cancer. 

He was asymptomatic, with no abdominal pain, palpable masses, or lymphadenopathy on examination. Laboratory studies showed:
- Lactate dehydrogenase 239 U/L (elevated)
- Creatinine 1.14 mg/dL (elevated)
- Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 41 U/mL (elevated)
- Other serum biochemistry and tumor markers within normal limits
- Urine cytology: no atypical cells (class II)

Enhanced abdominal CT demonstrated a 30 × 13.7 mm soft-tissue mass involving the left ureter at the level of the common iliac bifurcation; the lesion had grown from 16.7 × 10.3 mm over 17 months. There was no hydronephrosis or lymphadenopathy. FDG-PET–CT showed focal uptake in the same region (SUVmax 8.93) and no other abnormal accumulations. Ureterography and colonoscopy were unremarkable.

Given the slow growth and significant FDG uptake, concern arose for lymph node recurrence or peritoneal seeding of colon cancer, and a decision was made to proceed with laparoscopic excisional biopsy of the mass.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Colon cancer recurrence was considered because of the lesion’s slow growth and FDG avidity — “Due to the possible lymph node recurrence or peritoneal seeding of colon cancer indicated by the slow growth and significant FDG uptake…” 2. Secondary retroperitoneal fibrosis from drugs, radiation, bleeding, malignancy, or infection was in the differential but was excluded based on the absence of relevant symptoms, history, or findings — “secondary retroperitoneal fibrosis caused by drugs, radiation, bleeding, malignancy, and infection had to be considered… In our case, these factors were excluded based on symptoms, medical history, and examination findings.” 3. A primary ureteral tumor was excluded because preoperative ureterography showed no abnormalities and urine cytology revealed no malignant cells — “The ureteral tumor was also excluded because there were no abnormal findings on the preoperative ureterography or malignant cells in the urine cytology.”

title: New-onset axillary lymphangioma: a case report
case_prompt:
A 28-year-old African woman, with three prior full-term pregnancies and recent immigration from East Africa, presented with a large, tender left axillary mass. She first noticed a small, nontender lump 3 years earlier—6 months after placement of a left-arm etonogestrel implant, 8 months after her most recent delivery, and 2 years after arriving in the United States. The mass grew slowly without pain, drainage, skin changes, or neurologic symptoms, and did not interfere with breastfeeding. Six months before presentation, the mass enlarged rapidly and became tender, limiting full adduction of the left arm. Her implant had been replaced 2 months before this acceleration. She denied breast symptoms, trauma, infection, systemic complaints, or other exposures; her only medication was the implant device.

On exam, there was a softball-sized, mobile, nonreducible mass in the left axillary fossa with overlying skin striations; no regional lymphadenopathy; intact vascular, sensory, and motor function; and mild discomfort with arm adduction. Vital signs and the remainder of the examination were unremarkable.

Prior MRI of the left axilla (with and without contrast) demonstrated a 16 × 11 × 11 cm multiloculated subcutaneous cystic lesion displacing but not invading adjacent structures, without intrathoracic communication or lymphadenopathy. MR lymphangiogram—after subcutaneous contrast injection into the interdigital web spaces—showed no lymphatic communication with the mass, although lymphatic drainage led to nodes over the lesion. Echinococcal IgG antibody testing was equivocal. Initial laboratory studies were otherwise unremarkable, without evidence of systemic infection.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hydatid cyst remained a concern given patient’s origin, as “hydatid cyst could not definitively be ruled out” and serology was equivocal (“IgG testing was performed with equivocal results, indicating possible but not definitive presence of Echinococcus”). 2. Simple cyst was considered but excluded because “imaging was not consistent with typical appearance of … simple cyst.” 3. Abscess was on the initial differential but ruled out since “imaging was not consistent with typical appearance of abscess.” 4. Cavernous hemangioma was considered but excluded because “imaging was not consistent with typical appearance of … cavernous hemangioma.”

title: Bilateral acute tibial osteomyelitis in a patient without an underlying disease: a case report
case_prompt: A 5-year-old boy was brought with a 40-day history of pain, swelling, and redness in both legs. He had been treated 30 days earlier elsewhere with a 10-day course of oral antibiotics for fever. He had no history of chronic illness or trauma. On examination, both legs showed swelling, erythema, warmth, and purulent discharge from the proximal tibiae. Vital signs were within normal limits except for mild tachycardia. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 15,640/mm3, C-reactive protein 5.29 μg/mL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 63 mm/h. Serologic tests for Brucella and Salmonella and purified protein derivative tests for tuberculosis, previously negative twice, were again negative; chest radiograph was normal. Radiographs of both tibiae showed cortical destruction and periosteal reaction at the proximal tibiae and distal left tibia. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated bone-marrow edema throughout both tibiae, purulent debris within the medullary canals and metaphyses, and adjacent pyomyositis. Radionuclide bone scanning revealed increased technetium uptake in the metaphyses of both tibiae.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Brucellosis — “The results of a Brucella tube agglutination test ... were negative.” 2. Salmonella osteomyelitis — “Gruber Widal test (for Salmonella) ... was negative.”

title: Acute Allograft Rejection in Kidney Transplant Recipients Treated With Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: An Educational Case Report
journal: Canadian Journal of Kidney Health and Disease
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old man with end-stage kidney disease secondary to congenital oligomeganephronia underwent a living-donor kidney transplant 20 years earlier. Twenty-nine days before presentation, his maintenance immunosuppression (cyclosporine and prednisone) was stopped after an 8-cm clear cell carcinoma was diagnosed in the transplant. Six days before presentation, he received his first doses of nivolumab and ipilimumab.  

He presented to the emergency department with a 1-day history of fever. Vital signs showed a heart rate of 146 beats per minute; examination revealed tenderness over the transplant site, otherwise unremarkable.  

Laboratory studies demonstrated a serum creatinine concentration of 275 μmol/L (baseline 150–180 μmol/L), urinalysis with 3.0 g/L protein and a small amount of blood, negative urine and blood cultures, and no laboratory evidence of tumor lysis syndrome. Chest radiograph was unremarkable.  

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed minimal interval increase in the size of the transplant mass and transplant renal vein findings consistent with tumor thrombus. Doppler ultrasound confirmed a near-occlusive thrombus in the transplant renal vein.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered acute allograft rejection — “Given the sudden decrease in kidney function in the context of recently discontinuing immunosuppression, starting immunotherapy, and the presence of graft-site tenderness, his presentation was felt to be most suspicious for acute allograft rejection…” 2. Considered renal vein thrombosis — “CT of the abdomen and pelvis … showed … transplant renal vein findings consistent with tumor thrombus, and a subsequent ultrasound confirmed a near occlusive thrombus in the transplant renal vein.” 3. Considered tumor lysis syndrome — “There was no laboratory evidence of tumor lysis syndrome.”

title: Clozapine‐induced pulmonary embolism in a patient with no pre‐existing risk factors: A case report
case_prompt:
A 51-year-old Caucasian woman with no significant medical history was admitted to a psychiatric unit for new‐onset auditory hallucinations and paranoid delusions, leading to a diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia. Trials of olanzapine and haloperidol yielded no symptom remission and caused sedation and extrapyramidal side effects, respectively. After two ineffective antipsychotics over adequate durations, clozapine was initiated and titrated to 200 mg daily over 14 days. Baseline ECG and blood tests were normal aside from mild lipid elevations. 

During the first two weeks of clozapine therapy, she developed known side effects including hypersalivation, sedation, constipation, lethargy, and persistent tachycardia (100–120 bpm). She reported a dry cough but three consecutive COVID-19 PCR tests were negative. ECGs showed only sinus tachycardia, and troponin and CRP remained within normal limits, leading to no evidence of myocarditis. 

On day 15, monitoring labs revealed an increase in neutrophils (8.48 × 10^9/L), monocytes, and CRP (23 mg/L). That night, she developed coryzal symptoms, fever (37.8 °C), and left‐sided pleuritic chest pain. Vital signs were: HR 136 bpm, RR 18/min, BP 132/78 mm Hg, SpO2 95% on room air. Examination of chest, heart, abdomen, and calves was normal. Repeat ECGs remained unchanged. CRP rose to 81 mg/L; troponin was 5 ng/L. Urinalysis was unremarkable. As her symptoms resolved spontaneously, she was started on amoxicillin for a presumed lower respiratory tract infection.

On day 18, she acutely deteriorated with HR 150 bpm, RR 24/min, BP 92/46 mm Hg, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 38.7 °C. Arterial blood gas showed pO2 6.0 kPa and pCO2 4.2 kPa. Labs demonstrated lymphopenia (0.77 × 10^9/L), neutrophilia (8.85 × 10^9/L), CRP 197 mg/L, and D-dimer 506 mg/L; coagulation studies were normal. Chest radiograph showed no airspace disease. A third COVID-19 swab was negative. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal ventricular size and function without pericardial or valvular abnormalities.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Clozapine-induced myocarditis was considered — “She was investigated for clozapine-induced-myocarditis, though successive ECGs exhibited sinus tachycardia only… while troponin and C‐reactive protein (CRP) levels remained normal.”

title: Bilateral anophthalmia and intrahepatic biliary atresia, two unusual components of Fraser syndrome: a case report
case_prompt:
A 19-year-old gravida 2, para 1 woman, with no significant medical or surgical history and a nonconsanguineous marriage, attended routine prenatal visits. 
20-week ultrasound showed hyperechogenic lungs, ascites, a normal right kidney, a three-vessel umbilical cord, and a normal sucking reflex. 
At 25 weeks, ultrasound demonstrated relative shortening of the upper and lower limbs, nuchal edema, bilateral syndactyly of both hands and feet, hydrops fetalis, lung enlargement, and cardiac compression; estimated fetal weight was 4000 g. 
At 39 weeks’ gestation, the mother underwent cesarean delivery for cephalopelvic disproportion; the fetus was stillborn, weighing 3600 g, with respiratory insufficiency.

Gross examination of the stillborn revealed bilateral anophthalmia, pseudo-hypertelorism, low-set ears, flat nasal bridge, bilateral cutaneous syndactyly of the hands and feet, generalized edema, large-volume ascites, and ambiguous external genitalia.

Autopsy findings:
- Airways were patent; lungs were enlarged; no significant cardiac anomalies.
- Right renal agenesis; on the left, a gonad and Müllerian structures were noted.
- Liver capsule was congested.

Microscopic examination:
- Lung: dilated pleural lymphatic vessels, interstitial fibroblast hypertrophy, and vascular wall thickening.
- Spleen: congestion of red pulp with hemosiderin and immature white pulp.
- Liver: cholestasis with biliary atresia, biliary plugs, portal-space fibrosis, diffuse congestion, and chronic lymphocytic infiltrates.
- Kidney: increased subcapsular nephrogenic structures, reduced glomerular subcapsular spaces, and cuboidal change of Bowman's capsule parietal epithelium.
- Gonad/Müllerian derivative: clusters of crowded immature primordial follicles and epithelium resembling Fallopian tube.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered plexiform pulmonary arteriopathy but excluded because of multisystem congenital anomalies — “Plexiform pulmonary arteriopathy and lenz microphthalmia syndrome were also differential diagnoses for our case, but the presence of congenital malformations led us to exclude plexiform pulmonary arteriopathy.” 2. Fraser syndrome best explains the combination of anophthalmia, syndactyly, renal agenesis, and ambiguous genitalia according to established criteria — “we suspected: plexiform pulmonary arteriopathy, Fraser Syndrome and Lenz microphthalmia syndrome as differential diagnosis for our case, but Fraser Syndrome was the most suitable one depending on Thomas et al. criteria.”

title: Primary hyperparathyroidism diagnosed after surgical ablation of a costal mass mistaken for giant-cell bone tumor: a case report
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old woman was evaluated for a Raynaud-like disorder when a chest radiograph revealed a peripheral 8-cm lesion involving the third right rib with cortical expansion; a concurrent CT scan confirmed this finding. She reported oligomenorrhea since age 26 but was otherwise asymptomatic. Physical examination was unremarkable. 

Further evaluation included 99mTc bone scintigraphy, which showed focal uptake in the rib lesion and the medial condyle of the right femur. MRI of the chest demonstrated an aneurysmal cystic bone lesion at the same rib site. A percutaneous biopsy of the rib lesion revealed numerous osteoclast-like multinucleated giant cells without necrosis, mitoses, or histologic features of malignancy, a picture compatible with a giant cell tumor of bone.

Six months later, the lesion was excised with local curettage, including resection of the right pectoralis major and portions of the II, III, and IV ribs. Grossly, the specimen was a well-circumscribed, tan, fleshy mass measuring 6.0 × 4.5 × 2.2 cm, confluent with two rib segments. 

Microscopic examination showed:
- A diffuse distribution of multinucleated giant cells  
- A background of spindle-shaped mononuclear stromal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and oval, non-atypical nuclei  
- No necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypical mitoses (mitotic index 6 per 10 HPF)
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Giant cell tumor of bone was the primary diagnosis given that biopsy specimens were “compatible with GCT of bone” and surgical histology again showed a “picture compatible with GCT.”

title: Transitory Spontaneous Remission of Myelodysplasia in an Elderly Man while Receiving Intravesical Bacillus Calmette-Guérin for Bladder Cancer: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 69-year-old man with a 7-year history of refractory anemia–type myelodysplastic syndrome presented in 2008 with macrocytic anemia (hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, MCV 114 fL), a normal white-cell count, normal platelets, and a low reticulocyte count; B12, folate, TSH, and liver function tests were normal, and bone marrow biopsy showed refractory anemia with blasts <5%. By 2011, his hemoglobin fell to 7.0 g/dL and platelets to 50,000/µL despite normal vitamin and thyroid studies; repeat marrow showed hypercellularity with dysplasia, blasts <5%, and a normal karyotype. He required monthly red-cell transfusions to maintain a hemoglobin >8 g/dL and occasional platelet transfusions. 

In October 2013, he noted painless gross hematuria. His hemoglobin was 7.5 g/dL, platelets 13,000/µL, and coagulation studies were normal. CT urography revealed a 16 mm lobulated lesion near the left ureteral orifice. With transfusional support, cystoscopy demonstrated five superficial bladder tumors; transurethral resection showed low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma without invasion. After resection, transfusion requirements decreased but did not resolve. 

Beginning March 2015, he received intravesical BCG (weekly ×6, biweekly ×4, then monthly ×12). While receiving BCG, his hemoglobin rose to 13.7 g/dL, platelets to 317,000/µL, and he became transfusion independent.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Recovery due to reduced bleeding after tumor resection — “by removing the tumor, there was no further bleeding … decreased consumption of platelets and decreased loss of red cells resulted in increased levels of both blood components. However, this theory falls short because it does not explain the rapid decrease in red cell and platelet levels after BCG therapy …” 2. Transfusion-related graft-versus-leukemia effect or anti-leukemic antibodies — “Second possibility is the use of blood transfusions; cytotoxic antibodies against leukemic cells have been previously described or transfused allogeneic lymphocytes causing a ‘graft versus leukemia effect’.”

title: Awakening the sleeping kidney in a dialysis-dependent patient with fibromuscular dysplasia: A case report and review of literature
case_prompt:
A 25-year-old woman was referred after new-onset hypertension (systolic 180–225 mmHg, diastolic 110–130 mmHg) was noted during hospitalization for repair of a tibia-fibula fracture. She was asymptomatic with blood pressure refractory to hydrochlorothiazide and amlodipine. Her body mass index was 23 kg/m2, with no features of endocrine hypertension. Pulses were equal; no bruits were heard. Serum creatinine was 67 µmol/L.

Renal ultrasound showed the left kidney measured 72 mm (poor perfusion), and the right kidney measured 117 mm (resistive index 60%). CTA demonstrated the left renal artery encased in fibrous tissue with near-complete occlusion from 8 mm beyond the ostium to the hilum, and a 50% stenosis of the right renal artery 17 mm from its ostium; the remainder of the aorta and its branches appeared normal. A diagnosis of fibromuscular dysplasia was made.

Over the next months, her serum creatinine rose to 87 µmol/L with resistant hypertension on multiple agents. In January 2016, she developed malignant hypertension, flash pulmonary edema, and a serum creatinine of 1807 µmol/L despite a urine output of 700–1000 mL/day. Repeat ultrasound showed the right kidney had decreased to 92 mm with a resistive index of 70%. A diuretic renogram revealed 80% function in the right kidney and 20% in the left, with poor global function. Hemodialysis was initiated, and creatinine fell to 650 µmol/L; dialysis was then stopped. Three weeks later, malignant hypertension and pulmonary edema recurred, creatinine rose to 1509 µmol/L, and hemodialysis was resumed. Throughout, urine output remained at ~1000 mL/day.

Repeat CTA showed extensive collateral blood supply to the right kidney. A biopsy of the right kidney revealed normal-appearing, viable tissue.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Takayasu’s arteritis — “the lumina of the aorta and its branches were not narrowed and had a smooth wall, and no post-stenotic dilatations were noted. Also, the origins of the stenosis in the renal arteries were not at the ostia but rather at the mid-vessel level.”

title: Rapidly reversible multiorgan failure after ingestion of “Molly” (pure 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine): a case report
case_prompt:
A 24-year-old Hispanic man was brought to the emergency department after a witnessed tonic–clonic seizure with urinary incontinence, which resolved after intravenous lorazepam. He had a remote history of seizures but was not on antiepileptic medications. On arrival he was postictal, afebrile, tachycardic (124 bpm), and had a blood pressure of 144/66 mmHg. Initial laboratory studies showed a lactate level of 17 mmol/L that normalized rapidly. A routine urine toxicology screen was positive for cannabinoids and benzodiazepines and negative for cocaine, phencyclidine, opiates, and amphetamines.

On hospital day 1, he developed abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting, and progressive agitation with confusion. His serum lipase was normal, but the serum creatinine rose from 1.4 mg/dL on admission to 4.3 mg/dL and continued to increase. Several hours later, he became hypoxic with an arterial oxygen saturation of 70%, febrile to 38.9°C, tachycardic at 140 bpm, and hypertensive at 163/104 mmHg. He failed a trial of noninvasive ventilation and was intubated. A chest radiograph showed bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Despite high–fractional inspired oxygen, his oxygenation improved only with high positive end-expiratory pressure.

Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics were started. Bronchoscopy revealed normal airways and clear secretions; bronchoalveolar lavage fluid was obtained. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% with global wall-motion abnormalities, suggesting cardiac dysfunction and fluid overload as contributors to his respiratory failure. The causes of his acute kidney injury and congestive heart failure remained unclear. He underwent three sessions of hemodialysis, after which his oxygenation, urine output, and creatinine level improved. He was extubated on hospital day 8.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Aspiration pneumonitis or bacterial pneumonia — “Bronchoscopy revealed normal airways and clear secretions, not compatible with aspiration or bacterial pneumonia.”

title: Gastric (gastrointestinal)-type endometrial adenocarcinoma presenting as a solitary endometrial polyp: a case report and literature review on a novel and potentially aggressive endometrial cancer histotype
case_prompt: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presented with abnormal vaginal bleeding. Hysteroscopy identified and resected a 2 cm polypoid mass in the right lateral uterine wall. Histologic examination of the biopsy showed mucinous adenocarcinoma with glandular and solid architecture, intra- and extra-glandular necrosis, neoplastic cells with pale eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm, distinct cell borders, variable intracytoplasmic mucin, mild to moderate nuclear atypia, and a high mitotic rate with atypical figures. Immunohistochemistry revealed diffuse positivity for CK20, CDX2, and p16; focal positivity for CK7 and PAX8; negative estrogen and progesterone receptors; a wild-type p53 pattern; focal (< 10%) chromogranin A and synaptophysin positivity; and intact MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, and MSH6 expression. A diagnosis of high-grade adenocarcinoma with mucinous features, highly suspicious for metastatic origin, was initially rendered. Subsequent ultrasound demonstrated only a superficial minimal residual endometrial lesion without myometrial or adnexal involvement. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy, PET, and MRI were all unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Metastatic gastrointestinal carcinoma was suspected given mucinous features and gastrointestinal markers — “Diagnosis of high-grade adenocarcinoma with mucinous features, highly suspicious for metastatic origin was initially rendered” — and was excluded when “esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy and imaging studies (PET-and MRI) all of which were unremarkable.” 2. Endometrioid adenocarcinoma with mucinous differentiation was considered because of the mucinous morphology — “endometrioid adenocarcinoma with mucinous differentiation usually shows areas with typical endometrioid morphology; moreover, mucinous differentiation in these tumors recapitulates the endocervical epithelium rather than the gastric-type mucinous epithelium” — and was excluded by lack of an endometrioid component and hormone receptor expression: “Positive immunohistochemical staining for estrogen and progesterone receptors further support the diagnosis of endometrioid histotype.”

title: Indolent T‐cell‐rich small B‐cell hepatic lymphoma in a Golden Retriever
case_prompt:
An 11-year-old spayed female Golden Retriever was referred for evaluation of an incidentally detected hepatic mass. Five days earlier, she had pollakiuria and mild abdominal discomfort; abdominal ultrasound then showed a 5.2 × 5.0 cm round hypoechoic mass in the left lateral liver lobe and a smaller hypoechoic lesion near the right middle lobe. Urinalysis was notable only for struvite crystals and mild proteinuria; empirical amoxicillin resolved her urinary signs. CBC and serum chemistry were within reference limits. 

Her only chronic issues were bilateral elbow osteoarthritis and cruciate ligament injuries, for which she was receiving tramadol, amantadine, meloxicam, and supplements. On presentation, she was bright, alert, obese (BCS 8/9), with a left forelimb lameness. Abdominal palpation and peripheral lymph nodes were unremarkable. Repeat abdominal ultrasound confirmed a 4.5 × 5.7 cm heterogeneous, well-vascularized hypoechoic mass in the left lateral liver lobe. Thoracic radiographs were normal.

Ultrasound‐guided fine‐needle aspiration of the hepatic mass yielded a mixed lymphoid population: predominantly intermediate‐sized lymphocytes (12–15 μm, indented nuclei, coarse chromatin, pale blue cytoplasm) and fewer small lymphocytes (8–12 μm, condensed chromatin). There was a lymphoglandular background, few hepatocyte clusters with mild hydropic change, and no expanded lymphocytes in adjacent parenchyma. The cytology was most consistent with lymphoma, and marked inflammation was deemed unlikely.

Flow cytometry on the aspirate demonstrated predominantly small CD3+ T cells (mixed CD4+ and CD8+), about 5% small CD21+ B cells, and 15% CD45+ MHC class II+ leukocytes lacking lineage markers. PCR for antigen receptor rearrangements (PARR) revealed a reproducible clonal immunoglobulin heavy‐chain rearrangement and polyclonal T‐cell receptor rearrangements.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Reactive lymphocytic inflammation — “cytology of the liver mass was most consistent with lymphoma, although the presentation of a solitary hepatic nodule and mixed nature of the lymphocytes were unusual. Marked lymphocytic inflammation was considered much less likely.”

title: Gliomatosis cerebri with blindness: A case report with literature review
case_prompt:
A 35-year-old man in a drug rehabilitation center presented with a 4-month history of headaches and one seizure the day before admission. He reported a 2-month history of gradual, painless, progressive visual loss that acutely worsened in the days before hospitalization. He had no history of immunodeficiency, autoimmune disease, or malignancy and denied ongoing substance abuse. On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, and fully conscious. Neurologic assessment showed bilateral visual acuity reduced to light perception on the right and hand motion on the left, with mild to moderate bilateral optic disc swelling. Pupils were mid-size, isochoric, and reactive to light.

Noncontrast head CT revealed bilateral temporal-lobe hypodensity with loss of gray–white differentiation extending into the occipital lobes and centrum semiovale, with mild left uncal herniation but no mass lesion or hemorrhage. Empiric IV aciclovir was started for presumed herpes simplex encephalitis, and corticosteroids with mannitol were administered for raised intracranial pressure. Lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid, an opening pressure of 38 cm H₂O, mild glucose elevation, and otherwise normal cell counts and chemistry. CSF PCR for HSV and blood and urine toxin assays, as well as HIV and hepatitis B and C serologies, were negative.

Brain MRI showed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the temporal lobes, insular regions (worse on the left), with extension into the corpus callosum, centrum semiovale, left basal ganglia, left thalamus, optic chiasm, and periventricular occipital white matter. The lesions were mildly expanded, with no diffusion restriction on DWI, no enhancement on postcontrast images, and no hemorrhagic foci on SWI.

Ophthalmologic testing with optical coherence tomography revealed increased retinal nerve fiber–layer thickness consistent with disc swelling, and visual evoked potentials showed absent bilateral waveforms.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Herpes simplex encephalitis — “HSE and LE were excluded based on incompatible clinical presentation, imaging findings, and laboratory data.” 2. Limbic encephalitis — “HSE and LE were excluded based on incompatible clinical presentation, imaging findings, and laboratory data.” 3. Infiltrative low-grade glioma — “Furthermore, due to volume expansion and facilitated diffusion of involved areas, we had to consider a differential diagnosis of neoplastic lesions such as infiltrative low-grade gliomas.” 4. Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy — “Lack of a previous history of immunodeficiency along with negative test result of HIV, absence of a typical pattern of enhancement and restriction seen in PML as well as the presence of mass effect and cortical involvement lessens the possibility of PML in our patient.” 5. Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis — “…but grey matter involvement and mass effect were not consistent with the diagnosis of SSPE.” 6. Toxic leukoencephalopathy, lymphomatosis cerebri, HIV encephalopathy, and PACNS — “We ruled out these differential diagnoses with laboratory results and lack of their specific imaging findings.”

title: From an incidental lipoma to ileo-ileal intussusception in an adult: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 55-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with 4 hours of severe cramping abdominal pain in the periumbilical and right iliac regions, associated with dry heaving, nausea, and three episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He reported a three-month history of milder, intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort attributed to indigestion. He denied fever, changes in bowel habits, hematochezia, flatus difficulty, weight loss, or urinary symptoms.

On examination, he was hemodynamically stable and afebrile. Abdominal examination revealed a soft, non-distended abdomen with tenderness on deep palpation in the periumbilical and right iliac regions; no masses, guarding, organomegaly, or ascites were noted.

Initial management included intravenous fluids, ondansetron, a proton-pump inhibitor, and analgesia, but his pain persisted. Differential diagnoses considered included acute gastritis, appendicitis, incarcerated or strangulated inguinal hernia, kidney stones, mesenteric ischemia, and small-bowel obstruction.

Investigations:
– Ultrasound of the abdomen and pelvis: normal.  
– Urinalysis: unremarkable except 1+ ketones.  
– Complete blood count, electrolytes, lipase, amylase, liver function tests, troponin I, D-dimer, and C-reactive protein: within normal limits.  
– Serum lactate: 2.9 mmol/L.  
– CT KUB: small left renal calcific density suggestive of a non-obstructing calculus; otherwise normal.  
– Abdominal radiograph: small-bowel distension and absence of ileocecal gas.  
– CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast: small intramural lipoma in the terminal ileum, thought to be an incidental finding; no evidence of ischemia or obstruction.  
– Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy on hospital day 3: normal, although the colonoscope could not be advanced beyond the terminal ileum.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute gastritis was considered but excluded — “Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy were performed on the third day of admission and yielded normal findings.” 2. Appendicitis was considered but excluded — “Ultrasound of the abdomen and pelvis was normal.” 3. Incarcerated or strangulated inguinal hernia was considered but excluded — “No masses or lumps appreciated upon palpation.” 4. Kidney stones were considered but excluded as the cause of obstruction — “CT KUB revealed a small left renal calcific density suggestive of a non-obstructing calculus.” 5. Mesenteric ischemia was considered but excluded — “CT angiography ... showed no other obvious abnormality to suggest small bowel ischemia or intussusception.” 6. Small-bowel obstruction due to an intramural lesion was suspected, but the lipoma was deemed incidental on CT — “CT angiography abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a small intramural lipoma in the terminal ileum which was thought to be an incidental finding.” 7. Intermittent intussusception was ultimately suspected given waxing and waning symptoms and inconclusive standard imaging modalities, prompting further evaluation.

title: Diagnostic conundrum in an ambiguous posterior mandibular osseous lesion: Case report and radiological review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt: A 43-year-old woman presented with a two-year history of a slowly enlarging swelling on the left side of her jaw that was asymptomatic until two months ago, when she experienced dull, nonradiating pain that intensified during meals and resolved spontaneously. Her medical history was notable for type 1 diabetes mellitus and dyslipidemia under treatment. On examination, there was a hard, non-tender mass adherent to the lower border of the left posterior mandible. Intraoral examination and bimanual palpation of the submandibular gland were unremarkable. Panoramic radiography revealed a well-defined, irregular radiopaque lesion in the left posterior mandible. Ultrasonography showed a normal submandibular gland and duct. Cone-beam computed tomography demonstrated a 19×12 mm radiopaque mass arising from the lingual cortex of the left posterior mandible.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Peripheral osteoma was considered because the lesion was uniformly radiopaque and located on the posterior mandible’s lingual aspect — “Radiographically they are uniformly radiopaque when they are composed solely of compact bone ... Osteomas are seen more often on the posterior aspect of the mandible and the lingual side of the ramus ... Since the features mentioned above were found in our patient, a provisional diagnosis of osteoma was made.” 2. Submandibular sialolith was initially suspected due to meal-related pain and a radiopaque mass on panoramic radiograph — “The clinical feature of pain that intensified during meal times, together with the panoramic radiograph, which revealed a radiopaque mass ... probably led to ... diagnosis of a submandibular sialolith in the two previous centers.” It was excluded after normal palpation and imaging of the gland — “Intraoral examination and bimanual palpation of the submandibular gland did not reveal any relevant findings ...” and “USG ... showed normal, typical glandular structure and duct.” 3. Calcified submandibular lymph nodes were considered because they can appear at the mandibular border, but these lesions are lobulated like cauliflower, which was not seen — “Radiologically the distinguishing feature of the lymph node calcifications is the irregular, well-defined, and lobulated appearance ... similar to that of a cauliflower. In our patient, the lesion was well-defined and irregular but not lobulated.” 4. Tonsillolith was considered given its radiopacity, but such calcifications localize mid-ramus and are smaller — “Radiographically, they are found around the mid-portion of the mandibular ramus ... though this patient has a radiopaque lesion like a cortical bone, the location of the lesion was below that of a tonsillolith. The size ... was also much larger than the regular tonsillolith.” 5. Phlebolith was considered among radiopaque soft-tissue calcifications, but phleboliths are small, multiple, round or oval with concentric rings — “Phlebolith are also radiopaque lesions that are round or oval with a smooth periphery ... they occur as smaller and multiple lesions with concentric radiopaque and radiolucent rings. These features were not found in the radiograph of this patient.”

title: Hyperaesthesia Following Genital Herpes: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old female teacher presented with six weeks of burning pain and sensitivity in both inner thighs, the left buttock, left leg, and sole of the left foot. Six weeks earlier she had been treated empirically for a urinary tract infection, but her dysuria persisted and was soon followed by acute bilateral vulval blisters. She was diagnosed with genital herpes and treated with valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily for one week, with resolution of blisters in five days. Despite a further week of valacyclovir, she developed persistent burning and exquisite hypersensitivity in the S1–S3 dermatomes on the left. After six weeks of valacyclovir with minimal improvement and no recurrent ulcers, she was referred. 

She denied systemic or prodromal symptoms. She was in a monogamous relationship; her partner was HSV-2 positive and on suppressive therapy. She had no prior history of genital herpes or shingles; childhood chickenpox only. Her medications included escitalopram started four months earlier. 

Examination showed normal genital skin and hyperaesthesia in left S1–S3 dermatomes without motor deficit or other sensory loss. A PCR swab of healed vulval sites was negative for HSV and VZV; first‐catch urine PCR was negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. She declined HSV serology. 

Pain management was discussed; four days later she began amitriptyline, titrated to 20 mg at night, with complete resolution of pain after four weeks. Three months later she had a milder recurrence of vulval blisters, self-treated with topical acyclovir; lesions healed in two days, but she again experienced hyperaesthesia in the same distribution, which resolved after three weeks of amitriptyline.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Varicella zoster virus with postherpetic neuralgia was considered given the dermatomal pain — “We considered the diagnosis of PHN complicating vulval zoster in the patient. Unlike HSV, however, VZV reactivations are rare in immunocompetent individuals due to the differences in molecular mechanisms controlling latency of these viruses.”

title: Diabetes Insipidus Complicating Diabetes Mellitus Type 1: A Pituitary Abscess Diagnosis
case_prompt:
A 14-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes mellitus since age 7 was admitted for a 2-month history of progressive asthenia, weight loss, headaches, and glycemic instability characterized by multiple unexplained hypoglycemic episodes. Over the preceding 2 weeks she noted increased urine output and polydipsia without a clear relationship to blood glucose levels. She also reported a 2-month history of secondary amenorrhea. Past medical history was notable for an appendectomy at age 8 and chronic autoimmune thyroiditis with normal thyroid function. She was on multiple daily insulin injections at 0.8 U/kg/day.  
On examination, she was Tanner stage V, 159.8 cm (48th percentile), and 51.7 kg (55th percentile). Vital signs, neurologic examination, and funduscopic exam were normal.  
Initial studies—including chest radiography, abdominal ultrasonography, and electrocardiography—were unremarkable. Laboratory evaluation showed HbA1c 7.18% (54 mmol/mol), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, normal electrolytes, liver and lipid panels, and negative inflammatory markers.  
Endocrine testing revealed:  
• Morning ACTH 13.94 pg/mL and cortisol 3.81 μg/dL; low-dose ACTH stimulation yielded a peak cortisol of 338 nmol/L, consistent with central adrenal insufficiency.  
• Free T4 0.91 ng/dL and TSH 0.03 μIU/mL, indicating central hypothyroidism.  
• Urine osmolality 59 mOsm/kg with 5–6 L/day water intake/output, diagnostic of diabetes insipidus.  
• Undetectable 17-β-estradiol and elevated prolactin (≈90 ng/mL) on serial measurements.  
Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Visual field testing showed an upper central isopter defect and enlarged blind spot.  
Brain magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a solid sellar-suprasellar mass with postcontrast enhancement, central necrotic area without hemorrhage, thickening of the pituitary stalk, and absence of the normal posterior pituitary bright spot. Computed tomography confirmed sella turcica enlargement without evidence of sinus disease.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hypophyseal adenoma was considered as a common sellar mass, but imaging showed atypical features — “solid sellar-suprasellar expansive process with postcontrast enhancement, central necrotic area without intralesional bleeding…” 2. Pituitary inflammation (hypophysitis) was considered given stalk thickening, yet inflammatory markers were unremarkable — “Inflammatory markers were negative.” 3. Germinoma was included in the differential, although necrosis is unusual in this tumor — “germinoma, although the central necrotic component is not common in this condition…” 4. Pituitary apoplexy was considered, but the absence of hemorrhage and the presence of diabetes insipidus argued against it — “pituitary apoplexy, although the lack of the posterior pituitary bright spot and diabetes insipidus are not typically associated with this condition.”

title: An Abnormal Case of Malignant Catatonia in a Previously Healthy Young Female With Unexplained Neurological Symptoms
case_prompt: A 26-year-old woman with a history of unspecified bipolar disorder and recent antipsychotic treatment was brought to the emergency department after 10 days of worsening mental status. Collateral history revealed progressive fatigue, inability to feed herself, and dependence for activities of daily living. On arrival, she was unresponsive; blood pressure was 175/108 mm Hg, heart rate 170 beats per minute, temperature 37.8 °C, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. She had adequate urine output but required assistance with toileting. Physical examination showed rigidity of all four extremities, areflexia, inability to follow commands, and warm skin. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 12,000 per mm³ and a creatine kinase level of 6,500 IU/L. A noncontrast CT scan of the head was unremarkable. The initial differential diagnosis included neuroleptic malignant syndrome, malignant catatonia, encephalitis, and sepsis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome was considered because of recent antipsychotic exposure and similar presentation — “differential diagnosis at this time included NMS, malignant catatonia, encephalitis, and sepsis.”

title: G‐CSF‐induced TIPIC syndrome and large vessel vasculitis: A case report
case_prompt: A 73-year-old woman with intermediate-2–risk MDS in complete remission after azacitidine and on venetoclax presented with acute odynophagia beginning 2 days earlier, followed by left-sided neck pain at the carotid bifurcation and swelling. She had received G-CSF 4 days prior for severe neutropenia (neutrophils 290/mm^3). She remained SARS-CoV-2 PCR–positive but without hypoxia. On day 6 of neck pain, she had fever to 38.5 °C and a C-reactive protein of 328 mg/L. Contrast CT demonstrated circumferential thickening around the left internal carotid artery at its bifurcation extending into the common carotid artery, and concentric thickening of the aortic arch. Cervical ultrasound confirmed an eccentric, isoechoic, circumferential perivascular infiltrate at the left carotid bifurcation. Two cytopunctures of the pericarotid tissue yielded predominantly blood with neutrophils and lymphocytes, but no giant cells or malignant cells. She was transferred to the internal medicine and vascular department for further evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Paraganglioma was considered in carotid occlusion but excluded by inflammatory wall thickening without a mass or flow disturbance on imaging — “Isoechoic, circumferential wall thickening extending to the origins of the internal and external carotid arteries.”

title:
Spongiform leukoencephalopathy: A unique case of biopsy confirmed
leukoencephalopathy secondary to toxic, non-inflammatory exposure
case_prompt:
A 60-year-old man with a history of hypothyroidism and alcohol abuse was brought to an outside hospital after being found unresponsive by his wife. Over the preceding month, he had experienced a subacute, rapidly progressive cognitive decline. He was treated for sepsis and presumed aspiration pneumonia in the setting of a suspected accidental opioid and benzodiazepine overdose; his course was complicated by acute respiratory failure requiring intubation and a 4-day ICU stay. Although he recovered from his respiratory failure and completed rehabilitation, his mental status did not return to baseline. One week after discharge, he developed behavioral changes, including leaving water running, walking around naked, and defecating in the closet. Twenty-four days after his initial admission, he was readmitted with continued neuropsychiatric decline progressing to near akinetic mutism.

During the second hospitalization, laboratory testing—including ammonia, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, antinuclear antibody, striated muscle antibody, acetylcholine-receptor antibody, and N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor antibody—a paraneoplastic antibody panel, and lumbar puncture (normal opening pressure; CSF glucose, encephalitis panel, VDRL, cytology, West Nile virus, and protein 14-3-3) were unremarkable except for an elevated CSF protein of 86 mg/dL. Head CT, brain MRI with and without gadolinium, MR angiography of the head and neck, and cerebral angiography showed only T2-weighted hyperintensities in the bilateral basal ganglia and diffuse increased signal in the cerebral white matter, without enhancement or restricted diffusion. Initial EEG showed intermittent left frontotemporal slowing and sharp waves; one week later, it showed diffuse slowing. A trial of lacosamide and a 3-day course of IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day) produced no clinical improvement. He was then transferred to Tampa General Hospital.

On arrival, he was awake with eyes open but nonverbal, could not follow commands, and had only limited purposeful movements; there was no focal or asymmetric weakness. Extensive serum studies—including CBC, CMP, urinalysis, thyroid function tests, vitamin and micronutrient levels, homocysteine, ammonia, ceruloplasmin, methylmalonic acid, hemoglobin electrophoresis, serum and urine protein electrophoreses, heavy-metal panel, autoimmune serologies, and infectious serologies—were unrevealing; inflammatory markers were mildly elevated (CRP 0.79 mg/L; ESR 23 mm/h). A repeat lumbar puncture (opening pressure 22 cm H₂O) showed CSF analyses negative for meningitis panel, cryptococcal antigen, Lyme, arboviruses, CMV, HSV, VDRL, gram stain, and cultures, with oligoclonal bands absent and protein mildly elevated at 70 mg/dL. Brain MRI again demonstrated extensive nonenhancing white matter hyperintensities. A 24-hour continuous EEG revealed a mild to moderate diffuse encephalopathy without epileptiform activity. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was negative for malignancy, mass, or lymphadenopathy.

Steroid-responsive encephalopathy with thyroiditis (SREAT) was considered, and the patient received a 5-day course of IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day) without improvement. He then received intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g total over 4 days), but his clinical status remained unchanged.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Steroid-responsive encephalopathy with thyroiditis was considered but not supported clinically — “SREAT was considered in the differential ... However, he demonstrated no clinical improvement.” 2. Primary CNS lymphoma was considered but imaging showed no tumor or lymphadenopathy — “CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis ... was negative for any underlying malignancy, mass, or lymphadenopathy.”

title: Primary Amenorrhea in a 15‐Year‐Old Girl Leading to the Diagnosis of Bardet‐Biedl Syndrome: A Case Report
case_prompt:
A 15-year-old girl with congenital intellectual disability and blindness presents with primary amenorrhea and a 2-year history of cyclical lower abdominal and pelvic pain lasting 5 days each month. The pain has worsened over the past 2 months and is partially relieved by oral analgesics. She exhibits irritability, reduced attention span, impaired perceptual reasoning, and limited functional independence. A sister has a similar clinical profile.

Vital signs: blood pressure 100/60 mmHg; pulse 96/min; respiratory rate 18/min. Examination shows pallor, moon face, submental fat, height 151 cm, weight 85 kg (BMI 37.2). Post-axial polydactyly is present on the left hand. Tanner stage 5 secondary sexual characteristics. Genital exam reveals vaginal atresia, a rudimentary uterus, and a transverse vaginal septum. Ophthalmologic exam shows astigmatism, strabismus, and on funduscopy numerous whitish-yellow equatorial spots, optic nerve pallor, retinal vessel constriction, and macular degeneration.

Abdominal and pelvic ultrasonography demonstrates a 13.9-cm cystic structure posterior to the bladder within the vaginal canal, communicating with a small amount of fluid in the endometrial cavity. MRI confirms hematometrocolpos with moderate bilateral hydrosalpinx. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL); renal, hepatic, metabolic profiles, and electrolytes are normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. McKusick–Kaufman syndrome was initially considered because of hydrometrocolpos and polydactyly — “Initial clinical findings suggested MKS.” 2. MKS was excluded as the reproductive-tract anomalies were more extensive, aligning with BBS, since MKS primarily involves distal vaginal abnormalities while BBS includes Müllerian agenesis — “MKS primarily involves distal vaginal abnormalities, whereas BBS can include extensive malformations like Müllerian agenesis.”

title: A unique case of a huge mixed squamous cell and glandular papilloma of non-endobronchial origin with a peripheral growth
case_prompt: A 76-year-old Japanese man with a 40 pack-year smoking history was referred after an abnormal chest radiograph at a routine exam. He reported no respiratory symptoms. Laboratory studies revealed elevated squamous cell carcinoma antigen (24.5 ng/mL; normal <1.5), cytokeratin 19 fragment (10.3 ng/mL; normal <3.5), carcinoembryonic antigen (7.3 ng/mL; normal <3.5), and surfactant protein D (299 ng/mL; normal <110). Chest radiograph showed an irregular opacity in the right lower lobe. Chest CT demonstrated a large area of consolidation with air bronchograms in the right lower lobe and bilateral reticular opacities with honeycombing. FDG-PET/CT revealed high uptake in the right lung lesion (SUVmax 9.38 at 1 hour; 13.03 at 2 hours). Bronchoscopic examination showed no endobronchial lesion. Transbronchial lung biopsy and bronchial brushing yielded cytology interpreted as squamous cell carcinoma, but occasional ciliated epithelial cells and mucin-containing columnar cells without atypia were also noted.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Initial consideration of squamous cell carcinoma — “Squamous cell carcinoma was suspected based on the cytological examination of the specimen.” 2. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma — “This type of tumor arises in the central airways containing bronchial mucous glands, and lacks ciliated cells. These characteristics also differ from those of the present tumor.”
final_diagnosis: Mixed squamous cell and glandular papilloma

title: Neutrophilic Myositis without Cutaneous Involvement as the First Manifestation of Acute Myeloid Leukemia
journal: The Korean Journal of Internal Medicine
case_prompt:
A 35-year-old man with no past medical history presented with intermittent fever and myalgia of 1 month’s duration. On examination, his temperature was 39.2°C. Both upper arms were swollen and exquisitely tender, but the overlying skin appeared normal.  
  
Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 20 440/mm3 with a differential of 7% segmented neutrophils, 22% lymphocytes, and 40% blast cells, and a lactate dehydrogenase level of 977 U/L.  

Ultrasonography of the upper arms showed diffusely increased echogenicity and thickening of the triceps and brachialis muscles. MRI confirmed diffuse muscle edema without abscess formation.  

A muscle biopsy of the left triceps and brachialis revealed severe myositis with dense infiltration of mature neutrophils, disruption of muscle architecture, and tissue necrosis. Microbiological cultures and stains for bacteria and fungi were negative, and serologic testing for autoantibodies was also negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Connective‐tissue disorders such as polymyositis or dermatomyositis were excluded as “serology for auto‐antibodies were negative” and histology showed “predominantly dense infiltration of mature neutrophils” rather than lymphocytic infiltrates.

title: Family Transmission of COVID-19 Including a Child with MIS-C and Acute Pancreatitis
case_prompt:
A previously healthy 13‐year‐old girl was evaluated on day 40 of illness with fever and rash. Four weeks after a mild febrile illness, she developed fever to 39–41 °C, fatigue, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, profuse sweating and diarrhea. On day 40, she awoke with a red rash on her palms, which rapidly spread to her arms, feet and knees.

On arrival to the emergency department, vital signs were: blood pressure 87/56 mmHg; heart rate 137 bpm; respiratory rate 16/min; oxygen saturation 95% on room air; temperature 39.1 °C. She was alert but fatigued with mild respiratory distress. Physical examination revealed conjunctival injection, slight bilateral eye redness, minimally erythematous throat, dry mucous membranes, dry cracked lips, no lymphadenopathy, a grade II apical systolic murmur, clear lungs, and an erythematous rash on palms, arms and feet.

Initial laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein of 255 mg/L, white blood count 8.1×10^9/L, hemoglobin 117 g/L. A nasopharyngeal swab for SARS-CoV-2 PCR was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Kawasaki disease–like syndrome was suspected due to mucocutaneous signs and fever — “Together with mucocutaneous-eye symptoms and high fever this fulfilled criteria for a diagnosis of Kawasaki-like disease.”

title: COVID-19 myocarditis and postinfection Bell’s palsy
case_prompt:
A 37-year-old man with no past medical history developed fever, headache, and a painful left-sided neck swelling while working offshore. He denied cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or recent travel and had no known COVID-19 contacts. On admission: temperature 41 °C, heart rate 115 beats/min, blood pressure 119/76 mm Hg, respiratory rate 12–22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Physical examination revealed a tender, non-fluctuant swelling extending from the left mandibular angle to the sternocleidomastoid muscle and a red area on the posterior palate; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.  
ECG showed sinus tachycardia with moderately flattened T-waves. Chest radiograph was normal. Arterial blood gas on room air demonstrated respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.55, pCO2 3.9 kPa, pO2 8.4 kPa, HCO3 23 mmol/L). Laboratory studies revealed CRP 230 mg/L (0–5), procalcitonin 2.1 µg/L (0–0.10), WBC 7.1 × 10^9/L (3.5–11.0), troponin T 90 ng/L (0–15), and NT-proBNP 160 ng/L (0–85).  
Contrast CT of the neck showed left-sided subcutaneous edema with multiple enlarged lymph nodes up to 2.7 cm. Transthoracic echocardiography on admission demonstrated normal ventricular function without hypokinesia. On day 2, echocardiography revealed a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 40%, while CRP rose to 344 mg/L and procalcitonin to 12.9 µg/L, with persistently normal WBC count.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sepsis was ruled out as he did not meet systemic criteria — “He did not fulfil the criteria for sepsis.” 2. Myocardial infarction was thought to be unlikely in the absence of chest pain — “In the absence of chest pain myocardial infarction was thought to be less likely…” 3. Myocarditis was suggested based on elevated troponin, raised NT-proBNP, and declining ejection fraction — “…but myocarditis was suggested as differential diagnosis.”

title: Diagnostic Imaging of Primary Cutaneous Osteoma of the Forehead
case_prompt: A 20-year-old Japanese woman presented with a slowly enlarging subcutaneous nodule on her left forehead, first noticed at age 15. On examination, a firm, poorly mobile 7-mm nodule was palpable beneath the skin. Ultrasonography showed a well-defined, dome-shaped, solid subcutaneous mass (~7 mm) without vascular flow on Doppler; there was apparent continuity with the frontal bone, though the interface with subcutaneous tissue was unclear. MRI of the head demonstrated a 7-mm lesion within the frontalis muscle that was uniformly low signal on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences, with no obvious connection to the frontal bone.
