Noncontrast CT revealed a high-attenuation nodule beneath the frontalis muscle, measuring approximately 7 mm, with a small zone of decreased attenuation separating it from the underlying bone. Based on these findings, benign entities such as calcifying epithelioma and skull osteoma were considered in the preoperative differential.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Osteosarcoma was considered from reported frontal bone cases but ruled out due to its irregular MRI signal pattern, in contrast to the uniform low signal seen — “Previous literature has reported osteosarcoma occurring in the frontal bone, similar to calcifying epithelioma, which exhibited an irregular signal pattern on MRI.” 2. Malignant skull tumors were kept in mind but deemed unlikely given their rarity and the benign imaging appearance — “Malignant tumors on the skull are exceedingly rare; however, malignant and benign tumors should be considered.”

title: A rare cause of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia
case_prompt:
A 6-year-old boy presented after awakening with nonbloody, nonbilious emesis, lightheadedness, and approximately one minute of eye fluttering with unresponsiveness but no tonic–clonic movements or incontinence. Post-ictally, he had headache, photophobia, and vision changes. He had intermittent morning vomiting for one month but no fevers, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, or prior seizures. Family history was notable for brain tumors in maternal relatives. 

On exam, blood pressure was 161/102 mm Hg, heart rate 90–110 beats/min, temperature 37.9 °C, and respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. He was neurologically intact with bilateral upgoing toes. Abdominal exam revealed suprapubic distension without tenderness or organomegaly.

Noncontrast head CT showed a left‐hemisphere hypodense region with artifact. Brain MRI demonstrated bilateral parieto-occipital subcortical T2 prolongation and gyral swelling consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome. EEG captured subclinical seizures from bilateral occipital regions, which resolved with lorazepam, levetiracetam, and fosphenytoin.

Initial laboratory studies showed WBC 29.7×10^6/mL, hemoglobin 12 g/dL, platelets 265×10^6/mL, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL. Urinalysis revealed 30 mg/dL protein without blood. Lumbar puncture yielded 0 nucleated cells, 0 red blood cells, protein 43 mg/dL, and glucose 69 mg/dL. Renal ultrasound revealed bilaterally enlarged (11.6 cm) echogenic kidneys without Doppler abnormalities and a distended bladder. Transthoracic echocardiogram was normal without left ventricular hypertrophy. Despite blood pressure control with intravenous nicardipine transitioned to oral agents, the etiology of his hypertension and renal enlargement remained unclear.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Renal artery stenosis was excluded since “His renal ultrasound with Doppler was inconsistent with renal artery stenosis.” 2. Microangiopathic hemolysis was unlikely because “he had no anemia suggestive of microangiopathy.” 3. Chronic hypertension was considered but deemed unlikely as “His hypertension was not thought to be long‐standing, given the absence of left ventricular hypertrophy on TTE.”

title: Presumed veterinary niclosamide-induced retinal toxicity in a human: a case report
case_prompt:
A 28-year-old Iranian woman presented with a 3-year history of bilateral vision loss and photopsia. Three years earlier, she had accidentally ingested four 1250 mg boluses of veterinary niclosamide. A few days after ingestion, she noted visual disturbances and was treated empirically with a short course of high-dose oral corticosteroids for presumed bilateral optic neuritis; her vision neither improved nor worsened. Over the next 3 years, her visual symptoms remained stable. She denied other medication use, alcohol, smoking, night blindness, or photophobia. There was no family history of vision loss or retinal disease, and parents were nonconsanguineous. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. Best-corrected visual acuities were 20/50 in the right eye and 20/63 in the left eye. Color vision and pupillary reactions were normal. Anterior segment and vitreous examinations were unremarkable; intraocular pressures were normal. Fundus examination revealed pink optic discs, cup-to-disc ratios of 0.2, and parafoveal retinal pigment epithelial atrophy manifesting as bilateral bull’s-eye maculopathy. Fundus autofluorescence demonstrated annular hyperautofluorescence in the parafoveal region. Optical coherence tomography showed parafoveal ellipsoid zone disruption, outer retinal thinning, and relative foveal sparing (“flying saucer” sign). Full-field electroretinography revealed decreased scotopic and photopic amplitudes with normal waveforms in both eyes. Electrooculography showed Arden ratios of 240% (right eye) and 202% (left eye).
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Chloroquine toxicity was contemplated based on imaging patterns — “Significant thinning of central foveal thickness, loss of ellipsoid zone in the parafovea, and relative central foveal sparing resemble chloroquine toxicity.” 2. Quinine- or cytarabine/daunorubicin-induced optic neuropathy with bull’s-eye maculopathy was noted but excluded by history — “Optic neuropathy with bull’s eye maculopathy has been reported as an adverse effect of some drugs such as quinine and chemotherapy with a combination of cytarabine and daunorubicin.”

title: Rare Gingival Metastasis by Hepatocellular Carcinoma
case_prompt:
A 60-year-old man with a 20-year history of hepatitis B and 1-year history of liver cirrhosis presented initially with chronic abdominal pain. His alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) level was 2494.2 ng/mL (normal <10.9). CT scans showed a 4×3 cm mass in the left liver lobe and multiple pulmonary nodules. He underwent transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) and two cycles of gemcitabine–oxaliplatin, but his liver tumor enlarged and AFP rose to 8683.0 ng/mL. A bean-sized gingival nodule was noted, which had mild pain, discharged “pus” on puncture, and recurred quickly. He received further TACE and sorafenib, with stable disease for 6 months, then disease progression. 

On readmission, he reported breathlessness, progressive weight loss, severe back pain, and right upper quadrant pain. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 91 g/L, platelets 97×10^9/L, albumin 33.4 g/L, AFP 4325.0 ng/mL, AST 66 U/L, LDH 296 U/L, direct bilirubin 9.4 µmol/L, indirect bilirubin 13.2 µmol/L, and normal coagulation. 

Physical examination revealed a 2×2 cm reddish nodule on the alveolar mucosa distal to the upper right first premolar; it was soft, sharply demarcated, centrally ulcerated, swollen, mildly tender, and bled occasionally. Radiography showed no bony destruction.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Considered inflammatory or benign reactive lesions — “which is easily confused with inflammatory or benign reactive lesions.” 2. Considered secondary invasion from jawbone metastasis — “it may be difficult to discriminate whether a gingival neoplasm originates from the secondary invasion of metastasized jaw tumor.”

title: Giant insulinoma in a 15-year-old man: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt:
A 15-year-old man presented to the emergency department with acute lightheadedness, diaphoresis, and palpitations in the evening after a 7-hour fast. He had experienced recurrent episodes of dizziness for 2 months without weight change. Point-of-care blood glucose was 1.5 mmol/L, and his symptoms resolved after IV dextrose.

He had no family history of insulinoma or multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1. Vital signs were normal. On abdominal examination, there was a large, firm, palpable mass in the left upper quadrant.

Serum testing during hypoglycemia showed an inappropriately elevated insulin level of 13.34 μU/mL (normal <3 μU/mL) and a C-peptide level of 2.2 ng/mL (normal <0.6 ng/mL). Serum intact parathyroid hormone, calcium, and prolactin levels were all within reference ranges.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a well-defined, lobulated, hypervascular mass measuring 12 cm in the pancreatic tail with areas of low attenuation and calcifications. There was no pancreatic duct dilatation, no hepatic lesions, and no significant lymphadenopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. NIPHS was considered but excluded because “NIPHS could cause hypoglycemia from endogenous hyperinsulinism, but palpable mass in this patient could not explain this syndrome.” 2. An ectopic insulin-secreting tumor was considered but made unlikely after CT localization “helped rule out NIPHS and made ectopic insulin secreting tumor unlikely.”

title: Inflammation clashing onto myocardial susceptibility: a tale of two rare diseases—a case report
case_prompt:
A 40-year-old man was referred for evaluation of newly diagnosed, asymptomatic left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction discovered after premature ventricular contractions were noted on a pre-sports electrocardiogram. His history was notable for cutaneous and pulmonary sarcoidosis diagnosed at age 25 and in remission since 2017 after corticosteroid therapy. He had no known cardiovascular disease, no relevant family history, and took no regular medications. Physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed NT-proBNP 110 pg/mL, high-sensitivity troponin T 20 ng/dL, soluble interleukin-2 receptor 2287 pg/mL (normal 458–1997), and angiotensin-converting enzyme 155 U/L (normal 8–52). A 24-hour Holter monitor revealed sinus rhythm with 9 polymorphic PVCs per hour. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a severely dilated LV (indexed end-diastolic volume 112 mL/m²), basal thinning (“scooping”) of the anterior septum, and moderate systolic dysfunction (LVEF 41%, global longitudinal strain –15.4%). The right ventricle was slightly dilated but had normal systolic function. No valvular abnormalities or pericardial effusion were noted.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed diffuse myocardial edema (T2 mapping 51 ± 6 ms; institutional cut-off 50 ms) in anterior, anterolateral, and inferolateral walls, and extensive LGE (26% of LV mass) with intramural septal and subepicardial anterior, inferior, and lateral involvement, forming a “ring-like” pattern. Coronary CT angiography was normal. A presumptive diagnosis of cardiac sarcoidosis was made based on imaging. Whole-body 18F-FDG PET/CT revealed diffuse myocardial uptake without extracardiac involvement. Right ventricular endomyocardial biopsy was negative for granulomas.

The patient was started on prednisolone, methotrexate, ramipril, bisoprolol, and dapagliflozin. At three months, CMR showed normalization of T2 values (49 ms) and recovery of LVEF to 52%, but the LGE pattern persisted. A six-month 18F-FDG PET/CT scan demonstrated resolution of myocardial uptake.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Coronary artery disease was excluded as a cause of LV dysfunction — “Coronary CT angiography excluded coronary artery disease.” 2. Histological confirmation of CS was lacking, reducing diagnostic certainty — “The right ventricle endomyocardial biopsy (EMB) was negative for sarcoid…” 3. The “ring-like” LGE pattern is atypical for CS and suggests arrhythmogenic or other genetic cardiomyopathies — “It is often associated with arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy, mainly desmoplakin and filamin C-truncating variants…”

title: SLE strikes the heart! A rare presentation of SLE myocarditis presenting as cardiogenic shock
case_prompt:
A 20-year-old Filipino man with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed 6 months earlier and maintained on hydroxychloroquine presented with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, fatigue, dry cough, fevers, and a chronic progressive rash. He denied recent travel, sick contacts, high-risk sexual activity, intravenous drug use, smoking, or alcohol use. On examination, his temperature was 100.8 °F, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 118 bpm, and respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. Skin assessment revealed hyperpigmented to violaceous, scaly plaques with excoriated papules on the extremities, back, and chest.

Laboratory studies showed leukopenia, anemia, non-nephrotic proteinuria without active sediment, elevated troponin and N-terminal pro-BNP, high inflammatory markers, hypocomplementemia, and positive ANA with anti–Smith, RNP, chromatin, SS-A, double-stranded DNA, pANCA, and MPO antibodies. Initial chest radiograph was normal. Blood cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Transthoracic echocardiogram at admission showed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction. He was treated with intravenous antibiotics and high-dose corticosteroids for presumed sepsis and SLE flare.

On hospital day 5, after clearance of bacteremia, he developed worsening dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hemoptysis. Chest radiograph and CT of the chest showed bilateral pleural effusions, dense basal consolidations, pulmonary nodules, possible left subsegmental pulmonary emboli, and pericardial effusions. Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram revealed an LVEF of 25%, moderate global hypokinesis, a small pericardial effusion without tamponade, right atrial pressure of 15 mmHg, and mild pulmonary hypertension (43 mmHg). Cardiogenic shock was favored over septic shock.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. SLE myocarditis — “In the absence of any other etiology to explain such a dramatic decline, a diagnosis of SLE myocarditis was most likely.”

title: A rare cause of a common symptom, Anakinra is effective in the urticaria of Schnitzler Syndrome: a case report
case_prompt:
A 43-year-old man with a 6-month history of chronic urticaria presented to clinic. He had no systemic symptoms initially. Laboratory studies showed normal blood counts, electrolytes, liver and thyroid function tests, ESR, and CRP. Serum immunoglobulins revealed a polyclonal increase in IgG and IgA, and a small (<1 g/L) IgM kappa paraprotein. The rash was refractory to high-dose nonsedating antihistamines.

Six months later, he reported generalized fatigue, daily fevers, night sweats, arthralgias (notably right knee and tibial pain), weight loss, and bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Investigations revealed a persistent neutrophilia (>15×10^9/L), elevated ESR and CRP, ongoing polyclonal IgG, IgA, and IgM elevations with the IgM kappa paraprotein increased to 3.3 g/L, and a mildly elevated ferritin (361 μg/L; normal 18–325). While an inpatient, he had daily fevers above 38 °C. 

Isotope bone scan showed increased uptake at the proximal right tibia. MRI of the right knee demonstrated nonspecific marrow signal changes at the distal femur and proximal tibia. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed florid bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Autoimmune serologies (ANA, ENA, dsDNA, CCP, ANCA), rheumatoid factor, and complement levels were all normal or negative. Beta-2 microglobulin and plasma viscosity were slightly elevated; lymphocyte subsets and kappa/lambda ratios were normal; urine Bence Jones protein was negative. Extensive infectious workup, including serologies for EBV, CMV, HIV, hepatitis viruses, syphilis, Borrelia, Brucella, Chlamydia, Q fever, Mycoplasma, and HHV8, as well as mycobacterial cultures, was negative.

Skin biopsy showed an inflammatory epidermal infiltrate suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis or Still’s disease. Lymph node biopsy revealed polyclonal reactive hyperplasia with abundant plasma cells; Castleman disease was considered but further expert review excluded it. Bone marrow biopsy showed only reactive changes. Genetic testing for MVK, TNFRSF1A, and NLRP3 mutations was negative, making Hyper‐IgD syndrome, TNF receptor–associated periodic syndrome, and Muckle Wells/familial cold urticaria unlikely. 

A tentative diagnosis of Adult Onset Still’s Disease was made.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Castleman disease — “Castleman disease was considered but excluded after a further biopsy and review by several expert histopathologists.” 2. Hyper-IgD syndrome — “Mutations were not found in the coding regions of MVK … making the diagnoses of Hyper IgD syndrome … unlikely.” 3. Schnitzler syndrome — “It was first described by L. Schnitzler, as a clinical entity consisting of chronic urticaria, fever, bone pain, arthralgia or arthritis, and a monoclonal IgM gammopathy.”

title: Rapidly Destructive Inflammatory Arthritis of the Hip
case_prompt: A 57-year-old woman presented with 6 months of progressive right hip and groin pain without trauma or steroid use. She had a 3 cm right leg-length discrepancy, severely limited right hip motion, and no neurovascular deficits. Over 5 months she became unable to ambulate. She also had a 3-year history of chronic metatarsophalangeal joint and toe pain with deformities and recent swelling, followed by right knee pain with warmth and swelling. Morning stiffness lasted approximately 2 hours. On examination her swollen joint count was initially 8/66 (right knee and multiple MTPs), rising to 11/66 on subsequent visits. She had an unintentional 50 lb weight loss. Laboratory studies showed elevated inflammatory markers, a weakly positive ANA, and negative RF and anti-CCP. All blood cultures (aerobic, anaerobic, fungal, mycobacterial) were negative. Metabolic panel and ACE level were normal. Malignancy work-up—including serum and urine electrophoresis, CEA, CA-125, total‐body PET scan, and bone scan—was normal. CT thorax showed two benign pulmonary nodules. CT abdomen and pelvis revealed a soft‐tissue calcified mass in the right sacroiliac fossa and gluteal muscles. Initial right hip and pelvis radiographs showed femoral head lucencies compatible with subchondral cysts, moderate diffuse joint-space loss, and flattening of the femoral head. Five months later films demonstrated complete destruction of the femoral head, acetabular erosion, and lateral subluxation of the proximal femur. CT pelvis with contrast showed fragmented bone within the acetabular fossa. MRI of the right hip revealed extensive synovial hypertrophy, minimal bone marrow edema, an iliopsoas bursal fluid collection extending to the sciatic notch, and hip joint–capsule enlargement. Foot radiographs showed erosive changes in the MTPs; hand radiographs showed periarticular osteopenia of the MCPs and ulnar deviation. Imaging of the knees, shoulders, and spine revealed degenerative changes. Three hip aspirations yielded bloody fluid with 0.6 × 10^9 nucleated cells (17% neutrophils) and only extracellular CPPD crystals; a right knee aspiration showed bloody fluid with 35 × 10^9 nucleated cells (96% neutrophils) and no crystals.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Multicentric histiocytosis and sarcoidosis excluded — “Other potential etiologies…including systemic diseases such as multicentric histiocytosis and sarcoidosis, but the patient lacked any other features of these diseases.” 2. Avascular necrosis excluded — “An avascular type necrosis (AVN)…was possible, but unusual without a history of steroidal use… The imaging was also not classic for AVN and the patient did not have monoarthritis.” 3. Chronic infection excluded — “All cultures, including three sets of anaerobic and aerobic cultures and one set of systemic fungal and mycobacterial culture, were negative.”

title: Rare Causes of Musculoskeletal Pain: Thinking beyond Common Rheumatologic Diseases
case_prompt:
A 64-year-old man with a 35-year history of back pain, stiffness, and hip pain was evaluated for persistent symptoms despite previous treatments.  
• At age 29, he was diagnosed with ankylosing spondylitis based on progressive back pain, stiffness, hip pain, joint-space narrowing, and a history of ankle surgery for enthesopathy at age 19.  
• Imaging showed diffuse spinal and Achilles enthesophytes, interspinous ligament calcification, bilateral sacroiliac joint fusion (“severe AS”), and he had undergone bilateral hip arthroplasties.  
• He reported ongoing pain managed with hydrocodone and had derived no benefit from indomethacin or other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; he had also failed adalimumab therapy.  
• Atypical features included bilateral bowing of the proximal femoral shafts with subtrochanteric lateral proximal insufficiency fractures and progressive hearing loss since age 9.  
• A 20-year dental record review revealed chronic poor dentition with multiple abscesses, dental fractures, and extractions.  
• Laboratory studies showed inorganic phosphate 1.9 mg/dL (2.5–4.5), alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (40–129), creatinine 0.74 mg/dL (0.7–1.3), spot urine creatinine 76 mg/dL (20–320), parathyroid hormone 115 pg/mL (16–77), 1,25(OH)₂ vitamin D 49.9 pg/mL (19.7–79.3), and serum calcium 9.8 mg/dL (8.4–10.5).  
• A 24-hour urine phosphate was 1300 mg (226–1797), inappropriately normal in the setting of hypophosphatemia.  
• HLA-B27 testing was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis was considered due to ectopic bone formation in the spine — “considered a diagnosis of diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH), due to ectopic bone formation in the spine,” but this was inconsistent because “DISH affects older individuals, ligamentous calcification typically is limited to the thoracic spine, and enthesophytes are asymmetric.”

title: Hyperammonemic Coma in an Adult due to Ornithine Transcarbamylase Deficiency
case_prompt:
A 53-year-old man was found unarousable by his wife after two weeks of progressive fatigue without fevers, weight loss, nausea, or abdominal pain. His medical history included benign prostatic hypertrophy and hyperlipidemia; medications were terazosin, tamsulosin, and a recent short methylprednisolone taper; simvastatin was started one month earlier. He denied alcohol abuse, liver disease, recent surgery, or chemotherapy, and had no symptoms after using a permethrin‐based insecticide.

On initial evaluation at an outside hospital he was afebrile with normal vital signs but intermittently arousable only to verbal stimuli; the remainder of the neurologic exam was nonfocal. A head CT, MRI/MRA of the brain, and routine labs—including CBC, electrolytes, liver function tests, alcohol and drug screens—were unremarkable except an ammonia level of 202 µmol/L (normal 11–35). AST was 19 U/L, ALT 70 U/L, total bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL, and INR 1.12. Abdominal CT showed fatty infiltration of the liver without cirrhosis.

Despite aggressive lactulose therapy, his mental status worsened and ammonia remained elevated. He was intubated for airway protection and transferred. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 10. Labs showed leukocytosis (16.6 × 10^9/L), sodium 155 mEq/L, calcium 8.8 mg/dL, AST 34 U/L, ALT 50 U/L, ammonia 215 µmol/L, and INR 1.21. Brain and cervical‐spine MRI remained normal. EEG demonstrated diffuse slowing with triphasic waves. Over the next 12 hours ammonia rose to 516 µmol/L, and moderate‐flux continuous venovenous hemodialysis was initiated, reducing ammonia to 291 µmol/L without clinical improvement.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. High-dose chemotherapy–induced hyperammonemia was unlikely because he had no such history — “His past medical history was significant only for benign prostatic hypertrophy and hyperlipidemia.” 2. Glycine toxicity after prostate surgery was improbable as he had no transurethral procedures — “His only recent toxin exposure was the use of a permethrin-based insecticide, and he reported no symptoms during or after this exposure.”

title: Kimura's Disease Involving Thoracic and Abdominal Lymph Nodes in a Hemodialysis Patient
journal: The Korean Journal of Internal Medicine
case_prompt:
A 45-year-old man on maintenance hemodialysis for 3 years (ESRD due to chronic glomerulonephritis) presented with a 4-month history of progressively enlarging, painless neck masses. He denied fever, night sweats, or weight loss. His past history included tuberculous pleurisy 25 years earlier and hepatitis B surface antigen carriage; he reported no allergies.

On examination, there were firm, movable, non-tender masses (largest 3×3×2 cm) in the right anterior cervical triangle, as well as palpable lymph nodes in both axillary and inguinal regions; there was no hepatosplenomegaly or overlying skin changes.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL, WBC count 6.2×10^9/L with 12.1% eosinophils, platelet count 200×10^9/L, serum IgE 261 IU/mL, BUN 56 mg/dL, creatinine 4.8 mg/dL. Stool examinations, including ova and parasite evaluations, were negative. Fine-needle aspiration cytology of a cervical node revealed nonspecific reactive lymphadenitis.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest demonstrated enlarging lymph nodes adjacent to both sternocleidomastoid muscles and numerous nodes (>1 cm) in axillary, paratracheal, subcarinal, and intrapulmonary regions. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed multiple enhancing lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, peripancreatic, celiac, and paraaortic regions (largest 4.5 cm). A Gallium-67 scan revealed increased uptake in cervical, axillary, paraaortic, iliac, and inguinal lymph nodes.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignant lymphoma or metastatic cancer were strongly considered because of the widespread lymphadenopathy on imaging — “Clinically, KD may often be confused with malignant lymphoma or other metastatic cancers.” 2. Parasitic infection was evaluated in light of the peripheral eosinophilia — “Stool examinations, including ova and parasite evaluations, were negative.” 3. Tuberculosis was considered given his history and regional lymphadenopathy — “possibly as neoplastic disease or infection, such as tuberculosis, in Asian patients.”

title: Primary acinic cell carcinoma of mandible, report of a case and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_prompt: A 73-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a slow-growing mass of the left mandible noted over the past 15 months. His medical history was notable only for well-controlled hypertension. On intraoral examination, there was a painless bony expansion of the buccal aspect of the left posterior mandible; the overlying mucosa was intact, and no tooth mobility was present. Palpation of the neck revealed no lymphadenopathy. A panoramic radiograph showed a multilocular radiolucent lesion, measuring approximately 6.0 × 3.5 cm, extending from the ascending ramus to the periapical region of the left canine and involving the inferior alveolar canal. CT scans demonstrated a large expansile lytic lesion of the left mandibular body with cortical destruction. Ultrasound of the neck showed only reactive lymph nodes on the left. A contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no evidence of distant metastatic disease.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of ameloblastoma — “Considering the radiological appearance of this lesion, it could be in differential diagnosis with odontogenic jaw neoplasms, particularly ameloblastoma.” 2. Odontogenic carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma — “Primary intraosseous malignancies of the jaws consisted of three major histologic malignancies; salivary gland carcinomas, odontogenic carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas.”

title: Bartonella Neuroretinitis with Initial Seronegativity and an Absent Macular Star: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_prompt: A 23-year-old Malay man was referred for sudden-onset, painless, central blurring of vision in the right eye without floaters, photopsia, or eye-movement pain. Two days before visual symptoms, he had headache, low-grade fever (37.7 °C), and painful cervical lymphadenopathy treated with amoxicillin/clavulanic acid. He had no notable travel, sexual history, or sick contacts. Examination showed right-eye BCVA counting fingers at 1 meter, left-eye 6/6; a right grade-1 relative afferent pupillary defect with red desaturation; and normal left optic nerve function. Funduscopy of the right eye revealed diffuse optic disc swelling and macular edema, with a normal left fundus. Left submandibular lymphadenopathy was noted. No cranial nerve deficits were present. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (12.76×10^9 cells/L), neutrophilia (9.29×10^9/L), elevated C-reactive protein (62.6 mg/L), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (44 mm/hr). A pre-steroid workup was unremarkable. Autoimmune antibodies (anti-aquaporin 4, anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein, antinuclear antibody, anti–double-stranded DNA) were negative. Initial Bartonella and Borrelia IgG titres were both <1:64. Static automated perimetry demonstrated a right centrocecal scotoma. Optical coherence tomography of the right eye suggested intraretinal and subretinal fluid at the optic nerve extending to the macula. Fundus fluorescein angiography showed right-disc leakage. MRI of the brain, orbits, and anterior visual pathways with contrast and lumbar puncture were unremarkable. The patient was observed as an inpatient; his right-eye visual acuity improved spontaneously to 6/60-1. Five days after onset, hard exudates formed a partial macular star, progressing temporally.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Demyelinating optic neuritis was considered given the isolated disc swelling, but was excluded because “The appearance of a macular star generally precludes the possibility of a demyelinating aetiology.” 2. Malignant hypertension was considered because it can present with a macular star, but was unlikely since it “usually presents bilaterally and is often mistaken for neuroretinitis.” 3. Other optic neuropathies—including ischaemic optic neuropathy, papillitis, and papilloedema—were considered as “various causes of optic neuropathy (e.g., ischaemic optic neuropathy, papillitis, papilloedema)” but clinical context and investigations did not support them. 4. Haematological malignancies such as lymphoproliferative disorders were considered given systemic lymphadenopathy, but were unlikely as part of “CSD can mimic … haematological malignancies (e.g., lymphoproliferative disorders)” and systemic workup was unremarkable.

title: Extraosseous Calcifying Odontogenic Cyst Initially Interpreted as a Parulis
case_prompt: An 11-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of a nodule on the palatal mucosa behind the right upper central incisor. Eight months earlier, he had sustained dental trauma with a crown fracture of that tooth without pulp exposure. On examination, there was a sessile‐based nodule, narrow in the anteroposterior dimension, with a smooth surface, slight white coloration, and diffuse posterior erythema; it was non‐tender on palpation. Panoramic and periapical radiographs showed no bone involvement. Thermal sensitivity testing confirmed that the adjacent teeth were vital, effectively ruling out an acute or chronic periapical lesion.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of an acute or chronic periapical lesion — “thermal sensitivity testing confirmed that the teeth in the area were vital, ruling out an acute or chronic periapical lesion.” 2. Initial presumption of a parulis was excluded by vitality tests — “after ruling out the possibility of an endodontic origin of the lesion…” 3. Reactive lesions are common in young patients — “in the young population, reactive lesions seem to be the most recurrent differential diagnostic.” 4. Adult gingival cysts and peripheral odontogenic tumors are typical adult considerations — “in the adult population, adult gingival cyst and peripheral odontogenic tumors are the major differential diagnostic considerations; in the young population, reactive lesions seem…”

title: A rare case of liver regenerative and non-neoplastic lesion resembling a well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma
case_prompt: A 49-year-old man was referred for evaluation of two liver tumors detected on routine abdominal ultrasonography. He had no medical history, medication use, alcohol intake, or known viral hepatitis. On examination, he was afebrile, had no stigmata of chronic liver disease, and his body-mass index was 22.7. Laboratory tests, including alpha-fetoprotein and protein induced by vitamin K absence-II, were within normal limits. Abdominal sonography showed two well-circumscribed, hypoechoic lesions measuring 2.1 cm in segment 8 and 2.2 cm in segment 7 in a mildly fatty liver. Plain CT demonstrated both lesions as iso- to hyperdense relative to liver. On contrast-enhanced CT, the lesions showed arterial‐phase enhancement with delayed washout in the portal venous phase. On MRI, each lesion exhibited high-intensity rims and low-intensity centers on T1-weighted images, low intensity on T2-weighted images, and signal drop on opposed‐phase compared with in-phase images, indicating intralesional fat. Both lesions retained ethoxybenzyl contrast on hepatobiliary-phase imaging. Based on these findings, well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma was suspected, with benign nodules such as nodular regenerative hyperplasia or focal nodular hyperplasia–like lesions in the differential diagnosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma was strongly suspected preoperatively on the basis of contrast enhancement patterns in a patient with fatty liver — “Based on these findings, we suspected well‐differentiated HCC based on fatty liver…”

title: Williams–Campbell syndrome: an unusual presentation in an adult patient
case_prompt:
A 62-year-old woman presented with a two-day history of acute onset shortness of breath and productive cough, associated with fever, chills, generalized body aches, and pleuritic chest pain. She is a lifetime non-smoker with no alcohol use. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, asthma, osteoporosis, and multiple hospital admissions for recurrent pneumonia since childhood, including infections with Nocardia and Moraxella and one episode of Mycobacterium avium‐intracellulare in the past five years. She has no history of pulmonary tuberculosis or known tuberculosis contacts; her father had emphysema.

On examination, her temperature was 38.1 °C, pulse 112 beats/min, blood pressure 112/56 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 91% on room air. She was in mild respiratory distress with expiratory wheezes; examination of other systems was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 13,500/µl with immature granulocytosis and no eosinophilia; the basic metabolic panel was normal. A respiratory viral PCR panel and blood and urine cultures were negative.

Chest radiographs demonstrated hyperexpansion of the lungs, bronchiectasis in the right suprahilar region and left lung base, and a right-sided aortic arch. An inspiratory noncontrast CT scan of the chest showed hyperinflation of both lungs and multiple ballooning cystic bronchiectasis distal to the third-generation bronchi bilaterally. End-expiratory CT images revealed collapse of these bronchiectatic airways. Coronal minimal-intensity-projection images in inspiration confirmed cystic bronchiectasis in the fourth- to sixth-order bronchi in the right upper and left lower lobes, with calcified hilar lymph nodes and a right-sided aortic arch; expiratory coronal images again demonstrated airway collapse. An oblique volume-rendered reconstruction showed cystic bronchiectasis in the left lower lobe.

Pulmonary-function testing revealed an obstructive pattern with progression to fixed airway obstruction and no reversibility after bronchodilator administration. Quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE), serum α-1 antitrypsin levels, and sweat chloride were all within normal limits. Sputum acid-fast bacilli smear and fungal cultures were negative. Anti–nuclear antibody, rheumatoid factor, and Aspergillus‐specific immunoglobulins were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cystic fibrosis was excluded by a normal sweat chloride test — “The sweat chloride test was negative.” 2. Hypogammaglobulinemia and other immunodeficiencies were excluded by normal quantitative immunoglobulin levels — “quantification of serum IgG, IgA, IgE and IgM levels, which were normal.” 3. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis was excluded by normal Aspergillus‐specific immunoglobulins — “Anti Aspergillus immunoglobulins were within the normal range.” 4. Tuberculosis and nontuberculous mycobacterial infection were excluded by negative sputum acid-fast bacilli smear and culture and absence of risk factors — “the sputum sample for acid-fast bacilli was negative.” 5. Fungal pulmonary infection was excluded by negative sputum fungal cultures — “sputum sample sent for fungal cultures did not report any growth.” 6. Rheumatic causes of bronchiectasis were excluded by negative serologies — “Anti-nuclear antibody and serum rheumatoid factors were negative.”

title: A case of mistaken identity: When lupus masquerades as primary myelofibrosis
case_prompt:
A 44-year-old African American man with a family history of systemic lupus erythematosus presented with progressive fatigue, severe anemia, and night sweats over several months. He also reported photosensitivity and nonspecific arthralgias. His vital signs were stable, and physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory evaluation:
• He required red blood cell transfusions every 1–2 weeks for a full year.  
• Lactate dehydrogenase was mildly elevated.  
• Peripheral blood smear did not show leukoerythroblastosis.  

Bone marrow studies:
• Aspirate and core biopsy showed 95% hypercellularity with panhyperplasia and moderate reticulin fibrosis (grade 2).  
• There was a slight increase in immature precursors and megakaryocytic proliferation without atypia or dysplasia.  
• Touch preparations revealed 3% blasts.  
• Molecular testing for JAK2 (V617F and exon 12) and MPL mutations was negative.

Clinical course:
He remained transfusion-dependent for one year, then unexpectedly became transfusion-independent with normalization of hemoglobin. One year later, anemia recurred and he again became transfusion-dependent. A direct Coomb’s test was negative, and his reticulocyte count and haptoglobin level were within normal limits. He developed multiple red cell alloantibodies and was treated with dexamethasone, resulting in resolution of anemia for several months. After steroids were discontinued, anemia worsened. The absence of typical PMF-related bone marrow findings, family history of SLE, and connective tissue disease–related symptoms prompted an evaluation for SLE.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hemolytic anemia was excluded given a negative direct Coomb’s test and normal reticulocyte count and haptoglobin levels — “direct Coomb’s test was negative, while his reticulocyte counts and haptoglobin levels were within normal limits.” 2. Autoimmune myelofibrosis related to systemic lupus erythematosus was suspected based on family history of SLE, photosensitivity, arthralgias, and atypical marrow features for PMF — “The absence of typical PMF-related BM findings, family history of SLE, and the presence of connective tissue disease–related symptoms prompted an evaluation for SLE.”
